Chapter I.
The Shade of Cardinal Richelieu.


In a splendid chamber of the Palais Royal, formerly styled the Palais
Cardinal, a man was sitting in deep reverie, his head supported on his
hands, leaning over a gilt and inlaid table which was covered with
letters and papers. Behind this figure glowed a vast fireplace alive
with leaping flames; great logs of oak blazed and crackled on the
polished brass andirons whose flicker shone upon the superb habiliments
of the lonely tenant of the room, which was illumined grandly by twin
candelabra rich with wax-lights.

Any one who happened at that moment to contemplate that red simar芒聙聰the
gorgeous robe of office芒聙聰and the rich lace, or who gazed on that pale
brow, bent in anxious meditation, might, in the solitude of that
apartment, combined with the silence of the ante-chambers and the
measured paces of the guards upon the landing-place, have fancied that
the shade of Cardinal Richelieu lingered still in his accustomed haunt.

It was, alas! the ghost of former greatness. France enfeebled, the
authority of her sovereign contemned, her nobles returning to their
former turbulence and insolence, her enemies within her frontiers芒聙聰all
proved the great Richelieu no longer in existence.

In truth, that the red simar which occupied the wonted place was his no
longer, was still more strikingly obvious from the isolation which
seemed, as we have observed, more appropriate to a phantom than a
living creature芒聙聰from the corridors deserted by courtiers, and courts
crowded with guards芒聙聰from that spirit of bitter ridicule, which, arising
from the streets below, penetrated through the very casements of the
room, which resounded with the murmurs of a whole city leagued against
the minister; as well as from the distant and incessant sounds of guns
firing芒聙聰let off, happily, without other end or aim, except to show to
the guards, the Swiss troops and the military who surrounded the Palais
Royal, that the people were possessed of arms.

The shade of Richelieu was Mazarin. Now Mazarin was alone and
defenceless, as he well knew.

芒聙聹Foreigner!芒聙聺 he ejaculated, 芒聙聹Italian! that is their mean yet mighty
byword of reproach芒聙聰the watchword with which they assassinated, hanged,
and made away with Concini; and if I gave them their way they would
assassinate, hang, and make away with me in the same manner, although
they have nothing to complain of except a tax or two now and then.
Idiots! ignorant of their real enemies, they do not perceive that it is
not the Italian who speaks French badly, but those who can say fine
things to them in the purest Parisian accent, who are their real foes.

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 Mazarin continued, whilst his wonted smile, full of
subtlety, lent a strange expression to his pale lips; 芒聙聹yes, these
noises prove to me, indeed, that the destiny of favorites is
precarious; but ye shall know I am no ordinary favorite. No! The Earl
of Essex, 芒聙聶tis true, wore a splendid ring, set with diamonds, given him
by his royal mistress, whilst I芒聙聰I have nothing but a simple circlet of
gold, with a cipher on it and a date; but that ring has been blessed in
the chapel of the Palais Royal,* so they will never ruin me, as they
long to do, and whilst they shout, 芒聙聵Down with Mazarin!芒聙聶 I, unknown, and
unperceived by them, incite them to cry out, 芒聙聵Long live the Duke de
Beaufort芒聙聶 one day; another, 芒聙聵Long live the Prince de Cond脙漏;芒聙聶 and again,
芒聙聵Long live the parliament!芒聙聶芒聙聺 And at this word the smile on the
cardinal芒聙聶s lips assumed an expression of hatred, of which his mild
countenance seemed incapable. 芒聙聹The parliament! We shall soon see how to
dispose,芒聙聺 he continued, 芒聙聹of the parliament! Both Orl脙漏ans and Montargis
are ours. It will be a work of time, but those who have begun by crying
out: Down with Mazarin! will finish by shouting out, Down with all the
people I have mentioned, each in his turn.

* It is said that Mazarin, who, though a cardinal, had not taken such
vows as to prevent it, was secretly married to Anne of Austria.芒聙聰La
Porte芒聙聶s Memoirs.

芒聙聹Richelieu, whom they hated during his lifetime and whom they now
praise after his death, was even less popular than I am. Often he was
driven away, oftener still had he a dread of being sent away. The queen
will never banish me, and even were I obliged to yield to the populace
she would yield with me; if I fly, she will fly; and then we shall see
how the rebels will get on without either king or queen.

芒聙聹Oh, were I not a foreigner! were I but a Frenchman! were I but of
gentle birth!芒聙聺

The position of the cardinal was indeed critical, and recent events had
added to his difficulties. Discontent had long pervaded the lower ranks
of society in France. Crushed and impoverished by taxation芒聙聰imposed by
Mazarin, whose avarice impelled him to grind them down to the very
dust芒聙聰the people, as the Advocate-General Talon described it, had
nothing left to them except their souls; and as those could not be sold
by auction, they began to murmur. Patience had in vain been recommended
to them by reports of brilliant victories gained by France; laurels,
however, were not meat and drink, and the people had for some time been
in a state of discontent.

Had this been all, it might not, perhaps, have greatly signified; for
when the lower classes alone complained, the court of France, separated
as it was from the poor by the intervening classes of the gentry and
the _bourgeoisie_, seldom listened to their voice; but unluckily,
Mazarin had had the imprudence to attack the magistrates and had sold
no less than twelve appointments in the Court of Requests, at a high
price; and as the officers of that court paid very dearly for their
places, and as the addition of twelve new colleagues would necessarily
lower the value of each place, the old functionaries formed a union
amongst themselves, and, enraged, swore on the Bible not to allow of
this addition to their number, but to resist all the persecutions which
might ensue; and should any one of them chance to forfeit his post by
this resistance, to combine to indemnify him for his loss.

Now the following occurrences had taken place between the two
contending parties.

On the seventh of January between seven and eight hundred tradesmen had
assembled in Paris to discuss a new tax which was to be levied on house
property. They deputed ten of their number to wait upon the Duke of
Orl脙漏ans, who, according to his custom, affected popularity. The duke
received them and they informed him that they were resolved not to pay
this tax, even if they were obliged to defend themselves against its
collectors by force of arms. They were listened to with great
politeness by the duke, who held out hopes of easier measures, promised
to speak in their behalf to the queen, and dismissed them with the
ordinary expression of royalty, 芒聙聹We will see what we can do.芒聙聺

Two days afterward these same magistrates appeared before the cardinal
and their spokesman addressed Mazarin with so much fearlessness and
determination that the minister was astounded and sent the deputation
away with the same answer as it had received from the Duke of
Orl脙漏ans芒聙聰that he would see what could be done; and in accordance with
that intention a council of state was assembled and the superintendent
of finance was summoned.

This man, named Emery, was the object of popular detestation, in the
first place because he _was_ superintendent of finance, and every
superintendent of finance deserved to be hated; in the second place,
because he rather deserved the odium which he had incurred.

He was the son of a banker at Lyons named Particelli, who, after
becoming a bankrupt, chose to change his name to Emery; and Cardinal
Richelieu having discovered in him great financial aptitude, had
introduced him with a strong recommendation to Louis XIII. under his
assumed name, in order that he might be appointed to the post he
subsequently held.

芒聙聹You surprise me!芒聙聺 exclaimed the monarch. 芒聙聹I am rejoiced to hear you
speak of Monsieur d芒聙聶Emery as calculated for a post which requires a man
of probity. I was really afraid that you were going to force that
villain Particelli upon me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 replied Richelieu, 芒聙聹rest assured that Particelli, the man to
whom your majesty refers, has been hanged.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah; so much the better!芒聙聺 exclaimed the king. 芒聙聹It is not for nothing
that I am styled Louis the Just,芒聙聺 and he signed Emery芒聙聶s appointment.

This was the same Emery who became eventually superintendent of
finance.

He was sent for by the ministers and he came before them pale and
trembling, declaring that his son had very nearly been assassinated the
day before, near the palace. The mob had insulted him on account of the
ostentatious luxury of his wife, whose house was hung with red velvet
edged with gold fringe. This lady was the daughter of Nicholas de
Camus, who arrived in Paris with twenty francs in his pocket, became
secretary of state, and accumulated wealth enough to divide nine
millions of francs among his children and to keep an income of forty
thousand for himself.

The fact was that Emery芒聙聶s son had run a great chance of being
suffocated, one of the rioters having proposed to squeeze him until he
gave up all the gold he had swallowed. Nothing, therefore, was settled
that day, as Emery芒聙聶s head was not steady enough for business after such
an occurrence.

On the next day Mathieu Mol脙漏, the chief president, whose courage at
this crisis, says the Cardinal de Retz, was equal to that of the Duc de
Beaufort and the Prince de Cond脙漏芒聙聰in other words, of the two men who
were considered the bravest in France芒聙聰had been attacked in his turn.
The people threatened to hold him responsible for the evils that hung
over them. But the chief president had replied with his habitual
coolness, without betraying either disturbance or surprise, that should
the agitators refuse obedience to the king芒聙聶s wishes he would have
gallows erected in the public squares and proceed at once to hang the
most active among them. To which the others had responded that they
would be glad to see the gallows erected; they would serve for the
hanging of those detestable judges who purchased favor at court at the
price of the people芒聙聶s misery.

Nor was this all. On the eleventh the queen in going to mass at Notre
Dame, as she always did on Saturdays, was followed by more than two
hundred women demanding justice. These poor creatures had no bad
intentions. They wished only to be allowed to fall on their knees
before their sovereign, and that they might move her to compassion; but
they were prevented by the royal guard and the queen proceeded on her
way, haughtily disdainful of their entreaties.

At length parliament was convoked; the authority of the king was to be
maintained.

One day芒聙聰it was the morning of the day my story begins芒聙聰the king, Louis
XIV., then ten years of age, went in state, under pretext of returning
thanks for his recovery from the small-pox, to Notre Dame. He took the
opportunity of calling out his guard, the Swiss troops and the
musketeers, and he had planted them round the Palais Royal, on the
quays, and on the Pont Neuf. After mass the young monarch drove to the
Parliament House, where, upon the throne, he hastily confirmed not only
such edicts as he had already passed, but issued new ones, each one,
according to Cardinal de Retz, more ruinous than the others芒聙聰a
proceeding which drew forth a strong remonstrance from the chief
president, Mol脙漏芒聙聰whilst President Blancmesnil and Councillor Broussel
raised their voices in indignation against fresh taxes.

The king returned amidst the silence of a vast multitude to the Palais
Royal. All minds were uneasy, most were foreboding, many of the people
used threatening language.

At first, indeed, they were doubtful whether the king芒聙聶s visit to the
parliament had been in order to lighten or increase their burdens; but
scarcely was it known that the taxes were to be still further
increased, when cries of 芒聙聹Down with Mazarin!芒聙聺 芒聙聹Long live Broussel!芒聙聺
芒聙聹Long live Blancmesnil!芒聙聺 resounded through the city. For the people had
learned that Broussel and Blancmesnil had made speeches in their
behalf, and, although the eloquence of these deputies had been without
avail, it had none the less won for them the people芒聙聶s good-will. All
attempts to disperse the groups collected in the streets, or silence
their exclamations, were in vain. Orders had just been given to the
royal guards and the Swiss guards, not only to stand firm, but to send
out patrols to the streets of Saint Denis and Saint Martin, where the
people thronged and where they were the most vociferous, when the mayor
of Paris was announced at the Palais Royal.

He was shown in directly; he came to say that if these offensive
precautions were not discontinued, in two hours Paris would be under
arms.

Deliberations were being held when a lieutenant in the guards, named
Comminges, made his appearance, with his clothes all torn, his face
streaming with blood. The queen on seeing him uttered a cry of surprise
and asked him what was going on.

As the mayor had foreseen, the sight of the guards had exasperated the
mob. The tocsin was sounded. Comminges had arrested one of the
ringleaders and had ordered him to be hanged near the cross of Du
Trahoir; but in attempting to execute this command the soldiery were
attacked in the market-place with stones and halberds; the delinquent
had escaped to the Rue des Lombards and rushed into a house. They broke
open the doors and searched the dwelling, but in vain. Comminges,
wounded by a stone which had struck him on the forehead, had left a
picket in the street and returned to the Palais Royal, followed by a
menacing crowd, to tell his story.

This account confirmed that of the mayor. The authorities were not in a
condition to cope with serious revolt. Mazarin endeavored to circulate
among the people a report that troops had only been stationed on the
quays and on the Pont Neuf, on account of the ceremonial of the day,
and that they would soon withdraw. In fact, about four o芒聙聶clock they
were all concentrated about the Palais Royal, the courts and ground
floors of which were filled with musketeers and Swiss guards, and there
awaited the outcome of all this disturbance.

Such was the state of affairs at the very moment we introduced our
readers to the study of Cardinal Mazarin芒聙聰once that of Cardinal
Richelieu. We have seen in what state of mind he listened to the
murmurs from below, which even reached him in his seclusion, and to the
guns, the firing of which resounded through that room. All at once he
raised his head; his brow slightly contracted like that of a man who
has formed a resolution; he fixed his eyes upon an enormous clock that
was about to strike ten, and taking up a whistle of silver gilt that
stood upon the table near him, he shrilled it twice.

A door hidden in the tapestry opened noiselessly and a man in black
silently advanced and stood behind the chair on which Mazarin sat.

芒聙聹Bernouin,芒聙聺 said the cardinal, not turning round, for having whistled,
he knew that it was his _valet-de-chambre_ who was behind him; 芒聙聹what
musketeers are now within the palace?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Black Musketeers, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What company?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tr脙漏ville芒聙聶s company.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is there any officer belonging to this company in the ante-chamber?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lieutenant d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A man on whom we can depend, I hope.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Give me a uniform of one of these musketeers and help me to put it
on.芒聙聺

The valet went out as silently as he had entered and appeared in a few
minutes bringing the dress demanded.

The cardinal, in deep thought and in silence, began to take off the
robes of state he had assumed in order to be present at the sitting of
parliament, and to attire himself in the military coat, which he wore
with a certain degree of easy grace, owing to his former campaigns in
Italy. When he was completely dressed he said:

芒聙聹Send hither Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

The valet went out of the room, this time by the centre door, but still
as silently as before; one might have fancied him an apparition.

When he was left alone the cardinal looked at himself in the glass with
a feeling of self-satisfaction. Still young芒聙聰for he was scarcely
forty-six years of age芒聙聰he possessed great elegance of form and was
above the middle height; his complexion was brilliant and beautiful;
his glance full of expression; his nose, though large, was well
proportioned; his forehead broad and majestic; his hair, of a chestnut
color, was curled slightly; his beard, which was darker than his hair,
was turned carefully with a curling iron, a practice that greatly
improved it. After a short time the cardinal arranged his shoulder
belt, then looked with great complacency at his hands, which were most
elegant and of which he took the greatest care; and throwing on one
side the large kid gloves tried on at first, as belonging to the
uniform, he put on others of silk only. At this instant the door
opened.

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said the _valet-de-chambre_.

An officer, as he spoke, entered the apartment. He was a man between
thirty-nine and forty years of age, of medium height but a very well
proportioned figure; with an intellectual and animated physiognomy; his
beard black, and his hair turning gray, as often happens when people
have found life either too gay or too sad, more especially when they
happen to be of swart complexion.

D芒聙聶Artagnan advanced a few steps into the apartment.

How perfectly he remembered his former entrance into that very room!
Seeing, however, no one there except a musketeer of his own troop, he
fixed his eyes upon the supposed soldier, in whose dress, nevertheless,
he recognized at the first glance the cardinal.

The lieutenant remained standing in a dignified but respectful posture,
such as became a man of good birth, who had in the course of his life
been frequently in the society of the highest nobles.

The cardinal looked at him with a cunning rather than serious glance,
yet he examined his countenance with attention and after a momentary
silence said:

芒聙聹You are Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am that individual,芒聙聺 replied the officer.

Mazarin gazed once more at a countenance full of intelligence, the play
of which had been, nevertheless, subdued by age and experience; and
D芒聙聶Artagnan received the penetrating glance like one who had formerly
sustained many a searching look, very different, indeed, from those
which were inquiringly directed on him at that instant.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 resumed the cardinal, 芒聙聹you are to come with me, or rather, I am
to go with you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am at your command, my lord,芒聙聺 returned D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹I wish to visit in person the outposts which surround the Palais
Royal; do you suppose that there is any danger in so doing?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Danger, my lord!芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan with a look of astonishment,
芒聙聹what danger?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am told that there is a general insurrection.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The uniform of the king芒聙聶s musketeers carries a certain respect with
it, and even if that were not the case I would engage with four of my
men to put to flight a hundred of these clowns.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Did you witness the injury sustained by Comminges?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Comminges is in the guards and not in the musketeers芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Which means, I suppose, that the musketeers are better soldiers than
the guards.芒聙聺 The cardinal smiled as he spoke.

芒聙聹Every one likes his own uniform best, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Myself excepted,芒聙聺 and again Mazarin smiled; 芒聙聹for you perceive that I

芒聙聹Lord bless us! this is modesty indeed!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Had I such

芒聙聹Yes, but for to-night芒聙聶s adventure I don芒聙聶t suppose my dress would have

The valet instantly brought to his master a regimental hat with a wide
brim. The cardinal put it on in military style.

芒聙聹Your horses are ready saddled in their stables, are they not?芒聙聺 he

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, let us set out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How many men does your eminence wish to escort you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You say that with four men you will undertake to disperse a hundred

hundred, take eight芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹As many as my lord wishes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will follow you. This way芒聙聰light us downstairs Bernouin.芒聙聺

The valet held a wax-light; the cardinal took a key from his bureau and
opening the door of a secret stair descended into the court of the
Palais Royal.




Chapter II.
A Nightly Patrol.


In ten minutes Mazarin and his party were traversing the street 芒聙聹Les
Bons Enfants芒聙聺 behind the theatre built by Richelieu expressly for the
play of 芒聙聹Mirame,芒聙聺 and in which Mazarin, who was an amateur of music,
but not of literature, had introduced into France the first opera that
was ever acted in that country.

The appearance of the town denoted the greatest agitation. Numberless
groups paraded the streets and, whatever D芒聙聶Artagnan might think of it,
it was obvious that the citizens had for the night laid aside their
usual forbearance, in order to assume a warlike aspect. From time to
time noises came in the direction of the public markets. The report of
firearms was heard near the Rue Saint Denis and occasionally church
bells began to ring indiscriminately and at the caprice of the
populace. D芒聙聶Artagnan, meantime, pursued his way with the indifference
of a man upon whom such acts of folly made no impression. When he
approached a group in the middle of the street he urged his horse upon
it without a word of warning; and the members of the group, whether
rebels or not, as if they knew with what sort of a man they had to
deal, at once gave place to the patrol. The cardinal envied that
composure, which he attributed to the habit of meeting danger; but none
the less he conceived for the officer under whose orders he had for the
moment placed himself, that consideration which even prudence pays to
careless courage. On approaching an outpost near the Barri脙篓re des
Sergens, the sentinel cried out, 芒聙聹Who芒聙聶s there?芒聙聺 and D芒聙聶Artagnan
answered芒聙聰having first asked the word of the cardinal芒聙聰芒聙聹Louis and
Rocroy.芒聙聺 After which he inquired if Lieutenant Comminges were not the
commanding officer at the outpost. The soldier replied by pointing out

the officer D芒聙聶Artagnan was seeking.

芒聙聹Here is Monsieur Comminges,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, returning to the

芒聙聹Well done, Guitant,芒聙聺 cried the cardinal to the equestrian; 芒聙聹I see

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 replied Guitant, 芒聙聹I was observing that we live in troublous

芒聙聹And what was Comminges saying to you in reply, my good Guitant?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Comminges, 芒聙聹I answered that to compose a Ligue only one

芒聙聹No, but they mean to make a Fronde, as they call it,芒聙聺 said Guitant.

芒聙聹And what is a Fronde?芒聙聺 inquired Mazarin.

芒聙聹My lord, Fronde is the name the discontented give to their party.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what is the origin of this name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It seems that some days since Councillor Bachaumont remarked at the

urchins who run off the moment the constable appears, only to return to

the word and the insurrectionists are called 芒聙聵Frondeurs,芒聙聶 and yesterday
every article sold was 芒聙聵脙聽 la Fronde;芒聙聶 bread 芒聙聵脙聽 la Fronde,芒聙聶 hats 芒聙聵脙聽 la
Fronde,芒聙聶 to say nothing of gloves, pocket-handkerchiefs, and fans; but
listen芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

At that moment a window opened and a man began to sing:

芒聙聹A tempest from the Fronde

Did blow to-day:

I think 芒聙聶twill blow

Sieur Mazarin away.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Insolent wretch!芒聙聺 cried Guitant.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Comminges, who, irritated by his wounds, wished for

And as he spoke he put his hand on the holster of his uncle芒聙聶s

芒聙聹Certainly not! certainly not,芒聙聺 exclaimed Mazarin. 芒聙聹_Diavolo!_ my dear

芒聙聹Just so,芒聙聺 muttered Comminges, looking after Mazarin. 芒聙聹True, I forgot;

The street of Saint Honor脙漏, when the cardinal and his party passed

D芒聙聶Artagnan passed through the very midst of this discontented mob just

芒聙聹Ah, captain!芒聙聺 said the officer, 芒聙聹everything is quiet hereabout芒聙聰if I

And he pointed to a magnificent hotel situated on the very spot whereon

芒聙聹In that hotel? it is the Hotel Rambouillet,芒聙聺 cried Guitant.

芒聙聹I really don芒聙聶t know what hotel it is; all I do know is that I observed

芒聙聹Just as you please, my lord. I do not understand Italian.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but you understand French,芒聙聺 and Mazarin laid his hand upon

芒聙聹Certainly. I have already answered that question in the affirmative;

芒聙聹Yes! ah yes!芒聙聺 Mazarin bit his lips as he spoke; 芒聙聹I know your devotion

芒聙聹I have been a captain in the queen芒聙聶s guards for twenty years,芒聙聺 was the

芒聙聹_En route_, Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said the cardinal; 芒聙聹all goes well in

D芒聙聶Artagnan, in the meantime, had taken the head of his detachment

He led the way toward the hill of Saint Roche. The Rue Richelieu and
the Rue Villedot were then, owing to their vicinity to the ramparts,
less frequented than any others in that direction, for the town was
thinly inhabited thereabout.

芒聙聹Who is in command here?芒聙聺 asked the cardinal.

芒聙聹_Diavolo!_ Speak to him yourself, for ever since you were deputed by

芒聙聹I am aware of that, and I have told him a hundred times that he was

芒聙聹Yes, but I could give him the order芒聙聰I, Guitant芒聙聰and I preferred to give

Guitant, without reply, rode forward and desired the sentinel to call

芒聙聹Ah! so you are here!芒聙聺 cried the officer, in the tone of ill-humor

芒聙聹I wish to know芒聙聰can you tell me, pray芒聙聰is anything fresh occurring in

芒聙聹What do you mean? People cry out, 芒聙聵Long live the king! down with

芒聙聹Well, well, Monsieur de Villequier,芒聙聺 Mazarin interposed, 芒聙聹I shall make

芒聙聹Stop,芒聙聺 exclaimed Villequier, 芒聙聹so Mazarin was here! so much the better.
I have been waiting for a long time to tell him what I think of him. I

He turned away and went off to his post, whistling a tune then popular

on his side except the queen; and then Anne of Austria had so often
deserted her friends that her support seemed most precarious. During
the whole of this nocturnal ride, during the whole time that he was
endeavoring to understand the various characters of Comminges, Guitant
and Villequier, Mazarin was, in truth, studying more especially one

The name of D芒聙聶Artagnan was not altogether new to Mazarin, who, although
he did not arrive in France before the year 1634 or 1635, that is to
say, about eight or nine years after the events which we have related
in a preceding narrative, * fancied he had heard it pronounced as that
of one who was said to be a model of courage, address and loyalty.

* 芒聙聹The Three Musketeers.芒聙聺

Possessed by this idea, the cardinal resolved to know all about
D芒聙聶Artagnan immediately; of course he could not inquire from D芒聙聶Artagnan
himself who he was and what had been his career; he remarked, however,

with a Gascon accent. Now the Italians and the Gascons are too much
alike and know each other too well ever to trust what any one of them
may say of himself; so in reaching the walls which surrounded the

D芒聙聶Artagnan and requesting him to wait in the court of the Palais Royal,
he made a sign to Guitant to follow him.

They both dismounted, consigned their horses to the lackey who had

芒聙聹My dear friend,芒聙聺 said the cardinal, leaning, as they walked through

芒聙聹Yes, it芒聙聶s true. I have,芒聙聺 returned Guitant.

芒聙聹Now, my dear Guitant, I have often remarked that in addition to your

芒聙聹Plague on芒聙聶t, my lord, have you brought me here to get my horoscope out

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan? I have had no occasion to notice him

Mazarin shook his head.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹some ministers are fortunate and find out all that they

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 replied Guitant, 芒聙聹such ministers do not weigh men in the

芒聙聹Eh, _pardieu!_芒聙聺 said Mazarin, with a grimace which he always made when

芒聙聹Does my lord seriously wish me to name any one who was mixed up in the

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s my concern; I will make him speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, my lord, 芒聙聶tis not easy to make people say what they don芒聙聶t wish to

芒聙聹The Comte de Rochefort!芒聙聺

芒聙聹He was Cardinal Richelieu芒聙聶s creature, my lord. I warn you, however,

芒聙聹Yes, yes, Guitant,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹Richelieu was a great man, a very

Here they separated and bidding adieu to Guitant in the court of the

It was D芒聙聶Artagnan, who was waiting for him.

芒聙聹Come hither,芒聙聺 said Mazarin in his softest voice; 芒聙聹I have an order to

D芒聙聶Artagnan bent low and following the cardinal up the secret staircase,

The cardinal seated himself before his bureau and taking a sheet of

without showing either impatience or curiosity. He was like a soldierly

The cardinal folded and sealed his letter.

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you are to take this dispatch to the

D芒聙聶Artagnan took the letter, touched his hat with his hand, turned round

the courtyard.




Chapter III.
Dead Animosities.


D芒聙聶Artagnan arrived at the Bastile just as it was striking half-past
eight. His visit was announced to the governor, who, on hearing that he
came from the cardinal, went to meet him and received him at the top of
the great flight of steps outside the door. The governor of the Bastile
was Monsieur du Tremblay, the brother of the famous Capuchin, Joseph,
that fearful favorite of Richelieu芒聙聶s, who went by the name of the Gray
Cardinal.

During the period that the Duc de Bassompierre passed in the
Bastile芒聙聰where he remained for twelve long years芒聙聰when his companions, in
their dreams of liberty, said to each other: 芒聙聹As for me, I shall go out
of the prison at such a time,芒聙聺 and another, at such and such a time,
the duke used to answer, 芒聙聹As for me, gentlemen, I shall leave only when
Monsieur du Tremblay leaves;芒聙聺 meaning that at the death of the cardinal
Du Tremblay would certainly lose his place at the Bastile and De
Bassompierre regain his at court.

His prediction was nearly fulfilled, but in a very different way from
that which De Bassompierre supposed; for after the death of Richelieu
everything went on, contrary to expectation, in the same way as before;
and Bassompierre had little chance of leaving his prison.

Monsieur du Tremblay received D芒聙聶Artagnan with extreme politeness and
invited him to sit down with him to supper, of which he was himself
about to partake.

芒聙聹I should be delighted to do so,芒聙聺 was the reply; 芒聙聹but if I am not

There was little change in the cardinal; still dressed with sedulous

芒聙聹It is as I have the honor of telling it to your excellency.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell me a little about it, my dear Monsieur de Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is somewhat difficult, my lord,芒聙聺 said Rochefort, with a smile.

芒聙聹Then he will tell it me himself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I doubt it, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why do you doubt it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because the secret does not belong to him; because, as I have told
you, it has to do with a great queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And he was alone in achieving an enterprise like that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, my lord, he had three colleagues, three brave men, men such as you
were wishing for just now.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And were these four men attached to each other, true in heart, really
united?芒聙聺

芒聙聹As if they had been one man芒聙聰as if their four hearts had pulsated in
one breast.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You pique my curiosity, dear Rochefort; pray tell me the whole story.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is impossible; but I will tell you a true story, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pray do so, I delight in stories,芒聙聺 cried the cardinal.

芒聙聹Listen, then,芒聙聺 returned Rochefort, as he spoke endeavoring to read in
that subtle countenance the cardinal芒聙聶s motive. 芒聙聹Once upon a time there
lived a queen芒聙聰a powerful monarch芒聙聰who reigned over one of the greatest
kingdoms of the universe; and a minister; and this minister wished much
to injure the queen, whom once he had loved too well. (Do not try, my
lord, you cannot guess who it is; all this happened long before you
came into the country where this queen reigned.) There came to the
court an ambassador so brave, so magnificent, so elegant, that every
woman lost her heart to him; and the queen had even the indiscretion to
give him certain ornaments so rare that they could never be replaced by
any like them.

芒聙聹As these ornaments were given by the king the minister persuaded his
majesty to insist upon the queen芒聙聶s appearing in them as part of her
jewels at a ball which was soon to take place. There is no occasion to
tell you, my lord, that the minister knew for a fact that these
ornaments had sailed away with the ambassador, who was far away, beyond
seas. This illustrious queen had fallen low as the least of her
subjects芒聙聰fallen from her high estate.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Indeed!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, my lord, four men resolved to save her. These four men were not
princes, neither were they dukes, neither were they men in power; they
were not even rich. They were four honest soldiers, each with a good
heart, a good arm and a sword at the service of those who wanted it.
They set out. The minister knew of their departure and had planted
people on the road to prevent them ever reaching their destination.
Three of them were overwhelmed and disabled by numerous assailants; one
of them alone arrived at the port, having either killed or wounded
those who wished to stop him. He crossed the sea and brought back the
set of ornaments to the great queen, who was able to wear them on her
shoulder on the appointed day; and this very nearly ruined the
minister. What do you think of that exploit, my lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is magnificent!芒聙聺 said Mazarin, thoughtfully.

芒聙聹Well, I know of ten such men.芒聙聺

Mazarin made no reply; he reflected.

Five or six minutes elapsed.

芒聙聹You have nothing more to ask of me, my lord?芒聙聺 said Rochefort.

芒聙聹Yes. And you say that Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan was one of those four men?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He led the enterprise.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who were the others?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I leave it to Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan to name them, my lord. They were his
friends and not mine. He alone would have any influence with them; I do
not even know them under their true names.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You suspect me, Monsieur de Rochefort; I want him and you and all to
aid me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Begin with me, my lord; for after five or six years of imprisonment it
is natural to feel some curiosity as to one芒聙聶s destination.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You, my dear Monsieur de Rochefort, shall have the post of confidence;
you shall go to Vincennes, where Monsieur de Beaufort is confined; you
will guard him well for me. Well, what is the matter?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The matter is that you have proposed to me what is impossible,芒聙聺 said
Rochefort, shaking his head with an air of disappointment.

芒聙聹What! impossible? And why is it impossible?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because Monsieur de Beaufort is one of my friends, or rather, I am one
of his. Have you forgotten, my lord, that it is he who answered for me
to the queen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Since then Monsieur de Beaufort has become an enemy of the State.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That may be, my lord; but since I am neither king nor queen nor
minister, he is not my enemy and I cannot accept your offer.芒聙聺

芒聙聹This, then, is what you call devotion! I congratulate you. Your
devotion does not commit you too far, Monsieur de Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And then, my lord,芒聙聺 continued Rochefort, 芒聙聹you understand that to
emerge from the Bastile in order to enter Vincennes is only to change
one芒聙聶s prison.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Say at once that you are on the side of Monsieur de Beaufort; that
will be the most sincere line of conduct,芒聙聺 said Mazarin.

芒聙聹My lord, I have been so long shut up, that I am only of one party芒聙聰I am
for fresh air. Employ me in any other way; employ me even actively, but
let it be on the high roads.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My dear Monsieur de Rochefort,芒聙聺 Mazarin replied in a tone of raillery,
芒聙聹you think yourself still a young man; your spirit is that of the
phoenix, but your strength fails you. Believe me, you ought now to take
a rest. Here!芒聙聺

芒聙聹You decide, then, nothing about me, my lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the contrary, I have come to a decision.芒聙聺

Bernouin came into the room.

芒聙聹Call an officer of justice,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹and stay close to me,芒聙聺 he
added, in a low tone.

The officer entered. Mazarin wrote a few words, which he gave to this
man; then he bowed.

芒聙聹Adieu, Monsieur de Rochefort,芒聙聺 he said.

Rochefort bent low.

芒聙聹I see, my lord, I am to be taken back to the Bastile.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are sagacious.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall return thither, my lord, but it is a mistake on your part not
to employ me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You? the friend of my greatest foes? Don芒聙聶t suppose that you are the
only person who can serve me, Monsieur de Rochefort. I shall find many
men as able as you are.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wish you may, my lord,芒聙聺 replied De Rochefort.

He was then reconducted by the little staircase, instead of passing
through the ante-chamber where D芒聙聶Artagnan was waiting. In the courtyard
the carriage and the four musketeers were ready, but he looked around
in vain for his friend.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 he muttered to himself, 芒聙聹this changes the situation, and if there
is still a crowd of people in the streets we will try to show Mazarin
that we are still, thank God, good for something else than keeping
guard over a prisoner;芒聙聺 and he jumped into the carriage with the
alacrity of a man of five-and-twenty.




Chapter IV.
Anne of Austria at the Age of Forty-six.


When left alone with Bernouin, Mazarin was for some minutes lost in
thought. He had gained much information, but not enough. Mazarin was a
cheat at the card-table. This is a detail preserved to us by Brienne.
He called it using his advantages. He now determined not to begin the
game with D芒聙聶Artagnan till he knew completely all his adversary芒聙聶s cards.

芒聙聹My lord, have you any commands?芒聙聺 asked Bernouin.

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 replied Mazarin. 芒聙聹Light me; I am going to the queen.芒聙聺

Bernouin took up a candlestick and led the way.

There was a secret communication between the cardinal芒聙聶s apartments and
those of the queen; and through this corridor* Mazarin passed whenever
he wished to visit Anne of Austria.

* This secret passage is still to be seen in the Palais Royal.

In the bedroom in which this passage ended, Bernouin encountered Madame
de Beauvais, like himself intrusted with the secret of these
subterranean love affairs; and Madame de Beauvais undertook to prepare
Anne of Austria, who was in her oratory with the young king, Louis
XIV., to receive the cardinal.

Anne, reclining in a large easy-chair, her head supported by her hand,
her elbow resting on a table, was looking at her son, who was turning
over the leaves of a large book filled with pictures. This celebrated
woman fully understood the art of being dull with dignity. It was her
practice to pass hours either in her oratory or in her room, without
either reading or praying.

When Madame de Beauvais appeared at the door and announced the
cardinal, the child, who had been absorbed in the pages of Quintus
Curtius, enlivened as they were by engravings of Alexander芒聙聶s feats of
arms, frowned and looked at his mother.

芒聙聹Why,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹does he enter without first asking for an audience?芒聙聺

Anne colored slightly.

芒聙聹The prime minister,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹is obliged in these unsettled days to
inform the queen of all that is happening from time to time, without
exciting the curiosity or remarks of the court.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But Richelieu never came in this manner,芒聙聺 said the pertinacious boy.

芒聙聹How can you remember what Monsieur de Richelieu did? You were too
young to know about such things.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not remember what he did, but I have inquired and I have been
told all about it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who told you about it?芒聙聺 asked Anne of Austria, with a movement of
impatience.

芒聙聹I know that I ought never to name the persons who answer my
questions,芒聙聺 answered the child, 芒聙聹for if I do I shall learn nothing
further.芒聙聺

At this very moment Mazarin entered. The king rose immediately, took
his book, closed it and went to lay it down on the table, near which he
continued standing, in order that Mazarin might be obliged to stand
also.

Mazarin contemplated these proceedings with a thoughtful glance. They
explained what had occurred that evening.

He bowed respectfully to the king, who gave him a somewhat cavalier
reception, but a look from his mother reproved him for the hatred
which, from his infancy, Louis XIV. had entertained toward Mazarin, and
he endeavored to receive the minister芒聙聶s homage with civility.

Anne of Austria sought to read in Mazarin芒聙聶s face the occasion of this
unexpected visit, since the cardinal usually came to her apartment only
after every one had retired.

The minister made a slight sign with his head, whereupon the queen said
to Madame Beauvais:

芒聙聹It is time for the king to go to bed; call Laporte.芒聙聺

The queen had several times already told her son that he ought to go to
bed, and several times Louis had coaxingly insisted on staying where he
was; but now he made no reply, but turned pale and bit his lips with
anger.

In a few minutes Laporte came into the room. The child went directly to
him without kissing his mother.

芒聙聹Well, Louis,芒聙聺 said Anne, 芒聙聹why do you not kiss me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I thought you were angry with me, madame; you sent me away.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not send you away, but you have had the small-pox and I am afraid
that sitting up late may tire you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You had no fears of my being tired when you ordered me to go to the
palace to-day to pass the odious decrees which have raised the people
to rebellion.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire!芒聙聺 interposed Laporte, in order to turn the subject, 芒聙聹to whom does
your majesty wish me to give the candle?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To any one, Laporte,芒聙聺 the child said; and then added in a loud voice,
芒聙聹to any one except Mancini.芒聙聺

Now Mancini was a nephew of Mazarin芒聙聶s and was as much hated by Louis as
the cardinal himself, although placed near his person by the minister.

And the king went out of the room without either embracing his mother
or even bowing to the cardinal.

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹I am glad to see that his majesty has been
brought up with a hatred of dissimulation.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why do you say that?芒聙聺 asked the queen, almost timidly.

芒聙聹Why, it seems to me that the way in which he left us needs no
explanation. Besides, his majesty takes no pains to conceal how little
affection he has for me. That, however, does not hinder me from being
entirely devoted to his service, as I am to that of your majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I ask your pardon for him, cardinal,芒聙聺 said the queen; 芒聙聹he is a child,
not yet able to understand his obligations to you.芒聙聺

The cardinal smiled.

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 continued the queen, 芒聙聹you have doubtless come for some important
purpose. What is it, then?芒聙聺

Mazarin sank into a chair with the deepest melancholy painted on his
countenance.

芒聙聹It is likely,芒聙聺 he replied, 芒聙聹that we shall soon be obliged to separate,
unless you love me well enough to follow me to Italy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why,芒聙聺 cried the queen; 芒聙聹how is that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because, as they say in the opera of 芒聙聵Thisbe,芒聙聶 芒聙聵The whole world
conspires to break our bonds.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹You jest, sir!芒聙聺 answered the queen, endeavoring to assume something of
her former dignity.

芒聙聹Alas! I do not, madame,芒聙聺 rejoined Mazarin. 芒聙聹Mark well what I say. The
whole world conspires to break our bonds. Now as you are one of the
whole world, I mean to say that you also are deserting me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Cardinal!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Heavens! did I not see you the other day smile on the Duke of Orl脙漏ans?
or rather at what he said?芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what was he saying?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He said this, madame: 芒聙聵Mazarin is a stumbling-block. Send him away and
all will then be well.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you wish me to do?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, madame! you are the queen!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Queen, forsooth! when I am at the mercy of every scribbler in the
Palais Royal who covers waste paper with nonsense, or of every country
squire in the kingdom.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nevertheless, you have still the power of banishing from your presence
those whom you do not like!芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is to say, whom _you_ do not like,芒聙聺 returned the queen.

芒聙聹I! persons whom _I_ do not like!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, indeed. Who sent away Madame de Chevreuse after she had been
persecuted twelve years under the last reign?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A woman of intrigue, who wanted to keep up against me the spirit of
cabal she had raised against M. de Richelieu.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who dismissed Madame de Hautefort, that friend so loyal that she
refused the favor of the king that she might remain in mine?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A prude, who told you every night, as she undressed you, that it was a
sin to love a priest, just as if one were a priest because one happens
to be a cardinal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who ordered Monsieur de Beaufort to be arrested?芒聙聺

芒聙聹An incendiary the burden of whose song was his intention to
assassinate me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You see, cardinal,芒聙聺 replied the queen, 芒聙聹that your enemies are mine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is not enough madame, it is necessary that your friends should be
also mine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friends, monsieur?芒聙聺 The queen shook her head. 芒聙聹Alas, I have them no
longer!芒聙聺

芒聙聹How is it that you have no friends in your prosperity when you had
many in adversity?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is because in my prosperity I forgot those old friends, monsieur;
because I have acted like Queen Marie de M脙漏dicis, who, returning from
her first exile, treated with contempt all those who had suffered for
her and, being proscribed a second time, died at Cologne abandoned by
every one, even by her own son.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, let us see,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹isn芒聙聶t there still time to repair the
evil? Search among your friends, your oldest friends.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you mean, monsieur?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing else than I say芒聙聰search.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas, I look around me in vain! I have no influence with any one.
Monsieur is, as usual, led by his favorite; yesterday it was Choisy,
to-day it is La Rivi脙篓re, to-morrow it will be some one else. Monsieur
le Prince is led by the coadjutor, who is led by Madame de Gu脙漏m脙漏n脙漏e.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Therefore, madame, I ask you to look, not among your friends of
to-day, but among those of other times.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Among my friends of other times?芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹Yes, among your friends of other times; among those who aided you to
contend against the Duc de Richelieu and even to conquer him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is he aiming at?芒聙聺 murmured the queen, looking uneasily at the

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 continued his eminence; 芒聙聹under certain circumstances, with that
strong and shrewd mind your majesty possesses, aided by your friends,
you were able to repel the attacks of that adversary.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I!芒聙聺 said the queen. 芒聙聹I suffered, that is all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹as women suffer in avenging themselves. Come, let
us come to the point. Do you know Monsieur de Rochefort?芒聙聺

芒聙聹One of my bitterest enemies芒聙聰the faithful friend of Cardinal
Richelieu.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know that, and we sent him to the Bastile,芒聙聺 said Mazarin.

芒聙聹Is he at liberty?芒聙聺 asked the queen.

芒聙聹No; still there, but I only speak of him in order that I may introduce
the name of another man. Do you know Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 he added,
looking steadfastly at the queen.

Anne of Austria received the blow with a beating heart.

芒聙聹Has the Gascon been indiscreet?芒聙聺 she murmured to herself, then said
aloud:

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan! stop an instant, the name seems certainly familiar.
D芒聙聶Artagnan! there was a musketeer who was in love with one of my women.
Poor young creature! she was poisoned on my account.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s all you know of him?芒聙聺 asked Mazarin.

The queen looked at him, surprised.

芒聙聹You seem, sir,芒聙聺 she remarked, 芒聙聹to be making me undergo a course of
cross-examination.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Which you answer according to your fancy,芒聙聺 replied Mazarin.

芒聙聹Tell me your wishes and I will comply with them.芒聙聺

The queen spoke with some impatience.

芒聙聹Well, madame,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, bowing, 芒聙聹I desire that you give me a
share in your friends, as I have shared with you the little industry
and talent that Heaven has given me. The circumstances are grave and it
will be necessary to act promptly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Still!芒聙聺 said the queen. 芒聙聹I thought that we were finally quit of
Monsieur de Beaufort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, you saw only the torrent that threatened to overturn everything
and you gave no attention to the still water. There is, however, a
proverb current in France relating to water which is quiet.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Continue,芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹Well, then, madame, not a day passes in which I do not suffer affronts
from your princes and your lordly servants, all of them automata who do
not perceive that I wind up the spring that makes them move, nor do
they see that beneath my quiet demeanor lies the still scorn of an
injured, irritated man, who has sworn to himself to master them one of
these days. We have arrested Monsieur de Beaufort, but he is the least
dangerous among them. There is the Prince de Cond脙漏芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹The hero of Rocroy. Do you think of _him?_芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame, often and often, but _pazienza_, as we say in Italy;
next, after Monsieur de Cond脙漏, comes the Duke of Orl脙漏ans.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What are you saying? The first prince of the blood, the king芒聙聶s uncle!芒聙聺

芒聙聹No! not the first prince of the blood, not the king芒聙聶s uncle, but the
base conspirator, the soul of every cabal, who pretends to lead the
brave people who are weak enough to believe in the honor of a prince of
the blood芒聙聰not the prince nearest to the throne, not the king芒聙聶s uncle, I
repeat, but the murderer of Chalais, of Montmorency and of Cinq-Mars,
who is playing now the same game he played long ago and who thinks that
he will win the game because he has a new adversary芒聙聰instead of a man
who threatened, a man who smiles. But he is mistaken; I shall not leave
so near the queen that source of discord with which the deceased
cardinal so often caused the anger of the king to rage above the
boiling point.芒聙聺

Anne blushed and buried her face in her hands.

芒聙聹What am I to do?芒聙聺 she said, bowed down beneath the voice of her
tyrant.

芒聙聹Endeavor to remember the names of those faithful servants who crossed
the Channel, in spite of Monsieur de Richelieu, tracking the roads
along which they passed by their blood, to bring back to your majesty
certain jewels given by you to Buckingham.芒聙聺

Anne arose, full of majesty, and as if touched by a spring, and looking
at the cardinal with the haughty dignity which in the days of her youth
had made her so powerful: 芒聙聹You are insulting me!芒聙聺 she said.

芒聙聹I wish,芒聙聺 continued Mazarin, finishing, as it were, the speech this
sudden movement of the queen had cut; 芒聙聹I wish, in fact, that you should
now do for your husband what you formerly did for your lover.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Again that accusation!芒聙聺 cried the queen. 芒聙聹I thought that calumny was
stifled or extinct; you have spared me till now, but since you speak of
it, once for all, I tell you芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, I do not ask you to tell me,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, astounded by this
returning courage.

芒聙聹I will tell you all,芒聙聺 replied Anne. 芒聙聹Listen: there were in truth, at
that epoch, four devoted hearts, four loyal spirits, four faithful
swords, who saved more than my life芒聙聰my honor芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! you confess it!芒聙聺 exclaimed Mazarin.

芒聙聹Is it only the guilty whose honor is at the sport of others, sir? and
cannot women be dishonored by appearances? Yes, appearances were
against me and I was about to suffer dishonor. However, I swear I was

芒聙聹I swear,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹by these sacred relics that Buckingham was not my

Chapter V.
The Gascon and the Italian.

In the meantime D芒聙聶Artagnan took his way toward the Rue Tiquetonne,

We will explain in a few words how D芒聙聶Artagnan had been led to choose

Chapter VI.
D芒聙聶Artagnan in his Fortieth Year.


Years have elapsed, many events have happened, alas! since, in our
romance of 芒聙聹The Three Musketeers,芒聙聺 we took leave of D芒聙聶Artagnan at No.
12 Rue des Fossoyeurs. D芒聙聶Artagnan had not failed in his career, but
circumstances had been adverse to him. So long as he was surrounded by
his friends he retained his youth and the poetry of his character. He
was one of those fine, ingenuous natures which assimilate themselves
easily to the dispositions of others. Athos imparted to him his
greatness of soul, Porthos his enthusiasm, Aramis his elegance. Had
D芒聙聶Artagnan continued his intimacy with these three men he would have
become a superior character. Athos was the first to leave him, in order
that he might retire to a little property he had inherited near Blois;
Porthos, the second, to marry an attorney芒聙聶s wife; and lastly, Aramis,
the third, to take orders and become an abb脙漏. From that day D芒聙聶Artagnan
felt lonely and powerless, without courage to pursue a career in which
he could only distinguish himself on condition that each of his three
companions should endow him with one of the gifts each had received
from Heaven.

Notwithstanding his commission in the musketeers, D芒聙聶Artagnan felt
completely solitary. For a time the delightful remembrance of Madame
Bonancieux left on his character a certain poetic tinge, perishable
indeed; for like all other recollections in this world, these
impressions were, by degrees, effaced. A garrison life is fatal even to
the most aristocratic organization; and imperceptibly, D芒聙聶Artagnan,
always in the camp, always on horseback, always in garrison, became (I
know not how in the present age one would express it) a typical
trooper. His early refinement of character was not only not lost, it
grew even greater than ever; but it was now applied to the little,
instead of to the great things of life芒聙聰to the martial condition of the
soldier芒聙聰comprised under the head of a good lodging, a rich table, a
congenial hostess. These important advantages D芒聙聶Artagnan found to his
own taste in the Rue Tiquetonne at the sign of the Roe.

From the time D芒聙聶Artagnan took quarters in that hotel, the mistress of
the house, a pretty and fresh looking Flemish woman, twenty-five or
twenty-six years old, had been singularly interested in him; and after
certain love passages, much obstructed by an inconvenient husband to
whom a dozen times D芒聙聶Artagnan had made a pretence of passing a sword
through his body, that husband had disappeared one fine morning, after
furtively selling certain choice lots of wine, carrying away with him
money and jewels. He was thought to be dead; his wife, especially, who
cherished the pleasing idea that she was a widow, stoutly maintained
that death had taken him. Therefore, after the connection had continued
three years, carefully fostered by D芒聙聶Artagnan, who found his bed and
his mistress more agreeable every year, each doing credit to the other,
the mistress conceived the extraordinary desire of becoming a wife and
proposed to D芒聙聶Artagnan that he should marry her.

芒聙聹Ah, fie!芒聙聺 D芒聙聶Artagnan replied. 芒聙聹Bigamy, my dear! Come now, you don芒聙聶t
really wish it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹But he is dead; I am sure of it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He was a very contrary fellow and might come back on purpose to have
us hanged.芒聙聺

芒聙聹All right; if he comes back you will kill him, you are so skillful and
so brave.芒聙聺

芒聙聹_Peste!_ my darling! another way of getting hanged.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So you refuse my request?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To be sure I do芒聙聰furiously!芒聙聺

The pretty landlady was desolate. She would have taken D芒聙聶Artagnan not

Then along toward the fourth year came the expedition of Franche-Comt脙漏.
D芒聙聶Artagnan was assigned to it and made his preparations to depart.
There were then great griefs, tears without end and solemn promises to
remain faithful芒聙聰all of course on the part of the hostess. D芒聙聶Artagnan
was too grand to promise anything; he purposed only to do all that he
could to increase the glory of his name.

As to that, we know D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s courage; he exposed himself freely to
danger and while charging at the head of his company he received a ball
through the chest which laid him prostrate on the field of battle. He
had been seen falling from his horse and had not been seen to rise;
every one, therefore, believed him to be dead, especially those to whom
his death would give promotion. One believes readily what he wishes to
believe. Now in the army, from the division-generals who desire the
death of the general-in-chief, to the soldiers who desire the death of
the corporals, all desire some one芒聙聶s death.

But D芒聙聶Artagnan was not a man to let himself be killed like that. After
he had remained through the heat of the day unconscious on the
battle-field, the cool freshness of the night brought him to himself.
He gained a village, knocked at the door of the finest house and was
received as the wounded are always and everywhere received in France.
He was petted, tended, cured; and one fine morning, in better health
than ever before, he set out for France. Once in France he turned his
course toward Paris, and reaching Paris went straight to Rue
Tiquetonne.

But D芒聙聶Artagnan found in his chamber the personal equipment of a man,
complete, except for the sword, arranged along the wall.

芒聙聹He has returned,芒聙聺 said he. 芒聙聹So much the worse, and so much the
better!芒聙聺

It need not be said that D芒聙聶Artagnan was still thinking of the husband.
He made inquiries and discovered that the servants were new and that
the mistress had gone for a walk.

芒聙聹Alone?芒聙聺 asked D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹With monsieur.芒聙聺

And this explains how D芒聙聶Artagnan had quarters in the Rue Tiquetonne, at

In his youth D芒聙聶Artagnan had often headed the _bourgeoisie_ against the

He took his key and candle and went upstairs to his bedroom. He had

芒聙聹In 芒聙聶43,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹just before the death of the late cardinal, I

Chapter VII.
Touches upon the Strange Effects a Half-pistole may have.


D芒聙聶Artagnan, as he crossed the Pont Neuf, congratulated himself on

situation which he held in Rue des Lombards, a connection with the

D芒聙聶Artagnan nearly jumped for joy; he had despaired of finding Bazin,

He knelt down just opposite the chapel in order not to lose sight of

Bazin wore his dress, it may be observed, with equal dignity and
saintly propriety. It was not difficult to understand that he had
gained the crown of his ambition and that the silver-mounted wand he

nose, but his cheeks, fattened out, each took a portion of it unto
themselves; his chin had joined his throat; his eyes were swelled up
with the puffiness of his cheeks; his hair, cut straight in holy guise,
covered his forehead as far as his eyebrows.

The officiating priest was just finishing mass whilst D芒聙聶Artagnan was

performed from time to time some of those early masses which the common
people, generally, alone attended.

* A sacerdotal officer.

Chapter VIII.
D芒聙聶Artagnan, Going to a Distance to discover Aramis.


On entering the hotel D芒聙聶Artagnan saw a man sitting in a corner by the
fire. It was Planchet, but so completely transformed, thanks to the old
clothes that the departing husband had left behind, that D芒聙聶Artagnan

entered D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s service.

The plan adopted by D芒聙聶Artagnan was soon perfected. He resolved not to
reach Noisy in the day, for fear of being recognized; he had therefore
plenty of time before him, for Noisy is only three or four leagues from

He began his day by breakfasting substantially芒聙聰a bad beginning when one
wants to employ the head, but an excellent precaution when one wants to

At about a league and a half from the city, D芒聙聶Artagnan, finding that in

seemed as if they were on the point of commencing some nightly

a stranger he beckoned to his companions, and two men who were drinking

D芒聙聶Artagnan, on his side, went up to the landlady, praised her
wine芒聙聰which was a horrible production from the country of Montreuil芒聙聰and

was a convent of Jesuits and was the property芒聙聰a by no means unusual
circumstance芒聙聰of these worthy fathers.

At four o芒聙聶clock D芒聙聶Artagnan recommenced his journey. He proceeded slowly
and in deep reverie. Planchet also was lost in thought, but the subject

One word which their landlady had pronounced had given a particular

Longueville.

That name was indeed one to inspire imagination and produce thought.
Madame de Longueville was one of the highest ladies in the realm; she
was also one of the greatest beauties at court. She had formerly been
suspected of an intimacy of too tender a nature with Coligny, who, for
her sake, had been killed in a duel, in the Place Royale, by the Duc de
Guise. She was now connected by bonds of a political nature with the
Prince de Marsillac, the eldest son of the old Duc de Rochefoucauld,
whom she was trying to inspire with an enmity toward the Duc de Cond脙漏,
her brother-in-law, whom she now hated mortally.

D芒聙聶Artagnan thought of all these matters. He remembered how at the
Louvre he had often seen, as she passed by him in the full radiance of
her dazzling charms, the beautiful Madame de Longueville. He thought of
Aramis, who, without possessing any greater advantages than himself,
had formerly been the lover of Madame de Chevreuse, who had been to a
former court what Madame de Longueville was in that day; and he
wondered how it was that there should be in the world people who
succeed in every wish, some in ambition, others in love, whilst others,
either from chance, or from ill-luck, or from some natural defect or
impediment, remain half-way upon the road toward fulfilment of their
hopes and expectations.

He was confessing to himself that he belonged to the latter unhappy
class, when Planchet approached and said:

Chapter IX.

芒聙聹Thus 芒聙聶tis quite decided,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that what I have to offer

芒聙聹Thou liest, subtile one,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to himself. 芒聙聹Thou alone, on

The friends embraced. They descended into the plain by the ladder.

But after he had gone about two hundred steps D芒聙聶Artagnan stopped short,

芒聙聹What芒聙聶s the matter?芒聙聺 asked Planchet.

芒聙聹This is the matter: be he ever so cunning he shall never say I was his

Having thus spoken, D芒聙聶Artagnan cleared the ditch by the roadside and

The moon had now risen and he could see well enough to retrace his
road.

He reached the hedge and hid himself behind it; in passing by the house

window was again lighted up and he was convinced that Aramis had not

In truth, in a few minutes he heard steps approaching and low whispers.

Close to the hedge the steps stopped.

D芒聙聶Artagnan knelt down near the thickest part of the hedge.

Two men, to the astonishment of D芒聙聶Artagnan, appeared shortly; soon,

He kissed her hand tenderly.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed the cavalier with a soft voice.

芒聙聹Do you not see that the wind has blown off my hat?芒聙聺

Aramis rushed after the fugitive hat. D芒聙聶Artagnan took advantage of the

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, rising and brushing his knees; 芒聙聹now I have
thee芒聙聰thou art a Frondeur and the lover of Madame de Longueville.芒聙聺




Chapter X.
Monsieur Porthos du Vallon de Bracieux de Pierrefonds.


Thanks to what Aramis had told him, D芒聙聶Artagnan, who knew already that
Porthos called himself Du Vallon, was now aware that he styled himself,
from his estate, De Bracieux; and that he was, on account of this
estate, engaged in a lawsuit with the Bishop of Noyon. It was, then, in
the neighborhood of Noyon that he must seek that estate. His itinerary
was promptly determined: he would go to Dammartin, from which place two
roads diverge, one toward Soissons, the other toward Compi脙篓gne; there
he would inquire concerning the Bracieux estate and go to the right or
to the left according to the information obtained.

Planchet, who was still a little concerned for his safety after his
recent escapade, declared that he would follow D芒聙聶Artagnan even to the
end of the world, either by the road to the right or by that to the
left; only he begged his former master to set out in the evening, for
greater security to himself. D芒聙聶Artagnan suggested that he should send
word to his wife, so that she might not be anxious about him, but
Planchet replied with much sagacity that he was very sure his wife
would not die of anxiety through not knowing where he was, while he,
Planchet, remembering her incontinence of tongue, would die of anxiety
if she did know.

This reasoning seemed to D芒聙聶Artagnan so satisfactory that he no further
insisted; and about eight o芒聙聶clock in the evening, the time when the
vapors of night begin to thicken in the streets, he left the Hotel de

way of the Saint Denis gate.

At midnight the two travelers were at Dammartin, but it was then too
late to make inquiries芒聙聰the host of the Cygne de la Croix had gone to
bed.

The next morning D芒聙聶Artagnan summoned the host, one of those sly Normans
who say neither yes nor no and fear to commit themselves by giving a

that the road to the right was the one he ought to take, and on that

reached Nanteuil and stopped for breakfast. His host here was a good
fellow from Picardy, who gave him all the information he needed. The
Bracieux estate was a few leagues from Villars-Cotterets.

D芒聙聶Artagnan was acquainted with Villars-Cotterets, having gone thither
with the court on several occasions; for at that time Villars-Cotterets
was a royal residence. He therefore shaped his course toward that place
and dismounted at the Dauphin d芒聙聶Or. There he ascertained that the
Bracieux estate was four leagues distant, but that Porthos was not at

Bishop of Noyon in regard to the Pierrefonds property, which adjoined
his own, and weary at length of a legal controversy which was beyond
his comprehension, he put an end to it by purchasing Pierrefonds and
added that name to his others. He now called himself Du Vallon de
Bracieux de Pierrefonds, and resided on his new estate.

The travelers were therefore obliged to stay at the hotel until the
next day; the horses had done ten leagues that day and needed rest. It
is true they might have taken others, but there was a great forest to
pass through and Planchet, as we have seen, had no liking for forests
after dark.

There was another thing that Planchet had no liking for and that was
starting on a journey with a hungry stomach. Accordingly, D芒聙聶Artagnan,
on awaking, found his breakfast waiting for him. It need not be said
that Planchet in resuming his former functions resumed also his former

It was nearly eight o芒聙聶clock when they set out again. Their course was
clearly defined: they were to follow the road toward Compi脙篓gne and on
emerging from the forest turn to the right.

The morning was beautiful, and in this early springtime the birds sang
on the trees and the sunbeams shone through the misty glades, like
curtains of golden gauze.

In other parts of the forest the light could scarcely penetrate through
the foliage, and the stems of two old oak trees, the refuge of the
squirrel, startled by the travelers, were in deep shadow.

There came up from all nature in the dawn of day a perfume of herbs,

As to Planchet, he thought of little or nothing, but was happy as a

At the extremity of the wood D芒聙聶Artagnan perceived the road that had

芒聙聹Oh! oh!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I fancied this castle belonged to the ancient

As he approached the castle which had first attracted his eye,

On arriving at the extremity of the castle D芒聙聶Artagnan found himself

Chapter XI.
Wealth does not necessarily produce Happiness.


D芒聙聶Artagnan passed through the iron gate and arrived in front of the

He ran to Porthos and threw himself into his arms; the whole body of

A third sigh from Porthos.

Chapter XII.
Porthos was Discontented with his Condition.


As they returned toward the castle, D芒聙聶Artagnan thought of the miseries
of poor human nature, always dissatisfied with what it has, ever
desirous of what it has not.

In the position of Porthos, D芒聙聶Artagnan would have been perfectly happy;
and to make Porthos contented there was wanting芒聙聰what? five letters to
put before his three names, a tiny coronet to paint upon the panels of
his carriage!

芒聙聹I shall pass all my life,芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹in seeking for a man
who is really contented with his lot.芒聙聺

Whilst making this reflection, chance seemed, as it were, to give him
the lie direct. When Porthos had left him to give some orders he saw
Mousqueton approaching. The face of the steward, despite one slight

absolute felicity.

芒聙聹Here is what I am looking for,芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹but alas! the

He then made a sign for Mousqueton to come to him.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said the servant, 芒聙聹I have a favour to ask you.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan smiled; Porthos wanted to lengthen out his names, Mousqueton

芒聙聹I go to-morrow, my friend,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan.

D芒聙聶Artagnan was secretly touched with remorse, not at inducing Porthos

He was thinking of these matters when Porthos summoned him to dinner.

芒聙聹What! to dinner?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹What time is it, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Into the service芒聙聰the king芒聙聶s service?芒聙聺 Mousqueton trembled; even his

These last words fell on Mousqueton like a thunderbolt. It was those

The two friends remained alone to speak of the future and to build

芒聙聹But芒聙聰formerly芒聙聰it appears,芒聙聺 began Mousqueton timidly.

To-day it芒聙聶s a sort of diplomatic arrangement; ask Planchet.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My Lord,芒聙聰I have already one man to offer to your eminence, and he is

Chapter XIII.
Two Angelic Faces.


The road was long, but the horses upon which D芒聙聶Artagnan and Planchet

芒聙聹And then,芒聙聺 resumed D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹to this decrepitude is probably added

Soon, as the road turned, the Ch脙垄teau de la Valli脙篓re appeared in view;

Whilst Planchet was standing on the steps before the house he heard a

芒聙聹What, Planchet, is Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan here?芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan received the compliments of those who were present in his

Strange! Athos was scarcely aged at all! His fine eyes, no longer

Meanwhile the guests, seeing that the two friends were longing to be

芒聙聹Ah! 芒聙聶tis Raoul, who is come home.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 replied the youth, with deep respect, 芒聙聹and I have performed

芒聙聹But what芒聙聶s the matter, Raoul?芒聙聺 said Athos, very anxiously. 芒聙聹You are

芒聙聹She was walking with her nurse Marceline, in the place where the

芒聙聹No, sir, Madame de Saint-Remy is at Blois with the Duchess of Orl脙漏ans.

芒聙聹I have brought her here, sir, and I have deposited her in charge of

The guests now all took leave of Athos, excepting the old Duc de Barbe,

芒聙聹You are right, sir,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹She will be the sooner with her

芒聙聹No, sir, I assure you,芒聙聺 muttered Raoul, 芒聙聹it is not.芒聙聺

The youth looked pleased. He again took in his strong arms the little

Chapter XIV.
The Castle of Bragelonne.


Whilst this scene was going on, D芒聙聶Artagnan remained with open mouth and

Athos, who had been observing him and guessing his thoughts, took his

芒聙聹Yes, I inherited this little property when I quitted the army, as I

The remembrance of Milady recurred to D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹And you are happy?芒聙聺 he said to his friend.

芒聙聹As happy as it is allowed to one of God芒聙聶s creatures to be on this

芒聙聹I have sometimes thought as you do, Athos.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He must be about twenty-three years of age,芒聙聺 said Athos, in a low

Chapter XV.
Athos as a Diplomatist.


D芒聙聶Artagnan retired to bed芒聙聰not to sleep, but to think over all he had
heard that evening. Being naturally goodhearted, and having had once a
liking for Athos, which had grown into a sincere friendship, he was
delighted at thus meeting a man full of intelligence and moral
strength, instead of a drunkard. He admitted without annoyance the
continued superiority of Athos over himself, devoid as he was of that
jealousy which might have saddened a less generous disposition; he was
delighted also that the high qualities of Athos appeared to promise
favorably for his mission. Nevertheless, it seemed to him that Athos
was not in all respects sincere and frank. Who was the youth he had
adopted and who bore so striking a resemblance to him? What could
explain Athos芒聙聶s having re-entered the world and the extreme sobriety he
had observed at table? The absence of Grimaud, whose name had never
once been uttered by Athos, gave D芒聙聶Artagnan uneasiness. It was evident
either that he no longer possessed the confidence of his friend, or
that Athos was bound by some invisible chain, or that he had been
forewarned of the lieutenant芒聙聶s visit.

He could not help thinking of M. Rochefort, whom he had seen in Notre
Dame; could De Rochefort have forestalled him with Athos? Again, the
moderate fortune which Athos possessed, concealed as it was, so
skillfully, seemed to show a regard for appearances and to betray a
latent ambition which might be easily aroused. The clear and vigorous
intellect of Athos would render him more open to conviction than a less
able man would be. He would enter into the minister芒聙聶s schemes with the
more ardor, because his natural activity would be doubled by necessity.

Resolved to seek an explanation on all these points on the following
day, D芒聙聶Artagnan, in spite of his fatigue, prepared for an attack and
determined that it should take place after breakfast. He determined to
cultivate the good-will of the youth Raoul and, either whilst fencing
with him or when out shooting, to extract from his simplicity some
information which would connect the Athos of old times with the Athos
of the present. But D芒聙聶Artagnan at the same time, being a man of extreme
caution, was quite aware what injury he should do himself, if by any
indiscretion or awkwardness he should betray has manoeuvering to the
experienced eye of Athos. Besides, to tell truth, whilst D芒聙聶Artagnan was
quite disposed to adopt a subtle course against the cunning of Aramis
or the vanity of Porthos, he was ashamed to equivocate with Athos,
true-hearted, open Athos. It seemed to him that if Porthos and Aramis
deemed him superior to them in the arts of diplomacy, they would like
him all the better for it; but that Athos, on the contrary, would
despise him.

芒聙聹Ah! why is not Grimaud, the taciturn Grimaud, here?芒聙聺 thought
D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹there are so many things his silence would have told me;

There reigned a perfect stillness in the house. D芒聙聶Artagnan had heard

the bedroom of Athos.

芒聙聹He is walking about and thinking,芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹but of what?

At length Athos went to bed, apparently, for the noise ceased.

Silence and fatigue together overcame D芒聙聶Artagnan and sleep overtook him
also. He was not, however, a good sleeper. Scarcely had dawn gilded his

The young man was opening the door of the stable. He then, with

From that moment he grew very guarded.

On his side D芒聙聶Artagnan became more cautious also.

芒聙聹You spoke to me,芒聙聺 Athos resumed, 芒聙聹of Porthos; have you persuaded him
to seek his fortune? But he has wealth, I believe, already.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Doubtless he has. But such is man, we always want something more than
we already have.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What does Porthos wish for?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To be a baron.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, true! I forgot,芒聙聺 said Athos, laughing.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true!芒聙聺 thought the Gascon, 芒聙聹where has he heard it? Does he
correspond with Aramis? Ah! if I knew that he did I should know all.芒聙聺

The conversation was interrupted by the entrance of Raoul.

芒聙聹Is our little neighbor worse?芒聙聺 asked D芒聙聶Artagnan, seeing a look of

芒聙聹Ah, sir!芒聙聺 replied Raoul, 芒聙聹her fall is a very serious one, and without
any ostensible injury, the physician fears she will be lame for life.芒聙聺

芒聙聹This is terrible,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹And what makes me all the more wretched, sir, is, that I was the cause
of this misfortune.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How so?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹It was to run to meet me that she leaped from that pile of wood.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There芒聙聶s only one remedy, dear Raoul芒聙聰that is, to marry her as a
compensation.芒聙聺 remarked D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Ah, sir!芒聙聺 answered Raoul, 芒聙聹you joke about a real misfortune; that is
cruel, indeed.芒聙聺

The good understanding between the two friends was not in the least
altered by the morning芒聙聶s skirmish. They breakfasted with a good
appetite, looking now and then at poor Raoul, who with moist eyes and a

After breakfast two letters arrived for Athos, who read them with

jumping up several times on seeing him read these epistles, in one of
