crossed.

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to Raoul, seeing that Athos wished to be alone,
芒聙聹come, let us take a turn in the fencing gallery; that will amuse you.芒聙聺

And they both went into a low room where there were foils, gloves,
masks, breastplates, and all the accessories for a fencing match.

In a quarter of an hour Athos joined them and at the same moment
Charles brought in a letter for D芒聙聶Artagnan, which a messenger had just
desired might be instantly delivered.

It was now Athos芒聙聶s turn to take a sly look.

D芒聙聶Artagnan read the letter with apparent calmness and said, shaking his
head:

芒聙聹See, dear friend, what it is to belong to the army. Faith, you are
indeed right not to return to it. Monsieur de Tr脙漏ville is ill, so my
company can芒聙聶t do without me; there! my leave is at an end!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you return to Paris?芒聙聺 asked Athos, quickly.

芒聙聹Egad! yes; but why don芒聙聶t you come there also?芒聙聺

Athos colored a little and answered:

芒聙聹Should I go, I shall be delighted to see you there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Halloo, Planchet!芒聙聺 cried the Gascon from the door, 芒聙聹we must set out in

Then turning to Athos he added:

芒聙聹I seem to miss something here. I am really sorry to go away without

芒聙聹Grimaud!芒聙聺 replied Athos. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m surprised you have never so much as
asked after him. I have lent him to a friend芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who will understand the signs he makes?芒聙聺 returned D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹I _hope_ so.芒聙聺

The friends embraced cordially; D芒聙聶Artagnan pressed Raoul芒聙聶s hand.

芒聙聹Will you not come with me?芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹I shall pass by Blois.芒聙聺

Raoul turned toward Athos, who showed him by a secret sign that he did

芒聙聹No, monsieur,芒聙聺 replied the young man; 芒聙聹I will remain with monsieur le

芒聙聹Adieu, then, to both, my good friends,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹may God

Athos waved his hand, Raoul bowed, and D芒聙聶Artagnan and Planchet set out.

The count followed them with his eyes, his hands resting on the

芒聙聹Raoul, we set out to-night for Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eh?芒聙聺 cried the young man, turning pale.

芒聙聹You may go and offer your adieux and mine to Madame de Saint-Remy. I

The young man bent low, with an expression of sorrow and gratitude

As to D芒聙聶Artagnan, scarcely, on his side, was he out of sight when he

芒聙聹Return immediately to Paris.芒聙聰J. M芒聙聰芒聙聰.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The epistle is laconic,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹and if there had not been a

And he read that welcome postscript, which made him forget the

芒聙聹P. S.芒聙聰Go to the king芒聙聶s treasurer, at Blois; tell him your name and
show him this letter; you will receive two hundred pistoles.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Assuredly,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I admire this piece of prose. The
cardinal writes better than I thought. Come, Planchet, let us pay a
visit to the king芒聙聶s treasurer and then set off.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Toward Paris, sir?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Toward Paris.芒聙聺

And they set out at as hard a canter as their horses could maintain.




Chapter XVI.
The Duc de Beaufort.


The circumstances that had hastened the return of D芒聙聶Artagnan to Paris
were as follows:

One evening, when Mazarin, according to custom, went to visit the
queen, in passing the guard-chamber he heard loud voices; wishing to
know on what topic the soldiers were conversing, he approached with his
wonted wolf-like step, pushed open the door and put his head close to
the chink.

There was a dispute among the guards.

芒聙聹I tell you,芒聙聺 one of them was saying, 芒聙聹that if Coysel predicted that,
芒聙聶tis as good as true; I know nothing about it, but I have heard say
that he芒聙聶s not only an astrologer, but a magician.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Deuce take it, friend, if he芒聙聶s one of thy friends thou wilt ruin him
in saying so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because he may be tried for it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! absurd! they don芒聙聶t burn sorcerers nowadays.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No? 芒聙聶Tis not a long time since the late cardinal burnt Urban Grandier,
though.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend, Urban Grandier wasn芒聙聶t a sorcerer, he was a learned man. He
didn芒聙聶t predict the future, he knew the past芒聙聰often a more dangerous
thing.芒聙聺

Mazarin nodded an assent, but wishing to know what this prediction was,
about which they disputed, he remained in the same place.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t say,芒聙聺 resumed the guard, 芒聙聹that Coysel is not a sorcerer, but I
say that if his prophecy gets wind, it芒聙聶s a sure way to prevent it芒聙聶s
coming true.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, in this way: if Coysel says loud enough for the cardinal to hear
him, on such or such a day such a prisoner will escape, 芒聙聶tis plain that
the cardinal will take measures of precaution and that the prisoner
will not escape.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good Lord!芒聙聺 said another guard, who might have been thought asleep on
a bench, but who had lost not a syllable of the conversation, 芒聙聹do you
suppose that men can escape their destiny? If it is written yonder, in
Heaven, that the Duc de Beaufort is to escape, he will escape; and all
the precautions of the cardinal will not prevent it.芒聙聺

Mazarin started. He was an Italian and therefore superstitious. He
walked straight into the midst of the guards, who on seeing him were
silent.

芒聙聹What were you saying?芒聙聺 he asked with his flattering manner; 芒聙聹that
Monsieur de Beaufort had escaped, were you not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, no, my lord!芒聙聺 said the incredulous soldier. 芒聙聹He芒聙聶s well guarded
now; we only said he would escape.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who said so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Repeat your story, Saint Laurent,芒聙聺 replied the man, turning to the
originator of the tale.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said the guard, 芒聙聹I have simply mentioned the prophecy I
heard from a man named Coysel, who believes that, be he ever so closely
watched and guarded, the Duke of Beaufort will escape before

芒聙聹Coysel is a madman!芒聙聺 returned the cardinal.

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied the soldier, tenacious in his credulity; 芒聙聹he has foretold
many things which have come to pass; for instance, that the queen would
have a son; that Monsieur Coligny would be killed in a duel with the
Duc de Guise; and finally, that the coadjutor would be made cardinal.
Well! the queen has not only one son, but two; then, Monsieur de
Coligny was killed, and芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹but the coadjutor is not yet made cardinal!芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, my lord, but he will be,芒聙聺 answered the guard.

Mazarin made a grimace, as if he meant to say, 芒聙聹But he does not wear
the cardinal芒聙聶s cap;芒聙聺 then he added:

芒聙聹So, my friend, it芒聙聶s your opinion that Monsieur de Beaufort will

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s my idea, my lord; and if your eminence were to offer to make me
at this moment governor of the castle of Vincennes, I should refuse it.
After Whitsuntide it would be another thing.芒聙聺

There is nothing so convincing as a firm conviction. It has its own
effect upon the most incredulous; and far from being incredulous,
Mazarin was superstitious. He went away thoughtful and anxious and
returned to his own room, where he summoned Bernouin and desired him to
fetch thither in the morning the special guard he had placed over
Monsieur de Beaufort and to awaken him whenever he should arrive.

The guard had, in fact, touched the cardinal in the tenderest point.
During the whole five years in which the Duc de Beaufort had been in
prison not a day had passed in which the cardinal had not felt a secret
dread of his escape. It was not possible, as he knew well, to confine
for the whole of his life the grandson of Henry IV., especially when
this young prince was scarcely thirty years of age. But however and
whensoever he did escape, what hatred he must cherish against him to
whom he owed his long imprisonment; who had taken him, rich, brave,
glorious, beloved by women, feared by men, to cut off his life芒聙聶s best,
happiest years; for it is not life, it is merely existence, in prison!
Meantime, Mazarin redoubled his surveillance over the duke. But like
the miser in the fable, he could not sleep for thinking of his
treasure. Often he awoke in the night, suddenly, dreaming that he had
been robbed of Monsieur de Beaufort. Then he inquired about him and had
the vexation of hearing that the prisoner played, drank, sang, but that
whilst playing, drinking, singing, he often stopped short to vow that
Mazarin should pay dear for all the amusements he had forced him to
enter into at Vincennes.

So much did this one idea haunt the cardinal even in his sleep, that
when at seven in the morning Bernouin came to arouse him, his first
words were: 芒聙聹Well, what芒聙聶s the matter? Has Monsieur de Beaufort escaped
from Vincennes?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not think so, my lord,芒聙聺 said Bernouin; 芒聙聹but you will hear about
him, for La Ramee is here and awaits the commands of your eminence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell him to come in,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, arranging his pillows, so that he
might receive the visitor sitting up in bed.

The officer entered, a large fat man, with an open physiognomy. His air
of perfect serenity made Mazarin uneasy.

芒聙聹Approach, sir,芒聙聺 said the cardinal.

The officer obeyed.

芒聙聹Do you know what they are saying here?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, your eminence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, they say that Monsieur de Beaufort is going to escape from
Vincennes, if he has not done so already.芒聙聺

The officer芒聙聶s face expressed complete stupefaction. He opened at once
his little eyes and his great mouth, to inhale better the joke his
eminence deigned to address to him, and ended by a burst of laughter,
so violent that his great limbs shook in hilarity as they would have
done in an ague.

芒聙聹Escape! my lord芒聙聰escape! Your eminence does not then know where
Monsieur de Beaufort is?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, I do, sir; in the donjon of Vincennes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir; in a room, the walls of which are seven feet thick, with
grated windows, each bar as thick as my arm.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Mazarin, 芒聙聹with perseverance one may penetrate through a
wall; with a watch-spring one may saw through an iron bar.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then my lord does not know that there are eight guards about him, four
in his chamber, four in the antechamber, and that they never leave

芒聙聹But he leaves his room, he plays at tennis at the Mall?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, those amusements are allowed; but if your eminence wishes it, we
will discontinue the permission.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, no!芒聙聺 cried Mazarin, fearing that should his prisoner ever leave
his prison he would be the more exasperated against him if he thus
retrenched his amusement. He then asked with whom he played.

芒聙聹My lord, either with the officers of the guard, with the other
prisoners, or with me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But does he not approach the walls while playing?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your eminence doesn芒聙聶t know those walls; they are sixty feet high and I
doubt if Monsieur de Beaufort is sufficiently weary of life to risk his
neck by jumping off.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hum!芒聙聺 said the cardinal, beginning to feel more comfortable. 芒聙聹You mean
to say, then, my dear Monsieur la Ramee芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹That unless Monsieur de Beaufort can contrive to metamorphose himself
into a little bird, I will continue answerable for him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Take care! you assert a great deal,芒聙聺 said Mazarin. 芒聙聹Monsieur de
Beaufort told the guards who took him to Vincennes that he had often
thought what he should do in case he were put into prison, and that he
had found out forty ways of escaping.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, if among these forty there had been one good way he would
have been out long ago.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, come; not such a fool as I fancied!芒聙聺 thought Mazarin.

芒聙聹Besides, my lord must remember that Monsieur de Chavigny is governor
of Vincennes,芒聙聺 continued La Ramee, 芒聙聹and that Monsieur de Chavigny is
not friendly to Monsieur de Beaufort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but Monsieur de Chavigny is sometimes absent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When he is absent I am there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But when you leave him, for instance?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! when I leave him, I place in my stead a bold fellow who aspires to
be his majesty芒聙聶s special guard. I promise you he keeps a good watch
over the prisoner. During the three weeks that he has been with me, I
have only had to reproach him with one thing芒聙聰being too severe with the
prisoners.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who is this Cerberus?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A certain Monsieur Grimaud, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what was he before he went to Vincennes?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He was in the country, as I was told by the person who recommended him
to me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who recommended this man to you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The steward of the Duc de Grammont.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is not a gossip, I hope?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lord a mercy, my lord! I thought for a long time that he was dumb; he
answers only by signs. It seems his former master accustomed him to

芒聙聹Well, dear Monsieur la Ramee,芒聙聺 replied the cardinal 芒聙聹let him prove a
true and thankful keeper and we will shut our eyes upon his rural
misdeeds and put on his back a uniform to make him respectable, and in
the pockets of that uniform some pistoles to drink to the king芒聙聶s

Mazarin was large in promises,芒聙聰quite unlike the virtuous Monsieur
Grimaud so bepraised by La Ramee; for he said nothing and did much.

It was now nine o芒聙聶clock. The cardinal, therefore, got up, perfumed
himself, dressed, and went to the queen to tell her what had detained

than the cardinal himself, and who was almost as superstitious as he

deputy. Then, when the cardinal had ended:

芒聙聹Alas, sir! why have we not a Grimaud near every prince?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Patience!芒聙聺 replied Mazarin, with his Italian smile; 芒聙聹that may happen
one day; but in the meantime芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, in the meantime?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall still take precautions.芒聙聺

And he wrote to D芒聙聶Artagnan to hasten his return.




Chapter XVII.
Duc de Beaufort amused his Leisure Hours in the Donjon of Vincennes.


The captive who was the source of so much alarm to the cardinal and
whose means of escape disturbed the repose of the whole court, was
wholly unconscious of the terror he caused at the Palais Royal.

He had found himself so strictly guarded that he soon perceived the
fruitlessness of any attempt at escape. His vengeance, therefore,
consisted in coining curses on the head of Mazarin; he even tried to
make some verses on him, but soon gave up the attempt, for Monsieur de
Beaufort had not only not received from Heaven the gift of versifying,
he had the greatest difficulty in expressing himself in prose.

The duke was the grandson of Henry IV. and Gabrielle d芒聙聶Estr脙漏es芒聙聰as
good-natured, as brave, as proud, and above all, as Gascon as his
ancestor, but less elaborately educated. After having been for some
time after the death of Louis XIII. the favorite, the confidant, the
first man, in short, at the court, he had been obliged to yield his
place to Mazarin and so became the second in influence and favor; and
eventually, as he was stupid enough to be vexed at this change of
position, the queen had had him arrested and sent to Vincennes in
charge of Guitant, who made his appearance in these pages in the
beginning of this history and whom we shall see again. It is
understood, of course, that when we say 芒聙聹the queen,芒聙聺 Mazarin is meant.

During the five years of this seclusion, which would have improved and
matured the intellect of any other man, M. de Beaufort, had he not
affected to brave the cardinal, despise princes, and walk alone without
adherents or disciples, would either have regained his liberty or made
partisans. But these considerations never occurred to the duke and
every day the cardinal received fresh accounts of him which were as
unpleasant as possible to the minister.

After having failed in poetry, Monsieur de Beaufort tried drawing. He
drew portraits, with a piece of coal, of the cardinal; and as his
talents did not enable him to produce a very good likeness, he wrote
under the picture that there might be little doubt regarding the
original: 芒聙聹Portrait of the Illustrious Coxcomb, Mazarin.芒聙聺 Monsieur de
Chavigny, the governor of Vincennes, waited upon the duke to request
that he would amuse himself in some other way, or that at all events,
if he drew likenesses, he would not put mottoes underneath them. The
next day the prisoner芒聙聶s room was full of pictures and mottoes. Monsieur
de Beaufort, in common with many other prisoners, was bent upon doing
things that were prohibited; and the only resource the governor had
was, one day when the duke was playing at tennis, to efface all these
drawings, consisting chiefly of profiles. M. de Beaufort did not
venture to draw the cardinal芒聙聶s fat face.

The duke thanked Monsieur de Chavigny for having, as he said, cleaned
his drawing-paper for him; he then divided the walls of his room into
compartments and dedicated each of these compartments to some incident
in Mazarin芒聙聶s life. In one was depicted the 芒聙聹Illustrious Coxcomb芒聙聺
receiving a shower of blows from Cardinal Bentivoglio, whose servant he
had been; another, the 芒聙聹Illustrious Mazarin芒聙聺 acting the part of
Ignatius Loyola in a tragedy of that name; a third, the 芒聙聹Illustrious
Mazarin芒聙聺 stealing the portfolio of prime minister from Monsieur de
Chavigny, who had expected to have it; a fourth, the 芒聙聹Illustrious
Coxcomb Mazarin芒聙聺 refusing to give Laporte, the young king芒聙聶s valet,
clean sheets, and saving that 芒聙聹it was quite enough for the king of
France to have clean sheets every three months.芒聙聺

The governor, of course, thought proper to threaten his prisoner that
if he did not give up drawing such pictures he should be obliged to
deprive him of all the means of amusing himself in that manner. To this
Monsieur de Beaufort replied that since every opportunity of
distinguishing himself in arms was taken from him, he wished to make
himself celebrated in the arts; since he could not be a Bayard, he
would become a Raphael or a Michael Angelo. Nevertheless, one day when
Monsieur de Beaufort was walking in the meadow his fire was put out,
his charcoal all removed, taken away; and thus his means of drawing
utterly destroyed.

The poor duke swore, fell into a rage, yelled, and declared that they
wished to starve him to death as they had starved the Mar脙漏chal Ornano
and the Grand Prior of Vend脙麓me; but he refused to promise that he would
not make any more drawings and remained without any fire in the room
all the winter.

His next act was to purchase a dog from one of his keepers. With this
animal, which he called Pistache, he was often shut up for hours alone,
superintending, as every one supposed, its education. At last, when
Pistache was sufficiently well trained, Monsieur de Beaufort invited
the governor and officers of Vincennes to attend a representation which
he was going to have in his apartment.

The party assembled, the room was lighted with waxlights, and the
prisoner, with a bit of plaster he had taken out of the wall of his
room, had traced a long white line, representing a cord, on the floor.
Pistache, on a signal from his master, placed himself on this line,
raised himself on his hind paws, and holding in his front paws a wand
with which clothes used to be beaten, he began to dance upon the line
with as many contortions as a rope-dancer. Having been several times up
and down it, he gave the wand back to his master and began without
hesitation to perform the same evolutions over again.

The intelligent creature was received with loud applause.

The first part of the entertainment being concluded Pistache was
desired to say what o芒聙聶clock it was; he was shown Monsieur de Chavigny芒聙聶s
watch; it was then half-past six; the dog raised and dropped his paw
six times; the seventh he let it remain upraised. Nothing could be
better done; a sun-dial could not have shown the hour with greater
precision.

Then the question was put to him who was the best jailer in all the
prisons in France.

The dog performed three evolutions around the circle and laid himself,
with the deepest respect, at the feet of Monsieur de Chavigny, who at
first seemed inclined to like the joke and laughed long and loud, but a
frown succeeded, and he bit his lips with vexation.

Then the duke put to Pistache this difficult question, who was the
greatest thief in the world?

Pistache went again around the circle, but stopped at no one, and at
last went to the door and began to scratch and bark.

芒聙聹See, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said M. de Beaufort, 芒聙聹this wonderful animal, not
finding here what I ask for, seeks it out of doors; you shall, however,
have his answer. Pistache, my friend, come here. Is not the greatest
thief in the world, Monsieur (the king芒聙聶s secretary) Le Camus, who came
to Paris with twenty francs in his pocket and who now possesses ten
millions?芒聙聺

The dog shook his head.

芒聙聹Then is it not,芒聙聺 resumed the duke, 芒聙聹the Superintendent Emery, who gave
his son, when he was married, three hundred thousand francs and a
house, compared to which the Tuileries are a heap of ruins and the
Louvre a paltry building?芒聙聺

The dog again shook his head as if to say 芒聙聹no.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said the prisoner, 芒聙聹let芒聙聶s think who it can be. Can it be, can
it possibly be, the 芒聙聵Illustrious Coxcomb, Mazarin de Piscina,芒聙聶 hey?芒聙聺

Pistache made violent signs that it was, by raising and lowering his
head eight or ten times successively.

芒聙聹Gentlemen, you see,芒聙聺 said the duke to those present, who dared not
even smile, 芒聙聹that it is the 芒聙聵Illustrious Coxcomb芒聙聶 who is the greatest
thief in the world; at least, according to Pistache.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us go on to another of his exercises.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Gentlemen!芒聙聺芒聙聰there was a profound silence in the room when the duke
again addressed them芒聙聰芒聙聹do you not remember that the Duc de Guise taught
all the dogs in Paris to jump for Mademoiselle de Pons, whom he styled
芒聙聵the fairest of the fair?芒聙聶 Pistache is going to show you how superior
he is to all other dogs. Monsieur de Chavigny, be so good as to lend me
your cane.芒聙聺

Monsieur de Chavigny handed his cane to Monsieur de Beaufort. Monsieur
de Beaufort placed it horizontally at the height of one foot.

芒聙聹Now, Pistache, my good dog, jump the height of this cane for Madame de
Montbazon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 interposed Monsieur de Chavigny, 芒聙聹it seems to me that Pistache
is only doing what other dogs have done when they jumped for
Mademoiselle de Pons.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Stop,芒聙聺 said the duke, 芒聙聹Pistache, jump for the queen.芒聙聺 And he raised
his cane six inches higher.

The dog sprang, and in spite of the height jumped lightly over it.

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said the duke, raising it still six inches higher, 芒聙聹jump for
the king.芒聙聺

The dog obeyed and jumped quickly over the cane.

芒聙聹Now, then,芒聙聺 said the duke, and as he spoke, lowered the cane almost
level with the ground; 芒聙聹Pistache, my friend, jump for the 芒聙聵Illustrious
Coxcomb, Mazarin de Piscina.芒聙聶芒聙聺

The dog turned his back to the cane.

芒聙聹What,芒聙聺 asked the duke, 芒聙聹what do you mean?芒聙聺 and he gave him the cane
again, first making a semicircle from the head to the tail of Pistache.
芒聙聹Jump then, Monsieur Pistache.芒聙聺

But Pistache, as at first, turned round on his legs and stood with his
back to the cane.

Monsieur de Beaufort made the experiment a third time, but by this time
Pistache芒聙聶s patience was exhausted; he threw himself furiously upon the
cane, wrested it from the hands of the prince and broke it with his
teeth.

Monsieur de Beaufort took the pieces out of his mouth and presented
them with great formality to Monsieur de Chavigny, saying that for that
evening the entertainment was ended, but in three months it should be
repeated, when Pistache would have learned a few new tricks.

Three days afterward Pistache was found dead芒聙聰poisoned.

Then the duke said openly that his dog had been killed by a drug with
which they meant to poison him; and one day after dinner he went to
bed, calling out that he had pains in his stomach and that Mazarin had
poisoned him.

This fresh impertinence reached the ears of the cardinal and alarmed
him greatly. The donjon of Vincennes was considered very unhealthy and
Madame de Rambouillet had said that the room in which the Mar脙漏chal
Ornano and the Grand Prior de Vend脙麓me had died was worth its weight in
arsenic芒聙聰a _bon mot_ which had great success. So it was ordered the
prisoner was henceforth to eat nothing that had not previously been
tasted, and La Ramee was in consequence placed near him as taster.

Every kind of revenge was practiced upon the duke by the governor in
return for the insults of the innocent Pistache. De Chavigny, who,
according to report, was a son of Richelieu芒聙聶s, and had been a creature
of the late cardinal芒聙聶s, understood tyranny. He took from the duke all
the steel knives and silver forks and replaced them with silver knives
and wooden forks, pretending that as he had been informed that the duke
was to pass all his life at Vincennes, he was afraid of his prisoner
attempting suicide. A fortnight afterward the duke, going to the tennis
court, found two rows of trees about the size of his little finger
planted by the roadside; he asked what they were for and was told that
they were to shade him from the sun on some future day. One morning the
gardener went to him and told him, as if to please him, that he was
going to plant a bed of asparagus for his especial use. Now, since, as
every one knows, asparagus takes four years in coming to perfection,
this civility infuriated Monsieur de Beaufort.

At last his patience was exhausted. He assembled his keepers, and
notwithstanding his well-known difficulty of utterance, addressed them
as follows:

芒聙聹Gentlemen! will you permit a grandson of Henry IV. to be overwhelmed
with insults and ignominy?

芒聙聹Odds fish! as my grandfather used to say, I once reigned in Paris! do
you know that? I had the king and Monsieur the whole of one day in my
care. The queen at that time liked me and called me the most honest man
in the kingdom. Gentlemen and citizens, set me free; I shall go to the
Louvre and strangle Mazarin. You shall be my body-guard. I will make
you all captains, with good pensions! Odds fish! On! march forward!芒聙聺

But eloquent as he might be, the eloquence of the grandson of Henry IV.
did not touch those hearts of stone; not one man stirred, so Monsieur
de Beaufort was obliged to be satisfied with calling them all kinds of
rascals underneath the sun.

Sometimes, when Monsieur de Chavigny paid him a visit, the duke used to
ask him what he should think if he saw an army of Parisians, all fully
armed, appear at Vincennes to deliver him from prison.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 answered De Chavigny, with a low bow, 芒聙聹I have on the
ramparts twenty pieces of artillery and in my casemates thirty thousand
guns. I should bombard the troops till not one grain of gunpowder was
unexploded.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but after you had fired off your thirty thousand guns they would
take the donjon; the donjon being taken, I should be obliged to let
them hang you芒聙聰at which I should be most unhappy, certainly.芒聙聺

And in his turn the duke bowed low to Monsieur de Chavigny.

芒聙聹For myself, on the other hand, my lord,芒聙聺 returned the governor, 芒聙聹when
the first rebel should pass the threshold of my postern doors I should
be obliged to kill you with my own hand, since you were confided
peculiarly to my care and as I am obliged to give you up, dead or
alive.芒聙聺

And once more he bowed low before his highness.

These bitter-sweet pleasantries lasted ten minutes, sometimes longer,
but always finished thus:

Monsieur de Chavigny, turning toward the door, used to call out:
芒聙聹Halloo! La Ramee!芒聙聺

La Ramee came into the room.

芒聙聹La Ramee, I recommend Monsieur le Duc to you, particularly; treat him
as a man of his rank and family ought to be treated; that is, never
leave him alone an instant.芒聙聺

La Ramee became, therefore, the duke芒聙聶s dinner guest by compulsion芒聙聰an
eternal keeper, the shadow of his person; but La Ramee芒聙聰gay, frank,
convivial, fond of play, a great hand at tennis, had one defect in the
duke芒聙聶s eyes芒聙聰his incorruptibility.

Now, although La Ramee appreciated, as of a certain value, the honor of
being shut up with a prisoner of so great importance, still the
pleasure of living in intimacy with the grandson of Henry IV. hardly
compensated for the loss of that which he had experienced in going from
time to time to visit his family.

One may be a jailer or a keeper and at the same time a good father and
husband. La Ramee adored his wife and children, whom now he could only
catch a glimpse of from the top of the wall, when in order to please
him they used to walk on the opposite side of the moat. 芒聙聶Twas too brief
an enjoyment, and La Ramee felt that the gayety of heart he had
regarded as the cause of health (of which it was perhaps rather the
result) would not long survive such a mode of life.

He accepted, therefore, with delight, an offer made to him by his
friend the steward of the Duc de Grammont, to give him a substitute; he
also spoke of it to Monsieur de Chavigny, who promised that he would
not oppose it in any way芒聙聰that is, if he approved of the person
proposed.

We consider it useless to draw a physical or moral portrait of Grimaud;
if, as we hope, our readers have not wholly forgotten the first part of
this work, they must have preserved a clear idea of that estimable
individual, who is wholly unchanged, except that he is twenty years
older, an advance in life that has made him only more silent; although,
since the change that had been working in himself, Athos had given
Grimaud permission to speak.

But Grimaud had for twelve or fifteen years preserved habitual silence,
and a habit of fifteen or twenty years芒聙聶 duration becomes second nature.




Chapter XVIII.
Grimaud begins his Functions.


Grimaud thereupon presented himself with his smooth exterior at the
donjon of Vincennes. Now Monsieur de Chavigny piqued himself on his
infallible penetration; for that which almost proved that he was the
son of Richelieu was his everlasting pretension; he examined
attentively the countenance of the applicant for place and fancied that
the contracted eyebrows, thin lips, hooked nose, and prominent
cheek-bones of Grimaud were favorable signs. He addressed about twelve
words to him; Grimaud answered in four.

芒聙聹Here芒聙聶s a promising fellow and it is I who have found out his merits,芒聙聺
said Monsieur de Chavigny. 芒聙聹Go,芒聙聺 he added, 芒聙聹and make yourself agreeable
to Monsieur la Ramee, and tell him that you suit me in all respects.芒聙聺

Grimaud had every quality that could attract a man on duty who wishes
to have a deputy. So, after a thousand questions which met with only a
word in reply, La Ramee, fascinated by this sobriety in speech, rubbed
his hands and engaged Grimaud.

芒聙聹My orders?芒聙聺 asked Grimaud.

芒聙聹They are these; never to leave the prisoner alone; to keep away from
him every pointed or cutting instrument, and to prevent his conversing
any length of time with the keepers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Those are all?芒聙聺 asked Grimaud.

芒聙聹All now,芒聙聺 replied La Ramee.

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 answered Grimaud; and he went right to the prisoner.

The duke was in the act of combing his beard, which he had allowed to
grow, as well as his hair, in order to reproach Mazarin with his
wretched appearance and condition. But having some days previously seen
from the top of the donjon Madame de Montbazon pass in her carriage,
and still cherishing an affection for that beautiful woman, he did not
wish to be to her what he wished to be to Mazarin, and in the hope of
seeing her again, had asked for a leaden comb, which was allowed him.
The comb was to be a leaden one, because his beard, like that of most
fair people, was rather red; he therefore dyed it thus whilst combing
it.

As Grimaud entered he saw this comb on the tea-table; he took it up,
and as he took it he made a low bow.

The duke looked at this strange figure with surprise. The figure put
the comb in its pocket.

芒聙聹Ho! hey! what芒聙聶s that?芒聙聺 cried the duke. 芒聙聹Who is this creature?芒聙聺

Grimaud did not answer, but bowed a second time.

芒聙聹Art thou dumb?芒聙聺 cried the duke.

Grimaud made a sign that he was not.

芒聙聹What art thou, then? Answer! I command thee!芒聙聺 said the duke.

芒聙聹A keeper,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud.

芒聙聹A keeper!芒聙聺 reiterated the duke; 芒聙聹there was nothing wanting in my
collection, except this gallows-bird. Halloo! La Ramee! some one!芒聙聺

La Ramee ran in haste to obey the call.

芒聙聹Who is this wretch who takes my comb and puts it in his pocket?芒聙聺 asked
the duke.

芒聙聹One of your guards, my prince; a man of talent and merit, whom you
will like, as I and Monsieur de Chavigny do, I am sure.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why does he take my comb?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why do you take my lord芒聙聶s comb?芒聙聺 asked La Ramee.

Grimaud drew the comb from his pocket and passing his fingers over the
largest teeth, pronounced this one word, 芒聙聹Pointed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹True,芒聙聺 said La Ramee.

芒聙聹What does the animal say?芒聙聺 asked the duke.

芒聙聹That the king has forbidden your lordship to have any pointed
instrument.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you mad, La Ramee? You yourself gave me this comb.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was very wrong, my lord, for in giving it to you I acted in
opposition to my orders.芒聙聺

The duke looked furiously at Grimaud.

芒聙聹I perceive that this creature will be my particular aversion,芒聙聺 he
muttered.

Grimaud, nevertheless, was resolved for certain reasons not at once to
come to a full rupture with the prisoner; he wanted to inspire, not a
sudden repugnance, but a good, sound, steady hatred; he retired,
therefore, and gave place to four guards, who, having breakfasted,
could attend on the prisoner.

A fresh practical joke now occurred to the duke. He had asked for
crawfish for his breakfast on the following morning; he intended to
pass the day in making a small gallows and hang one of the finest of
these fish in the middle of his room芒聙聰the red color evidently conveying
an allusion to the cardinal芒聙聰so that he might have the pleasure of
hanging Mazarin in effigy without being accused of having hung anything
more significant than a crawfish.

The day was employed in preparations for the execution. Every one grows
childish in prison, but the character of Monsieur de Beaufort was
particularly disposed to become so. In the course of his morning芒聙聶s walk
he collected two or three small branches from a tree and found a small
piece of broken glass, a discovery that quite delighted him. When he
came home he formed his handkerchief into a loop.

Nothing of all this escaped Grimaud, but La Ramee looked on with the
curiosity of a father who thinks that he may perhaps get a cheap idea
concerning a new toy for his children. The guards looked on it with
indifference. When everything was ready, the gallows hung in the middle
of the room, the loop made, and when the duke had cast a glance upon
the plate of crawfish, in order to select the finest specimen among
them, he looked around for his piece of glass; it had disappeared.

芒聙聹Who has taken my piece of glass?芒聙聺 asked the duke, frowning. Grimaud
made a sign to denote that he had done so.

芒聙聹What! thou again! Why didst thou take it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes芒聙聰why?芒聙聺 asked La Ramee.

Grimaud, who held the piece of glass in his hand, said: 芒聙聹Sharp.芒聙聺

芒聙聹True, my lord!芒聙聺 exclaimed La Ramee. 芒聙聹Ah! deuce take it! we have a
precious fellow here!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur Grimaud!芒聙聺 said the duke, 芒聙聹for your sake I beg of you, never
come within the reach of my fist!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hush! hush!芒聙聺 cried La Ramee, 芒聙聹give me your gibbet, my lord. I will
shape it out for you with my knife.芒聙聺

And he took the gibbet and shaped it out as neatly as possible.

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s it,芒聙聺 said the duke, 芒聙聹now make me a little hole in the floor
whilst I go and fetch the culprit.芒聙聺

La Ramee knelt down and made a hole in the floor; meanwhile the duke
hung the crawfish up by a thread. Then he placed the gibbet in the
middle of the room, bursting with laughter.

La Ramee laughed also and the guards laughed in chorus; Grimaud,
however, did not even smile. He approached La Ramee and showing him the
crawfish hung up by the thread:

芒聙聹Cardinal,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Hung by order of his Highness the Duc de Beaufort!芒聙聺 cried the
prisoner, laughing violently, 芒聙聹and by Master Jacques Chrysostom La
Ramee, the king芒聙聶s commissioner.芒聙聺

La Ramee uttered a cry of horror and rushed toward the gibbet, which he
broke at once and threw the pieces out of the window. He was going to
throw the crawfish out also, when Grimaud snatched it from his hands.

芒聙聹Good to eat!芒聙聺 he said, and put it in his pocket.

This scene so enchanted the duke that at the moment he forgave Grimaud
for his part in it; but on reflection he hated him more and more, being
convinced he had some evil motive for his conduct.

But the story of the crab made a great noise through the interior of
the donjon and even outside. Monsieur de Chavigny, who at heart
detested the cardinal, took pains to tell the story to two or three
friends, who put it into immediate circulation.

The prisoner happened to remark among the guards one man with a very
good countenance; and he favored this man the more as Grimaud became
the more and more odious to him. One morning he took this man on one
side and had succeeded in speaking to him, when Grimaud entered and
seeing what was going on approached the duke respectfully, but took the
guard by the arm.

芒聙聹Go away,芒聙聺 he said.

The guard obeyed.

芒聙聹You are insupportable!芒聙聺 cried the duke; 芒聙聹I shall beat you.芒聙聺

Grimaud bowed.

芒聙聹I will break every bone in your body!芒聙聺 cried the duke.

Grimaud bowed, but stepped back.

芒聙聹Mr. Spy,芒聙聺 cried the duke, more and more enraged, 芒聙聹I will strangle you
with my own hands.芒聙聺

And he extended his hands toward Grimaud, who merely thrust the guard
out and shut the door behind him. At the same time he felt the duke芒聙聶s
arms on his shoulders like two iron claws; but instead either of
calling out or defending himself, he placed his forefinger on his lips
and said in a low tone:

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 smiling as he uttered the word.

A gesture, a smile and a word from Grimaud, all at once, were so
unusual that his highness stopped short, astounded.

Grimaud took advantage of that instant to draw from his vest a charming
little note with an aristocratic seal, and presented it to the duke
without a word.

The duke, more and more bewildered, let Grimaud loose and took the
note.

芒聙聹From Madame de Montbazon?芒聙聺 he cried.

Grimaud nodded assent.

The duke tore open the note, passed his hands over his eyes, for he was
dazzled and confused, and read:

芒聙聹My Dear Duke,芒聙聰You may entirely confide in the brave lad who will give
you this note; he has consented to enter the service of your keeper and
to shut himself up at Vincennes with you, in order to prepare and
assist your escape, which we are contriving. The moment of your
deliverance is at hand; have patience and courage and remember that in
spite of time and absence all your friends continue to cherish for you
the sentiments they have so long professed and truly entertained.

芒聙聹Yours wholly and most affectionately,

芒聙聹Marie de Montbazon.

芒聙聹P.S.芒聙聰I sign my full name, for I should be vain if I could suppose that
after five years of absence you would remember my initials.芒聙聺

The poor duke became perfectly giddy. What for five years he had been
wanting芒聙聰a faithful servant, a friend, a helping hand芒聙聰seemed to have
fallen from Heaven just when he expected it the least.

芒聙聹Oh, dearest Marie! she thinks of me, then, after five years of
separation! Heavens! there is constancy!芒聙聺 Then turning to Grimaud, he
said:

芒聙聹And thou, my brave fellow, thou consentest thus to aid me?芒聙聺

Grimaud signified his assent.

芒聙聹And you have come here with that purpose?芒聙聺

Grimaud repeated the sign.

芒聙聹And I was ready to strangle you!芒聙聺 cried the duke.

Grimaud smiled.

芒聙聹Wait, then,芒聙聺 said the duke, fumbling in his pocket. 芒聙聹Wait,芒聙聺 he
continued, renewing his fruitless search; 芒聙聹it shall not be said that
such devotion to a grandson of Henry IV. went without recompense.芒聙聺

The duke芒聙聶s endeavors evinced the best intention in the world, but one
of the precautions taken at Vincennes was that of allowing prisoners to
keep no money. Whereupon Grimaud, observing the duke芒聙聶s disappointment,
drew from his pocket a purse filled with gold and handed it to him.

芒聙聹Here is what you are looking for,芒聙聺 he said.

The duke opened the purse and wanted to empty it into Grimaud芒聙聶s hands,
but Grimaud shook his head.

芒聙聹Thank you, monseigneur,芒聙聺 he said, drawing back; 芒聙聹I am paid.芒聙聺

The duke went from one surprise to another. He held out his hand.
Grimaud drew near and kissed it respectfully. The grand manner of Athos
had left its mark on Grimaud.

芒聙聹What shall we do? and when? and how proceed?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is now eleven,芒聙聺 answered Grimaud. 芒聙聹Let my lord at two o芒聙聶clock ask
leave to make up a game at tennis with La Ramee and let him send two or
three balls over the ramparts.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your highness will approach the walls and call out to a man who works
in the moat to send them back again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I understand,芒聙聺 said the duke.

Grimaud made a sign that he was going away.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 cried the duke, 芒聙聹will you not accept any money from me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wish my lord would make me one promise.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! speak!芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis this: when we escape together, that I shall go everywhere and be
always first; for if my lord should be overtaken and caught, there芒聙聶s
every chance of his being brought back to prison, whereas if I am
caught the least that can befall me is to be芒聙聰hung.芒聙聺

芒聙聹True, on my honor as a gentleman it shall be as thou dost suggest.芒聙聺

Grimaud saluted in his politest manner and withdrew, as was his custom

La Ramee went out, but in five minutes one of the officers of the guard
entered in compliance with the strict orders of the cardinal that the
prisoner should never be left alone a moment.

But during these five minutes the duke had had time to read again the
note from Madame de Montbazon, which proved to the prisoner that his
friends were concerting plans for his deliverance, but in what way he
knew not.

But his confidence in Grimaud, whose petty persecutions he now
perceived were only a blind, increased, and he conceived the highest
opinion of his intellect and resolved to trust entirely to his
guidance.




Chapter XIX.
P脙垄t脙漏s made by the Successor of Father Marteau are described.


In half an hour La Ramee returned, full of glee, like most men who have
eaten, and more especially drank to their heart芒聙聶s content. The p脙垄t脙漏s
were excellent, the wine delicious.

The weather was fine and the game at tennis took place in the open air.

At two o芒聙聶clock the tennis balls began, according to Grimaud芒聙聶s

proposed to send some one to pick them up, but the duke remarked that
it would be losing time; and going near the rampart himself and looking

芒聙聹Hey, friend!芒聙聺 cried the duke.

The man raised his head and the duke was about to utter a cry of

芒聙聹Well? Who芒聙聶s up there?芒聙聺 said the man.

The gardener nodded and began to fling up the balls, which were picked

La Ramee was in ecstasies at having beaten a prince of the blood.

The duke went indoors and retired to bed, where he spent, indeed, the

carried off all his clothes, in order to be certain that the duke would
not stir. However, the duke contrived to hide the ball under his

芒聙聹Your highness芒聙聶s most devoted servant,

芒聙聹In the Bastile, as elsewhere,

芒聙聹Comte de Rochefort.芒聙聺

The duke, who had latterly been allowed a fire, burned the letter, but

芒聙聹Ah, my friend!芒聙聺 cried the duke, 芒聙聹you are so good; if I could but do as

芒聙聹Well, my lord,芒聙聺 said La Ramee, falling into the trap, 芒聙聹what is there

芒聙聹You are right,芒聙聺 said the duke. 芒聙聹If I am to remain here permanently, as

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 said the Duc de Beaufort to himself, 芒聙聹every man in order that

The proposal was seductive, but La Ramee was an old stager, acquainted
with all the traps a prisoner was likely to set. Monsieur de Beaufort

proposed breakfast cover some stratagem? He reflected, but he
remembered that he himself would have charge of the food and the wine

The duke had followed with anxiety La Ramee芒聙聶s unspoken soliloquy,

exempt芒聙聶s face suddenly brightened.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 he asked, 芒聙聹that will do, will it not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺

Nothing could be more agreeable to the duke, however, he had presence

芒聙聹I will direct him to stand behind your chair, and since he doesn芒聙聶t

The duke shrugged his shoulders.

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 with well acted good-humor, 芒聙聹I allow Grimaud, but no one

芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺 replied the prince; 芒聙聹surely, at the death of Mazarin. I am

faster芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

La Ramee was going out. He stopped, however, at the door for an

芒聙聹Whom does your highness wish me to send to you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Any one, except Grimaud.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The officer of the guard, then, with his chessboard?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

Five minutes afterward the officer entered and the duke seemed to be
immersed in the sublime combinations of chess.

A strange thing is the mind, and it is wonderful what revolutions may
be wrought in it by a sign, a word, a hope. The duke had been five
years in prison, and now to him, looking back upon them, those five
years, which had passed so slowly, seemed not so long a time as were
the two days, the forty-eight hours, which still parted him from the
time fixed for his escape. Besides, there was one thing that engaged
his most anxious thought芒聙聰in what way was the escape to be effected?
They had told him to hope for it, but had not told him what was to be
hidden in the mysterious p脙垄t脙漏. And what friends awaited him without? He
had friends, then, after five years in prison? If that were so he was
indeed a highly favored prince. He forgot that besides his friends of
his own sex, a woman, strange to say, had remembered him. It is true
that she had not, perhaps, been scrupulously faithful to him, but she
had remembered him; that was something.

So the duke had more than enough to think about; accordingly he fared
at chess as he had fared at tennis; he made blunder upon blunder and
the officer with whom he played found him easy game.

But his successive defeats did service to the duke in one way芒聙聰they
killed time for him till eight o芒聙聶clock in the evening; then would come
night, and with night, sleep. So, at least, the duke believed; but
sleep is a capricious fairy, and it is precisely when one invokes her
presence that she is most likely to keep him waiting. The duke waited
until midnight, turning on his mattress like St. Laurence on his
gridiron. Finally he slept.

But at daybreak he awoke. Wild dreams had disturbed his repose. He
dreamed that he was endowed with wings芒聙聰he wished to fly away. For a
time these wings supported him, but when he reached a certain height
this new aid failed him. His wings were broken and he seemed to sink
into a bottomless abyss, whence he awoke, bathed in perspiration and
nearly as much overcome as if he had really fallen. He fell asleep
again and another vision appeared. He was in a subterranean passage by
which he was to leave Vincennes. Grimaud was walking before him with a
lantern. By degrees the passage narrowed, yet the duke continued his
course. At last it became so narrow that the fugitive tried in vain to
proceed. The sides of the walls seem to close in, even to press against
him. He made fruitless efforts to go on; it was impossible.
Nevertheless, he still saw Grimaud with his lantern in front,
advancing. He wished to call out to him but could not utter a word.
Then at the other extremity he heard the footsteps of those who were
pursuing him. These steps came on, came fast. He was discovered; all
hope of flight was gone. Still the walls seemed to be closing on him;
they appeared to be in concert with his enemies. At last he heard the
voice of La Ramee. La Ramee took his hand and laughed aloud. He was
captured again, and conducted to the low and vaulted chamber, in which
Ornano, Puylaurens, and his uncle had died. Their three graves were
there, rising above the ground, and a fourth was also there, yawning
for its ghastly tenant.

The duke was obliged to make as many efforts to awake as he had done to
go to sleep; and La Ramee found him so pale and fatigued that he
inquired whether he was ill.

芒聙聹In fact,芒聙聺 said one of the guards who had remained in the chamber and
had been kept awake by a toothache, brought on by the dampness of the
atmosphere, 芒聙聹my lord has had a very restless night and two or three
times, while dreaming, he called for help.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is the matter with your highness?芒聙聺 asked La Ramee.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis your fault, you simpleton,芒聙聺 answered the duke. 芒聙聹With your idle
nonsense yesterday about escaping, you worried me so that I dreamed
that I was trying to escape and broke my neck in doing so.芒聙聺

La Ramee laughed.

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹芒聙聶tis a warning from Heaven. Never commit such an
imprudence as to try to escape, except in your dreams.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you are right, my dear La Ramee,芒聙聺 said the duke, wiping away the

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 said La Ramee; and one by one he sent away the guards, on

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 asked the duke when they were alone.

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 replied La Ramee, 芒聙聹your supper is ordered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! and what is it to be? Monsieur, my majordomo, will there be a

芒聙聹I should think so, indeed芒聙聰almost as high as a tower.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 exclaimed the duke, rubbing his hands.

芒聙聹Two poniards, a knotted rope and a _poire d芒聙聶angoisse_.芒聙聺 *

* This _poire d芒聙聶angoisse_ was a famous gag, in the form of a pear,

芒聙聹Yes, I understand.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My dear Grimaud, thou speakest seldom, but when thou dost, one must do
thee justice芒聙聰thy words are words of gold.芒聙聺




Chapter XX.
One of Marie Michon芒聙聶s Adventures.


Whilst these projects were being formed by the Duc de Beaufort and

Athos cast a glance at his feet, hands and hair芒聙聰those three marks of

Athos followed the footman, who led him through a long succession of

Madame de Chevreuse, whose name appears so often in our story 芒聙聹The

unlike the girl who had jumped, with Anne of Austria, over the moat of
the Tuileries in 1563. In all other respects she was the same mad
creature who threw over her amours such an air of originality as to
make them proverbial for eccentricity in her family.

She was in a little boudoir, hung with blue damask, adorned by red
flowers, with a foliage of gold, looking upon a garden; and reclined

She held a book in her hand and her arm was supported by a cushion.

Athos appeared.

He was dressed in violet-tinted velvet, trimmed with silk of the same

芒聙聹A year after, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what said the abb脙漏?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

As to his title and fortune芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Upon my soul, monsieur,芒聙聺 said the duchess, 芒聙聹you are a true nobleman!

芒聙聹There, in the _salon_. I will have him come in, if you really wish

Athos moved toward the door; the duchess held him back.

芒聙聹Is he handsome?芒聙聺 she asked.

Athos smiled.

芒聙聹He resembles his mother.芒聙聺

The duchess could not restrain a cry of joy on seeing so handsome a

芒聙聹Better for him in future that he should be supposed never to have seen

芒聙聹Oh, sir!芒聙聺 cried Raoul.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis well; I will go there also, for a few minutes,芒聙聺 said the duchess;

Chapter XXI.
The Abb脙漏 Scarron.


There was once in the Rue des Tournelles a house known by all the sedan
chairmen and footmen of Paris, and yet, nevertheless, this house was
neither that of a great lord nor of a rich man. There was neither
dining, nor playing at cards, nor dancing in that house. Nevertheless,
it was the rendezvous of the great world and all Paris went there. It
was the abode of the little Abb脙漏 Scarron.

In the home of the witty abb脙漏 dwelt incessant laughter; there all the
items of the day had their source and were so quickly transformed,
misrepresented, metamorphosed, some into epigrams, some into
falsehoods, that every one was anxious to pass an hour with little
Scarron, listening to what he said, reporting it to others.

The diminutive Abb脙漏 Scarron, who, however, was an abb脙漏 only because he
owned an abbey, and not because he was in orders, had formerly been one
of the gayest prebendaries in the town of Mans, which he inhabited. On
a day of the carnival he had taken a notion to provide an unusual
entertainment for that good town, of which he was the life and soul. He
had made his valet cover him with honey; then, opening a feather bed,
he had rolled in it and had thus become the most grotesque fowl it is
possible to imagine. He then began to visit his friends of both sexes,
in that strange costume. At first he had been followed through
astonishment, then with derisive shouts, then the porters had insulted
him, then children had thrown stones at him, and finally he was obliged
to run, to escape the missiles. As soon as he took to flight every one
pursued him, until, pressed on all sides, Scarron found no way of
escaping his escort, except by throwing himself into the river; but the
water was icy cold. Scarron was heated, the cold seized on him, and
when he reached the farther bank he found himself crippled.

Every means had been employed in vain to restore the use of his limbs.
He had been subjected to a severe disciplinary course of medicine, at
length he sent away all his doctors, declaring that he preferred the
disease to the treatment, and came to Paris, where the fame of his wit
had preceded him. There he had a chair made on his own plan, and one
day, visiting Anne of Austria in this chair, she asked him, charmed as
she was with his wit, if he did not wish for a title.

芒聙聹Yes, your majesty, there is a title which I covet much,芒聙聺 replied
Scarron.

芒聙聹And what is that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That of being _your_ invalid,芒聙聺 answered Scarron.

So he was called the queen芒聙聶s invalid, with a pension of fifteen hundred
francs.

From that lucky moment Scarron led a happy life, spending both income
and principal. One day, however, an emissary of the cardinal芒聙聶s gave him
to understand that he was wrong in receiving the coadjutor so often.

芒聙聹And why?芒聙聺 asked Scarron; 芒聙聹is he not a man of good birth?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Agreeable?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Undeniably.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Witty?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He has, unfortunately, too much wit.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, why do you wish me to give up seeing such a man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because he is an enemy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of whom?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of the cardinal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 answered Scarron, 芒聙聹I continue to receive Monsieur Gilles
Despreaux, who thinks ill of _me_, and you wish me to give up seeing
the coadjutor, because he thinks ill of another man. Impossible!芒聙聺

The conversation had rested there and Scarron, through sheer obstinacy,
had seen Monsieur de Gondy only the more frequently.

Now, the very morning of which we speak was that of his quarter-day
payment, and Scarron, as usual, had sent his servant to get his money
at the pension-office, but the man had returned and said that the
government had no more money to give Monsieur Scarron.

It was on Thursday, the abb脙漏芒聙聶s reception day; people went there in
crowds. The cardinal芒聙聶s refusal to pay the pension was known about the
town in half an hour and he was abused with wit and vehemence.

In the Rue Saint Honor脙漏 Athos fell in with two gentlemen whom he did
not know, on horseback like himself, followed by a lackey like himself,
and going in the same direction that he was. One of them, hat in hand,
said to him:

芒聙聹Would you believe it, monsieur? that contemptible Mazarin has stopped
poor Scarron芒聙聶s pension.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is unreasonable,芒聙聺 said Athos, saluting in his turn the two
cavaliers. And they separated with courteous gestures.

芒聙聹It happens well that we are going there this evening,芒聙聺 said Athos to
the vicomte; 芒聙聹we will pay our compliments to that poor man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What, then, is this Monsieur Scarron, who thus puts all Paris in
commotion? Is he some minister out of office?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, no, not at all, vicomte,芒聙聺 Athos replied; 芒聙聹he is simply a gentleman
of great genius who has fallen into disgrace with the cardinal through
having written certain verses against him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do gentlemen, then, make verses?芒聙聺 asked Raoul, na脙炉vely, 芒聙聹I thought it
was derogatory.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So it is, my dear vicomte,芒聙聺 said Athos, laughing, 芒聙聹to make bad ones;
but to make good ones increases fame芒聙聰witness Monsieur de Rotrou.
Nevertheless,芒聙聺 he continued, in the tone of one who gives wholesome
advice, 芒聙聹I think it is better not to make them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹this Monsieur Scarron is a poet?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; you are warned, vicomte. Consider well what you do in that house.
Talk only by gestures, or rather always listen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, monsieur,芒聙聺 replied Raoul.

芒聙聹You will see me talking with one of my friends, the Abb脙漏 d芒聙聶Herblay, of
whom you have often heard me speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I remember him, monsieur.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come near to us from time to time, as if to speak; but do not speak,
and do not listen. That little stratagem may serve to keep off
interlopers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well, monsieur; I will obey you at all points.芒聙聺

Athos made two visits in Paris; at seven o芒聙聶clock he and Raoul directed
their steps to the Rue des Tournelles; it was stopped by porters,
horses and footmen. Athos forced his way through and entered, followed

a canopy of tapestry, under which there moved, enveloped in a quilt of

joking, complimenting芒聙聰yet suffering芒聙聰and toying nervously with a small
switch.

Around this kind of rolling tent pressed a crowd of gentlemen and
ladies. The room was neatly, comfortably furnished. Large valances of
silk, embroidered with flowers of gay colors, which were rather faded,
fell from the wide windows; the fittings of the room were simple, but
in excellent taste. Two well trained servingmen were in attendance on
the company. On perceiving Athos, Aramis advanced toward him, took him
by the hand and presented him to Scarron. Raoul remained silent, for he
was not prepared for the dignity of the _bel esprit_.

After some minutes the door opened and a footman announced Mademoiselle
Paulet.

Athos touched the shoulder of the vicomte.

芒聙聹Look at this lady, Raoul, she is an historic personage; it was to
visit her King Henry IV. was going when he was assassinated.芒聙聺

Every one thronged around Mademoiselle Paulet, for she was always very
much the fashion. She was a tall woman, with a slender figure and a
forest of golden curls, such as Raphael was fond of and Titian has
painted all his Magdalens with. This fawn-colored hair, or, perhaps the
sort of ascendancy which she had over other women, gave her the name of
芒聙聹La Lionne.芒聙聺 Mademoiselle Paulet took her accustomed seat, but before
sitting down, she cast, in all her queen-like grandeur, a look around

Athos smiled.

芒聙聹Mademoiselle Paulet has observed you, vicomte; go and bow to her;
don芒聙聶t try to appear anything but what you are, a true country youth; on
no account speak to her of Henry IV.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When shall we two walk together?芒聙聺 Athos then said to Aramis.

芒聙聹Presently芒聙聰there are not a sufficient number of people here yet; we
shall be remarked.芒聙聺

At this moment the door opened and in walked the coadjutor.

At this name every one looked around, for his was already a very
celebrated name. Athos did the same. He knew the Abb脙漏 de Gondy only by

He saw a little dark man, ill made and awkward with his hands in

Scarron turned around toward him and came to meet him in his chair.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, on seeing him, 芒聙聹you are in disgrace, then,
abb脙漏?芒聙聺

This was the orthodox phrase. It had been said that evening a hundred

芒聙聹Monsieur, the Cardinal Mazarin has been so kind as to think of me,芒聙聺 he

芒聙聹Oh, no!芒聙聺 cried Scarron, 芒聙聹I should compromise you too much.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall borrow from the queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But her majesty has no property,芒聙聺 interposed Aramis.

At this moment the door opened and Madame de Chevreuse was announced.

In the midst of all the compliments that awaited her on her entrance,

芒聙聹Apropos,芒聙聺 she said, as if to drive away thoughts that pursued her in

芒聙聹Bravo bravo!芒聙聺 cried a tall man with an enormous mustache and a long

芒聙聹Who is this officer,芒聙聺 inquired Raoul of Athos, 芒聙聹who is speaking?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Scud脙漏ry, the author of 芒聙聵Cl脙漏lie,芒聙聶 and of 芒聙聵Le Grand Cyrus,芒聙聶

Raoul turned and saw two faces just arrived. One was perfectly

Raoul resolved not to quit the room without having spoken to the

芒聙聹Laugh, then,芒聙聺 said Monsieur de Retz; 芒聙聹they are looking at us.芒聙聺 And

As soon as the two friends met they began a conversation which was

Raoul then approached them as Athos had directed him to do.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis a rondeau by Monsieur Voiture that monsieur l芒聙聶abb脙漏 is repeating

芒聙聹At what time?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Six o芒聙聶clock.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where?芒聙聺

芒聙聹At Saint Mand脙漏.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Count de Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And then philosophic ideas are wholly wanting in Voiture芒聙聶s works, but
