芒聙聹Which means that you know very little about the nonsense which here
flows down our streets. So much the better, young man! so much the
better! Don芒聙聶t try to understand it芒聙聰you will only lose your time.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You forgive me, then, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹and you will deign to tell me
who is the person that you call the young Indian?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly; one of the most charming persons that lives芒聙聰Mademoiselle
Frances d芒聙聶Aubign脙漏.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Does she belong to the family of the celebrated Agrippa, the friend of
Henry IV.?芒聙聺

芒聙聹His granddaughter. She comes from Martinique, so I call her the
beautiful Indian.芒聙聺

Raoul looked surprised and his eyes met those of the young lady, who
smiled.

The company went on speaking of the poet Voiture.

芒聙聹Monsieur,芒聙聺 said Mademoiselle d芒聙聶Aubign脙漏 to Scarron, as if she wished to
join in the conversation he was engaged in with Raoul, 芒聙聹do you not
admire Monsieur Voiture芒聙聶s friends? Listen how they pull him to pieces
even whilst they praise him; one takes away from him all claim to good
sense, another robs him of his poetry, a third of his originality,
another of his humor, another of his independence of character, a
sixth芒聙聰but, good heavens! what will they _leave_ him? as Mademoiselle de
Scud脙漏ry remarks.芒聙聺

Scarron and Raoul laughed. The fair Indian, astonished at the sensation
her observation produced, looked down and resumed her air of na脙炉vet脙漏.

Athos, still within the inclosure of the window, watched this scene
with a smile of disdain on his lips.

芒聙聹Tell the Comte de la F脙篓re to come to me,芒聙聺 said Madame de Chevreuse, 芒聙聹I
want to speak to him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹want it to be thought that I do _not_
speak to him. I admire, I love him芒聙聰for I know his former adventures芒聙聰but
I shall not speak to him until the day after to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And why day after to-morrow?芒聙聺 asked Madame de Chevreuse.

芒聙聹You will know that to-morrow evening,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, smiling.

芒聙聹Really, my dear Gondy,芒聙聺 said the duchess, 芒聙聹you remind one of the
Apocalypse. Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聺 she added, turning toward Aramis,
芒聙聹will you be my servant once more this evening?芒聙聺

芒聙聹How can you doubt it?芒聙聺 replied Aramis; 芒聙聹this evening, to-morrow,
always; command me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will, then. Go and look for the Comte de la F脙篓re; I wish to speak
with him.芒聙聺

Aramis found Athos and brought him.

芒聙聹Monsieur le comte,芒聙聺 said the duchess, giving him a letter, 芒聙聹here is
what I promised you; our young friend will be extremely well received.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, he is very happy in owing any obligation to you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have no reason to envy him on that score, for I owe to you the
pleasure of knowing him,芒聙聺 replied the witty woman, with a smile which
recalled Marie Michon to Aramis and to Athos.

As she uttered that _bon mot_, she arose and asked for her carriage.
Mademoiselle Paulet had already gone; Mademoiselle de Scud脙漏ry was
going.

芒聙聹Vicomte,芒聙聺 said Athos to Raoul, 芒聙聹follow the duchess; beg her to do you
the favor to take your arm in going downstairs, and thank her as you
descend.芒聙聺

The fair Indian approached Scarron.

芒聙聹You are going already?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹One of the last, as you see; if you hear anything of Monsieur Voiture,
be so kind as to send me word to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 said Scarron, 芒聙聹he may die now.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why?芒聙聺 asked the young girl with the velvet eyes.

芒聙聹Certainly; his panegyric has been uttered.芒聙聺

They parted, laughing, she turning back to gaze at the poor paralytic
man with interest, he looking after her with eyes of love.

One by one the several groups broke up. Scarron seemed not to observe
that certain of his guests had talked mysteriously, that letters had
passed from hand to hand and that the assembly had seemed to have a
secret purpose quite apart from the literary discussion carried on with
so much ostentation. What was all that to Scarron? At his house
rebellion could be planned with impunity, for, as we have said, since
that morning he had ceased to be 芒聙聹the queen芒聙聶s invalid.芒聙聺

As to Raoul, he had attended the duchess to her carriage, where, as she
took her seat, she gave him her hand to kiss; then, by one of those
wild caprices which made her so adorable and at the same time so
dangerous, she had suddenly put her arm around his neck and kissed his
forehead, saying:

芒聙聹Vicomte, may my good wishes and this kiss bring you good fortune!芒聙聺

Then she had pushed him away and directed the coachman to stop at the
Hotel de Luynes. The carriage had started, Madame de Chevreuse had made
a parting gesture to the young man, and Raoul had returned in a state
of stupefaction.

Athos surmised what had taken place and smiled. 芒聙聹Come, vicomte,芒聙聺 he
said, 芒聙聹it is time for you to go to bed; you will start in the morning
for the army of monsieur le prince. Sleep well your last night as
citizen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am to be a soldier then?芒聙聺 said the young man. 芒聙聹Oh, monsieur, I thank
you with all my heart.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, count,芒聙聺 said the Abb脙漏 d芒聙聶Herblay; 芒聙聹I return to my convent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, abb脙漏,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹I am to preach to-morrow and have
twenty texts to examine this evening.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the count; 芒聙聹I am going to sleep twenty-four
hours; I am just falling down with fatigue.芒聙聺

The three men saluted one another, whilst exchanging a last look.

Scarron followed their movements with a glance from the corner of his
eye.

芒聙聹Not one of them will do as he says,芒聙聺 he murmured, with his little
monkey smile; 芒聙聹but they may do as they please, the brave gentlemen! Who
knows if they will not manage to restore to me my pension? They can
move their arms, they can, and that is much. Alas, I have only my
tongue, but I will try to show that it is good for something. Ho,
there, Champenois! here, it is eleven o芒聙聶clock. Come and roll me to bed.
Really, that Demoiselle d芒聙聶Aubign脙漏 is very charming!芒聙聺

So the invalid disappeared soon afterward and went into his
sleeping-room; and one by one the lights in the _salon_ of the Rue des
Tournelles were extinguished.




Chapter XXII.
Saint Denis.


The day had begun to break when Athos arose and dressed himself. It was
plain, by a paleness still greater than usual, and by those traces
which loss of sleep leaves on the face, that he must have passed almost
the whole of the night without sleeping. Contrary to the custom of a
man so firm and decided, there was this morning in his personal
appearance something tardy and irresolute.

He was occupied with the preparations for Raoul芒聙聶s departure and was
seeking to gain time. In the first place he himself furbished a sword,
which he drew from its perfumed leather sheath; he examined it to see
if its hilt was well guarded and if the blade was firmly attached to
the hilt. Then he placed at the bottom of the valise belonging to the
young man a small bag of louis, called Olivain, the lackey who had
followed him from Blois, and made him pack the valise under his own
eyes, watchful to see that everything should be put in which might be
useful to a young man entering on his first campaign.

At length, after occupying about an hour in these preparations, he
opened the door of the room in which the vicomte slept, and entered.

The sun, already high, penetrated into the room through the window, the
curtains of which Raoul had neglected to close on the previous evening.
He was still sleeping, his head gracefully reposing on his arm.

Athos approached and hung over the youth in an attitude full of tender
melancholy; he looked long on this young man, whose smiling mouth and
half closed eyes bespoke soft dreams and lightest slumber, as if his
guardian angel watched over him with solicitude and affection. By
degrees Athos gave himself up to the charms of his reverie in the
proximity of youth, so pure, so fresh. His own youth seemed to
reappear, bringing with it all those savoury remembrances, which are
like perfumes more than thoughts. Between the past and the present was
an ineffable abyss. But imagination has the wings of an angel of light
and travels safely through or over the seas where we have been almost
shipwrecked, the darkness in which our illusions are lost, the
precipice whence our happiness has been hurled and swallowed up. He
remembered that all the first part of his life had been embittered by a
woman and he thought with alarm of the influence love might assume over
so fine, and at the same time so vigorous an organization as that of
Raoul.

In recalling all he had been through, he foresaw all that Raoul might
suffer; and the expression of the deep and tender compassion which
throbbed in his heart was pictured in the moist eye with which he gazed
on the young man.

At this moment Raoul awoke, without a cloud on his face without
weariness or lassitude; his eyes were fixed on those of Athos and
perhaps he comprehended all that passed in the heart of the man who was
awaiting his awakening as a lover awaits the awakening of his mistress,
for his glance, in return, had all the tenderness of love.

芒聙聹You are there, sir?芒聙聺 he said, respectfully.

芒聙聹Yes, Raoul,芒聙聺 replied the count.

芒聙聹And you did not awaken me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wished to leave you still to enjoy some moments of sleep, my child;
you must be fatigued from yesterday.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, sir, how good you are!芒聙聺

Athos smiled.

芒聙聹How do you feel this morning?芒聙聺 he inquired.

芒聙聹Perfectly well; quite rested, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are still growing,芒聙聺 Athos continued, with that charming and
paternal interest felt by a grown man for a youth.

芒聙聹Oh, sir, I beg your pardon!芒聙聺 exclaimed Raoul, ashamed of so much
attention; 芒聙聹in an instant I shall be dressed.芒聙聺

Athos then called Olivain.

芒聙聹Everything,芒聙聺 said Olivain to Athos, 芒聙聹has been done according to your
directions; the horses are waiting.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I was asleep,芒聙聺 cried Raoul, 芒聙聹whilst you, sir, you had the kindness
to attend to all these details. Truly, sir, you overwhelm me with
benefits!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Therefore you love me a little, I hope,芒聙聺 replied Athos, in a tone of
emotion.

芒聙聹Oh, sir! God knows how much I love, revere you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹See that you forget nothing,芒聙聺 said Athos, appearing to look about him,
that he might hide his emotion.

芒聙聹No, indeed, sir,芒聙聺 answered Raoul.

The servant then approached Athos and said, hesitatingly:

芒聙聹Monsieur le vicomte has no sword.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis well,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹I will take care of that.芒聙聺

They went downstairs, Raoul looking every now and then at the count to
see if the moment of farewell was at hand, but Athos was silent. When
they reached the steps Raoul saw three horses.

芒聙聹Oh, sir! then you are going with me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will accompany you a portion of the way,芒聙聺 said Athos.

Joy shone in Raoul芒聙聶s eyes and he leaped lightly to his saddle.

Athos mounted more slowly, after speaking in a low voice to the lackey,
who, instead of following them immediately, returned to their rooms.
Raoul, delighted at the count芒聙聶s companionship, perceived, or affected
to perceive nothing of this byplay.

They set out, passing over the Pont Neuf; they pursued their way along
the quay then called L芒聙聶Abreuvoir Pepin, and went along by the walls of
the Grand Ch脙垄telet. They proceeded to the Rue Saint Denis.

After passing through the Porte Saint Denis, Athos looked at Raoul芒聙聶s
way of riding and observed:

芒聙聹Take care, Raoul! I have already often told you of this; you must not
forget it, for it is a great defect in a rider. See! your horse is
tired already, he froths at the mouth, whilst mine looks as if he had
only just left the stable. You hold the bit too tight and so make his
mouth hard, so that you will not be able to make him man脜聯uvre quickly.
The safety of a cavalier often depends on the prompt obedience of his
horse. In a week, remember, you will no longer be performing your
man脜聯uvres for practice, but on a field of battle.芒聙聺

Then suddenly, in order not to give too uncomfortable an importance to
this observation:

芒聙聹See, Raoul!芒聙聺 he resumed; 芒聙聹what a fine plain for partridge shooting.芒聙聺

The young man stored in his mind the admonition whilst he admired the
delicate tenderness with which it was bestowed.

芒聙聹I have remarked also another thing,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹which is, that in
firing off your pistol you hold your arm too far outstretched. This
tension lessens the accuracy of the aim. So in twelve times you thrice
missed the mark.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Which you, sir, struck twelve times,芒聙聺 answered Raoul, smiling.

芒聙聹Because I bent my arm and rested my hand on my elbow芒聙聰so; do you
understand what I mean?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir. I have fired since in that manner and have been quite
successful.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What a cold wind!芒聙聺 resumed Athos; 芒聙聹a wintry blast. Apropos, if you
fire芒聙聰and you will do so, for you are recommended to a young general who
is very fond of powder芒聙聰remember that in single combat, which often
takes place in the cavalry, never to fire the first shot. He who fires
the first shot rarely hits his man, for he fires with the apprehension
of being disarmed, before an armed foe; then, whilst he fires, make
your horse rear; that man脜聯uvre has saved my life several times.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall do so, if only in gratitude芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eh!芒聙聺 cried Athos, 芒聙聹are not those fellows poachers they have arrested
yonder? They are. Then another important thing, Raoul: should you be
wounded in a battle, and fall from your horse, if you have any strength
left, disentangle yourself from the line that your regiment has formed;
otherwise, it may be driven back and you will be trampled to death by
the horses. At all events, should you be wounded, write to me that very
instant, or get some one at once to write to me. We are judges of
wounds, we old soldiers,芒聙聺 Athos added, smiling.

芒聙聹Thank you, sir,芒聙聺 answered the young man, much moved.

They arrived that very moment at the gate of the town, guarded by two
sentinels.

芒聙聹Here comes a young gentleman,芒聙聺 said one of them, 芒聙聹who seems as if he
were going to join the army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How do you make that out?芒聙聺 inquired Athos.

芒聙聹By his manner, sir, and his age; he芒聙聶s the second to-day.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Has a young man, such as I am, gone through this morning, then?芒聙聺 asked
Raoul.

芒聙聹Faith, yes, with a haughty presence, a fine equipage; such as the son
of a noble house would have.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He will be my companion on the journey, sir,芒聙聺 cried Raoul. 芒聙聹Alas! he
cannot make me forget what I shall have lost!芒聙聺

Thus talking, they traversed the streets, full of people on account of
the _f脙陋te_, and arrived opposite the old cathedral, where first mass
was going on.

芒聙聹Let us alight; Raoul,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹Olivain, take care of our horses
and give me my sword.芒聙聺

The two gentlemen then went into the church. Athos gave Raoul some of
the holy water. A love as tender as that of a lover for his mistress
dwells, undoubtedly, in some paternal hearts toward a son.

Athos said a word to one of the vergers, who bowed and proceeded toward
the basement.

芒聙聹Come, Raoul,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹let us follow this man.芒聙聺

The verger opened the iron grating that guarded the royal tombs and
stood on the topmost step, whilst Athos and Raoul descended. The
sepulchral depths of the descent were dimly lighted by a silver lamp on
the lowest step; and just below this lamp there was laid, wrapped in a
flowing mantle of violet velvet, worked with fleurs-de-lis of gold, a
catafalque resting on trestles of oak. The young man, prepared for this
scene by the state of his own feelings, which were mournful, and by the
majesty of the cathedral which he had passed through, descended in a
slow and solemn manner and stood with head uncovered before these
mortal spoils of the last king, who was not to be placed by the side of
his forefathers until his successor should take his place there; and
who appeared to abide on that spot, that he might thus address human
pride, so sure to be exalted by the glories of a throne: 芒聙聹Dust of the
earth! Here I await thee!芒聙聺

There was profound silence.

Then Athos raised his hand and pointing to the coffin:

芒聙聹This temporary sepulture is,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹that of a man who was of
feeble mind, yet one whose reign was full of great events; because over
this king watched the spirit of another man, even as this lamp keeps
vigil over this coffin and illumines it. He whose intellect was thus
supreme, Raoul, was the actual sovereign; the other, nothing but a
phantom to whom he lent a soul; and yet, so powerful is majesty amongst
us, this man has not even the honor of a tomb at the feet of him in
whose service his life was worn away. Remember, Raoul, this! If
Richelieu made the king, by comparison, seem small, he made royalty
great. The Palace of the Louvre contains two things芒聙聰the king, who must
die, and royalty, which never dies. The minister, so feared, so hated
by his master, has descended into the tomb, drawing after him the king,
whom he would not leave alone on earth, lest his work should be
destroyed. So blind were his contemporaries that they regarded the
cardinal芒聙聶s death as a deliverance; and I, even I, opposed the designs
of the great man who held the destinies of France within the hollow of
his hand. Raoul, learn how to distinguish the king from royalty; the
king is but a man; royalty is the gift of God. Whenever you hesitate as
to whom you ought to serve, abandon the exterior, the material
appearance for the invisible principle, for the invisible principle is
everything. Raoul, I seem to read your future destiny as through a
cloud. It will be happier, I think, than ours has been. Different in
your fate from us, you will have a king without a minister, whom you
may serve, love, respect. Should the king prove a tyrant, for power
begets tyranny, serve, love, respect royalty, that Divine right, that
celestial spark which makes this dust still powerful and holy, so that
we芒聙聰gentlemen, nevertheless, of rank and condition芒聙聰are as nothing in
comparison with the cold corpse there extended.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall adore God, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹respect royalty and ever serve
the king. And if death be my lot, I hope to die for the king, for
royalty and for God. Have I, sir, comprehended your instructions?芒聙聺

Athos smiled.

芒聙聹Yours is a noble nature.芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹here is your sword.芒聙聺

Raoul bent his knee to the ground.

芒聙聹It was worn by my father, a loyal gentleman. I have worn it in my turn
and it has sometimes not been disgraced when the hilt was in my hand
and the sheath at my side. Should your hand still be too weak to use
this sword, Raoul, so much the better. You will have the more time to
learn to draw it only when it ought to be used.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, putting the sword to his lips as he received it
from the count, 芒聙聹I owe you everything and yet this sword is the most
precious gift you have yet made me. I will wear it, I swear to you, as
a grateful man should do.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis well; arise, vicomte, embrace me.芒聙聺

Raoul arose and threw himself with emotion into the count芒聙聶s arms.

芒聙聹Adieu,芒聙聺 faltered the count, who felt his heart die away within him;
芒聙聹adieu, and think of me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! for ever and ever!芒聙聺 cried the youth; 芒聙聹oh! I swear to you, sir,
should any harm befall me, your name will be the last name that I shall
utter, the remembrance of you my last thought.芒聙聺

Athos hastened upstairs to conceal his emotion, and regained with
hurried steps the porch where Olivain was waiting with the horses.

芒聙聹Olivain,芒聙聺 said Athos, showing the servant Raoul芒聙聶s shoulder-belt,
芒聙聹tighten the buckle of the sword, it falls too low. You will accompany
monsieur le vicomte till Grimaud rejoins you. You know, Raoul, Grimaud
is an old and zealous servant; he will follow you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 answered Raoul.

芒聙聹Now to horse, that I may see you depart!芒聙聺

Raoul obeyed.

芒聙聹Adieu, Raoul,芒聙聺 said the count; 芒聙聹adieu, my dearest boy!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, sir, adieu, my beloved protector.芒聙聺

Athos waved his hand芒聙聰he dared not trust himself to speak: and Raoul
went away, his head uncovered. Athos remained motionless, looking after
him until he turned the corner of the street.

Then the count threw the bridle of his horse into the hands of a
peasant, remounted the steps, went into the cathedral, there to kneel
down in the darkest corner and pray.




Chapter XXIII.
One of the Forty Methods of Escape of the Duc de Beaufort.


Meanwhile time was passing on for the prisoner, as well as for those
who were preparing his escape; only for him it passed more slowly.
Unlike other men, who enter with ardor upon a perilous resolution and
grow cold as the moment of execution approaches, the Duc de Beaufort,
whose buoyant courage had become a proverb, seemed to push time before
him and sought most eagerly to hasten the hour of action. In his escape
alone, apart from his plans for the future, which, it must be admitted,
were for the present sufficiently vague and uncertain, there was a
beginning of vengeance which filled his heart. In the first place his
escape would be a serious misfortune to Monsieur de Chavigny, whom he
hated for the petty persecutions he owed to him. It would be a still
worse affair for Mazarin, whom he execrated for the greater offences he
had committed. It may be observed that there was a proper proportion in
his sentiments toward the governor of the prison and the
minister芒聙聰toward the subordinate and the master.

Then Monsieur de Beaufort, who was so familiar with the interior of the
Palais Royal, though he did not know the relations existing between the
queen and the cardinal, pictured to himself, in his prison, all that
dramatic excitement which would ensue when the rumor should run from
the minister芒聙聶s cabinet to the chamber of Anne of Austria: 芒聙聹Monsieur de
Beaufort has escaped!芒聙聺 Whilst saying that to himself, Monsieur de
Beaufort smiled pleasantly and imagined himself already outside,
breathing the air of the plains and the forests, pressing a strong
horse between his knees and crying out in a loud voice, 芒聙聹I am free!芒聙聺

It is true that on coming to himself he found that he was still within
four walls; he saw La Ramee twirling his thumbs ten feet from him, and
his guards laughing and drinking in the ante-chamber. The only thing
that was pleasant to him in that odious tableau芒聙聰such is the instability
of the human mind芒聙聰was the sullen face of Grimaud, for whom he had at
first conceived such a hatred and who now was all his hope. Grimaud
seemed to him an Antinous. It is needless to say that this
transformation was visible only to the prisoner芒聙聶s feverish imagination.
Grimaud was still the same, and therefore he retained the entire
confidence of his superior, La Ramee, who now relied upon him more than
he did upon himself, for, as we have said, La Ramee felt at the bottom
of his heart a certain weakness for Monsieur de Beaufort.

And so the good La Ramee made a festivity of the little supper with his
prisoner. He had but one fault芒聙聰he was a gourmand; he had found the
p脙垄t脙漏s good, the wine excellent. Now the successor of P脙篓re Marteau had
promised him a p脙垄t脙漏 of pheasant instead of a p脙垄t脙漏 of fowl, and
Chambertin wine instead of Macon. All this, set off by the presence of
that excellent prince, who was so good-natured, who invented so droll
tricks against Monsieur de Chavigny and so fine jokes against Mazarin,
made for La Ramee the approaching Pentecost one of the four great
feasts of the year. He therefore looked forward to six o芒聙聶clock with as
much impatience as the duke himself.

Since daybreak La Ramee had been occupied with the preparations, and
trusting no one but himself, he had visited personally the successor of
P脙篓re Marteau. The latter had surpassed himself; he showed La Ramee a
monstrous p脙垄t脙漏, ornamented with Monsieur de Beaufort芒聙聶s coat-of-arms. It
was empty as yet, but a pheasant and two partridges were lying near it.
La Ramee芒聙聶s mouth watered and he returned to the duke芒聙聶s chamber rubbing
his hands. To crown his happiness, Monsieur de Chavigny had started on
a journey that morning and in his absence La Ramee was deputy-governor
of the ch脙垄teau.

As for Grimaud, he seemed more sullen than ever.

In the course of the forenoon Monsieur de Beaufort had a game of tennis
with La Ramee; a sign from Grimaud put him on the alert. Grimaud, going
in advance, followed the course which they were to take in the evening.
The game was played in an inclosure called the little court of the
ch脙垄teau, a place quite deserted except when Monsieur de Beaufort was
playing; and even then the precaution seemed superfluous, the wall was
so high.

There were three gates to open before reaching the inclosure, each by a
different key. When they arrived Grimaud went carelessly and sat down
by a loophole in the wall, letting his legs dangle outside. It was
evident that there the rope ladder was to be attached.

This man脜聯uvre, transparent to the Duc de Beaufort, was quite
unintelligible to La Ramee.

The game at tennis, which, upon a sign from Grimaud, Monsieur de
Beaufort had consented to play, began in the afternoon. The duke was in
full strength and beat La Ramee completely.

Four of the guards, who were constantly near the prisoner, assisted in
picking up the tennis balls. When the game was over, the duke, laughing
at La Ramee for his bad play, offered these men two louis d芒聙聶or to go
and drink his health, with their four other comrades.

The guards asked permission of La Ramee, who gave it to them, but not
till the evening, however; until then he had business and the prisoner
was not to be left alone.

Six o芒聙聶clock came and, although they were not to sit down to table until
seven o芒聙聶clock, dinner was ready and served up. Upon a sideboard
appeared the colossal pie with the duke芒聙聶s arms on it, and seemingly
cooked to a turn, as far as one could judge by the golden color which
illuminated the crust.

The rest of the dinner was to come.

Every one was impatient, La Ramee to sit down to table, the guards to
go and drink, the duke to escape.

Grimaud alone was calm as ever. One might have fancied that Athos had
educated him with the express forethought of such a great event.

There were moments when, looking at Grimaud, the duke asked himself if
he was not dreaming and if that marble figure was really at his service
and would grow animated when the moment came for action.

La Ramee sent away the guards, desiring them to drink to the duke芒聙聶s
health, and as soon as they were gone shut all the doors, put the keys

signified:

芒聙聹Whenever my lord pleases.芒聙聺

The prince looked at Grimaud, Grimaud looked at the clock; it was
hardly a quarter-past six. The escape was fixed to take place at seven
o芒聙聶clock; there was therefore three-quarters of an hour to wait.

The duke, in order to pass away another quarter of an hour, pretended
to be reading something that interested him and muttered that he wished

looked over his shoulder to see what sort of a book it was that had so

It was 芒聙聹C脙娄sar芒聙聶s Commentaries,芒聙聺 which La Ramee had lent him, contrary to
the orders of the governor; and La Ramee resolved never again to

Meantime he uncorked the bottles and went to smell if the pie was good.

At half-past six the duke arose and said very gravely:

芒聙聹Certainly, C脙娄sar was the greatest man of ancient times.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You think so, my lord?芒聙聺 answered La Ramee.

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, as for me, I prefer Hannibal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And why, pray, Master La Ramee?芒聙聺 asked the duke.

芒聙聹Because he left no Commentaries,芒聙聺 replied La Ramee, with his coarse

The duke vouchsafed no reply, but sitting down at the table made a sign

The duke smiled.

芒聙聹Zounds!芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t suppose there is a more contented man at

芒聙聹You are right, my lord duke,芒聙聺 answered the officer; 芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t know any

The duke, in his turn, bowed, and an imperceptible smile appeared on

芒聙聹My dear La Ramee,芒聙聺 said the duke, 芒聙聹you are the only man to turn such

芒聙聹No, my lord duke,芒聙聺 replied La Ramee, in the fullness of his heart; 芒聙聹I

芒聙聹To own the truth, I should be inconsolable if you were to leave

芒聙聹A droll way of showing your affliction.芒聙聺 The duke meant to say
芒聙聹affection.芒聙聺

Every folly you committed would embroil you with the court and they

芒聙聹Indeed!芒聙聺 exclaimed the duke, who from time to time looked at the

芒聙聹In short,芒聙聺 answered the duke, 芒聙聹if I comprehend you aright, La Ramee, I

芒聙聹Oh! my lord duke, 芒聙聶tis the height of ingratitude; but your highness

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 returned the duke, 芒聙聹I must confess I sometimes think of it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Still by one of your forty methods, your highness?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes, indeed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Willingly,芒聙聺 answered the duke, 芒聙聹give me the pie!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am listening,芒聙聺 said La Ramee, leaning back in his armchair and

The duke glanced at the clock. In ten minutes it would strike seven.

Grimaud placed the pie before the duke, who took a knife with a silver

芒聙聹Thank you, La Ramee,芒聙聺 said the prisoner.

芒聙聹Well, my lord! this famous invention of yours?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Must I tell you,芒聙聺 replied the duke, 芒聙聹on what I most reckon and what I
determine to try first?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, that芒聙聶s the thing, my lord!芒聙聺 cried his custodian, gaily.

芒聙聹Well, I should hope, in the first instance, to have for keeper an

芒聙聹And you have me, my lord. Well?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Having, then, a keeper like La Ramee, I should try also to have

芒聙聹Come, come,芒聙聺 said La Ramee, 芒聙聹that芒聙聶s not a bad idea.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Capital, isn芒聙聶t it? for instance, the former servingman of some brave

芒聙聹Hush! don芒聙聶t let us talk politics, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, yes! but how can the news be brought to you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing easier; in a game of tennis, for example.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In a game of tennis?芒聙聺 asked La Ramee, giving more serious attention to

芒聙聹Yes; see, I send a ball into the moat; a man is there who picks it up;

芒聙聹The devil it does! The devil it does!芒聙聺 said La Ramee, scratching his

The duke smiled.

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 resumed La Ramee, 芒聙聹that is only a way of corresponding.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And that is a great deal, it seems to me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But not enough.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pardon me; for instance, I say to my friends, Be on a certain day, on
a certain hour, at the other side of the moat with two horses.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, what then?芒聙聺 La Ramee began to be uneasy; 芒聙聹unless the horses have

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s not needed. I have,芒聙聺 replied the duke, 芒聙聹a way of descending
from the ramparts.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A rope ladder.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but,芒聙聺 answered La Ramee, trying to laugh, 芒聙聹a ladder of ropes
can芒聙聶t be sent around a ball, like a letter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but it may be sent in something else.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In something else芒聙聰in something else? In what?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In a p脙垄t脙漏, for example.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In a p脙垄t脙漏?芒聙聺 said La Ramee.

芒聙聹Yes. Let us suppose one thing,芒聙聺 replied the duke 芒聙聹let us suppose, for
instance, that my _ma脙庐tre d芒聙聶h脙麓tel_, Noirmont, has purchased the shop of
P脙篓re Marteau芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 said La Ramee, shuddering.

芒聙聹Well, La Ramee, who is a gourmand, sees his p脙垄t脙漏s, thinks them more
attractive than those of P脙篓re Marteau and proposes to me that I shall

o芒聙聶clock. Well, at a few minutes to seven芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹At a few minutes to seven?芒聙聺 cried La Ramee, cold sweat upon his brow.

芒聙聹At a few minutes to seven,芒聙聺 returned the duke (suiting the action to

The duke, in pronouncing these words, suited, as we have said, the

La Ramee followed all these objects with his eyes, his alarm every

芒聙聹Oh, my lord,芒聙聺 he cried, with an expression of stupefaction in his

芒聙聹No; not if thou dost not oppose my flight.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But, my lord, if I allow you to escape I am a ruined man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will compensate thee for the loss of thy place.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are determined to leave the ch脙垄teau?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By Heaven and earth! This night I am determined to be free.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And if I defend myself, or call, or cry out?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will kill thee, on the honor of a gentleman.芒聙聺

At this moment the clock struck.

芒聙聹Seven o芒聙聶clock!芒聙聺 said Grimaud, who had not spoken a word.

La Ramee made one movement, in order to satisfy his conscience. The

penetrated through his clothes, was close to his heart.

芒聙聹Let us dispatch,芒聙聺 said the duke.

芒聙聹My lord, one last favor.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What? speak, make haste.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bind my arms, my lord, fast.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why bind thee?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That I may not be considered as your accomplice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your hands?芒聙聺 asked Grimaud.

芒聙聹Not before me, behind me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But with what?芒聙聺 asked the duke.

芒聙聹With your belt, my lord!芒聙聺 replied La Ramee.

The duke undid his belt and gave it to Grimaud, who tied La Ramee in
such a way as to satisfy him.

芒聙聹Your feet, too,芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

La Ramee stretched out his legs, Grimaud took a table-cloth, tore it
into strips and tied La Ramee芒聙聶s feet together.

芒聙聹Now, my lord,芒聙聺 said the poor man, 芒聙聹let me have the _poire d芒聙聶angoisse_.
I ask for it; without it I should be tried in a court of justice

Grimaud prepared to comply with this request, when the officer made a

芒聙聹Speak,芒聙聺 said the duke.

芒聙聹Now, my lord, do not forget, if any harm happens to me on your

芒聙聹Rest assured; put the gag in, Grimaud.芒聙聺

In a second La Ramee was gagged and laid prostrate. Two or three chairs
were thrown down as if there had been a struggle. Grimaud then took
from the pocket of the officer all the keys it contained and first
opened the door of the room in which they were, then shut it and
double-locked it, and both he and the duke proceeded rapidly down the
gallery which led to the little inclosure. At last they reached the
tennis court. It was completely deserted. No sentinels, no one at any
of the windows. The duke ran to the rampart and perceived on the other
side of the ditch, three cavaliers with two riding horses. The duke
exchanged a signal with them. It was indeed for him that they were
there.

Grimaud, meantime, undid the means of escape.

This was not, however, a rope ladder, but a ball of silk cord, with a
narrow board which was to pass between the legs, the ball to unwind
itself by the weight of the person who sat astride upon the board.

芒聙聹Go!芒聙聺 said the duke.

芒聙聹First, my lord?芒聙聺 inquired Grimaud.

芒聙聹Certainly. If I am caught, I risk nothing but being taken back again
to prison. If they catch thee, thou wilt be hung.芒聙聺

芒聙聹True,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud.

And instantly, Grimaud, sitting upon the board as if on horseback,
commenced his perilous descent.

The duke followed him with his eyes, with involuntary terror. He had
gone down about three-quarters of the length of the wall when the cord
broke. Grimaud fell芒聙聰precipitated into the moat.

The duke uttered a cry, but Grimaud did not give a single moan. He must
have been dreadfully hurt, for he did not stir from the place where he
fell.

Immediately one of the men who were waiting slipped down into the moat,
tied under Grimaud芒聙聶s shoulders the end of a cord, and the remaining
two, who held the other end, drew Grimaud to them.

芒聙聹Descend, my lord,芒聙聺 said the man in the moat. 芒聙聹There are only fifteen
feet more from the top down here, and the grass is soft.芒聙聺

The duke had already begun to descend. His task was the more difficult,
as there was no board to support him. He was obliged to let himself
down by his hands and from a height of fifty feet. But as we have said
he was active, strong, and full of presence of mind. In less than five
minutes he arrived at the end of the cord. He was then only fifteen
feet from the ground, as the gentlemen below had told him. He let go
the rope and fell upon his feet, without receiving any injury.

He instantly began to climb up the slope of the moat, on the top of
which he met De Rochefort. The other two gentlemen were unknown to him.
Grimaud, in a swoon, was tied securely to a horse.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the duke, 芒聙聹I will thank you later; now we have not a
moment to lose. On, then! on! those who love me, follow me!芒聙聺

And he jumped on his horse and set off at full gallop, snuffing the
fresh air in his triumph and shouting out, with an expression of face
which it would be impossible to describe:

芒聙聹Free! free! free!芒聙聺




Chapter XXIV.
The timely Arrival of D芒聙聶Artagnan in Paris.


At Blois, D芒聙聶Artagnan received the money paid to him by Mazarin for any
future service he might render the cardinal.

From Blois to Paris was a journey of four days for ordinary travelers,
but D芒聙聶Artagnan arrived on the third day at the Barri脙篓re Saint Denis. In
turning the corner of the Rue Montmartre, in order to reach the Rue
Tiquetonne and the Hotel de la Chevrette, where he had appointed
Porthos to meet him, he saw at one of the windows of the hotel, that
friend himself dressed in a sky-blue waistcoat, embroidered with
silver, and gaping, till he showed every one of his white teeth; whilst
the people passing by admiringly gazed at this gentleman, so handsome
and so rich, who seemed to weary of his riches and his greatness.

D芒聙聶Artagnan and Planchet had hardly turned the corner when Porthos
recognized them.

芒聙聹Eh! D芒聙聶Artagnan!芒聙聺 he cried. 芒聙聹Thank God you have come!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eh! good-day, dear friend!芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan.

Porthos came down at once to the threshold of the hotel.

芒聙聹Ah, my dear friend!芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹what bad stabling for my horses here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Indeed!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I am most unhappy to hear it, on account of
those fine animals.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I, also芒聙聰I was also wretchedly off,芒聙聺 he answered, moving backward
and forward as he spoke; 芒聙聹and had it not been for the hostess,芒聙聺 he
added, with his air of vulgar self-complacency, 芒聙聹who is very agreeable
and understands a joke, I should have got a lodging elsewhere.芒聙聺

The pretty Madeleine, who had approached during this colloquy, stepped

thought the scene with the Swiss was about to be repeated. But to her

angry he laughed, and said to Porthos:

芒聙聹Yes, I understand, the air of La Rue Tiquetonne is not like that of
Pierrefonds; but console yourself, I will soon conduct you to one much
better.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When will you do that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Immediately, I hope.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! so much the better!芒聙聺

To that exclamation of Porthos芒聙聶s succeeded a groaning, low and
profound, which seemed to come from behind a door. D芒聙聶Artagnan, who had
just dismounted, then saw, outlined against the wall, the enormous
stomach of Mousqueton, whose down-drawn mouth emitted sounds of

芒聙聹And you, too, my poor Monsieur Mouston, are out of place in this poor

indicate either compassion or mockery.

芒聙聹He finds the cooking detestable,芒聙聺 replied Porthos.

芒聙聹Why, then, doesn芒聙聶t he attend to it himself, as at Chantilly?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, monsieur, I have not here, as I had there, the ponds of monsieur
le prince, where I could catch those beautiful carp, nor the forests of
his highness to provide me with partridges. As for the cellar, I have
searched every part and poor stuff I found.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur Mouston,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I should indeed condole with you

Then taking Porthos aside:

芒聙聹Yes, my friend.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A presentation? indeed!芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but it agitates me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! don芒聙聶t be distressed; you have to deal with a cardinal of another

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis the same thing芒聙聰you understand me, D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聰a court.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, you may as well take Mousqueton. As to Planchet, he has certain

芒聙聹Oh, he doesn芒聙聶t stand well with his eminence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, take a more stylish horse, Ph脜聯bus or Superbe; we are going with

Mouston breathed a sigh; he couldn芒聙聶t understand visits of ceremony made

芒聙聹Indeed,芒聙聺 said Porthos, looking complacently at his old lackey as he

D芒聙聶Artagnan smiled.

芒聙聹But you, my friend芒聙聰are you not going to change your dress?芒聙聺

They set out on Vulcan and Bayard, followed by Mousqueton on Ph脜聯bus,

Mousqueton also attracted attention; and as the romance of Don Quixote
was then the fashion, they said that he was Sancho, who, after having
lost one master, had found two.

On reaching the palace, D芒聙聶Artagnan sent to his eminence the letter in

芒聙聹Courage!芒聙聺 he whispered to Porthos, as they proceeded. 芒聙聹Do not be

Mazarin was in his study, working at a list of pensions and benefices,

芒聙聶Tis well. Welcome to ye.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The best swordsman in the kingdom, my lord,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

Mazarin was as fond of fine soldiers as, in later times, Frederick of

芒聙聹And your two other friends?芒聙聺 he asked.

芒聙聹And this gentleman, being disengaged, takes to the service willingly?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Rich!芒聙聺 said Mazarin, whom that single word always inspired with a

芒聙聹Fifty thousand francs a year,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

These were the first words he had spoken.

芒聙聹And your friend, for instance; what does he expect to have at the end

芒聙聹Well, my lord, my friend has three magnificent estates: that of

D芒聙聶Artagnan bowed.

芒聙聹Well, that is not impossible. We will see, gentlemen, we will see.

Porthos opened his mouth to reply.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹Monsieur de Vallon is like me, he prefers

That boastfulness was not displeasing to Mazarin; he fell into

In fact, a great noise was heard in the ante-chamber; at the same time

Mazarin, perceiving by the paleness and agitation of the messenger that

D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos withdrew to a corner of the cabinet.

Mazarin uttered a cry and became paler than the man who had brought the

芒聙聹He was out of range.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Chavigny芒聙聰where was he?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Absent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And La Ramee?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Was found locked up in the prisoner芒聙聶s room, a gag in his mouth and a

芒聙聹But the man who was under him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Was an accomplice of the duke芒聙聶s and escaped along with him.芒聙聺

Mazarin groaned.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, advancing toward the cardinal, 芒聙聹it seems to

芒聙聹And who is to pursue him?芒聙聺 cried Mazarin.

芒聙聹I, _pardieu!_芒聙聺

芒聙聹You would arrest the Duc de Beaufort, armed, in the field?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Battle!芒聙聺 cried Porthos.

芒聙聹To horse, Porthos!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, taking his friend by the hand.

芒聙聹Here I am,芒聙聺 smiled Porthos, with his sublime composure.

They descended the great staircase, taking with them all the guards

芒聙聹Well, baron, I promise you some good exercise!芒聙聺 said the Gascon.

芒聙聹Yes, my captain.芒聙聺

As they went, the citizens, awakened, left their doors and the street

course as though their steeds had wings.

Alas! there are no unimportant events in this world and we shall see
that this apparently slight incident came near endangering the
monarchy.




Chapter XXV.
An Adventure on the High Road.


The musketeers rode the whole length of the Faubourg Saint Antoine and
of the road to Vincennes, and soon found themselves out of the town,
then in a forest and then within sight of a village.

The horses seemed to become more lively with each successive step;
their nostrils reddened like glowing furnaces. D芒聙聶Artagnan, freely
applying his spurs, was in advance of Porthos two feet at the most;
Mousqueton followed two lengths behind; the guards were scattered
according to the varying excellence of their respective mounts.

From the top of an eminence D芒聙聶Artagnan perceived a group of people
collected on the other side of the moat, in front of that part of the
donjon which looks toward Saint Maur. He rode on, convinced that in
this direction he would gain intelligence of the fugitive. In five
minutes he had arrived at the place, where the guards joined him,
coming up one by one.

The several members of that group were much excited. They looked at the
cord, still hanging from the loophole and broken at about twenty feet
from the ground. Their eyes measured the height and they exchanged
conjectures. On the top of the wall sentinels went and came with a
frightened air.

A few soldiers, commanded by a sergeant, drove away idlers from the
place where the duke had mounted his horse. D芒聙聶Artagnan went straight to
the sergeant.

芒聙聹My officer,芒聙聺 said the sergeant, 芒聙聹it is not permitted to stop here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That prohibition is not for me,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Have the fugitives
been pursued?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my officer; unfortunately, they are well mounted.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How many are there?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Four, and a fifth whom they carried away wounded.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Four!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, looking at Porthos. 芒聙聹Do you hear, baron? They
are only four!芒聙聺

A joyous smile lighted Porthos芒聙聶s face.

芒聙聹How long a start have they?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two hours and a quarter, my officer.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two hours and a quarter芒聙聰that is nothing; we are well mounted, are we
not, Porthos?芒聙聺

Porthos breathed a sigh; he thought of what was in store for his poor
horses.

芒聙聹Very good,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹and now in what direction did they set
out?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That I am forbidden to tell.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan drew from his pocket a paper. 芒聙聹Order of the king,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Speak to the governor, then.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And where is the governor?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In the country.芒聙聺

Anger mounted to D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s face; he frowned and his cheeks were
colored.

芒聙聹Ah, you scoundrel!芒聙聺 he said to the sergeant, 芒聙聹I believe you are
impudent to me! Wait!芒聙聺

He unfolded the paper, presented it to the sergeant with one hand and
with the other took a pistol from his holsters and cocked it.

芒聙聹Order of the king, I tell you. Read and answer, or I will blow out
your brains!芒聙聺

The sergeant saw that D芒聙聶Artagnan was in earnest. 芒聙聹The Vendomois road,芒聙聺
he replied.

芒聙聹And by what gate did they go out?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By the Saint Maur gate.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If you are deceiving me, rascal, you will be hanged to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And if you catch up with them you won芒聙聶t come back to hang me,芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan shrugged his shoulders, made a sign to his escort and
started.

芒聙聹This way, gentlemen, this way!芒聙聺 he cried, directing his course toward
the gate that had been pointed out.

But, now that the duke had escaped, the concierge had seen fit to
fasten the gate with a double lock. It was necessary to compel him to
open it, as the sergeant had been compelled to speak, and this took
another ten minutes. This last obstacle having been overcome, the troop
pursued their course with their accustomed ardor; but some of the

an hour芒聙聶s gallop, and one fell down.

D芒聙聶Artagnan, who never turned his head, did not perceive it. Porthos

芒聙聹If only we two arrive,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹it will be enough, since the
duke芒聙聶s troop are only four in number.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is true,芒聙聺 said Porthos

And he spurred his courser on.

At the end of another two hours the horses had gone twelve leagues
without stopping; their legs began to tremble, and the foam they shed
whitened the doublets of their masters.

芒聙聹Let us rest here an instant to give these poor creatures breathing

芒聙聹Let us rather kill them! yes, kill them!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I see

In fact, the road was trodden by horses芒聙聶 feet, visible even in the

They set out; after a run of two leagues, Mousqueton芒聙聶s horse sank.

芒聙聹Gracious me!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹there芒聙聶s Ph脜聯bus ruined.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The cardinal will pay you a hundred pistoles.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶m above that.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us set out again, at full gallop.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, if we can.芒聙聺

But at last the lieutenant芒聙聶s horse refused to go on; he could not

芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺 exclaimed Porthos; 芒聙聹there芒聙聶s Vulcan foundered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Zounds!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹then we must stop! Give me your horse,

芒聙聹By Jove, I am falling, or rather, Bayard is falling,芒聙聺 answered

All three then cried: 芒聙聹All芒聙聶s over.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹What is it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hear a horse.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It belongs to one of our companions, who is overtaking us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹it is in advance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is another thing,芒聙聺 said Porthos; and he listened toward the

芒聙聹Monsieur,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, who, abandoning his horse on the high

hold out and芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Silence!芒聙聺 said Porthos.

In fact, at that moment a second neighing was borne to them on the
night wind.

芒聙聹It is five hundred feet from here, in advance,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹True, monsieur,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton; 芒聙聹and five hundred feet from here is
a small hunting-house.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Mousqueton, thy pistols,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹I have them at hand, monsieur.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Porthos, take yours from your holsters.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, seizing his own; 芒聙聹now you understand,
Porthos?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not too well.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are out on the king芒聙聶s service.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For the king芒聙聶s service we need horses.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is true,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

Chapter XXVI.
The Rencontre.


They rode on in this way for ten minutes. Suddenly two dark forms

D芒聙聶Artagnan recognized the voice of De Rochefort.

芒聙聹Bear witness that we have done all that we could.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos were now alone with a man who held by the

D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos went on, meantime, to Paris. They were passed by

Mazarin had passed a very bad night when this letter was brought to

Chapter XXVII.
The four old Friends prepare to meet again.


Well,芒聙聺 said Porthos, seated in the courtyard of the Hotel de la

Planchet began to sing:

芒聙聹Un vent de fronde

    S芒聙聶est lev脙漏 ce matin;

Je crois qu芒聙聶il gronde

    Contre le Mazarin.

Un vent de fronde

    S芒聙聶est lev脙漏 ce matin.芒聙聺

They then advanced two and two, and saluted each other politely.

芒聙聹Now where will it be agreeable to you that we hold our conference?芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan darted a look into the obscurity of the Place. Porthos

芒聙聹And Aramis wouldn芒聙聶t swear?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Attention, then!芒聙聺

Athos did not lose sight of the two speakers. Aramis opened the gate
and faced around in order that D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos might enter. In
passing through the gate, the hilt of the lieutenant芒聙聶s sword was caught
in the grating and he was obliged to pull off his cloak; in doing so he
showed the butt end of his pistols and a ray of the moon was reflected
on the shining metal.

芒聙聹Do you see?芒聙聺 whispered Aramis to Athos, touching his shoulder with one
hand and pointing with the other to the arms which the Gascon wore
under his belt.

芒聙聹Alas! I do!芒聙聺 replied Athos, with a deep sigh.

He entered third, and Aramis, who shut the gate after him, last. The
two serving-men waited without; but as if they likewise mistrusted each
other, they kept their respective distances.




Chapter XXVIII.
The Place Royale.


They proceeded silently to the centre of the Place, but as at this very
moment the moon had just emerged from behind a cloud, they thought they
might be observed if they remained on that spot and therefore regained
the shade of the lime-trees.

There were benches here and there; the four gentlemen stopped near
them; at a sign from Athos, Porthos and D芒聙聶Artagnan sat down, the two
others stood in front of them.

After a few minutes of silent embarrassment, Athos spoke.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹our presence here is the best proof of former
friendship; not one of us has failed the others at this rendezvous; not
one has, therefore, to reproach himself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hear me, count,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹instead of making compliments to
each other, let us explain our conduct to each other, like men of right
and honest hearts.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wish for nothing more; have you any cause of complaint against me or
Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay? If so, speak out,芒聙聺 answered Athos.

芒聙聹I have,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹When I saw you at your ch脙垄teau at
Bragelonne, I made certain proposals to you which you perfectly
understood; instead of answering me as a friend, you played with me as
a child; the friendship, therefore, that you boast of was not broken
yesterday by the shock of swords, but by your dissimulation at your

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan!芒聙聺 said Athos, reproachfully.

芒聙聹You asked for candor and you have it. You ask what I have against you;
I tell you. And I have the same sincerity to show you, if you wish,
Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay; I acted in a similar way to you and you also
deceived me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Really, monsieur, you say strange things,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹You came
seeking me to make to me certain proposals, but did you make them? No,
you sounded me, nothing more. Very well what did I say to you? that
Mazarin was contemptible and that I wouldn芒聙聶t serve Mazarin. But that is
all. Did I tell you that I wouldn芒聙聶t serve any other? On the contrary, I
gave you to understand, I think, that I adhered to the princes. We even

contingency of your being charged by the cardinal with my arrest. Were

too, belong to a party? You had your secret and we had ours; we didn芒聙聶t

芒聙聹I do not reproach you, monsieur,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹芒聙聶tis only because
Monsieur de la F脙篓re has spoken of friendship that I question your

芒聙聹And what do you find in it that is worthy of blame?芒聙聺 asked Aramis,
haughtily.

The blood mounted instantly to the temples of D芒聙聶Artagnan, who arose,
and replied:

芒聙聹I consider it worthy conduct of a pupil of Jesuits.芒聙聺

On seeing D芒聙聶Artagnan rise, Porthos rose also; these four men were
therefore all standing at the same time, with a menacing aspect,
opposite to each other.

Upon hearing D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s reply, Aramis seemed about to draw his sword,
when Athos prevented him.

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you are here to-night, still infuriated by
yesterday芒聙聶s adventure. I believed your heart noble enough to enable a
friendship of twenty years to overcome an affront of a quarter of an

Say it then; if I am in fault I will avow the error.芒聙聺

The grave and harmonious tones of that beloved voice seemed to have
still its ancient influence, whilst that of Aramis, which had become
harsh and tuneless in his moments of ill-humor, irritated him. He
answered therefore:

芒聙聹I think, monsieur le comte, that you had something to communicate to
me at your ch脙垄teau of Bragelonne, and that gentleman芒聙聺芒聙聰he pointed to
Aramis芒聙聰芒聙聹had also something to tell me when I was in his convent. At
that time I was not concerned in the adventure, in the course of which
you have so successfully estopped me! However, because I was prudent
you must not take me for a fool. If I had wished to widen the breach

ladder and those whom he receives with a wooden ladder, I could have
spoken out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What are you meddling with?芒聙聺 cried Aramis, pale with anger, suspecting
that D芒聙聶Artagnan had acted as a spy on him and had seen him with Madame
de Longueville.

芒聙聹I never meddle save with what concerns me, and I know how to make
believe that I haven芒聙聶t seen what does not concern me; but I hate
hypocrites, and among that number I place musketeers who are abb脙漏s and
abb脙漏s who are musketeers; and,芒聙聺 he added, turning to Porthos 芒聙聹here芒聙聶s a
gentleman who芒聙聶s of the same opinion as myself.芒聙聺

Porthos, who had not spoken one word, answered merely by a word and a
gesture.

He said 芒聙聹yes芒聙聺 and he put his hand on his sword.

Aramis started back and drew his. D芒聙聶Artagnan bent forward, ready either
to attack or to stand on his defense.

Athos at that moment extended his hand with the air of supreme command
which characterized him alone, drew out his sword and the scabbard at
the same time, broke the blade in the sheath on his knee and threw the
pieces to his right. Then turning to Aramis:

芒聙聹Aramis,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹break your sword.芒聙聺

Aramis hesitated.

芒聙聹It must be done,芒聙聺 said Athos; then in a lower and more gentle voice,
he added. 芒聙聹I wish it.芒聙聺

Then Aramis, paler than before, but subdued by these words, snapped the
serpent blade between his hands, and then folding his arms, stood

These proceedings made D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos draw back. D芒聙聶Artagnan did
not draw his sword; Porthos put his back into the sheath.

芒聙聹Never!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, raising his right hand to Heaven, 芒聙聹never! I
swear before God, who seeth us, and who, in the darkness of this night
heareth us, never shall my sword cross yours, never my eye express a
glance of anger, nor my heart a throb of hatred, at you. We lived
together, we loved, we hated together; we shed, we mingled our blood
together, and too probably, I may still add, that there may be yet a
bond between us closer even than that of friendship; perhaps there may
be the bond of crime; for we four, we once did condemn, judge and slay
a human being whom we had not any right to cut off from this world,
although apparently fitter for hell than for this life. D芒聙聶Artagnan, I
have always loved you as my son; Porthos, we slept six years side by

you, as I love you now and as I have ever loved you. What can Cardinal
Mazarin be to us, to four men who compelled such a man as Richelieu to
act as we pleased? What is such or such a prince to us, who fixed the
diadem upon a great queen芒聙聶s head? D芒聙聶Artagnan, I ask your pardon for
having yesterday crossed swords with you; Aramis does the same to
