even if you do hate me, retain esteem and friendship for you. I repeat
my words, Aramis, and then, if you desire it, and if they desire it,
let us separate forever from our old friends.芒聙聺

There was a solemn, though momentary silence, which was broken by
Aramis.

芒聙聹I swear,芒聙聺 he said, with a calm brow and kindly glance, but in a voice
still trembling with recent emotion, 芒聙聹I swear that I no longer bear
animosity to those who were once my friends. I regret that I ever
crossed swords with you, Porthos; I swear not only that it shall never
again be pointed at your breast, but that in the bottom of my heart
there will never in future be the slightest hostile sentiment; now,
Athos, come.芒聙聺

Athos was about to retire.

芒聙聹Oh! no! no! do not go away!芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan, impelled by one of
those irresistible impulses which showed the nobility of his nature,
the native brightness of his character; 芒聙聹I swear that I would give the
last drop of my blood and the last fragment of my limbs to preserve the
friendship of such a friend as you, Athos芒聙聰of such a man as you,
Aramis.芒聙聺 And he threw himself into the arms of Athos.

芒聙聹My son!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, pressing him in his arms.

芒聙聹And as for me,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I swear nothing, but I芒聙聶m choked.
Forsooth! If I were obliged to fight against you, I think I should
allow myself to be pierced through and through, for I never loved any
one but you in the wide world;芒聙聺 and honest Porthos burst into tears as
he embraced Athos.

芒聙聹My friends,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹this is what I expected from such hearts as
yours. Yes, I have said it and I now repeat it: our destinies are
irrevocably united, although we now pursue divergent roads. I respect
your convictions, and whilst we fight for opposite sides, let us remain
friends. Ministers, princes, kings, will pass away like mountain
torrents; civil war, like a forest flame; but we芒聙聰we shall remain; I
have a presentiment that we shall.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹let us still be musketeers, and let us
retain as our battle-standard that famous napkin of the bastion St.
Gervais, on which the great cardinal had three fleurs-de-lis
embroidered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Be it so,芒聙聺 cried Aramis. 芒聙聹Cardinalists or Frondeurs, what matters it?
Let us meet again as capital seconds in a duel, devoted friends in
business, merry companions in our ancient pleasures.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And whenever,芒聙聺 added Athos, 芒聙聹we meet in battle, at this word, 芒聙聵Place
Royale!芒聙聶 let us put our swords into our left hands and shake hands with
the right, even in the very lust and music of the hottest carnage.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You speak charmingly,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹And are the first of men!芒聙聺 added D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹You excel us all.芒聙聺

Athos smiled with ineffable pleasure.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis then all settled. Gentlemen, your hands; are we not pretty good
Christians?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Egad!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹by Heaven! yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We should be so on this occasion, if only to be faithful to our oath,芒聙聺
said Aramis.

芒聙聹Ah, I芒聙聶m ready to do what you will,芒聙聺 cried Porthos; 芒聙聹even to swear by
Mahomet. Devil take me if I芒聙聶ve ever been so happy as at this moment.芒聙聺

And he wiped his eyes, still moist.

芒聙聹Has not one of you a cross?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

Aramis smiled and drew from his vest a cross of diamonds, which was
hung around his neck by a chain of pearls. 芒聙聹Here is one,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 resumed Athos, 芒聙聹swear on this cross, which, in spite of its
magnificent material, is still a cross; swear to be united in spite of
everything, and forever, and may this oath bind us to each other, and
even, also, our descendants! Does this oath satisfy you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said they all, with one accord.

芒聙聹Ah, traitor!芒聙聺 muttered D芒聙聶Artagnan, leaning toward Aramis and
whispering in his ear, 芒聙聹you have made us swear on the crucifix of a
Frondeuse.芒聙聺




Chapter XXIX.
The Ferry across the Oise.


We hope that the reader has not quite forgotten the young traveler whom
we left on the road to Flanders.

In losing sight of his guardian, whom he had quitted, gazing after him
in front of the royal basilican, Raoul spurred on his horse, in order
not only to escape from his own melancholy reflections, but also to
hide from Olivain the emotion his face might betray.

One hour芒聙聶s rapid progress, however, sufficed to disperse the gloomy
fancies that had clouded the young man芒聙聶s bright anticipations; and the
hitherto unfelt pleasure of freedom芒聙聰a pleasure which is sweet even to
those who have never known dependence芒聙聰seemed to Raoul to gild not only
Heaven and earth, but especially that blue but dim horizon of life we
call the future.

Nevertheless, after several attempts at conversation with Olivain he
foresaw that many days passed thus would prove exceedingly dull; and
the count芒聙聶s agreeable voice, his gentle and persuasive eloquence,
recurred to his mind at the various towns through which they journeyed
and about which he had no longer any one to give him those interesting
details which he would have drawn from Athos, the most amusing and the
best informed of guides. Another recollection contributed also to
sadden Raoul: on their arrival at Sonores he had perceived, hidden
behind a screen of poplars, a little ch脙垄teau which so vividly recalled
that of La Valli脙篓re to his mind that he halted for nearly ten minutes
to gaze at it, and resumed his journey with a sigh too abstracted even
to reply to Olivain芒聙聶s respectful inquiry about the cause of so much
fixed attention. The aspect of external objects is often a mysterious
guide communicating with the fibres of memory, which in spite of us
will arouse them at times; this thread, like that of Ariadne, when once
unraveled will conduct one through a labyrinth of thought, in which one
loses one芒聙聶s self in endeavoring to follow that phantom of the past
which is called recollection.

Now the sight of this ch脙垄teau had taken Raoul back fifty leagues
westward and had caused him to review his life from the moment when he
had taken leave of little Louise to that in which he had seen her for
the first time; and every branch of oak, every gilded weathercock on
roof of slates, reminded him that, instead of returning to the friends
of his childhood, every instant estranged him further and that perhaps
he had even left them forever.

With a full heart and burning head he desired Olivain to lead on the
horses to a wayside inn, which he observed within gunshot range, a
little in advance of the place they had reached.

As for himself, he dismounted and remained under a beautiful group of
chestnuts in flower, amidst which were murmuring a multitude of happy
bees, and bade Olivain send the host to him with writing paper and ink,
to be placed on a table which he found there, conveniently ready.
Olivain obeyed and continued on his way, whilst Raoul remained sitting,
with his elbow leaning on the table, from time to time gently shaking
the flowers from his head, which fell upon him like snow, and gazing
vaguely on the charming landscape spread out before him, dotted over
with green fields and groups of trees. Raoul had been there about ten
minutes, during five of which he was lost in reverie, when there
appeared within the circle comprised in his rolling gaze a man with a
rubicund face, who, with a napkin around his body, another under his
arm, and a white cap upon his head, approached him, holding paper, pen
and ink in hand.

芒聙聹Ha! ha!芒聙聺 laughed the apparition, 芒聙聹every gentleman seems to have the
same fancy, for not a quarter of an hour ago a young lad, well mounted
like you, as tall as you and of about your age, halted before this
clump of trees and had this table and this chair brought here, and
dined here, with an old gentleman who seemed to be his tutor, upon a
pie, of which they haven芒聙聶t left a mouthful, and two bottles of Macon
wine, of which they haven芒聙聶t left a drop, but fortunately we have still
some of the same wine and some of the same pies left, and if your
worship will but give your orders芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, friend,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, smiling, 芒聙聹I am obliged to you, but at this
moment I want nothing but the things for which I have asked芒聙聰only I
shall be very glad if the ink prove black and the pen good; upon these
conditions I will pay for the pen the price of the bottle, and for the
ink the price of the pie.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well, sir,芒聙聺 said the host, 芒聙聹I芒聙聶ll give the pie and the bottle of
wine to your servant, and in this way you will have the pen and ink
into the bargain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do as you like,芒聙聺 said Raoul, who was beginning his apprenticeship with
that particular class of society, who, when there were robbers on the
highroads, were connected with them, and who, since highwaymen no
longer exist, have advantageously and aptly filled their vacant place.

The host, his mind at ease about his bill, placed pen, ink and paper
upon the table. By a lucky chance the pen was tolerably good and Raoul
began to write. The host remained standing in front of him, looking
with a kind of involuntary admiration at his handsome face, combining
both gravity and sweetness of expression. Beauty has always been and
always will be all-powerful.

芒聙聹He芒聙聶s not a guest like the other one here just now,芒聙聺 observed mine host
to Olivain, who had rejoined his master to see if he wanted anything,
芒聙聹and your young master has no appetite.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My master had appetite enough three days ago, but what can one do? he
lost it the day before yesterday.芒聙聺

And Olivain and the host took their way together toward the inn,
Olivain, according to the custom of serving-men well pleased with their
place, relating to the tavern-keeper all that he could say in favor of
the young gentleman; whilst Raoul wrote on thus:

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聰After a four hours芒聙聶 march I stop to write to you, for I miss you
every moment, and I am always on the point of turning my head as if to
reply when you speak to me. I was so bewildered by your departure and
so overcome with grief at our separation, that I am sure I was able to
but very feebly express all the affection and gratitude I feel toward
you. You will forgive me, sir, for your heart is of such a generous
nature that you can well understand all that has passed in mine. I
entreat you to write to me, for you form a part of my existence, and,
if I may venture to tell you so, I also feel anxious. It seemed to me
as if you were yourself preparing for some dangerous undertaking, about
which I did not dare to question you, since you told me nothing. I
have, therefore, as you see, great need of hearing from you. Now that
you are no longer beside me I am afraid every moment of erring. You
sustained me powerfully, sir, and I protest to you that to-day I feel
very lonely. Will you have the goodness, sir, should you receive news
from Blois, to send me a few lines about my little friend Mademoiselle
de la Valli脙篓re, about whose health, when we left, so much anxiety was
felt? You can understand, honored and dear guardian, how precious and
indispensable to me is the remembrance of the years that I have passed
with you. I hope that you will sometimes, too, think of me, and if at
certain hours you should miss me, if you should feel any slight regret
at my absence, I shall be overwhelmed with joy at the thought that you
appreciate my affection for and my devotion to yourself, and that I
have been able to prove them to you whilst I had the happiness of
living with you.芒聙聺

After finishing this letter Raoul felt more composed; he looked well
around him to see if Olivain and the host might not be watching him,
whilst he impressed a kiss upon the paper, a mute and touching caress,
which the heart of Athos might well divine on opening the letter.

During this time Olivain had finished his bottle and eaten his pie; the
horses were also refreshed. Raoul motioned to the host to approach,
threw a crown upon the table, mounted his horse, and posted his letter
at Senlis. The rest that had been thus afforded to men and horses
enabled them to continue their journey at a good round pace. At
Verberie, Raoul desired Olivain to make some inquiry about the young
man who was preceding them; he had been observed to pass only
three-quarters of an hour previously, but he was well mounted, as the
tavern-keeper had already said, and rode at a rapid pace.

芒聙聹Let us try and overtake this gentleman,芒聙聺 said Raoul to Olivain; 芒聙聹like
ourselves he is on his way to join the army and may prove agreeable
company.芒聙聺

It was about four o芒聙聶clock in the afternoon when Raoul arrived at
Compi脙篓gne; there he dined heartily and again inquired about the young
gentleman who was in advance of them. He had stopped, like Raoul, at
the Hotel of the Bell and Bottle, the best at Compi脙篓gne; and had
started again on his journey, saying that he should sleep at Noyon.

芒聙聹Well, let us sleep at Noyon,芒聙聺 said Raoul.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Olivain, respectfully, 芒聙聹allow me to remark that we have
already much fatigued the horses this morning. I think it would be well
to sleep here and to start again very early to-morrow. Eighteen leagues
is enough for the first stage.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re wished me to hasten on,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, 芒聙聹that I
might rejoin the prince on the morning of the fourth day; let us push
on, then, to Noyon; it will be a stage similar to those we traveled
from Blois to Paris. We shall arrive at eight o芒聙聶clock. The horses will
have a long night芒聙聶s rest, and at five o芒聙聶clock to-morrow morning we can
be again on the road.芒聙聺

Olivain dared offer no opposition to this determination but he followed
his master, grumbling.

芒聙聹Go on, go on,芒聙聺 said he, between his teeth, 芒聙聹expend your ardor the
first day; to-morrow, instead of journeying twenty leagues, you will
travel ten, the day after to-morrow, five, and in three days you will
be in bed. There you must rest; young people are such braggarts.芒聙聺

It was easy to see that Olivain had not been taught in the school of
the Planchets and the Grimauds. Raoul really felt tired, but he was
desirous of testing his strength, and, brought up in the principles of
Athos and certain of having heard him speak a thousand times of stages
of twenty-five leagues, he did not wish to fall far short of his model.
D芒聙聶Artagnan, that man of iron, who seemed to be made of nerve and muscle
only, had struck him with admiration. Therefore, in spite of Olivain芒聙聶s
remarks, he continued to urge his steed more and more, and following a
pleasant little path, leading to a ferry, and which he had been assured
shortened the journey by the distance of one league, he arrived at the
summit of a hill and perceived the river flowing before him. A little
troop of men on horseback were waiting on the edge of the stream, ready
to embark. Raoul did not doubt this was the gentleman and his escort;
he called out to him, but they were too distant to be heard; then, in
spite of the weariness of his beast, he made it gallop but the rising
ground soon deprived him of all sight of the travelers, and when he had
again attained a new height, the ferryboat had left the shore and was
making for the opposite bank. Raoul, seeing that he could not arrive in
time to cross the ferry with the travelers, halted to wait for Olivain.
At this moment a shriek was heard that seemed to come from the river.
Raoul turned toward the side whence the cry had sounded, and shaded his
eyes from the glare of the setting sun with his hand.

芒聙聹Olivain!芒聙聺 he exclaimed, 芒聙聹what do I see below there?芒聙聺

A second scream, more piercing than the first, now sounded.

芒聙聹Oh, sir!芒聙聺 cried Olivain, 芒聙聹the rope which holds the ferryboat has
broken and the boat is drifting. But what do I see in the
water芒聙聰something struggling?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, yes,芒聙聺 exclaimed Raoul, fixing his glance on one point in the
stream, splendidly illumined by the setting sun, 芒聙聹a horse, a rider!芒聙聺

芒聙聹They are sinking!芒聙聺 cried Olivain in his turn.

It was true, and Raoul was convinced that some accident had happened
and that a man was drowning; he gave his horse its head, struck his
spurs into its sides, and the animal, urged by pain and feeling that he
had space open before him, bounded over a kind of paling which inclosed
the landing place, and fell into the river, scattering to a distance
waves of white froth.

芒聙聹Ah, sir!芒聙聺 cried Olivain, 芒聙聹what are you doing? Good God!芒聙聺

Raoul was directing his horse toward the unhappy man in danger. This
was, in fact, a custom familiar to him. Having been brought up on the
banks of the Loire, he might have been said to have been cradled on its
waves; a hundred times he had crossed it on horseback, a thousand times
had swum across. Athos, foreseeing the period when he should make a
soldier of the viscount, had inured him to all kinds of arduous
undertakings.

芒聙聹Oh, heavens!芒聙聺 continued Olivain, in despair, 芒聙聹what would the count say
if he only saw you now!芒聙聺

芒聙聹The count would do as I do,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, urging his horse
vigorously forward.

芒聙聹But I芒聙聰but I,芒聙聺 cried Olivain, pale and disconsolate rushing about on
the shore, 芒聙聹how shall I cross?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Leap, coward!芒聙聺 cried Raoul, swimming on; then addressing the traveler,
who was struggling twenty yards in front of him: 芒聙聹Courage, sir!芒聙聺 said
he, 芒聙聹courage! we are coming to your aid.芒聙聺

Olivain advanced, retired, then made his horse rear芒聙聰turned it and then,
struck to the core by shame, leaped, as Raoul had done, only repeating:

芒聙聹I am a dead man! we are lost!芒聙聺

In the meantime, the ferryboat had floated away, carried down by the
stream, and the shrieks of those whom it contained resounded more and
more. A man with gray hair had thrown himself from the boat into the
river and was swimming vigorously toward the person who was drowning;
but being obliged to go against the current he advanced but slowly.
Raoul continued his way and was visibly gaining ground; but the horse
and its rider, of whom he did not lose sight, were evidently sinking.
The nostrils of the horse were no longer above water, and the rider,
who had lost the reins in struggling, fell with his head back and his
arms extended. One moment longer and all would disappear.

芒聙聹Courage!芒聙聺 cried Raoul, 芒聙聹courage!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Too late!芒聙聺 murmured the young man, 芒聙聹too late!芒聙聺

The water closed above his head and stifled his voice.

Raoul sprang from his horse, to which he left the charge of its own
preservation, and in three or four strokes was at the gentleman芒聙聶s side;
he seized the horse at once by the curb and raised its head above
water; the animal began to breathe again and, as if he comprehended
that they had come to his aid, redoubled his efforts. Raoul at the same
time seized one of the young man芒聙聶s hands and placed it on the mane,
which it grasped with the tenacity of a drowning man. Thus, sure that
the rider would not release his hold, Raoul now only directed his
attention to the horse, which he guided to the opposite bank, helping
it to cut through the water and encouraging it with words.

All at once the horse stumbled against a ridge and then placed its foot
on the sand.

芒聙聹Saved!芒聙聺 exclaimed the man with gray hair, who also touched bottom.

芒聙聹Saved!芒聙聺 mechanically repeated the young gentleman, releasing the mane
and sliding from the saddle into Raoul芒聙聶s arms; Raoul was but ten yards
from the shore; there he bore the fainting man, and laying him down
upon the grass, unfastened the buttons of his collar and unhooked his
doublet. A moment later the gray-headed man was beside him. Olivain
managed in his turn to land, after crossing himself repeatedly; and the
people in the ferryboat guided themselves as well as they were able
toward the bank, with the aid of a pole which chanced to be in the
boat.

Thanks to the attentions of Raoul and the man who accompanied the young
gentleman, the color gradually returned to the pale cheeks of the dying
man, who opened his eyes, at first entirely bewildered, but who soon
fixed his gaze upon the person who had saved him.

芒聙聹Ah, sir,芒聙聺 he exclaimed, 芒聙聹it was you! Without you I was a dead
man芒聙聰thrice dead.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But one recovers, sir, as you perceive,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, 芒聙聹and we have
but had a little bath.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! sir, what gratitude I feel!芒聙聺 exclaimed the man with gray hair.

芒聙聹Ah, there you are, my good D芒聙聶Arminges; I have given you a great
fright, have I not? but it is your own fault. You were my tutor, why
did you not teach me to swim?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, monsieur le comte,芒聙聺 replied the old man, 芒聙聹had any misfortune
happened to you, I should never have dared to show myself to the
marshal again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But how did the accident happen?芒聙聺 asked Raoul.

芒聙聹Oh, sir, in the most natural way possible,芒聙聺 replied he to whom they
had given the title of count. 芒聙聹We were about a third of the way across
the river when the cord of the ferryboat broke. Alarmed by the cries
and gestures of the boatmen, my horse sprang into the water. I cannot
swim, and dared not throw myself into the river. Instead of aiding the
movements of my horse, I paralyzed them; and I was just going to drown
myself with the best grace in the world, when you arrived just in time
to pull me out of the water; therefore, sir, if you will agree,
henceforward we are friends until death.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, bowing, 芒聙聹I am entirely at your service, I assure
you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am called the Count de Guiche,芒聙聺 continued the young man; 芒聙聹my father
is the Mar脙漏chal de Grammont; and now that you know who I am, do me the
honor to inform me who you are.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am the Viscount de Bragelonne,芒聙聺 answered Raoul, blushing at being
unable to name his father, as the Count de Guiche had done.

芒聙聹Viscount, your countenance, your goodness and your courage incline me
toward you; my gratitude is already due. Shake hands芒聙聰I crave your
friendship.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, returning the count芒聙聶s pressure of the hand, 芒聙聹I like
you already, from my heart; pray regard me as a devoted friend, I
beseech you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now, where are you going, viscount?芒聙聺 inquired De Guiche.

芒聙聹To join the army, under the prince, count.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I, too!芒聙聺 exclaimed the young man, in a transport of joy. 芒聙聹Oh, so
much the better, we will fire the first shot together.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is well; be friends,芒聙聺 said the tutor; 芒聙聹young as you both are, you
were perhaps born under the same star and were destined to meet. And
now,芒聙聺 continued he, 芒聙聹you must change your clothes; your servants, to
whom I gave directions the moment they had left the ferryboat, ought to
be already at the inn. Linen and wine are both being warmed; come.芒聙聺

The young men had no objection to this proposition; on the contrary,
they thought it very timely.

They mounted again at once, whilst looks of admiration passed between
them. They were indeed two elegant horsemen, with figures slight and
upright, noble faces, bright and proud looks, loyal and intelligent
smiles.

De Guiche might have been about eighteen years of age, but he was
scarcely taller than Raoul, who was only fifteen.




Chapter XXX.
Skirmishing.


The halt at Noyon was but brief, every one there being wrapped in
profound sleep. Raoul had desired to be awakened should Grimaud arrive,
but Grimaud did not arrive. Doubtless, too, the horses on their part
appreciated the eight hours of repose and the abundant stabling which
was granted them. The Count de Guiche was awakened at five o芒聙聶clock in
the morning by Raoul, who came to wish him good-day. They breakfasted
in haste, and at six o芒聙聶clock had already gone ten miles.

The young count芒聙聶s conversation was most interesting to Raoul, therefore
he listened much, whilst the count talked well and long. Brought up in
Paris, where Raoul had been but once; at the court, which Raoul had
never seen; his follies as page; two duels, which he had already found
the means of fighting, in spite of the edicts against them and, more
especially, in spite of his tutor芒聙聶s vigilance芒聙聰these things excited the
greatest curiosity in Raoul. Raoul had only been at M. Scarron芒聙聶s house;
he named to Guiche the people whom he had seen there. Guiche knew
everybody芒聙聰Madame de Neuillan, Mademoiselle d芒聙聶Aubign脙漏, Mademoiselle de
Scud脙漏ry, Mademoiselle Paulet, Madame de Chevreuse. He criticised
everybody humorously. Raoul trembled, lest he should laugh among the
rest at Madame de Chevreuse, for whom he entertained deep and genuine
sympathy, but either instinctively, or from affection for the duchess,
he said everything in her favor. His praises increased Raoul芒聙聶s
friendship twofold. Then came the question of gallantry and love
affairs. Under this head, also, Bragelonne had much more to hear than
to tell. He listened attentively and fancied that he discovered through
three or four rather frivolous adventures, that the count, like
himself, had a secret to hide in the depths of his heart.

De Guiche, as we have said before, had been educated at the court, and
the intrigues of this court were not unknown to him. It was the same
court of which Raoul had so often heard the Comte de la F脙篓re speak,
except that its aspect had much changed since the period when Athos had
himself been part of it; therefore everything which the Count de Guiche
related was new to his traveling companion. The young count, witty and
caustic, passed all the world in review; the queen herself was not
spared, and Cardinal Mazarin came in for his share of ridicule.

The day passed away as rapidly as an hour. The count芒聙聶s tutor, a man of
the world and a _bon vivant_, up to his eyes in learning, as his pupil
described him, often recalled the profound erudition, the witty and
caustic satire of Athos to Raoul; but as regarded grace, delicacy, and
nobility of external appearance, no one in these points was to be
compared to the Comte de la F脙篓re.

The horses, which were more kindly used than on the previous day,
stopped at Arras at four o芒聙聶clock in the evening. They were approaching
the scene of war; and as bands of Spaniards sometimes took advantage of
the night to make expeditions even as far as the neighborhood of Arras,
they determined to remain in the town until the morrow. The French army
held all between Pont-脙聽-Marc as far as Valenciennes, falling back upon
Douai. The prince was said to be in person at Bethune.

The enemy芒聙聶s army extended from Cassel to Courtray; and as there was no
species of violence or pillage it did not commit, the poor people on
the frontier quitted their isolated dwellings and fled for refuge into
the strong cities which held out a shelter to them. Arras was
encumbered with fugitives. An approaching battle was much spoken of,
the prince having man脜聯uvred, until that movement, only in order to
await a reinforcement that had just reached him.

The young men congratulated themselves on having arrived so
opportunely. The evening was employed in discussing the war; the grooms
polished their arms; the young men loaded the pistols in case of a
skirmish, and they awoke in despair, having both dreamed that they had
arrived too late to participate in the battle. In the morning it was
rumored that Prince de Cond脙漏 had evacuated Bethune and fallen back on
Carvin, leaving, however, a strong garrison in the former city.

But as there was nothing positively certain in this report, the young
warriors decided to continue their way toward Bethune, free on the road
to diverge to the right and march to Carvin if necessary.

The count芒聙聶s tutor was well acquainted with the country; he consequently
proposed to take a crossroad, which lay between that of Lens and that
of Bethune. They obtained information at Ablain, and a statement of
their route was left for Grimaud. About seven o芒聙聶clock in the morning
they set out. De Guiche, who was young and impulsive, said to Raoul,
芒聙聹Here we are, three masters and three servants. Our valets are well
armed and yours seems to be tough enough.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have never seen him put to the test,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, 芒聙聹but he is a
Breton, which promises something.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 resumed De Guiche; 芒聙聹I am sure he can fire a musket when
required. On my side I have two sure men, who have been in action with
my father. We therefore represent six fighting men; if we should meet a
little troop of enemies, equal or even superior in number to our own,
shall we charge them, Raoul?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly, sir,芒聙聺 replied the viscount.

芒聙聹Holloa! young people芒聙聰stop there!芒聙聺 said the tutor, joining in the
conversation. 芒聙聹Zounds! how you man脜聯uvre my instructions, count! You
seem to forget the orders I received to conduct you safe and sound to
his highness the prince! Once with the army you may be killed at your
good pleasure; but until that time, I warn you that in my capacity of
general of the army I shall order a retreat and turn my back on the
first red coat we come across.芒聙聺 De Guiche and Raoul glanced at each
other, smiling.

They arrived at Ablain without accident. There they inquired and
learned that the prince had in reality quitted Bethune and stationed
himself between Cambria and La Venthie. Therefore, leaving directions
at every place for Grimaud, they took a crossroad which conducted the
little troop by the bank of a small stream flowing into the Lys. The
country was beautiful, intersected by valleys as green as the emerald.
Here and there they passed little copses crossing the path which they
were following. In anticipation of some ambuscade in each of these
little woods the tutor placed his two servants at the head of the band,
thus forming the advance guard. Himself and the two young men
represented the body of the army, whilst Olivain, with his rifle upon
his knee and his eyes upon the watch, protected the rear.

They had observed for some time before them, on the horizon, a rather
thick wood; and when they had arrived at a distance of a hundred steps
from it, Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges took his usual precautions and sent on in
advance the count芒聙聶s two grooms. The servants had just disappeared under
the trees, followed by the tutor, and the young men were laughing and
talking about a hundred yards off. Olivain was at the same distance in
the rear, when suddenly there resounded five or six musket-shots. The
tutor cried halt; the young men obeyed, pulling up their steeds, and at
the same moment the two valets were seen returning at a gallop.

The young men, impatient to learn the cause of the firing, spurred on
toward the servants. The tutor followed them.

芒聙聹Were you stopped?芒聙聺 eagerly inquired the two youths.

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied the servants, 芒聙聹it is even probable that we have not been
seen; the shots were fired about a hundred paces in advance of us, in
the thickest part of the wood, and we returned to ask your advice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My advice is this,芒聙聺 said Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges, 芒聙聹and if needs be, my
will, that we beat a retreat. There may be an ambuscade concealed in
this wood.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Did you see nothing there?芒聙聺 asked the count.

芒聙聹I thought I saw,芒聙聺 said one of the servants, 芒聙聹horsemen dressed in
yellow, creeping along the bed of the stream.

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s it,芒聙聺 said the tutor. 芒聙聹We have fallen in with a party of
Spaniards. Come back, sirs, back.芒聙聺

The two youths looked at each other, and at this moment a pistol-shot
and cries for help were heard. Another glance between the young men
convinced them both that neither had any wish to go back, and as the
tutor had already turned his horse芒聙聶s head, they both spurred forward,
Raoul crying: 芒聙聹Follow me, Olivain!芒聙聺 and the Count de Guiche: 芒聙聹Follow,
Urban and Planchet!芒聙聺 And before the tutor could recover from his
surprise they had both disappeared into the forest. Whilst they spurred
their steeds they held their pistols ready also. In five minutes they
arrived at the spot whence the noise had proceeded, and then
restraining their horses, they advanced cautiously.

芒聙聹Hush,芒聙聺 whispered De Guiche, 芒聙聹these are cavaliers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, three on horseback and three who have dismounted.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Can you see what they are doing?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, they appear to be searching a wounded or dead man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is some cowardly assassination,芒聙聺 said De Guiche.

芒聙聹They are soldiers, though,芒聙聺 resumed De Bragelonne.

芒聙聹Yes, skirmishers; that is to say, highway robbers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹At them!芒聙聺 cried Raoul. 芒聙聹At them!芒聙聺 echoed De Guiche.

芒聙聹Oh! gentlemen! gentlemen! in the name of Heaven!芒聙聺 cried the poor
tutor.

But he was not listened to, and his cries only served to arouse the
attention of the Spaniards.

The men on horseback at once rushed at the two youths, leaving the
three others to complete the plunder of the dead or wounded travelers;
for on approaching nearer, instead of one extended figure, the young
men discovered two. De Guiche fired the first shot at ten paces and
missed his man; and the Spaniard, who had advanced to meet Raoul, aimed
in his turn, and Raoul felt a pain in the left arm, similar to that of
a blow from a whip. He let off his fire at but four paces. Struck in
the breast and extending his arms, the Spaniard fell back on the
crupper, and the terrified horse, turning around, carried him off.

Raoul at this moment perceived the muzzle of a gun pointed at him, and
remembering the recommendation of Athos, he, with the rapidity of
lightning, made his horse rear as the shot was fired. His horse bounded
to one side, losing its footing, and fell, entangling Raoul芒聙聶s leg under
its body. The Spaniard sprang forward and seized the gun by its muzzle,
in order to strike Raoul on the head with the butt. In the position in
which Raoul lay, unfortunately, he could neither draw his sword from
the scabbard, nor his pistols from their holsters. The butt end of the
musket hovered over his head, and he could scarcely restrain himself
from closing his eyes, when with one bound Guiche reached the Spaniard
and placed a pistol at his throat. 芒聙聹Yield!芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹or you are a
dead man!芒聙聺 The musket fell from the soldier芒聙聶s hands, who yielded on the
instant. Guiche summoned one of his grooms, and delivering the prisoner
into his charge, with orders to shoot him through the head if he
attempted to escape, he leaped from his horse and approached Raoul.

芒聙聹Faith, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, smiling, although his pallor betrayed the
excitement consequent on a first affair, 芒聙聹you are in a great hurry to
pay your debts and have not been long under any obligation to me.
Without your aid,芒聙聺 continued he, repeating the count芒聙聶s words 芒聙聹I should
have been a dead man芒聙聰thrice dead.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My antagonist took flight,芒聙聺 replied De Guiche 芒聙聹and left me at liberty
to come to your assistance. But are you seriously wounded? I see you
are covered with blood!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I believe,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹that I have got something like a scratch on
the arm. If you will help me to drag myself from under my horse I hope
nothing need prevent us continuing our journey.芒聙聺

Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges and Olivain had already dismounted and were
attempting to raise the struggling horse. At last Raoul succeeded in
drawing his foot from the stirrup and his leg from under the animal,
and in a second he was on his feet again.

芒聙聹Nothing broken?芒聙聺 asked De Guiche.

芒聙聹Faith, no, thank Heaven!芒聙聺 replied Raoul; 芒聙聹but what has become of the
poor wretches whom these scoundrels were murdering?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I fear we arrived too late. They have killed them, I think, and taken
flight, carrying off their booty. My servants are examining the
bodies.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us go and see whether they are quite dead, or if they can still be
helped,芒聙聺 suggested Raoul. 芒聙聹Olivain, we have come into possession of two
horses, but I have lost my own. Take for yourself the better of the two
and give me yours.芒聙聺

They approached the spot where the unfortunate victims lay.




Chapter XXXI.
The Monk.


Two men lay prone upon the ground, one bathed in blood and motionless,
with his face toward the earth; this one was dead. The other leaned
against a tree, supported there by the two valets, and was praying
fervently, with clasped hands and eyes raised to Heaven. He had
received a ball in his thigh, which had broken the bone. The young men
first approached the dead man.

芒聙聹He is a priest,芒聙聺 said Bragelonne, 芒聙聹he has worn the tonsure. Oh, the
scoundrels! to lift their hands against a minister of God.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come here, sir,芒聙聺 said Urban, an old soldier who had served under the
cardinal duke in all his campaigns; 芒聙聹come here, there is nothing to be
done with him, whilst we may perhaps be able to save the other.芒聙聺

The wounded man smiled sadly. 芒聙聹Save me! Oh, no!芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹but help me
to die, if you can.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you a priest?芒聙聺 asked Raoul.

芒聙聹No sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I ask, as your unfortunate companion appeared to me to belong to the
church.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is the curate of Bethune, sir, and was carrying the holy vessels
belonging to his church, and the treasure of the chapter, to a safe
place, the prince having abandoned our town yesterday; and as it was
known that bands of the enemy were prowling about the country, no one
dared to accompany the good man, so I offered to do so.

芒聙聹And, sir,芒聙聺 continued the wounded man, 芒聙聹I suffer much and would like,
if possible, to be carried to some house.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where you can be relieved?芒聙聺 asked De Guiche.

芒聙聹No, where I can confess.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But perhaps you are not so dangerously wounded as you think,芒聙聺 said
Raoul.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied the wounded man, 芒聙聹believe me, there is no time to lose;
the ball has broken the thigh bone and entered the intestines.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you a surgeon?芒聙聺 asked De Guiche.

芒聙聹No, but I know a little about wounds, and mine, I know, is mortal.
Try, therefore, either to carry me to some place where I may see a
priest or take the trouble to send one to me here. It is my soul that
must be saved; as for my body, it is lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To die whilst doing a good deed! It is impossible. God will help you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Gentlemen, in the name of Heaven!芒聙聺 said the wounded man, collecting
all his forces, as if to get up, 芒聙聹let us not lose time in useless
words. Either help me to gain the nearest village or swear to me on
your salvation that you will send me the first monk, the first cur脙漏,
the first priest you may meet. But,芒聙聺 he added in a despairing tone,
芒聙聹perhaps no one will dare to come for it is known that the Spaniards
are ranging through the country, and I shall die without absolution. My
God! my God! Good God! good God!芒聙聺 added the wounded man, in an accent
of terror which made the young men shudder; 芒聙聹you will not allow that?
that would be too terrible!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Calm yourself, sir,芒聙聺 replied De Guiche. 芒聙聹I swear to you, you shall
receive the consolation that you ask. Only tell us where we shall find
a house at which we can demand aid and a village from which we can
fetch a priest.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank you, and God reward you! About half a mile from this, on the
same road, there is an inn, and about a mile further on, after leaving
the inn, you will reach the village of Greney. There you must find the
curate, or if he is not at home, go to the convent of the Augustines,
which is the last house on the right, and bring me one of the brothers.
Monk or priest, it matters not, provided only that he has received from
holy church the power of absolving _in articulo mortis_.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, 芒聙聹remain beside this unfortunate
man and see that he is removed as gently as possible. The vicomte and
myself will go and find a priest.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, sir,芒聙聺 replied the tutor; 芒聙聹but in Heaven芒聙聶s name do not expose
yourself to danger!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do not fear. Besides, we are safe for to-day; you know the axiom,
芒聙聵_Non bis in idem_.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Courage, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul to the wounded man. 芒聙聹We are going to execute
your wishes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹May Heaven prosper you!芒聙聺 replied the dying man, with an accent of
gratitude impossible to describe.

The two young men galloped off in the direction mentioned and in ten
minutes reached the inn. Raoul, without dismounting, called to the host
and announced that a wounded man was about to be brought to his house
and begged him in the meantime to prepare everything needful. He
desired him also, should he know in the neighborhood any doctor or
chirurgeon, to fetch him, taking on himself the payment of the
messenger.

The host, who saw two young noblemen, richly clad, promised everything
they required, and our two cavaliers, after seeing that preparations
for the reception were actually begun, started off again and proceeded
rapidly toward Greney.

They had gone rather more than a league and had begun to descry the
first houses of the village, the red-tiled roofs of which stood out
from the green trees which surrounded them, when, coming toward them
mounted on a mule, they perceived a poor monk, whose large hat and gray
worsted dress made them take him for an Augustine brother. Chance for
once seemed to favor them in sending what they were so assiduously
seeking. He was a man about twenty-two or twenty-three years old, but
who appeared much older from ascetic exercises. His complexion was
pale, not of that deadly pallor which is a kind of neutral beauty, but
of a bilious, yellow hue; his colorless hair was short and scarcely
extended beyond the circle formed by the hat around his head, and his
light blue eyes seemed destitute of any expression.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 began Raoul, with his usual politeness, 芒聙聹are you an
ecclesiastic?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why do you ask me that?芒聙聺 replied the stranger, with a coolness which
was barely civil.

芒聙聹Because we want to know,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, haughtily.

The stranger touched his mule with his heel and continued his way.

In a second De Guiche had sprung before him and barred his passage.
芒聙聹Answer, sir,芒聙聺 exclaimed he; 芒聙聹you have been asked politely, and every
question is worth an answer.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I suppose I am free to say or not to say who I am to two strangers who
take a fancy to ask me.芒聙聺

It was with difficulty that De Guiche restrained the intense desire he
had of breaking the monk芒聙聶s bones.

芒聙聹In the first place,芒聙聺 he said, making an effort to control himself, 芒聙聹we
are not people who may be treated anyhow; my friend there is the
Viscount of Bragelonne and I am the Count de Guiche. Nor was it from
caprice we asked the question, for there is a wounded and dying man who
demands the succor of the church. If you be a priest, I conjure you in
the name of humanity to follow me to aid this man; if you be not, it is
a different matter, and I warn you in the name of courtesy, of which
you appear profoundly ignorant, that I shall chastise you for your
insolence.芒聙聺

The pale face of the monk became so livid and his smile so strange,
that Raoul, whose eyes were still fixed upon him, felt as if this smile
had struck to his heart like an insult.

芒聙聹He is some Spanish or Flemish spy,芒聙聺 said he, putting his hand to his
pistol. A glance, threatening and transient as lightning, replied to
Raoul.

芒聙聹Well, sir,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, 芒聙聹are you going to reply?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am a priest,芒聙聺 said the young man.

芒聙聹Then, father,芒聙聺 said Raoul, forcing himself to convey a respect by
speech that did not come from his heart, 芒聙聹if you are a priest you have
an opportunity, as my friend has told you, of exercising your vocation.
At the next inn you will find a wounded man, now being attended by our
servants, who has asked the assistance of a minister of God.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will go,芒聙聺 said the monk.

And he touched his mule.

芒聙聹If you do not go, sir,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, 芒聙聹remember that we have two
steeds able to catch your mule and the power of having you seized
wherever you may be; and then I swear your trial will be summary; one
can always find a tree and a cord.芒聙聺

The monk芒聙聶s eye again flashed, but that was all; he merely repeated his
phrase, 芒聙聹I will go,芒聙聺芒聙聰and he went.

芒聙聹Let us follow him,芒聙聺 said De Guiche; 芒聙聹it will be the surest plan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was about to propose so doing,芒聙聺 answered De Bragelonne.

In the space of five minutes the monk turned around to ascertain
whether he was followed or not.

芒聙聹You see,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹we have done wisely.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What a horrible face that monk has,芒聙聺 said De Guiche.

芒聙聹Horrible!芒聙聺 replied Raoul, 芒聙聹especially in expression.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, 芒聙聹a strange face; but these monks are
subject to such degrading practices; their fasts make them pale, the
blows of the discipline make them hypocrites, and their eyes become
inflamed through weeping for the good things of this life we common
folk enjoy, but they have lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹the poor man will get his priest, but, by Heaven,

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed De Guiche, 芒聙聹you must understand that this is one of

芒聙聹As ugly?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but reasonably hideous.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pshaw!芒聙聺 said De Guiche. 芒聙聹Absolution comes not from him who administers

芒聙聹Yes, count, it is a strange thing and one which might astonish you,

芒聙聹Never,芒聙聺 answered De Guiche.

芒聙聹Well, it has happened to me to do so in our Blaisois forests, and I

fixed, pale and as though fascinated, until the moment when the Comte
de la F脙篓re芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your father?芒聙聺 asked De Guiche.

芒聙聹No, my guardian,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, blushing.

芒聙聹Very well芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Until the moment when the Comte de la F脙篓re,芒聙聺 resumed Raoul, 芒聙聹said,
芒聙聵Come, Bragelonne, draw your sword;芒聙聶 then only I rushed upon the

芒聙聹Then you regret that you did not cut your serpent in two morsels?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faith, yes, almost,芒聙聺 said Raoul.

They had now arrived within sight of the little inn and could see on

芒聙聹There is the wounded man,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, passing close to the

As for Raoul, he avoided the monk by the whole width of the road and

The young men rode up to the wounded man to announce that they were

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said the youths, 芒聙聹we have done all we can for you; and as we

芒聙聹Go, my young sirs,芒聙聺 said the sick man, 芒聙聹and may you both be blessed

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said De Guiche to his tutor, 芒聙聹we will precede you, and you can

The host was at his door and everything was prepared芒聙聰bed, bandages, and

芒聙聹Everything,芒聙聺 said he to Raoul, 芒聙聹shall be done as you desire; but you

芒聙聹Oh, my wound芒聙聰mine芒聙聰芒聙聶tis nothing,芒聙聺 replied the viscount; 芒聙聹it will be

芒聙聹Would it not be safer and more certain if I should ask him his name

芒聙聹There is no harm in over-precaution. I am the Viscount de Bragelonne

At this moment the wounded man arrived from one direction and the monk

芒聙聹Sir monk,芒聙聺 said De Guiche, 芒聙聹confess well that brave man; and be not

芒聙聹Come, count,芒聙聺 said Raoul, who seemed instinctively to dislike the

The litter, borne by two servants, now entered the house. The host and

芒聙聹Well, what芒聙聶s the matter?芒聙聺 asked the latter, 芒聙聹are you going to be ill

芒聙聹No, but look,芒聙聺 replied the hostess, pointing to the wounded man; 芒聙聹I

芒聙聹That man芒聙聰wait a bit.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Truly,芒聙聺 cried the host, 芒聙聹misfortune is coming on our house; it is the

芒聙聹The former executioner of Bethune!芒聙聺 murmured the young monk, shrinking

Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges, who was at the door, perceived his hesitation.

芒聙聹Sir monk,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹whether he is now or has been an executioner,
this unfortunate being is none the less a man. Render to him, then, the

The monk made no reply, but silently wended his way to the room where
the two valets had deposited the dying man on a bed. D芒聙聶Arminges and
Olivain and the two grooms then mounted their horses, and all four

芒聙聹What does your worship want?芒聙聺 demanded the host, pale and trembling

The traveler made a sign as if he wished to drink, and then pointed to
his horse and gesticulated like a man who is brushing something.

芒聙聹Ah, _diable!_芒聙聺 said the host to himself; 芒聙聹this man seems dumb. And
where will your worship drink?芒聙聺

芒聙聹There,芒聙聺 answered the traveler, pointing to the table.

芒聙聹I was mistaken,芒聙聺 said the host, 芒聙聹he芒聙聶s not quite dumb. And what else

芒聙聹To know if you have seen a young man pass, fifteen years of age,

芒聙聹The Viscount de Bragelonne?

芒聙聹Just so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then you are called Monsieur Grimaud?芒聙聺

The traveler made a sign of assent.

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 said the host, 芒聙聹your young master was here a quarter of
an hour ago; he will dine at Mazingarbe and sleep at Cambrin.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How far is Mazingarbe?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two miles and a half.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank you.芒聙聺

Grimaud was drinking his wine silently and had just placed his glass on
the table to be filled a second time, when a terrific scream resounded

芒聙聹What is that?芒聙聺 said he; 芒聙聹whence comes that cry?芒聙聺

芒聙聹From the wounded man芒聙聶s room,芒聙聺 replied the host.

芒聙聹What wounded man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The former executioner of Bethune, who has just been brought in here,

芒聙聹The old executioner of Bethune,芒聙聺 muttered Grimaud; 芒聙聹a man between

芒聙聹That is he, except that his beard has turned gray and his hair is

芒聙聹I have seen him once,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud, a cloud darkening his

At this instant a second cry, less piercing than the first, but

The three listeners looked at one another in alarm.

芒聙聹We must see what it is,芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

芒聙聹It sounds like the cry of one who is being murdered,芒聙聺 murmured the

芒聙聹_Mon Dieu!_芒聙聺 said the woman, crossing herself.

If Grimaud was slow in speaking, we know that he was quick to act; he

芒聙聹Open the door!芒聙聺 cried the host; 芒聙聹open it instantly, sir monk!芒聙聺

No reply.

芒聙聹Unfasten it, or I will break it in!芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

The same silence, and then, ere the host could oppose his design,

芒聙聹The monk!芒聙聺 cried the host; 芒聙聹where is the monk?芒聙聺

Grimaud sprang toward an open window which looked into the courtyard.

芒聙聹He has escaped by this means,芒聙聺 exclaimed he.

芒聙聹Do you think so?芒聙聺 said the host, bewildered; 芒聙聹boy, see if the mule

芒聙聹There is no mule,芒聙聺 cried he to whom this question was addressed.

The host clasped his hands and looked around him suspiciously, whilst

芒聙聹There can be no longer any doubt but that it is himself,芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Does he still live?芒聙聺 inquired the innkeeper.

Making no reply, Grimaud opened the poor man芒聙聶s jacket to feel if the

芒聙聹Run! run for help!芒聙聺 cried Grimaud, 芒聙聹and I will remain beside him
here.芒聙聺

The host quitted the room in agitation, and as for his wife, she had
fled at the sound of her husband芒聙聶s cries.




Chapter XXXII.
The Absolution.


This is what had taken place: We have seen that it was not of his own
free will, but, on the contrary, very reluctantly, that the monk
attended the wounded man who had been recommended to him in so strange
a manner. Perhaps he would have sought to escape by flight had he seen
any possibility of doing so. He was restrained by the threats of the
two gentlemen and by the presence of their attendants, who doubtless
had received their instructions. And besides, he considered it most
expedient, without exhibiting too much ill-will, to follow to the end
his r脙麓le as confessor.

The monk entered the chamber and approached the bed of the wounded man.
The executioner searched his face with the quick glance peculiar to
those who are about to die and have no time to lose. He made a movement
of surprise and said:

芒聙聹Father, you are very young.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Men who bear my robe have no age,芒聙聺 replied the monk, dryly.

芒聙聹Alas, speak to me more gently, father; in my last moments I need a
friend.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you suffer much?芒聙聺 asked the monk.

芒聙聹Yes, but in my soul much more than in my body.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We will save your soul,芒聙聺 said the young man; 芒聙聹but are you really the
executioner of Bethune, as these people say?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is to say,芒聙聺 eagerly replied the wounded man, who doubtless feared
that the name of executioner would take from him the last help that he
could claim芒聙聰芒聙聹that is to say, I was, but am no longer; it is fifteen
years since I gave up the office. I still assist at executions, but no
longer strike the blow myself芒聙聰no, indeed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have, then, a repugnance to your profession?芒聙聺

芒聙聹So long as I struck in the name of the law and of justice my
profession allowed me to sleep quietly, sheltered as I was by justice
and law; but since that terrible night when I became an instrument of
private vengeance and when with personal hatred I raised the sword over
one of God芒聙聶s creatures芒聙聰since that day芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

The executioner paused and shook his head with an expression of
despair.

芒聙聹Tell me about it,芒聙聺 said the monk, who, sitting on the foot of the bed,
began to be interested in a story so strangely introduced.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 cried the dying man, with all the effusiveness of a grief
declared after long suppression, 芒聙聹ah! I have sought to stifle remorse
by twenty years of good deeds; I have assuaged the natural ferocity of
those who shed blood; on every occasion I have exposed my life to save
those who were in danger, and I have preserved lives in exchange for
that I took away. That is not all; the money gained in the exercise of
my profession I have distributed to the poor; I have been assiduous in
attending church and those who formerly fled from me have become
accustomed to seeing me. All have forgiven me, some have even loved me;
but I think that God has not pardoned me, for the memory of that
execution pursues me constantly and every night I see that woman芒聙聶s
ghost rising before me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A woman! You have assassinated a woman, then?芒聙聺 cried the monk.

芒聙聹You also!芒聙聺 exclaimed the executioner, 芒聙聹you use that word which sounds
ever in my ears芒聙聰芒聙聵assassinated!芒聙聶 I have assassinated, then, and not
executed! I am an assassin, then, and not an officer of justice!芒聙聺 and
he closed his eyes with a groan.

The monk doubtless feared that he would die without saying more, for he
exclaimed eagerly:

芒聙聹Go on, I know nothing, as yet; when you have finished your story, God
and I will judge.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, father,芒聙聺 continued the executioner, without opening his eyes, as
if he feared on opening them to see some frightful object, 芒聙聹it is
especially when night comes on and when I have to cross a river, that
this terror which I have been unable to conquer comes upon me; it then
seems as if my hand grew heavy, as if the cutlass was still in its
grasp, as if the water had the color of blood, and all the voices of
nature芒聙聰the whispering of the trees, the murmur of the wind, the lapping
of the wave芒聙聰united in a voice tearful, despairing, terrible, crying to
me, 芒聙聵Place for the justice of God!芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Delirium!芒聙聺 murmured the monk, shaking his head.

The executioner opened his eyes, turned toward the young man and
grasped his arm.

芒聙聹芒聙聵Delirium,芒聙聶芒聙聺 he repeated; 芒聙聹芒聙聵delirium,芒聙聶 do you say? Oh, no! I remember
too well. It was evening; I had thrown the body into the river and
those words which my remorse repeats to me are those which I in my
pride pronounced. After being the instrument of human justice I aspired
to be that of the justice of God.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But let me see, how was it done? Speak,芒聙聺 said the monk.

芒聙聹It was at night. A man came to me and showed me an order and I
followed him. Four other noblemen awaited me. They led me away masked.
I reserved the right of refusing if the office they required of me
should seem unjust. We traveled five or six leagues, serious, silent,
and almost without speaking. At length, through the window of a little
hut, they showed me a woman sitting, leaning on a table, and said,
芒聙聵there is the person to be executed.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Horrible!芒聙聺 said the monk. 芒聙聹And you obeyed?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Father, that woman was a monster. It was said that she had poisoned
her second husband; she had tried to assassinate her brother-in-law;
she had just poisoned a young woman who was her rival, and before
leaving England she had, it was believed, caused the favorite of the
king to be murdered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Buckingham?芒聙聺 cried the monk.

芒聙聹Yes, Buckingham.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The woman was English, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, she was French, but she had married in England.芒聙聺

The monk turned pale, wiped his brow and went and bolted the door. The
executioner thought that he had abandoned him and fell back, groaning,
upon his bed.

芒聙聹No, no; I am here,芒聙聺 said the monk, quickly coming back to him. 芒聙聹Go on;
who were those men?芒聙聺

芒聙聹One of them was a foreigner, English, I think. The four others were
French and wore the uniform of musketeers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Their names?芒聙聺 asked the monk.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t know them, but the four other noblemen called the Englishman
芒聙聵my lord.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Was the woman handsome?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Young and beautiful. Oh, yes, especially beautiful. I see her now, as
on her knees at my feet, with her head thrown back, she begged for
life. I have never understood how I could have laid low a head so
beautiful, with a face so pale.芒聙聺

The monk seemed agitated by a strange emotion; he trembled all over; he
seemed eager to put a question which yet he dared not ask. At length,
with a violent effort at self-control:

芒聙聹The name of that woman?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t know what it was. As I have said, she was twice married, once
in France, the second time in England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was young, you say?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Twenty-five years old.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Beautiful?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ravishingly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Blond?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Abundance of hair芒聙聰falling over her shoulders?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eyes of an admirable expression?芒聙聺

芒聙聹When she chose. Oh, yes, it is she!芒聙聺

芒聙聹A voice of strange sweetness?芒聙聺

芒聙聹How do you know it?芒聙聺

The executioner raised himself on his elbow and gazed with a frightened
air at the monk, who became livid.

芒聙聹And you killed her?芒聙聺 the monk exclaimed. 芒聙聹You were the tool of those
cowards who dared not kill her themselves? You had no pity for that
youthfulness, that beauty, that weakness? you killed that woman?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas! I have already told you, father, that woman, under that angelic
appearance, had an infernal soul, and when I saw her, when I recalled
all the evil she had done to me芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹To you? What could she have done to you? Come, tell me!芒聙聺

芒聙聹She had seduced and ruined my brother, a priest. She had fled with him
from her convent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹With your brother?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my brother was her first lover, and she caused his death. Oh,
father, do not look in that way at me! Oh, I am guilty, then; you will
not pardon me?芒聙聺

The monk recovered his usual expression.

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I will pardon you if you tell me all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 cried the executioner, 芒聙聹all! all! all!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Answer, then. If she seduced your brother芒聙聰you said she seduced him,
did you not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If she caused his death芒聙聰you said that she caused his death?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 repeated the executioner.

芒聙聹Then you must know what her name was as a young girl.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, _mon Dieu!_芒聙聺 cried the executioner, 芒聙聹I think I am dying.
Absolution, father! absolution.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell me her name and I will give it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Her name was芒聙聰芒聙聰My God, have pity on me!芒聙聺 murmured the executioner; and
he fell back on the bed, pale, trembling, and apparently about to die.

芒聙聹Her name!芒聙聺 repeated the monk, bending over him as if to tear from him
the name if he would not utter it; 芒聙聹her name! Speak, or no absolution!芒聙聺

The dying man collected all his forces.

The monk芒聙聶s eyes glittered.

芒聙聹Anne de Bueil,芒聙聺 murmured the wounded man.

芒聙聹Anne de Bueil!芒聙聺 cried the monk, standing up and lifting his hands to
Heaven. 芒聙聹Anne de Bueil! You said Anne de Bueil, did you not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes, that was her name; and now absolve me, for I am dying.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I, absolve you!芒聙聺 cried the priest, with a laugh which made the dying
man芒聙聶s hair stand on end; 芒聙聹I, absolve you? I am not a priest.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are not a priest!芒聙聺 cried the executioner. 芒聙聹What, then, are you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am about to tell you, wretched man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, _mon Dieu!_芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am John Francis de Winter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not know you,芒聙聺 said the executioner.

芒聙聹Wait, wait; you are going to know me. I am John Francis de Winter,芒聙聺 he
repeated, 芒聙聹and that woman芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, that woman?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Was my mother!芒聙聺

The executioner uttered the first cry, that terrible cry which had been
first heard.

芒聙聹Oh, pardon me, pardon me!芒聙聺 he murmured; 芒聙聹if not in the name of God, at
least in your own name; if not as priest, then as son.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pardon you!芒聙聺 cried the pretended monk, 芒聙聹pardon you! Perhaps God will
pardon you, but I, never!芒聙聺

芒聙聹For pity芒聙聶s sake,芒聙聺 said the executioner, extending his arms.

芒聙聹No pity for him who had no pity! Die, impenitent, die in despair, die
and be damned!芒聙聺 And drawing a poniard from beneath his robe he thrust
it into the breast of the wounded man, saying, 芒聙聹Here is my absolution!芒聙聺

Then was heard that second cry, not so loud as the first and followed
by a long groan.

The executioner, who had lifted himself up, fell back upon his bed. As
to the monk, without withdrawing the poniard from the wound, he ran to
the window, opened it, leaped out into the flowers of a small garden,
glided onward to the stable, took out his mule, went out by a back
gate, ran to a neighbouring thicket, threw off his monkish garb, took
from his valise the complete habiliment of a cavalier, clothed himself
in it, went on foot to the first post, secured there a horse and
continued with a loose rein his journey to Paris.




Chapter XXXIII.
Grimaud Speaks.


Grimaud was left alone with the executioner, who in a few moments
opened his eyes.

芒聙聹Help, help,芒聙聺 he murmured; 芒聙聹oh, God! have I not a single friend in the
world who will aid me either to live or to die?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Take courage,芒聙聺 said Grimaud; 芒聙聹they are gone to find assistance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who are you?芒聙聺 asked the wounded man, fixing his half opened eyes on
Grimaud.

芒聙聹An old acquaintance,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud.

芒聙聹You?芒聙聺 and the wounded man sought to recall the features of the person
now before him.

芒聙聹Under what circumstances did we meet?芒聙聺 he asked again.

芒聙聹One night, twenty years ago, my master fetched you from Bethune and
conducted you to Armenti脙篓res.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know you well now,芒聙聺 said the executioner; 芒聙聹you were one of the four
grooms.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Just so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where do you come from now?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was passing by and drew up at this inn to rest my horse. They told
me the executioner of Bethune was here and wounded, when you uttered
two piercing cries. At the first we ran to the door and at the second
forced it open.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And the monk?芒聙聺 exclaimed the executioner, 芒聙聹did you see the monk?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What monk?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The monk that was shut in with me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, he was no longer here; he appears to have fled by the window. Was
he the man that stabbed you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said the executioner.

Grimaud moved as if to leave the room.

芒聙聹What are you going to do?芒聙聺 asked the wounded man.

芒聙聹He must be apprehended.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do not attempt it; he has revenged himself and has done well. Now I
may hope that God will forgive me, since my crime is expiated.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Explain yourself.芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

芒聙聹The woman whom you and your masters commanded me to kill芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Milady?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, Milady; it is true you called her thus.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What has the monk to do with this Milady?芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was his mother.芒聙聺

Grimaud trembled and stared at the dying man in a dull and leaden
manner.

芒聙聹His mother!芒聙聺 he repeated.

芒聙聹Yes, his mother.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But does he know this secret, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I mistook him for a monk and revealed it to him in confession.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Unhappy man!芒聙聺 cried Grimaud, whose face was covered with sweat at the
bare idea of the evil results such a revelation might cause; 芒聙聹unhappy
man, you named no one, I hope?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I pronounced no name, for I knew none, except his mother芒聙聶s, as a young
girl, and it was by this name that he recognized her, but he knows that
his uncle was among her judges.芒聙聺

Thus speaking, he fell back exhausted. Grimaud, wishing to relieve him,
advanced his hand toward the hilt of the dagger.

芒聙聹Touch me not!芒聙聺 said the executioner; 芒聙聹if this dagger is withdrawn I
shall die.芒聙聺

Grimaud remained with his hand extended; then, striking his forehead,
he exclaimed:

芒聙聹Oh! if this man should ever discover the names of the others, my
master is lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Haste! haste to him and warn him,芒聙聺 cried the wounded man, 芒聙聹if he still
lives; warn his friends, too. My death, believe me, will not be the end
of this atrocious misadventure.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where was the monk going?芒聙聺 asked Grimaud.

芒聙聹Toward Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who stopped him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two young gentlemen, who were on their way to join the army and the
name of one of whom I heard his companion mention芒聙聰the Viscount de
Bragelonne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And it was this young man who brought the monk to you? Then it was the
will of God that it should be so and this it is which makes it all so
awful,芒聙聺 continued Grimaud. 芒聙聹And yet that woman deserved her fate; do
you not think so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On one芒聙聶s death-bed the crimes of others appear very small in
comparison with one芒聙聶s own,芒聙聺 said the executioner; and falling back
exhausted he closed his eyes.

Grimaud was reluctant to leave the man alone and yet he perceived the
necessity of starting at once to bear these tidings to the Comte de la
F脙篓re. Whilst he thus hesitated the host re-entered the room, followed
not only by a surgeon, but by many other persons, whom curiosity had
attracted to the spot. The surgeon approached the dying man, who seemed
to have fainted.

芒聙聹We must first extract the steel from the side,芒聙聺 said he, shaking his
head in a significant manner.

The prophecy which the wounded man had just uttered recurred to
Grimaud, who turned away his head. The weapon, as we have already
stated, was plunged into the body to the hilt, and as the surgeon,
taking it by the end, drew it forth, the wounded man opened his eyes
and fixed them on him in a manner truly frightful. When at last the
blade had been entirely withdrawn, a red froth issued from the mouth of
the wounded man and a stream of blood spouted afresh from the wound
when he at length drew breath; then, fixing his eyes upon Grimaud with
a singular expression, the dying man uttered the last death-rattle and
expired.

Then Grimaud, lifting the dagger from the pool of blood which was
gliding along the room, to the horror of all present, made a sign to
the host to follow him, paid him with a generosity worthy of his master
and again mounted his horse. Grimaud芒聙聶s first intention had been to
return to Paris, but he remembered the anxiety which his prolonged
absence might occasion Raoul, and reflecting that there were now only
two miles between the vicomte and himself and a quarter of an hour芒聙聶s
riding would unite them, and that the going, returning and explanation
would not occupy an hour, he put spurs to his horse and a few minutes
after had reached the only inn of Mazingarbe.

Raoul was seated at table with the Count de Guiche and his tutor, when
all at once the door opened and Grimaud presented himself,
travel-stained, dirty, and sprinkled with the blood of the unhappy
executioner.

芒聙聹Grimaud, my good Grimaud!芒聙聺 exclaimed Raoul 芒聙聹here you are at last!
Excuse me, sirs, this is not a servant, but a friend. How did you leave
the count?芒聙聺 continued he. 芒聙聹Does he regret me a little? Have you seen
him since I left him? Answer, for I have many things to tell you, too;
indeed, the last three days some odd adventures have happened芒聙聰but what
is the matter? how pale you are! and blood, too! What is this?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the blood of the unfortunate man whom you left at the inn and
who died in my arms.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In your arms?芒聙聰that man! but know you who he was?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He used to be the headsman of Bethune.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You knew him? and he is dead?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, sir,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Arminges, 芒聙聹it is the common lot; even an
executioner is not exempted. I had a bad opinion of him the moment I
saw his wound, and since he asked for a monk you know that it was his
opinion, too, that death would follow.芒聙聺

At the mention of the monk, Grimaud became pale.

芒聙聹Come, come,芒聙聺 continued D芒聙聶Arminges, 芒聙聹to dinner;芒聙聺 for like most men of
his age and generation he did not allow sentiment or sensibility to
interfere with a repast.

芒聙聹You are right, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul. 芒聙聹Come, Grimaud, order dinner for
yourself and when you have rested a little we can talk.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, no,芒聙聺 said Grimaud. 芒聙聹I cannot stop a moment; I must start for
Paris again immediately.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What? You start for Paris? You are mistaken; it is Olivain who leaves
me; you are to remain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the contrary, Olivain is to stay and I am to go. I have come for
nothing else but to tell you so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what is the meaning of this change?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I cannot tell you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Explain yourself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I cannot explain myself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, tell me, what is the joke?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur le vicomte knows that I never joke.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but I know also that Monsieur le Comte de la F脙篓re arranged that
you were to remain with me and that Olivain should return to Paris. I
shall follow the count芒聙聶s directions.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not under present circumstances, monsieur.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Perhaps you mean to disobey me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, monsieur, I must.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You persist, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, I am going; may you be happy, monsieur,芒聙聺 and Grimaud saluted and
turned toward the door to go out.

Raoul, angry and at the same time uneasy, ran after him and seized him
by the arm. 芒聙聹Grimaud!芒聙聺 he cried; 芒聙聹remain; I wish it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud, 芒聙聹you wish me to allow monsieur le comte to be
killed.芒聙聺 He saluted and made a movement to depart.

芒聙聹Grimaud, my friend,芒聙聺 said the viscount, 芒聙聹will you leave me thus, in
such anxiety? Speak, speak, in Heaven芒聙聶s name!芒聙聺 And Raoul fell back
trembling upon his chair.

芒聙聹I can tell you but one thing, sir, for the secret you wish to know is
not my own. You met a monk, did you not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

The young men looked at each other with an expression of fear.

芒聙聹You conducted him to the wounded man and you had time to observe him,
and perhaps you would know him again were you to meet him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes!芒聙聺 cried both young men.

芒聙聹Very well; if ever you meet him again, wherever it may be, whether on
the high road or in the street or in a church, anywhere that he or you
may be, put your foot on his neck and crush him without pity, without
mercy, as you would crush a viper or a scorpion! destroy him utterly
and quit him not until he is dead; the lives of five men are not safe,
in my opinion, as long as he is on the earth.芒聙聺

And without adding another word, Grimaud, profiting by the astonishment
and terror into which he had thrown his auditors, rushed from the room.
Two minutes later the thunder of a horse芒聙聶s hoofs was heard upon the
road; it was Grimaud, on his way to Paris. When once in the saddle
Grimaud reflected on two things; first, that at the pace he was going
his horse would not carry him ten miles, and secondly, that he had no
money. But Grimaud芒聙聶s ingenuity was more prolific than his speech, and
therefore at the first halt he sold his steed and with the money
obtained from the purchase took post horses.




Chapter XXXIV.
On the Eve of Battle.


Raoul was aroused from his sombre reflections by his host, who rushed
into the apartment crying out, 芒聙聹The Spaniards! the Spaniards!芒聙聺

That cry was of such importance as to overcome all preoccupation. The
young men made inquiries and ascertained that the enemy was advancing
by way of Houdin and Bethune.

While Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges gave orders for the horses to be made ready
for departure, the two young men ascended to the upper windows of the
house and saw in the direction of Marsin and of Lens a large body of
infantry and cavalry. This time it was not a wandering troop of
partisans; it was an entire army. There was therefore nothing for them
to do but to follow the prudent advice of Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges and beat
a retreat. They quickly went downstairs. Monsieur d芒聙聶Arminges was
already mounted. Olivain had ready the horses of the young men, and the
lackeys of the Count de Guiche guarded carefully between them the
Spanish prisoner, mounted on a pony which had been bought for his use.
As a further precaution they had bound his hands.

The little company started off at a trot on the road to Cambrin, where
they expected to find the prince. But he was no longer there, having
withdrawn on the previous evening to La Bassee, misled by false
intelligence of the enemy芒聙聶s movements. Deceived by this intelligence he
had concentrated his forces between Vieille-Chapelle and La Venthie;
and after a reconnoissance along the entire line, in company with
Marshal de Grammont, he had returned and seated himself before a table,
with his officers around him. He questioned them as to the news they
had each been charged to obtain, but nothing positive had been learned.
The hostile army had disappeared two days before and seemed to have
gone out of existence.

Now an enemy is never so near and consequently so threatening, as when
he has completely disappeared. The prince was, therefore, contrary to
his custom, gloomy and anxious, when an officer entered and announced
to Marshal de Grammont that some one wished to see him.

The Duc de Grammont received permission from the prince by a glance and
went out. The prince followed him with his eyes and continued looking
at the door; no one ventured to speak, for fear of disturbing him.

Suddenly a dull and heavy noise was heard. The prince leaped to his
feet, extending his hand in the direction whence came the sound, there
was no mistaking it芒聙聰it was the noise of cannon. Every one stood up.

At that moment the door opened.

芒聙聹Monseigneur,芒聙聺 said Marshal de Grammont, with a radiant face, 芒聙聹will
your highness permit my son, Count de Guiche, and his traveling
companion, Viscount de Bragelonne, to come in and give news of the
enemy, whom they have found while we were looking for him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 eagerly replied the prince, 芒聙聹will I permit? I not only permit,
I desire; let them come in.芒聙聺

The marshal introduced the two young men and placed them face to face
with the prince.

芒聙聹Speak, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the prince, saluting them; 芒聙聹first speak; we
shall have time afterward for the usual compliments. The most urgent
thing now is to learn where the enemy is and what he is doing.芒聙聺

It fell naturally to the Count de Guiche to make reply; not only was he
the elder, but he had been presented to the prince by his father.
Besides, he had long known the prince, whilst Raoul now saw him for the
first time. He therefore narrated to the prince what they had seen from
the inn at Mazingarbe.

Meanwhile Raoul closely observed the young general, already made so
famous by the battles of Rocroy, Fribourg, and Nordlingen.

Louis de Bourbon, Prince de Cond脙漏, who, since the death of his father,
Henri de Bourbon, was called, in accordance with the custom of that
period, Monsieur le Prince, was a young man, not more than twenty-six
or twenty-seven years old, with the eye of an eagle芒聙聰_agl芒聙聶 occhi
grifani_, as Dante says芒聙聰aquiline nose, long, waving hair, of medium
height, well formed, possessed of all the qualities essential to the
successful soldier芒聙聰that is to say, the rapid glance, quick decision,
fabulous courage. At the same time he was a man of elegant manners and
strong mind, so that in addition to the revolution he had made in war,
by his new contributions to its methods, he had also made a revolution
at Paris, among the young noblemen of the court, whose natural chief he
was and who, in distinction from the social leaders of the ancient
court, modeled after Bassompierre, Bellegarde and the Duke d芒聙聶Angouleme,
were called the _petits-ma脙庐tres_.

At the first words of the Count de Guiche, the prince, having in mind
the direction whence came the sound of cannon, had understood
everything. The enemy was marching upon Lens, with the intention,
doubtless, of securing possession of that town and separating from
France the army of France. But in what force was the enemy? Was it a
corps sent out to make a diversion? Was it an entire army? To this
question De Guiche could not respond.

Now, as these questions involved matters of gravest consequence, it was
these to which the prince had especially desired an answer, exact,
precise, positive.

Raoul conquered the very natural feeling of timidity he experienced and
approaching the prince:

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹will you permit me to hazard a few words on that
subject, which will perhaps relieve you of your uncertainty?芒聙聺

The prince turned and seemed to cover the young man with a single
glance; he smiled on perceiving that he was a child hardly fifteen
years old.

芒聙聹Certainly, monsieur, speak,芒聙聺 he said, softening his stern, accented
tones, as if he were speaking to a woman.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Raoul, blushing, 芒聙聹might examine the Spanish prisoner.芒聙聺
