芒聙聹Have you a Spanish prisoner?芒聙聺 cried the prince.

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, that is true,芒聙聺 said De Guiche; 芒聙聹I had forgotten it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is easily understood; it was you who took him, count,芒聙聺 said
Raoul, smiling.

The old marshal turned toward the viscount, grateful for that praise of
his son, whilst the prince exclaimed:

芒聙聹The young man is right; let the prisoner be brought in.芒聙聺

Meanwhile the prince took De Guiche aside and asked him how the
prisoner had been taken and who this young man was.

芒聙聹Monsieur,芒聙聺 said the prince, turning toward Raoul, 芒聙聹I know that you
have a letter from my sister, Madame de Longueville; but I see that you
have preferred commending yourself to me by giving me good counsel.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Raoul, coloring up, 芒聙聹I did not wish to interrupt your
highness in a conversation so important as that in which you were
engaged with the count. But here is the letter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well,芒聙聺 said the prince; 芒聙聹give it to me later. Here is the
prisoner; let us attend to what is most pressing.芒聙聺

The prisoner was one of those military adventurers who sold their blood
to whoever would buy, and grew old in stratagems and spoils. Since he
had been taken he had not uttered a word, so that it was not known to
what country he belonged. The prince looked at him with unspeakable
distrust.

芒聙聹Of what country are you?芒聙聺 asked the prince.

The prisoner muttered a few words in a foreign tongue.

芒聙聹Ah! ah! it seems that he is a Spaniard. Do you speak Spanish,
Grammont?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faith, my lord, but indifferently.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I not at all,芒聙聺 said the prince, laughing. 芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 he said,
turning to those who were near him 芒聙聹can any one of you speak Spanish
and serve me as interpreter?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I can, my lord,芒聙聺 said Raoul.

芒聙聹Ah, you speak Spanish?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Enough, I think, to fulfill your highness芒聙聶s wishes on this occasion.芒聙聺

Meanwhile the prisoner had remained impassive and as if he had no
understanding of what was taking place.

芒聙聹My lord asks of what country you are,芒聙聺 said the young man, in the
purest Castilian.

芒聙聹_Ich bin ein Deutscher_,芒聙聺 replied the prisoner.

芒聙聹What in the devil does he say?芒聙聺 asked the prince. 芒聙聹What new gibberish
is that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He says he is German, my lord,芒聙聺 replied Raoul; 芒聙聹but I doubt it, for
his accent is bad and his pronunciation defective.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then you speak German, also?芒聙聺 asked the prince.

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well enough to question him in that language?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Question him, then.芒聙聺

Raoul began the examination, but the result justified his opinion. The
prisoner did not understand, or seemed not to understand, what Raoul
said to him; and Raoul could hardly understand his replies, containing
a mixture of Flemish and Alsatian. However, amidst all the prisoner芒聙聶s
efforts to elude a systematic examination, Raoul had recognized his
natural accent.

芒聙聹_Non siete Spagnuolo_,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹_non siete Tedesco; siete
Italiano_.芒聙聺

The prisoner started and bit his lips.

芒聙聹Ah, that,芒聙聺 said the prince, 芒聙聹I understand that language thoroughly;
and since he is Italian I will myself continue the examination. Thank
you, viscount,芒聙聺 continued the prince, laughing, 芒聙聹and I appoint you from
this moment my interpreter.芒聙聺

But the prisoner was not less unwilling to respond in Italian than in
the other languages; his aim was to elude the examination. Therefore,
he knew nothing either of the enemy芒聙聶s numbers, or of those in command,
or of the purpose of the army.

芒聙聹Very good,芒聙聺 said the prince, understanding the reason of that
ignorance; 芒聙聹the man was caught in the act of assassination and robbery;
he might have purchased his life by speaking; he doesn芒聙聶t wish to speak.
Take him out and shoot him.芒聙聺

The prisoner turned pale. The two soldiers who had brought him in took
him, each by one arm, and led him toward the door, whilst the prince,
turning to Marshal de Grammont, seemed to have already forgotten the
order he had given.

When he reached the threshold of the door the prisoner stopped. The
soldiers, who knew only their orders, attempted to force him along.

芒聙聹One moment,芒聙聺 said the prisoner, in French. 芒聙聹I am ready to speak, my
lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! ah!芒聙聺 said the prince, laughing, 芒聙聹I thought we should come to that.
I have a sure method of limbering tongues. Young men, take advantage of
it against the time when you may be in command.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But on condition,芒聙聺 continued the prisoner, 芒聙聹that your highness will
swear that my life shall be safe.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Upon my honor,芒聙聺 said the prince.

芒聙聹Question, then, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where did the army cross the Lys?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Between Saint-Venant and Aire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By whom is it commanded?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By Count de Fuonsaldagna, General Beck and the archduke.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of how many does it consist?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eighteen thousand men and thirty-six cannon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And its aim is?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lens.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You see; gentlemen!芒聙聺 said the prince, turning with a triumphant air
toward Marshal de Grammont and the other officers.

芒聙聹Yes, my lord,芒聙聺 said the marshal, 芒聙聹you have divined all that was
possible to human genius.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Recall Le Plessis, Belli脙篓vre, Villequier and D芒聙聶Erlac,芒聙聺 said the
prince, 芒聙聹recall all the troops that are on this side of the Lys. Let
them hold themselves in readiness to march to-night. To-morrow,
according to all probability, we shall attack the enemy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But, my lord,芒聙聺 said Marshal de Grammont, 芒聙聹consider that when we have
collected all our forces we shall have hardly thirteen thousand men.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur le mar脙漏chal,芒聙聺 said the prince, with that wonderful glance
that was peculiar to him, 芒聙聹it is with small armies that great battles
are won.芒聙聺

Then turning toward the prisoner, 芒聙聹Take away that man,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹and
keep him carefully in sight. His life is dependent on the information
he has given us; if it is true, he shall be free; if false, let him be
shot.芒聙聺

The prisoner was led away.

芒聙聹Count de Guiche,芒聙聺 said the prince, 芒聙聹it is a long time since you saw
your father, remain here with him. Monsieur,芒聙聺 he continued, addressing
Raoul, 芒聙聹if you are not too tired, follow me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the end of the world, my lord!芒聙聺 cried Raoul, feeling an unknown
enthusiasm for that young general, who seemed to him so worthy of his
renown.

The prince smiled; he despised flatterers, but he appreciated
enthusiasts.

芒聙聹Come, monsieur,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you are good in council, as we have already
discovered; to-morrow we shall know if you are good in action.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I,芒聙聺 said the marshal, 芒聙聹what am I to do?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Wait here to receive the troops. I shall either return for them myself
or shall send a courier directing you to bring them to me. Twenty
guards, well mounted, are all that I shall need for my escort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is very few,芒聙聺 said the marshal.

芒聙聹It is enough,芒聙聺 replied the prince. 芒聙聹Have you a good horse, Monsieur de
Bragelonne?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My horse was killed this morning, my lord, and I am mounted
provisionally on my lackey芒聙聶s.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Choose for yourself in my stables the horse you like best. No false
modesty; take the best horse you can find. You will need it this
evening, perhaps; you will certainly need it to-morrow.芒聙聺

Raoul didn芒聙聶t wait to be told twice; he knew that with superiors,
especially when those superiors are princes, the highest politeness is
to obey without delay or argument; he went down to the stables, picked
out a pie-bald Andalusian horse, saddled and bridled it himself, for
Athos had advised him to trust no one with those important offices at a
time of danger, and went to rejoin the prince, who at that moment
mounted his horse.

芒聙聹Now, monsieur,芒聙聺 he said to Raoul, 芒聙聹will you give me the letter you
have brought?芒聙聺

Raoul handed the letter to the prince.

芒聙聹Keep near me,芒聙聺 said the latter.

The prince threw his bridle over the pommel of the saddle, as he was
wont to do when he wished to have both hands free, unsealed the letter
of Madame de Longueville and started at a gallop on the road to Lens,
attended by Raoul and his small escort, whilst messengers sent to
recall the troops set out with a loose rein in other directions. The
prince read as he hastened on.

芒聙聹Monsieur,芒聙聺 he said, after a moment, 芒聙聹they tell me great things of you.
I have only to say, after the little that I have seen and heard, that I
think even better of you than I have been told.芒聙聺

Raoul bowed.

Meanwhile, as the little troop drew nearer to Lens, the noise of the
cannon sounded louder. The prince kept his gaze fixed in the direction
of the sound with the steadfastness of a bird of prey. One would have
said that his gaze could pierce the branches of trees which limited his
horizon. From time to time his nostrils dilated as if eager for the
smell of powder, and he panted like a horse.

At length they heard the cannon so near that it was evident they were
within a league of the field of battle, and at a turn of the road they
perceived the little village of Aunay.

The peasants were in great commotion. The report of Spanish cruelty had
gone out and every one was frightened. The women had already fled,
taking refuge in Vitry; only a few men remained. On seeing the prince
they hastened to meet him. One of them recognized him.

芒聙聹Ah, my lord,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹have you come to drive away those rascal
Spaniards and those Lorraine robbers?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said the prince, 芒聙聹if you will serve me as guide.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Willingly, my lord. Where does your highness wish to go?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To some elevated spot whence I can look down on Lens and the
surrounding country芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case, I芒聙聶m your man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I can trust you芒聙聰you are a true Frenchman?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am an old soldier of Rocroy, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Here,芒聙聺 said the prince, handing him a purse, 芒聙聹here is for Rocroy. Now,
do you want a horse, or will you go afoot?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Afoot, my lord; I have served always in the infantry. Besides, I
expect to lead your highness into places where you will have to walk.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, then,芒聙聺 said the prince; 芒聙聹let us lose no time.芒聙聺

The peasant started off, running before the prince芒聙聶s horse; then, a
hundred steps from the village, he took a narrow road hidden at the
bottom of the valley. For a half league they proceeded thus, the
cannon-shot sounding so near that they expected at each discharge to
hear the hum of the balls. At length they entered a path which, going
out from the road, skirted the mountainside. The prince dismounted,
ordered one of his aids and Raoul to follow his example, and directed
the others to await his orders, keeping themselves meanwhile on the
alert. He then began to ascend the path.

In about ten minutes they reached the ruins of an old ch脙垄teau; those
ruins crowned the summit of a hill which overlooked the surrounding
country. At a distance of hardly a quarter of a league they looked down
on Lens, at bay, and before Lens the enemy芒聙聶s entire army.

With a single glance the prince took in the extent of country that lay
before him, from Lens as far as Vimy. In a moment the plan of the
battle which on the following day was to save France the second time
from invasion was unrolled in his mind. He took a pencil, tore a page
from his tablets and wrote:

芒聙聹My Dear Marshal,芒聙聰In an hour Lens will be in the enemy芒聙聶s possession.
Come and rejoin me; bring with you the whole army. I shall be at Vendin
to place it in position. To-morrow we shall retake Lens and beat the
enemy.芒聙聺

Then, turning toward Raoul: 芒聙聹Go, monsieur,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹ride fast and
give this letter to Monsieur de Grammont.芒聙聺

Raoul bowed, took the letter, went hastily down the mountain, leaped on
his horse and set out at a gallop. A quarter of an hour later he was
with the marshal.

A portion of the troops had already arrived and the remainder was
expected from moment to moment. Marshal de Grammont put himself at the
head of all the available cavalry and infantry and took the road to
Vendin, leaving the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon to await and bring on the rest.
All the artillery was ready to move, and started off at a moment芒聙聶s
notice.

It was seven o芒聙聶clock in the evening when the marshal arrived at the
appointed place. The prince awaited him there. As he had foreseen, Lens
had fallen into the hands of the enemy immediately after Raoul芒聙聶s
departure. The event was announced by the cessation of the firing.

As the shadows of night deepened the troops summoned by the prince
arrived in successive detachments. Orders were given that no drum
should be beaten, no trumpet sounded.

At nine o芒聙聶clock the night had fully come. Still a last ray of twilight
lighted the plain. The army marched silently, the prince at the head of
the column. Presently the army came in sight of Lens; two or three
houses were in flames and a dull noise was heard which indicated what
suffering was endured by a town taken by assault.

The prince assigned to every one his post. Marshal de Grammont was to
hold the extreme left, resting on Mericourt. The Duc de Ch脙垄tillon
commanded the centre. Finally, the prince led the right wing, resting
on Aunay. The order of battle on the morrow was to be that of the
positions taken in the evening. Each one, on awaking, would find
himself on the field of battle.

The movement was executed in silence and with precision. At ten o芒聙聶clock
every one was in his appointed position; at half-past ten the prince
visited the posts and gave his final orders for the following day.

Three things were especially urged upon the officers, who were to see
that the soldiers observed them scrupulously: the first, that the
different corps should so march that cavalry and infantry should be on
the same line and that each body should protect its gaps; the second,
to go to the charge no faster than a walk; the third, to let the enemy
fire first.

The prince assigned the Count de Guiche to his father and kept
Bragelonne near his own person; but the two young men sought the
privilege of passing the night together and it was accorded them. A
tent was erected for them near that of the marshal.

Although the day had been fatiguing, neither of them was inclined to
sleep. And besides, even for old soldiers the evening before a battle
is a serious time; it was so with greater reason to two young men who
were about to witness for the first time that terrible spectacle. On
the evening before a battle one thinks of a thousand things forgotten
till then; those who are indifferent to one another become friends and
those who are friends become brothers. It need not be said that if in
the depths of the heart there is a sentiment more tender, it reaches
then, quite naturally, the highest exaltation of which it is capable.
Some sentiment of this kind must have been cherished by each one of
these two friends, for each of them almost immediately sat down by
himself at an end of the tent and began to write.

The letters were long芒聙聰the four pages were covered with closely written
words. The writers sometimes looked up at each other and smiled; they
understood without speaking, their organizations were so delicate and
sympathetic. The letters being finished, each put his own into two
envelopes, so that no one, without tearing the first envelope, could
discover to whom the second was addressed; then they drew near to each
other and smilingly exchanged their letters.

芒聙聹In case any evil should happen to me,芒聙聺 said Bragelonne.

芒聙聹In case I should be killed,芒聙聺 said De Guiche.

They then embraced each other like two brothers, and each wrapping
himself in his cloak they soon passed into that kindly sleep of youth
which is the prerogative of birds, flowers and infants.




Chapter XXXV.
A Dinner in the Old Style.


The second interview between the former musketeers was not so formal
and threatening as the first. Athos, with his superior understanding,
wisely deemed that the supper table would be the most complete and
satisfactory point of reunion, and at the moment when his friends, in
deference to his deportment and sobriety, dared scarcely speak of some
of their former good dinners, he was the first to propose that they
should all assemble around some well spread table and abandon
themselves unreservedly to their own natural character and manners芒聙聰a
freedom which had formerly contributed so much to that good
understanding between them which gave them the name of the
inseparables. For different reasons this was an agreeable proposition
to them all, and it was therefore agreed that each should leave a very
exact address and that upon the request of any of the associates a
meeting should be convoked at a famous eating house in the Rue de la
Monnaie, of the sign of the Hermitage. The first rendezvous was fixed
for the following Wednesday, at eight o芒聙聶clock in the evening precisely.

On that day, in fact, the four friends arrived punctually at the hour,
each from his own abode or occupation. Porthos had been trying a new
horse; D芒聙聶Artagnan was on guard at the Louvre; Aramis had been to visit
one of his penitents in the neighborhood; and Athos, whose domicile was
established in the Rue Gu脙漏n脙漏gaud, found himself close at hand. They
were, therefore, somewhat surprised to meet altogether at the door of
the Hermitage, Athos starting out from the Pont Neuf, Porthos by the
Rue de la Roule, D芒聙聶Artagnan by the Rue des Fosse Saint Germain
l芒聙聶Auxerrois, and Aramis by the Rue de Bethisy.

The first words exchanged between the four friends, on account of the
ceremony which each of them mingled with their demonstration, were
somewhat forced and even the repast began with a kind of stiffness.
Athos perceived this embarrassment, and by way of supplying an
effectual remedy, called for four bottles of champagne.

At this order, given in Athos芒聙聶s habitually calm manner, the face of the
Gascon relaxed and Porthos芒聙聶s brow grew smooth. Aramis was astonished.
He knew that Athos not only never drank, but more, that he had a kind
of repugnance to wine. This astonishment was doubled when Aramis saw
Athos fill a bumper and toss it off with all his former enthusiasm. His
companions followed his example. In a very few minutes the four bottles
were empty and this excellent specific succeeded in dissipating even
the slightest cloud that might have rested on their spirits. Now the
four friends began to speak loud, scarcely waiting till one had
finished before another began, and each assumed his favorite attitude
on or at the table. Soon芒聙聰strange fact芒聙聰Aramis undid two buttons of his
doublet, seeing which, Porthos unfastened his entirely.

Battles, long journeys, blows given and received, sufficed for the
first themes of conversation, which turned upon the silent struggles
sustained against him who was now called the great cardinal.

芒聙聹Faith,芒聙聺 said Aramis, laughing, 芒聙聹we have praised the dead enough, let
us revile the living a little; I should like to say something evil of
Mazarin; is it permissible?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go on, go on,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, laughing heartily; 芒聙聹relate your
story and I will applaud it if it is a good one.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A great prince,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹with whom Mazarin sought an alliance,
was invited by him to send him a list of the conditions on which he
would do him the honor to negotiate with him. The prince, who had a
great repugnance to treat with such an ill-bred fellow, made out a
list, against the grain, and sent it. In this list there were three
conditions which displeased Mazarin and he offered the prince ten
thousand crowns to renounce them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, ha, ha!芒聙聺 laughed the three friends, 芒聙聹not a bad bargain; and there
was no fear of being taken at his word; what did the prince do then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The prince immediately sent fifty thousand francs to Mazarin, begging
him never to write to him again, and offered twenty thousand francs
more, on condition that he would never speak to him. What did Mazarin
do?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Stormed!芒聙聺 suggested Athos.

芒聙聹Beat the messenger!芒聙聺 cried Porthos.

芒聙聹Accepted the money!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹You have guessed it,芒聙聺 answered Aramis; and they all laughed so
heartily that the host appeared in order to inquire whether the
gentlemen wanted anything; he thought they were fighting.

At last their hilarity calmed down and:

芒聙聹Faith!芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan to the two friends, 芒聙聹you may well wish
ill to Mazarin; for I assure you, on his side he wishes you no good.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pooh! really?芒聙聺 asked Athos. 芒聙聹If I thought the fellow knew me by my
name I would be rebaptized, for fear it might be thought I knew him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He knows you better by your actions than your name; he is quite aware
that there are two gentlemen who greatly aided the escape of Monsieur
de Beaufort, and he has instigated an active search for them, I can
answer for it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By whom?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By me; and this morning he sent for me to ask me if I had obtained any
information.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what did you reply?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That I had none as yet; but that I was to dine to-day with two
gentlemen, who would be able to give me some.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You told him that?芒聙聺 said Porthos, a broad smile spreading over his
honest face. 芒聙聹Bravo! and you are not afraid of that, Athos?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹it is not the search of Mazarin that I fear.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹tell me a little what you do fear.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing for the present; at least, nothing in good earnest.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And with regard to the past?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹Oh! the past is another thing,芒聙聺 said Athos, sighing; 芒聙聹the past and the
future.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you afraid for your young Raoul?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹one is never killed in a first engagement.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nor in the second,芒聙聺 said Aramis

芒聙聹Nor in the third,芒聙聺 returned Porthos; 芒聙聹and even when one is killed, one
rises again, the proof of which is, that here we are!芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹it is not Raoul about whom I am anxious, for I trust
he will conduct himself like a gentleman; and if he is killed芒聙聰well, he
will die bravely; but hold芒聙聰should such a misfortune happen芒聙聰well芒聙聰芒聙聺 Athos
passed his hand across his pale brow.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹Well, I shall look upon it as an expiation.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I know what you mean.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I, too,芒聙聺 added Aramis; 芒聙聹but you must not think of that, Athos;
what is past, is past.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t understand,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹The affair at Armenti脙篓res,芒聙聺 whispered D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹The affair at Armenti脙篓res?芒聙聺 asked he again.

芒聙聹Milady.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, yes!芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹true, I had forgotten it!芒聙聺

Athos looked at him intently.

芒聙聹You have forgotten it, Porthos?芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Faith! yes, it is so long ago,芒聙聺 answered Porthos.

芒聙聹This affair does not, then, weigh upon your conscience?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faith, no.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you, D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聰I own that when my mind returns to that terrible period I have no
recollection of anything but the rigid corpse of poor Madame
Bonancieux. Yes, yes,芒聙聺 murmured he, 芒聙聹I have often felt regret for the
victim, but never the very slightest remorse for the assassin.芒聙聺

Athos shook his dead doubtfully.

芒聙聹Consider,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹if you admit divine justice and its
participation in the things of this world, that woman was punished by
the will of heaven. We were but the instruments, that is all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But as to free will, Aramis?芒聙聺

芒聙聹How acts the judge? He has a free will, yet he fearlessly condemns.
What does the executioner? He is master of his arm, yet he strikes
without remorse.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The executioner!芒聙聺 muttered Athos, as if arrested by some recollection.

芒聙聹I know that it is terrible,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹but when I reflect that
we have killed English, Rochellais, Spaniards, nay, even French, who
never did us any other harm but to aim at and to miss us, whose only
fault was to cross swords with us and to be unable to ward off our
blows芒聙聰I can, on my honor, find an excuse for my share in the murder of
that woman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As for me,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹now that you have reminded me of it, Athos,
I have the scene again before me, as if I now were there. Milady was
there, as it were, where _you_ sit.芒聙聺 (Athos changed color.) 芒聙聹I芒聙聰I was
where D芒聙聶Artagnan stands. I wore a long sword which cut like a
Damascus芒聙聰you remember it, Aramis for you always called it Balizarde.
Well, I swear to you, all three, that had the executioner of
Bethune芒聙聰was he not of Bethune?芒聙聰yes, egad! of Bethune!芒聙聰not been there, I
would have cut off the head of that infamous being without thinking of
it, or even after thinking of it. She was a most atrocious woman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And then,芒聙聺 said Aramis, with the tone of philosophical indifference
which he had assumed since he had belonged to the church and in which
there was more atheism than confidence in God, 芒聙聹what is the use of
thinking of it all? At the last hour we must confess this action and
God knows better than we can whether it is a crime, a fault, or a
meritorious deed. _I_ repent of it? Egad! no. Upon my honor and by the
holy cross; I only regret it because she was a woman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The most satisfactory part of the matter,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹is that
there remains no trace of it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹She had a son,芒聙聺 observed Athos.

芒聙聹Oh! yes, I know that,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹and you mentioned it to me;
but who knows what has become of him? If the serpent be dead, why not
its brood? Do you think his uncle De Winter would have brought up that
young viper? De Winter probably condemned the son as he had done the
mother.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹woe to De Winter, for the child had done no harm.芒聙聺

芒聙聹May the devil take me, if the child be not dead,芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹There
is so much fog in that detestable country, at least so D芒聙聶Artagnan
declares.芒聙聺

Just as the quaint conclusion reached by Porthos was about to bring
back hilarity to faces now more or less clouded, hasty footsteps were
heard upon the stair and some one knocked at the door.

芒聙聹Come in,芒聙聺 cried Athos.

芒聙聹Please your honors,芒聙聺 said the host, 芒聙聹a person in a great hurry wishes
to speak to one of you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To which of us?芒聙聺 asked all the four friends.

芒聙聹To him who is called the Comte de la F脙篓re.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is I,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and what is the name of the person?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Grimaud.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, turning pale. 芒聙聹Back already! What can have
happened, then, to Bragelonne?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let him enter,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹let him come up.芒聙聺

But Grimaud had already mounted the staircase and was waiting on the
last step; so springing into the room he motioned the host to leave it.
The door being closed, the four friends waited in expectation.
Grimaud芒聙聶s agitation, his pallor, the sweat which covered his face, the
dust which soiled his clothes, all indicated that he was the messenger
of some important and terrible news.

芒聙聹Your honors,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹that woman had a child; that child has become
a man; the tigress had a little one, the tiger has roused himself; he
is ready to spring upon you芒聙聰beware!芒聙聺

Athos glanced around at his friends with a melancholy smile. Porthos
turned to look at his sword, which was hanging on the wall; Aramis
seized his knife; D芒聙聶Artagnan arose.

芒聙聹What do you mean, Grimaud?芒聙聺 he exclaimed.

芒聙聹That Milady芒聙聶s son has left England, that he is in France, on his road
to Paris, if he be not here already.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The devil he is!芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹Are you sure of it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certain,芒聙聺 replied Grimaud.

This announcement was received in silence. Grimaud was so breathless,
so exhausted, that he had fallen back upon a chair. Athos filled a
beaker with champagne and gave it to him.

芒聙聹Well, after all,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹supposing that he lives, that he
comes to Paris; we have seen many other such. Let him come.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 echoed Porthos, glancing affectionately at his sword, still
hanging on the wall; 芒聙聹we can wait for him; let him come.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Moreover, he is but a child,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

Grimaud rose.

芒聙聹A child!芒聙聺 he exclaimed. 芒聙聹Do you know what he has done, this child?
Disguised as a monk he discovered the whole history in confession from
the executioner of Bethune, and having confessed him, after having
learned everything from him, he gave him absolution by planting this
dagger into his heart. See, it is on fire yet with his hot blood, for
it is not thirty hours since it was drawn from the wound.芒聙聺

And Grimaud threw the dagger on the table.

D芒聙聶Artagnan, Porthos and Aramis rose and in one spontaneous motion
rushed to their swords. Athos alone remained seated, calm and
thoughtful.

芒聙聹And you say he is dressed as a monk, Grimaud?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, as an Augustine monk.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What sized man is he?芒聙聺

芒聙聹About my height; thin, pale, with light blue eyes and tawny flaxen
hair.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And he did not see Raoul?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Yes, on the contrary, they met, and it was the viscount himself who
conducted him to the bed of the dying man.芒聙聺

Athos, in his turn, rising without speaking, went and unhooked his
sword.

芒聙聹Heigh, sir,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, trying to laugh, 芒聙聹do you know we look
very much like a flock of silly, mouse-evading women! How is it that
we, four men who have faced armies without blinking, begin to tremble
at the mention of a child?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is true,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹but this child comes in the name of Heaven.芒聙聺

And very soon they left the inn.




Chapter XXXVI.
A Letter from Charles the First.


The reader must now cross the Seine with us and follow us to the door
of the Carmelite Convent in the Rue Saint Jacques. It is eleven o芒聙聶clock
in the morning and the pious sisters have just finished saying mass for
the success of the armies of King Charles I. Leaving the church, a
woman and a young girl dressed in black, the one as a widow and the
other as an orphan, have re-entered their cell.

The woman kneels on a _prie-dieu_ of painted wood and at a short
distance from her stands the young girl, leaning against a chair,
weeping.

The woman must have once been handsome, but traces of sorrow have aged
her. The young girl is lovely and her tears only embellish her; the
lady appears to be about forty years of age, the girl about fourteen.

芒聙聹Oh, God!芒聙聺 prayed the kneeling suppliant, 芒聙聹protect my husband, guard my
son, and take my wretched life instead!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, God!芒聙聺 murmured the girl, 芒聙聹leave me my mother!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your mother can be of no use to you in this world, Henrietta,芒聙聺 said
the lady, turning around. 芒聙聹Your mother has no longer either throne or
husband; she has neither son, money nor friends; the whole world, my
poor child, has abandoned your mother!芒聙聺 And she fell back, weeping,
into her daughter芒聙聶s arms.

芒聙聹Courage, take courage, my dear mother!芒聙聺 said the girl.

芒聙聹Ah! 芒聙聶tis an unfortunate year for kings,芒聙聺 said the mother. 芒聙聹And no one
thinks of us in this country, for each must think about his own
affairs. As long as your brother was with me he kept me up; but he is
gone and can no longer send us news of himself, either to me or to your
father. I have pledged my last jewels, sold your clothes and my own to
pay his servants, who refused to accompany him unless I made this
sacrifice. We are now reduced to live at the expense of these daughters
of Heaven; we are the poor, succored by God.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But why not address yourself to your sister, the queen?芒聙聺 asked the
girl.

芒聙聹Alas! the queen, my sister, is no longer queen, my child. Another
reigns in her name. One day you will be able to understand how all this
is.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, to the king, your nephew. Shall I speak to him? You know
how much he loves me, my mother.

芒聙聹Alas! my nephew is not yet king, and you know Laporte has told us
twenty times that he himself is in need of almost everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then let us pray to Heaven,芒聙聺 said the girl.

The two women who thus knelt in united prayer were the daughter and
grand-daughter of Henry IV., the wife and daughter of Charles I.

They had just finished their double prayer, when a nun softly tapped at
the door of the cell.

芒聙聹Enter, my sister,芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹I trust your majesty will pardon this intrusion on her meditations,
but a foreign lord has arrived from England and waits in the parlor,
demanding the honor of presenting a letter to your majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, a letter! a letter from the king, perhaps. News from your father,
do you hear, Henrietta? And the name of this lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lord de Winter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lord de Winter!芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, 芒聙聹the friend of my husband. Oh,
bid him enter!芒聙聺

And the queen advanced to meet the messenger, whose hand she seized
affectionately, whilst he knelt down and presented a letter to her,
contained in a case of gold.

芒聙聹Ah! my lord!芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹you bring us three things which we have
not seen for a long time. Gold, a devoted friend, and a letter from the
king, our husband and master.芒聙聺

De Winter bowed again, unable to reply from excess of emotion.

On their side the mother and daughter retired into the embrasure of a
window to read eagerly the following letter:

芒聙聹Dear Wife,芒聙聰We have now reached the moment of decision. I have
concentrated here at Naseby camp all the resources Heaven has left me,
and I write to you in haste from thence. Here I await the army of my
rebellious subjects. I am about to struggle for the last time with
them. If victorious, I shall continue the struggle; if beaten, I am
lost. I shall try, in the latter case (alas! in our position, one must
provide for everything), I shall try to gain the coast of France. But
can they, will they receive an unhappy king, who will bring such a sad
story into a country already agitated by civil discord? Your wisdom and
your affection must serve me as guides. The bearer of this letter will
tell you, madame, what I dare not trust to pen and paper and the risks
of transit. He will explain to you the steps that I expect you to
pursue. I charge him also with my blessing for my children and with the
sentiments of my soul for yourself, my dearest sweetheart.芒聙聺

The letter bore the signature, not of 芒聙聹Charles, King,芒聙聺 but of
芒聙聹Charles芒聙聰still king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And let him be no longer king,芒聙聺 cried the queen. 芒聙聹Let him be
conquered, exiled, proscribed, provided he still lives. Alas! in these
days the throne is too dangerous a place for me to wish him to retain
it. But my lord, tell me,芒聙聺 she continued, 芒聙聹hide nothing from me芒聙聰what
is, in truth, the king芒聙聶s position? Is it as hopeless as he thinks?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas! madame, more hopeless than he thinks. His majesty has so good a
heart that he cannot understand hatred; is so loyal that he does not
suspect treason! England is torn in twain by a spirit of disturbance
which, I greatly fear, blood alone can exorcise.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But Lord Montrose,芒聙聺 replied the queen, 芒聙聹I have heard of his great and
rapid successes of battles gained. I heard it said that he was marching
to the frontier to join the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame; but on the frontier he was met by Lesly; he had tried
victory by means of superhuman undertakings. Now victory has abandoned
him. Montrose, beaten at Philiphaugh, was obliged to disperse the
remains of his army and to fly, disguised as a servant. He is at
Bergen, in Norway.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Heaven preserve him!芒聙聺 said the queen. 芒聙聹It is at least a consolation to
know that some who have so often risked their lives for us are safe.
And now, my lord, that I see how hopeless the position of the king is,
tell me with what you are charged on the part of my royal husband.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, madame,芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹the king wishes you to try and
discover the dispositions of the king and queen toward him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas! you know that even now the king is but a child and the queen a
woman weak enough. Here, Monsieur Mazarin is everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Does he desire to play the part in France that Cromwell plays in
England?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, no! He is a subtle, conscienceless Italian, who though he very
likely dreams of crime, dares not commit it; and unlike Cromwell, who
disposes of both Houses, Mazarin has had the queen to support him in
his struggle with the parliament.芒聙聺

芒聙聹More reason, then, he should protect a king pursued by parliament.芒聙聺

The queen shook her head despairingly.

芒聙聹If I judge for myself, my lord,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹the cardinal will do
nothing, and will even, perhaps, act against us. The presence of my
daughter and myself in France is already irksome to him; much more so
would be that of the king. My lord,芒聙聺 added Henrietta, with a melancholy
smile, 芒聙聹it is sad and almost shameful to be obliged to say that we have
passed the winter in the Louvre without money, without linen, almost
without bread, and often not rising from bed because we wanted fire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Horrible!芒聙聺 cried De Winter; 芒聙聹the daughter of Henry IV., and the wife
of King Charles! Wherefore did you not apply, then, madame, to the
first person you saw from us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Such is the hospitality shown to a queen by the minister from whom a
king demands it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But I heard that a marriage between the Prince of Wales and
Mademoiselle d芒聙聶Orl脙漏ans was spoken of,芒聙聺 said De Winter.

芒聙聹Yes, for an instant I hoped it was so. The young people felt a mutual
esteem; but the queen, who at first sanctioned their affection, changed
her mind, and Monsieur, the Duc d芒聙聶Orl脙漏ans, who had encouraged the
familiarity between them, has forbidden his daughter to think any more
about the union. Oh, my lord!芒聙聺 continued the queen, without restraining
her tears, 芒聙聹it is better to fight as the king has done, and to die, as
perhaps he will, than live in beggary like me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Courage, madame! courage! Do not despair! The interests of the French
crown, endangered at this moment, are to discountenance rebellion in a
neighboring nation. Mazarin, as a statesman, will understand the
politic necessity.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you sure,芒聙聺 said the queen doubtfully, 芒聙聹that you have not been
forestalled?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By whom?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By the Joices, the Prinns, the Cromwells?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By a tailor, a coachmaker, a brewer! Ah! I hope, madame, that the
cardinal will not enter into negotiations with such men!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! what is he himself?芒聙聺 asked Madame Henrietta.

芒聙聹But for the honor of the king芒聙聰of the queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, let us hope he will do something for the sake of their honor,芒聙聺
said the queen. 芒聙聹A true friend芒聙聶s eloquence is so powerful, my lord,
that you have reassured me. Give me your hand and let us go to the
minister; and yet,芒聙聺 she added, 芒聙聹suppose he should refuse and that the
king loses the battle?芒聙聺

芒聙聹His majesty will then take refuge in Holland, where I hear his
highness the Prince of Wales now is.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And can his majesty count upon many such subjects as yourself for his
flight?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas! no, madame,芒聙聺 answered De Winter; 芒聙聹but the case is provided for
and I am come to France to seek allies.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Allies!芒聙聺 said the queen, shaking her head.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied De Winter, 芒聙聹provided I can find some of my good old
friends of former times I will answer for anything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come then, my lord,芒聙聺 said the queen, with the painful doubt that is
felt by those who have suffered much; 芒聙聹come, and may Heaven hear you.芒聙聺




Chapter XXXVII.
Cromwell芒聙聶s Letter.


At the very moment when the queen quitted the convent to go to the
Palais Royal, a young man dismounted at the gate of this royal abode
and announced to the guards that he had something of importance to
communicate to Cardinal Mazarin. Although the cardinal was often
tormented by fear, he was more often in need of counsel and
information, and he was therefore sufficiently accessible. The true
difficulty of being admitted was not to be found at the first door, and
even the second was passed easily enough; but at the third watched,
besides the guard and the doorkeepers, the faithful Bernouin, a
Cerberus whom no speech could soften, no wand, even of gold, could
charm.

It was therefore at the third door that those who solicited or were
bidden to an audience underwent their formal interrogatory.

The young man having left his horse tied to the gate in the court,
mounted the great staircase and addressed the guard in the first
chamber.

芒聙聹Cardinal Mazarin?芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Pass on,芒聙聺 replied the guard.

The cavalier entered the second hall, which was guarded by the
musketeers and doorkeepers.

芒聙聹Have you a letter of audience?芒聙聺 asked a porter, advancing to the new
arrival.

芒聙聹I have one, but not one from Cardinal Mazarin.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Enter, and ask for Monsieur Bernouin,芒聙聺 said the porter, opening the
door of the third room. Whether he only held his usual post or whether
it was by accident, Monsieur Bernouin was found standing behind the
door and must have heard all that had passed.

芒聙聹You seek me, sir,芒聙聺 said he. 芒聙聹From whom may the letter be you bear to
his eminence?芒聙聺

芒聙聹From General Oliver Cromwell,芒聙聺 said the new comer. 芒聙聹Be so good as to
mention this name to his eminence and to bring me word whether he will
receive me芒聙聰yes or no.芒聙聺

Saying which, he resumed the proud and sombre bearing peculiar at that
time to Puritans. Bernouin cast an inquisitorial glance at the person
of the young man and entered the cabinet of the cardinal, to whom he
transmitted the messenger芒聙聶s words.

芒聙聹A man bringing a letter from Oliver Cromwell?芒聙聺 said Mazarin. 芒聙聹And what
kind of a man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A genuine Englishman, your eminence. Hair sandy-red芒聙聰more red than
sandy; gray-blue eyes芒聙聰more gray than blue; and for the rest, stiff and
proud.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let him give in his letter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹His eminence asks for the letter,芒聙聺 said Bernouin, passing back into
the ante-chamber.

芒聙聹His eminence cannot see the letter without the bearer of it,芒聙聺 replied
the young man; 芒聙聹but to convince you that I am really the bearer of a
letter, see, here it is; and kindly add,芒聙聺 continued he, 芒聙聹that I am not
a simple messenger, but an envoy extraordinary.芒聙聺

Bernouin re-entered the cabinet, returning in a few seconds. 芒聙聹Enter,
sir,芒聙聺 said he.

The young man appeared on the threshold of the minister芒聙聶s closet, in
one hand holding his hat, in the other the letter. Mazarin rose. 芒聙聹Have
you, sir,芒聙聺 asked he, 芒聙聹a letter accrediting you to me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹There it is, my lord,芒聙聺 said the young man.

Mazarin took the letter and read it thus:

芒聙聹Mr. Mordaunt, one of my secretaries, will remit this letter of
introduction to His Eminence, the Cardinal Mazarin, in Paris. He is
also the bearer of a second confidential epistle for his eminence.

芒聙聹Oliver Cromwell.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well, Monsieur Mordaunt,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹give me this second
letter and sit down.芒聙聺

The young man drew from his pocket a second letter, presented it to the
cardinal, and took his seat. The cardinal, however, did not unseal the
letter at once, but continued to turn it again and again in his hand;
then, in accordance with his usual custom and judging from experience
that few people could hide anything from him when he began to question
them, fixing his eyes upon them at the same time, he thus addressed the
messenger:

芒聙聹You are very young, Monsieur Mordaunt, for this difficult task of
ambassador, in which the oldest diplomatists often fail.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, I am twenty-three years of age; but your eminence is mistaken
in saying that I am young. I am older than your eminence, although I
possess not your wisdom. Years of suffering, in my opinion, count
double, and I have suffered for twenty years.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, yes, I understand,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹want of fortune, perhaps. You
are poor, are you not?芒聙聺 Then he added to himself: 芒聙聹These English
Revolutionists are all beggars and ill-bred.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, I ought to have a fortune of six millions, but it has been
taken from me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are not, then, a man of the people?芒聙聺 said Mazarin, astonished.

芒聙聹If I bore my proper title I should be a lord. If I bore my name you
would have heard one of the most illustrious names of England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is your name, then?芒聙聺 asked Mazarin.

芒聙聹My name is Mordaunt,芒聙聺 replied the young man, bowing.

Mazarin now understood that Cromwell芒聙聶s envoy desired to retain his
incognito. He was silent for an instant, and during that time he
scanned the young man even more attentively than he had done at first.
The messenger was unmoved.

芒聙聹Devil take these Puritans,芒聙聺 said Mazarin aside; 芒聙聹they are carved from
granite.芒聙聺 Then he added aloud, 芒聙聹But you have relations left you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have one remaining. Three times I presented myself to ask his
support and three times he ordered his servants to turn me away.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, _mon Dieu!_ my dear Mr. Mordaunt,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, hoping by a
display of affected pity to catch the young man in a snare, 芒聙聹how
extremely your history interests me! You know not, then, anything of
your birth芒聙聰you have never seen your mother?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord; she came three times, whilst I was a child, to my
nurse芒聙聶s house; I remember the last time she came as well as if it were
to-day.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have a good memory,芒聙聺 said Mazarin.

芒聙聹Oh! yes, my lord,芒聙聺 said the young man, with such peculiar emphasis
that the cardinal felt a shudder run through every vein.

芒聙聹And who brought you up?芒聙聺 he asked again.

芒聙聹A French nurse, who sent me away when I was five years old because no
one paid her for me, telling me the name of a relation of whom she had
heard my mother often speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What became of you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹As I was weeping and begging on the high road, a minister from
Kingston took me in, instructed me in the Calvinistic faith, taught me
all he knew himself and aided me in my researches after my family.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And these researches?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Were fruitless; chance did everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You discovered what had become of your mother?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I learned that she had been assassinated by my relation, aided by four
friends, but I was already aware that I had been robbed of my wealth
and degraded from my nobility by King Charles I.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! I now understand why you are in the service of Cromwell; you hate
the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord, I hate him!芒聙聺 said the young man.

Mazarin marked with surprise the diabolical expression with which the
young man uttered these words. Just as, ordinarily, faces are colored
by blood, his face seemed dyed by hatred and became livid.

芒聙聹Your history is a terrible one, Mr. Mordaunt, and touches me keenly;
but happily for you, you serve an all-powerful master; he ought to aid
you in your search; we have so many means of gaining information.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, to a well-bred dog it is only necessary to show one end of a
track; he is certain to reach the other.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But this relation you mentioned芒聙聰do you wish me to speak to him?芒聙聺 said
Mazarin, who was anxious to make a friend about Cromwell芒聙聶s person.

芒聙聹Thanks, my lord, I will speak to him myself. He will treat me better
the next time I see him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have the means, then, of touching him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have the means of making myself feared.芒聙聺

Mazarin looked at the young man, but at the fire which shot from his
glance he bent his head; then, embarrassed how to continue such a
conversation, he opened Cromwell芒聙聶s letter.

The young man芒聙聶s eyes gradually resumed their dull and glassy appearance
and he fell into a profound reverie. After reading the first lines of
the letter Mazarin gave a side glance at him to see if he was watching
the expression of his face as he read. Observing his indifference, he
shrugged his shoulders, saying:

芒聙聹Send on your business those who do theirs at the same time! Let us see
what this letter contains.芒聙聺

We here present the letter _verbatim:_

芒聙聹_To his Eminence, Monseigneur le Cardinal Mazarini:_

芒聙聹I have wished, monseigneur, to learn your intentions relating to the
existing state of affairs in England. The two kingdoms are so near that
France must be interested in our situation, as we are interested in
that of France. The English are almost of one mind in contending
against the tyranny of Charles and his adherents. Placed by popular
confidence at the head of that movement, I can appreciate better than
any other its significance and its probable results. I am at present in
the midst of war, and am about to deliver a decisive battle against
King Charles. I shall gain it, for the hope of the nation and the
Spirit of the Lord are with me. This battle won by me, the king will
have no further resources in England or in Scotland; and if he is not
captured or killed, he will endeavor to pass over into France to
recruit soldiers and to refurnish himself with arms and money. France
has already received Queen Henrietta, and, unintentionally, doubtless,
has maintained a centre of inextinguishable civil war in my country.
But Madame Henrietta is a daughter of France and was entitled to the
hospitality of France. As to King Charles, the question must be viewed
differently; in receiving and aiding him, France will censure the acts
of the English nation, and thus so essentially harm England, and
especially the well-being of the government, that such a proceeding
will be equivalent to pronounced hostilities.芒聙聺

At this moment Mazarin became very uneasy at the turn which the letter
was taking and paused to glance under his eyes at the young man. The
latter continued in thought. Mazarin resumed his reading:

芒聙聹It is important, therefore, monseigneur, that I should be informed as
to the intentions of France. The interests of that kingdom and those of
England, though taking now diverse directions, are very nearly the
same. England needs tranquillity at home, in order to consummate the
expulsion of her king; France needs tranquillity to establish on solid
foundations the throne of her young monarch. You need, as much as we
do, that interior condition of repose which, thanks to the energy of
our government, we are about to attain.

芒聙聹Your quarrels with the parliament, your noisy dissensions with the
princes, who fight for you to-day and to-morrow will fight against you,
the popular following directed by the coadjutor, President Blancmesnil,
and Councillor Broussel芒聙聰all that disorder, in short, which pervades the
several departments of the state, must lead you to view with uneasiness
the possibility of a foreign war; for in that event England, exalted by
the enthusiasm of new ideas, will ally herself with Spain, already
seeking that alliance. I have therefore believed, monseigneur, knowing
your prudence and your personal relation to the events of the present
time, that you will choose to hold your forces concentrated in the
interior of the French kingdom and leave to her own the new government
of England. That neutrality consists simply in excluding King Charles
from the territory of France and in refraining from helping him芒聙聰a
stranger to your country芒聙聰with arms, with money or with troops.

芒聙聹My letter is private and confidential, and for that reason I send it
to you by a man who shares my most intimate counsels. It anticipates,
through a sentiment which your eminence will appreciate, measures to be
taken after the events. Oliver Cromwell considered it more expedient to
declare himself to a mind as intelligent as Mazarin芒聙聶s than to a queen
admirable for firmness, without doubt, but too much guided by vain
prejudices of birth and of divine right.

芒聙聹Farewell, monseigneur; should I not receive a reply in the space of
fifteen days, I shall presume my letter will have miscarried.

芒聙聹Oliver Cromwell.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Mr. Mordaunt,芒聙聺 said the cardinal, raising his voice, as if to arouse
the dreamer, 芒聙聹my reply to this letter will be more satisfactory to
General Cromwell if I am convinced that all are ignorant of my having
given one; go, therefore, and await it at Boulogne-sur-Mer, and promise
me to set out to-morrow morning.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I promise, my lord,芒聙聺 replied Mordaunt; 芒聙聹but how many days does your
eminence expect me to await your reply?芒聙聺

芒聙聹If you do not receive it in ten days you can leave.芒聙聺

Mordaunt bowed.

芒聙聹That is not all, sir,芒聙聺 continued Mazarin; 芒聙聹your private adventures
have touched me to the quick; besides, the letter from Mr. Cromwell
makes you an important person as ambassador; come, tell me, what can I
do for you?芒聙聺

Mordaunt reflected a moment and, after some hesitation, was about to
speak, when Bernouin entered hastily and bending down to the ear of the
cardinal, whispered:

芒聙聹My lord, the Queen Henrietta Maria, accompanied by an English noble,
is entering the Palais Royal at this moment.芒聙聺

Mazarin made a bound from his chair, which did not escape the attention
of the young man and suppressed the confidence he was about to make.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said the cardinal, 芒聙聹you have heard me? I fix on Boulogne because
I presume that every town in France is indifferent to you; if you
prefer another, name it; but you can easily conceive that, surrounded
as I am by influences I can only muzzle by discretion, I desire your
presence in Paris to be unknown.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I go, sir,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, advancing a few steps to the door by which
he had entered.

芒聙聹No, not that way, I beg, sir,芒聙聺 quickly exclaimed the cardinal, 芒聙聹be so
good as to pass by yonder gallery, by which you can regain the hall. I
do not wish you to be seen leaving; our interview must be kept secret.芒聙聺

Mordaunt followed Bernouin, who led him through the adjacent chamber
and left him with a doorkeeper, showing him the way out.




Chapter XXXVIII.
Henrietta Maria and Mazarin.


The cardinal rose, and advanced in haste to receive the queen of
England. He showed the more respect to this queen, deprived of every
mark of pomp and stripped of followers, as he felt some self-reproach
for his own want of heart and his avarice. But supplicants for favor
know how to accommodate the expression of their features, and the
daughter of Henry IV. smiled as she advanced to meet a man she hated
and despised.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Mazarin to himself, 芒聙聹what a sweet face; does she come to
borrow money of me?芒聙聺

And he threw an uneasy glance at his strong box; he even turned inside
the bevel of the magnificent diamond ring, the brilliancy of which drew
every eye upon his hand, which indeed was white and handsome.

芒聙聹Your eminence,芒聙聺 said the august visitor, 芒聙聹it was my first intention to
speak of the matters that have brought me here to the queen, my sister,
but I have reflected that political affairs are more especially the
concern of men.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹your majesty overwhelms me with flattering
distinction.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is very gracious,芒聙聺 thought the queen; 芒聙聹can he have guessed my
errand?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Give,芒聙聺 continued the cardinal, 芒聙聹your commands to the most respectful
of your servants.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas, sir,芒聙聺 replied the queen, 芒聙聹I have lost the habit of commanding
and have adopted instead that of making petitions. I am here to
petition you, too happy should my prayer be favorably heard.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am listening, madame, with the greatest interest,芒聙聺 said Mazarin.

芒聙聹Your eminence, it concerns the war which the king, my husband, is now
sustaining against his rebellious subjects. You are perhaps ignorant
that they are fighting in England,芒聙聺 added she, with a melancholy smile,
芒聙聹and that in a short time they will fight in a much more decided
fashion than they have done hitherto.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am completely ignorant of it, madame,芒聙聺 said the cardinal,
accompanying his words with a slight shrug of the shoulders; 芒聙聹alas, our
own wars quite absorb the time and the mind of a poor, incapable,
infirm old minister like me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, your eminence,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹I must inform you that
Charles I., my husband, is on the eve of a decisive engagement. In case
of a check芒聙聺 (Mazarin made a slight movement), 芒聙聹one must foresee
everything; in the case of a check, he desires to retire into France
and to live here as a private individual. What do you say to this
project?芒聙聺

The cardinal had listened without permitting a single fibre of his face
to betray what he felt, and his smile remained as it ever was芒聙聰false and
flattering; and when the queen finished speaking, he said:

芒聙聹Do you think, madame, that France, agitated and disturbed as it is,
would be a safe retreat for a dethroned king? How will the crown, which
is scarce firmly set on the head of Louis XIV., support a double
weight?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The weight was not so heavy when I was in peril,芒聙聺 interrupted the
queen, with a sad smile, 芒聙聹and I ask no more for my husband than has
been done for me; you see that we are very humble monarchs, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, you, madame,芒聙聺 the cardinal hastened to say, in order to cut short
the explanation he foresaw was coming, 芒聙聹with regard to you, that is
another thing. A daughter of Henry IV., of that great, that sublime
sovereign芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹All which does not prevent you refusing hospitality to his son-in-law,
sir! Nevertheless, you ought to remember that that great, that sublime
monarch, when proscribed at one time, as my husband may be, demanded
aid from England and England accorded it to him; and it is but just to
say that Queen Elizabeth was not his niece.芒聙聺

芒聙聹_Peccato!_芒聙聺 said Mazarin, writhing beneath this simple eloquence,
芒聙聹your majesty does not understand me; you judge my intentions wrongly,
and that is partly because, doubtless, I explain myself in French.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Speak Italian, sir. Ere the cardinal, your predecessor, sent our
mother, Marie de M脙漏dicis, to die in exile, she taught us that language.
If anything yet remains of that great, that sublime king, Henry, of
whom you have just spoken, he would be much surprised at so little pity
for his family being united to such a profound admiration of himself.芒聙聺

The perspiration stood in large drops on Mazarin芒聙聶s brow.

芒聙聹That admiration is, on the contrary, so great, so real, madame,芒聙聺
returned Mazarin, without noticing the change of language offered to
him by the queen, 芒聙聹that if the king, Charles I.芒聙聰whom Heaven protect
from evil!芒聙聰came into France, I would offer him my house芒聙聰my own house;
but, alas! it would be but an unsafe retreat. Some day the people will
burn that house, as they burned that of the Mar脙漏chal d芒聙聶Ancre. Poor
Concino Concini! And yet he but desired the good of the people.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord, like yourself!芒聙聺 said the queen, ironically.

Mazarin pretended not to understand the double meaning of his own
sentence, but continued to compassionate the fate of Concino Concini.

芒聙聹Well then, your eminence,芒聙聺 said the queen, becoming impatient, 芒聙聹what
is your answer?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 cried Mazarin, more and more moved, 芒聙聹will your majesty permit
me to give you counsel?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Speak, sir,芒聙聺 replied the queen; 芒聙聹the counsels of so prudent a man as
yourself ought certainly to be available.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, believe me, the king ought to defend himself to the last.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He has done so, sir, and this last battle, which he encounters with
resources much inferior to those of the enemy, proves that he will not
yield without a struggle; but in case he is beaten?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, madame, in that case, my advice芒聙聰I know that I am very bold to
offer advice to your majesty芒聙聰my advice is that the king should not
leave his kingdom. Absent kings are very soon forgotten; if he passes
over into France his cause is lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 persisted the queen, 芒聙聹if such be your advice and you have his
interest at heart, send him help of men and money, for I can do nothing
for him; I have sold even to my last diamond to aid him. If I had had a
single ornament left, I should have bought wood this winter to make a
fire for my daughter and myself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, madame,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹your majesty knows not what you ask. On
the day when foreign succor follows in the train of a king to replace
him on his throne, it is an avowal that he no longer possesses the help
and love of his own subjects.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the point, sir,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹to the point, and answer me, yes
or no; if the king persists in remaining in England will you send him
succor? If he comes to France will you accord him hospitality? What do
you intend to do? Speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said the cardinal, affecting an effusive frankness of speech,
芒聙聹I shall convince your majesty, I trust, of my devotion to you and my
desire to terminate an affair which you have so much at heart. After
which your majesty will, I think, no longer doubt my zeal in your
behalf.芒聙聺

The queen bit her lips and moved impatiently on her chair.

芒聙聹Well, what do you propose to do?芒聙聺 she, said at length; 芒聙聹come, speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will go this instant and consult the queen, and we will refer the
affair at once to parliament.芒聙聺

芒聙聹With which you are at war芒聙聰is it not so? You will charge Broussel to
report it. Enough, sir, enough. I understand you or rather, I am wrong.
Go to the parliament, for it was from this parliament, the enemy of
monarchs, that the daughter of the great, the sublime Henry IV., whom
you so much admire, received the only relief this winter which
prevented her from dying of hunger and cold!芒聙聺

And with these words Henrietta rose in majestic indignation, whilst the
cardinal, raising his hands clasped toward her, exclaimed, 芒聙聹Ah, madame,
madame, how little you know me, _mon Dieu!_芒聙聺

But Queen Henrietta, without even turning toward him who made these
hypocritical pretensions, crossed the cabinet, opened the door for
herself and passing through the midst of the cardinal芒聙聶s numerous
guards, courtiers eager to pay homage, the luxurious show of a
competing royalty, she went and took the hand of De Winter, who stood
apart in isolation. Poor queen, already fallen! Though all bowed before
her, as etiquette required, she had now but a single arm on which she
could lean.

芒聙聹It signifies little,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, when he was alone. 芒聙聹It gave me
pain and it was an ungracious part to play, but I have said nothing
either to the one or to the other. Bernouin!芒聙聺

Bernouin entered.

芒聙聹See if the young man with the black doublet and the short hair, who
was with me just now, is still in the palace.芒聙聺

Bernouin went out and soon returned with Comminges, who was on guard.

芒聙聹Your eminence,芒聙聺 said Comminges, 芒聙聹as I was re-conducting the young man
for whom you have asked, he approached the glass door of the gallery,
and gazed intently upon some object, doubtless the picture by Raphael,
which is opposite the door. He reflected for a second and then
descended the stairs. I believe I saw him mount a gray horse and leave
the palace court. But is not your eminence going to the queen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For what purpose?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Guitant, my uncle, has just told me that her majesty had
received news of the army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is well; I will go.芒聙聺

Comminges had seen rightly, and Mordaunt had really acted as he had
related. In crossing the gallery parallel to the large glass gallery,
he perceived De Winter, who was waiting until the queen had finished
her negotiation.

At this sight the young man stopped short, not in admiration of
Raphael芒聙聶s picture, but as if fascinated at the sight of some terrible
object. His eyes dilated and a shudder ran through his body. One would
have said that he longed to break through the wall of glass which
separated him from his enemy; for if Comminges had seen with what an
expression of hatred the eyes of this young man were fixed upon De
Winter, he would not have doubted for an instant that the Englishman
was his eternal foe.

But he stopped, doubtless to reflect; for instead of allowing his first
impulse, which had been to go straight to Lord de Winter, to carry him
away, he leisurely descended the staircase, left the palace with his
head down, mounted his horse, which he reined in at the corner of the
Rue Richelieu, and with his eyes fixed on the gate, waited until the
queen芒聙聶s carriage had left the court.

He had not long to wait, for the queen scarcely remained a quarter of
an hour with Mazarin, but this quarter of an hour of expectation
appeared a century to him. At last the heavy machine, which was called
a chariot in those days, came out, rumbling against the gates, and De
Winter, still on horseback, bent again to the door to converse with her
majesty.

The horses started on a trot and took the road to the Louvre, which
they entered. Before leaving the convent of the Carmelites, Henrietta
had desired her daughter to attend her at the palace, which she had
inhabited for a long time and which she had only left because their
poverty seemed to them more difficult to bear in gilded chambers.

Mordaunt followed the carriage, and when he had watched it drive
beneath the sombre arches he went and stationed himself under a wall
over which the shadow was extended, and remained motionless, amidst the
moldings of Jean Goujon, like a _bas-relievo_, representing an
equestrian statue.




Chapter XXXIX.
How, sometimes, the Unhappy mistake Chance for Providence.


Well, madame,芒聙聺 said De Winter, when the queen had dismissed her
attendants.

芒聙聹Well, my lord, what I foresaw has come to pass.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What? does the cardinal refuse to receive the king? France refuse
hospitality to an unfortunate prince? Ay, but it is for the first time,
madame!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I did not say France, my lord; I said the cardinal, and the cardinal
is not even a Frenchman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But did you see the queen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is useless,芒聙聺 replied Henrietta, 芒聙聹the queen will not say yes when
the cardinal says no. Are you not aware that this Italian directs
everything, both indoors and out? And moreover, I should not be
surprised had we been forestalled by Cromwell. He was embarrassed
whilst speaking to me and yet quite firm in his determination to
refuse. Then did you not observe the agitation in the Palais Royal, the
passing to and fro of busy people? Can they have received any news, my
lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not from England, madame. I made such haste that I am certain of not
having been forestalled. I set out three days ago, passing miraculously
through the Puritan army, and I took post horses with my servant Tony;
the horses upon which we were mounted were bought in Paris. Besides,
the king, I am certain, awaits your majesty芒聙聶s reply before risking
anything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You will tell him, my lord,芒聙聺 resumed the queen, despairingly, 芒聙聹that I
can do nothing; that I have suffered as much as himself芒聙聰more than he
has芒聙聰obliged as I am to eat the bread of exile and to ask hospitality
from false friends who smile at my tears; and as regards his royal
person, he must sacrifice it generously and die like a king. I shall go
and die by his side.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, madame,芒聙聺 exclaimed De Winter, 芒聙聹your majesty abandons yourself
to despair; and yet, perhaps, there still remains some hope.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No friends left, my lord; no other friends left in the wide world but
yourself! Oh, God!芒聙聺 exclaimed the poor queen, raising her eyes to
Heaven, 芒聙聹have You indeed taken back all the generous hearts that once
existed in the world?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope not, madame,芒聙聺 replied De Winter, thoughtfully; 芒聙聹I once spoke to
you of four men.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What can be done with four?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Four devoted, resolute men can do much, assure yourself, madame; and
those of whom I speak performed great things at one time.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And where are these four men?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, that is what I do not know. It is twenty years since I saw them,
and yet whenever I have seen the king in danger I have thought of
them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And these men were your friends?芒聙聺

芒聙聹One of them held my life in his hands and gave it to me. I know not
whether he is still my friend, but since that time I have remained
his.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And these men are in France, my lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I believe so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell me their names; perhaps I may have heard them mentioned and might
be able to aid you in finding them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹One of them was called the Chevalier d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, my lord, if I mistake not, the Chevalier d芒聙聶Artagnan is lieutenant
of royal guards; but take care, for I fear that this man is entirely
devoted to the cardinal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That would be a misfortune,芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹and I shall begin to
think that we are really doomed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But the others,芒聙聺 said the queen, who clung to this last hope as a
shipwrecked man clings to the hull of his vessel. 芒聙聹The others, my
lord!芒聙聺

芒聙聹The second芒聙聰I heard his name by chance; for before fighting us, these
four gentlemen told us their names; the second was called the Comte de
la F脙篓re. As for the two others, I had so much the habit of calling them
by nicknames that I have forgotten their real ones.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, _mon Dieu_, it is a matter of the greatest urgency to find them
out,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹since you think these worthy gentlemen might be
so useful to the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, yes,芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹for they are the same men. Listen, madame,
and recall your remembrances. Have you never heard that Queen Anne of
Austria was once saved from the greatest danger ever incurred by a
queen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, at the time of her relations with Monsieur de Buckingham; it had
to do in some way with certain studs and diamonds.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, it was that affair, madame; these men are the ones who saved
her; and I smile with pity when I reflect that if the names of those
gentlemen are unknown to you it is because the queen has forgotten
them, who ought to have made them the first noblemen of the realm.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, my lord, they must be found; but what can four men, or
rather three men do芒聙聰for I tell you, you must not count on Monsieur
d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It will be one valiant sword the less, but there will remain still
three, without reckoning my own; now four devoted men around the king
to protect him from his enemies, to be at his side in battle, to aid
him with counsel, to escort him in flight, are sufficient, not to make
the king a conqueror, but to save him if conquered; and whatever
Mazarin may say, once on the shores of France your royal husband may
find as many retreats and asylums as the seabird finds in a storm.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Seek, then, my lord, seek these gentlemen; and if they will consent to
go with you to England, I will give to each a duchy the day that we
reascend the throne, besides as much gold as would pave Whitehall. Seek
them, my lord, and find them, I conjure you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will search for them, madame,芒聙聺 said De Winter 芒聙聹and doubtless I shall
find them; but time fails me. Has your majesty forgotten that the king
expects your reply and awaits it in agony?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then indeed we are lost!芒聙聺 cried the queen, in the fullness of a broken
heart.

At this moment the door opened and the young Henrietta appeared; then
the queen, with that wonderful strength which is the privilege of
parents, repressed her tears and motioned to De Winter to change the
subject.

But that act of self-control, effective as it was, did not escape the
eyes of the young princess. She stopped on the threshold, breathed a
sigh, and addressing the queen:

芒聙聹Why, then, do you always weep, mother, when I am away from you?芒聙聺 she
said.

The queen smiled, but instead of answering:

芒聙聹See, De Winter,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹I have at least gained one thing in being
only half a queen; and that is that my children call me 芒聙聵mother芒聙聶
instead of 芒聙聵madame.芒聙聶芒聙聺

Then turning toward her daughter:

芒聙聹What do you want, Henrietta?芒聙聺 she demanded.

芒聙聹My mother,芒聙聺 replied the young princess, 芒聙聹a cavalier has just entered
the Louvre and wishes to present his respects to your majesty; he
arrives from the army and has, he says, a letter to remit to you, on
the part of the Mar脙漏chal de Grammont, I think.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said the queen to De Winter, 芒聙聹he is one of my faithful adherents;
but do you not observe, my dear lord, that we are so poorly served that
it is left to my daughter to fill the office of doorkeeper?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, have pity on me,芒聙聺 exclaimed De Winter; 芒聙聹you wring my heart!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who is this cavalier, Henrietta?芒聙聺 asked the queen.

芒聙聹I saw him from the window, madame; he is a young man that appears
scarce sixteen years of age, and is called the Viscount de Bragelonne.芒聙聺

The queen, smiling, made a sign with her head; the young princess
opened the door and Raoul appeared on the threshold.

Advancing a few steps toward the queen, he knelt down.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹I bear to your majesty a letter from my friend the
Count de Guiche, who told me he had the honor of being your servant;
this letter contains important news and the expression of his respect.芒聙聺

At the name of the Count de Guiche a blush spread over the cheeks of
the young princess and the queen glanced at her with some degree of
severity.

芒聙聹You told me that the letter was from the Mar脙漏chal de Grammont,
Henrietta!芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹I thought so, madame,芒聙聺 stammered the young girl.

芒聙聹It is my fault, madame,芒聙聺 said Raoul. 芒聙聹I did announce myself, in truth,
as coming on the part of the Mar脙漏chal de Grammont; but being wounded in
the right arm he was unable to write and therefore the Count de Guiche
acted as his secretary.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There has been fighting, then?芒聙聺 asked the queen, motioning to Raoul to
rise.

芒聙聹Yes, madame,芒聙聺 said the young man.

At this announcement of a battle having taken place, the princess
opened her mouth as though to ask a question of interest; but her lips
closed again without articulating a word, while the color gradually
faded from her cheeks.

The queen saw this, and doubtless her maternal heart translated the
emotion, for addressing Raoul again:

芒聙聹And no evil has happened to the young Count de Guiche?芒聙聺 she asked;
芒聙聹for not only is he our servant, as you say, sir, but more芒聙聰he is one of
our friends.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, madame,芒聙聺 replied Raoul; 芒聙聹on the contrary, he gained great glory
and had the honor of being embraced by his highness, the prince, on the
field of battle.芒聙聺

The young princess clapped her hands; and then, ashamed of having been
betrayed into such a demonstration of joy, she half turned away and
bent over a vase of roses, as if to inhale their odor.

芒聙聹Let us see,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹what the count says.芒聙聺 And she opened the
letter and read:

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聰Being unable to have the honor of writing to you myself, by
reason of a wound I have received in my right hand, I have commanded my
son, the Count de Guiche, who, with his father, is equally your humble
servant, to write to tell you that we have just gained the battle of
Lens, and that this victory cannot fail to give great power to Cardinal
Mazarin and to the queen over the affairs of Europe. If her majesty
will have faith in my counsels she ought to profit by this event to
address at this moment, in favor of her august husband, the court of
France. The Vicomte de Bragelonne, who will have the honor of remitting
this letter to your majesty, is the friend of my son, who owes to him
his life; he is a gentleman in whom your majesty may confide entirely,
in case your majesty may have some verbal or written order to remit to
me.

芒聙聹I have the honor to be, with respect, etc.,

芒聙聹Mar脙漏chal de Grammont.芒聙聺

At the moment mention occurred of his having rendered a service to the
count, Raoul could not help turning his glance toward the young
princess, and then he saw in her eyes an expression of infinite
gratitude to the young man; he no longer doubted that the daughter of
King Charles I. loved his friend.

芒聙聹The battle of Lens gained!芒聙聺 said the queen; 芒聙聹they are lucky here
indeed; they can gain battles! Yes, the Mar脙漏chal de Grammont is right;
this will change the aspect of French affairs, but I much fear it will
do nothing for English, even if it does not harm them. This is recent
news, sir,芒聙聺 continued she, 芒聙聹and I thank you for having made such haste
to bring it to me; without this letter I should not have heard till
to-morrow, perhaps after to-morrow芒聙聰the last of all Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹the Louvre is but the second palace this news
has reached; it is as yet unknown to all, and I had sworn to the Count
de Guiche to remit this letter to your majesty before even I should
embrace my guardian.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your guardian! is he, too, a Bragelonne?芒聙聺 asked Lord de Winter. 芒聙聹I
once knew a Bragelonne芒聙聰is he still alive?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, he is dead; and I believe it is from him my guardian, whose
near relation he was, inherited the estate from which I take my name.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And your guardian, sir,芒聙聺 asked the queen, who could not help feeling
some interest in the handsome young man before her, 芒聙聹what is his name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re, madame,芒聙聺 replied the young man, bowing.
