De Winter made a gesture of surprise and the queen turned to him with a
start of joy.

芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re!芒聙聺 she cried. 芒聙聹Have you not mentioned that name to
me?芒聙聺

As for De Winter he could scarcely believe that he had heard aright.
芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re!芒聙聺 he cried in his turn. 芒聙聹Oh, sir, reply, I
entreat you芒聙聰is not the Comte de la F脙篓re a noble whom I remember,
handsome and brave, a musketeer under Louis XIII., who must be now
about forty-seven or forty-eight years of age?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir, you are right in every particular!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who served under an assumed name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Under the name of Athos. Latterly I heard his friend, Monsieur
d芒聙聶Artagnan, give him that name.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is it, madame, that is the same. God be praised! And he is in
Paris?芒聙聺 continued he, addressing Raoul; then turning to the queen: 芒聙聹We
may still hope. Providence has declared for us, since I have found this
brave man again in so miraculous a manner. And, sir, where does he
reside, pray?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re lodges in the Rue Gu脙漏n脙漏gaud, Hotel du Grand Roi
Charlemagne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thanks, sir. Inform this dear friend that he may remain within, that I
shall go and see him immediately.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, I obey with pleasure, if her majesty will permit me to depart.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, Monsieur de Bragelonne,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹and rest assured of our
affection.芒聙聺

Raoul bent respectfully before the two princesses, and bowing to De
Winter, departed.

The queen and De Winter continued to converse for some time in low
voices, in order that the young princess should not overhear them; but
the precaution was needless: she was in deep converse with her own
thoughts.

Then, when De Winter rose to take leave:

芒聙聹Listen, my lord,芒聙聺 said the queen; 芒聙聹I have preserved this diamond cross
which came from my mother, and this order of St. Michael which came
from my husband. They are worth about fifty thousand pounds. I had
sworn to die of hunger rather than part with these precious pledges;
but now that this ornament may be useful to him or his defenders,
everything must be sacrificed. Take them, and if you need money for
your expedition, sell them fearlessly, my lord. But should you find the
means of retaining them, remember, my lord, that I shall esteem you as
having rendered the greatest service that a gentleman can render to a
queen; and in the day of my prosperity he who brings me this order and
this cross shall be blessed by me and my children.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied De Winter, 芒聙聹your majesty will be served by a man
devoted to you. I hasten to deposit these two objects in a safe place,
nor should I accept them if the resources of our ancient fortune were
left to us, but our estates are confiscated, our ready money is
exhausted, and we are reduced to turn to service everything we possess.
In an hour hence I shall be with the Comte de la F脙篓re, and to-morrow
your majesty shall have a definite reply.芒聙聺

The queen tendered her hand to Lord de Winter, who, kissing it
respectfully, went out and traversed alone and unconducted those large,
dark and deserted apartments, brushing away tears which, blas脙漏 as he
was by fifty years spent as a courtier, he could not withhold at the
spectacle of royal distress so dignified, yet so intense.




Chapter XL.
Uncle and Nephew.


The horse and servant belonging to De Winter were waiting for him at
the door; he proceeded toward his abode very thoughtfully, looking
behind him from time to him to contemplate the dark and silent frontage
of the Louvre. It was then that he saw a horseman, as it were, detach
himself from the wall and follow him at a little distance. In leaving
the Palais Royal he remembered to have observed a similar shadow.

芒聙聹Tony,芒聙聺 he said, motioning to his groom to approach.

芒聙聹Here I am, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Did you remark that man who is following us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who is he?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not know, only he has followed your grace from the Palais Royal,
stopped at the Louvre to wait for you, and now leaves the Louvre with
you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Some spy of the cardinal,芒聙聺 said De Winter to him, aside. 芒聙聹Let us
pretend not to notice that he is watching us.芒聙聺

And spurring on he plunged into the labyrinth of streets which led to
his hotel, situated near the Marais, for having for so long a time
lived near the Place Royale, Lord de Winter naturally returned to lodge
near his ancient dwelling.

The unknown spurred his horse to a gallop.

De Winter dismounted at his hotel and went up into his apartment,
intending to watch the spy; but as he was about to place his gloves and
hat on a table, he saw reflected in a glass opposite to him a figure
which stood on the threshold of the room. He turned around and Mordaunt
stood before him.

There was a moment of frozen silence between these two.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹I thought I had already made you aware that I
am weary of this persecution; withdraw, then, or I shall call and have
you turned out as you were in London. I am not your uncle, I know you
not.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My uncle,芒聙聺 replied Mordaunt, with his harsh and bantering tone, 芒聙聹you
are mistaken; you will not have me turned out this time as you did in
London芒聙聰you dare not. As for denying that I am your nephew, you will
think twice about it, now that I have learned some things of which I
was ignorant a year ago.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And how does it concern me what you have learned?芒聙聺 said De Winter.

芒聙聹Oh, it concerns you very closely, my uncle, I am sure, and you will
soon be of my opinion,芒聙聺 added he, with a smile which sent a shudder
through the veins of him he thus addressed. 芒聙聹When I presented myself
before you for the first time in London, it was to ask you what had
become of my fortune; the second time it was to demand who had sullied
my name; and this time I come before you to ask a question far more
terrible than any other, to say to you as God said to the first
murderer: 芒聙聵Cain, what hast thou done to thy brother Abel?芒聙聶 My lord,
what have you done with your sister芒聙聰your sister, who was my mother?芒聙聺

De Winter shrank back from the fire of those scorching eyes.

芒聙聹Your mother?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Yes, my lord, my mother,芒聙聺 replied the young man, advancing into the
room until he was face to face with Lord de Winter, and crossing his
arms. 芒聙聹I have asked the headsman of Bethune,芒聙聺 he said, his voice hoarse
and his face livid with passion and grief. 芒聙聹And the headsman of Bethune
gave me a reply.芒聙聺

De Winter fell back in a chair as though struck by a thunderbolt and in
vain attempted a reply.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 continued the young man; 芒聙聹all is now explained; with this key I
open the abyss. My mother inherited an estate from her husband, you
have assassinated her; my name would have secured me the paternal
estate, you have deprived me of it; you have despoiled me of my
fortune. I am no longer astonished that you knew me not. I am not
surprised that you refused to recognize me. When a man is a robber it
is hard to call him nephew whom he has impoverished; when one is a
murderer, to recognize the man whom one has made an orphan.芒聙聺

These words produced a contrary effect to that which Mordaunt had
anticipated. De Winter remembered the monster that Milady had been; he
rose, dignified and calm, restraining by the severity of his look the
wild glance of the young man.

芒聙聹You desire to fathom this horrible secret?芒聙聺 said De Winter; 芒聙聹well,
then, so be it. Know, then, what manner of woman it was for whom to-day
you call me to account. That woman had, in all probability, poisoned my
brother, and in order to inherit from me she was about to assassinate
me in my turn. I have proof of it. What say you to that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I say that she was my mother.芒聙聺

芒聙聹She caused the unfortunate Duke of Buckingham to be stabbed by a man
who was, ere that, honest, good and pure. What say you to that crime,
of which I have the proof?芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was my mother.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On our return to France she had a young woman who was attached to one
of her opponents poisoned in the convent of the Augustines at Bethune.
Will this crime persuade you of the justice of her punishment芒聙聰for of
all this I have the proofs?芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was my mother!芒聙聺 cried the young man, who uttered these three
successive exclamations with constantly increasing force.

芒聙聹At last, charged with murders, with debauchery, hated by every one and
yet threatening still, like a panther thirsting for blood, she fell
under the blows of men whom she had rendered desperate, though they had
never done her the least injury; she met with judges whom her hideous
crimes had evoked; and that executioner you saw芒聙聰that executioner who
you say told you everything芒聙聰that executioner, if he told you
everything, told you that he leaped with joy in avenging on her his
brother芒聙聶s shame and suicide. Depraved as a girl, adulterous as a wife,
an unnatural sister, homicide, poisoner, execrated by all who knew her,
by every nation that had been visited by her, she died accursed by
Heaven and earth.芒聙聺

A sob which Mordaunt could not repress burst from his throat and his
livid face became suffused with blood; he clenched his fists, sweat
covered his face, his hair, like Hamlet芒聙聶s, stood on end, and racked
with fury he cried out:

芒聙聹Silence, sir! she was my mother! Her crimes, I know them not; her
disorders, I know them not; her vices, I know them not. But this I
know, that I had a mother, that five men leagued against one woman,
murdered her clandestinely by night芒聙聰silently芒聙聰like cowards. I know that
you were one of them, my uncle, and that you cried louder than the
others: 芒聙聵She must die.芒聙聶 Therefore I warn you, and listen well to my
words, that they may be engraved upon your memory, never to be
forgotten: this murder, which has robbed me of everything芒聙聰this murder,
which has deprived me of my name芒聙聰this murder, which has impoverished
me芒聙聰this murder, which has made me corrupt, wicked, implacable芒聙聰I shall
summon you to account for it first and then those who were your
accomplices, when I discover them!芒聙聺

With hatred in his eyes, foaming at his mouth, and his fist extended,
Mordaunt had advanced one more step, a threatening, terrible step,
toward De Winter. The latter put his hand to his sword, and said, with
the smile of a man who for thirty years has jested with death:

芒聙聹Would you assassinate me, sir? _Then_ I shall recognize you as my
nephew, for you would be a worthy son of such a mother.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied Mordaunt, forcing his features and the muscles of his
body to resume their usual places and be calm; 芒聙聹no, I shall not kill
you; at least not at this moment, for without you I could not discover
the others. But when I have found them, then tremble, sir. I stabbed to
the heart the headsman of Bethune, without mercy or pity, and he was
the least guilty of you all.芒聙聺

With these words the young man went out and descended the stairs with
sufficient calmness to pass unobserved; then upon the lowest landing
place he passed Tony, leaning over the balustrade, waiting only for a
call from his master to mount to his room.

But De Winter did not call; crushed, enfeebled, he remained standing
and with listening ear; then only when he had heard the step of the
horse going away he fell back on a chair, saying:

芒聙聹My God, I thank Thee that he knows me only.芒聙聺




Chapter XLI.
Paternal Affection.


Whilst this terrible scene was passing at Lord de Winter芒聙聶s, Athos,
seated near his window, his elbow on the table and his head supported
on his hand, was listening intently to Raoul芒聙聶s account of the
adventures he met with on his journey and the details of the battle.

Listening to the relation of those emotions so fresh and pure, the
fine, noble face of Athos betrayed indescribable pleasure; he inhaled
the tones of that young voice, as harmonious music. He forgot all that
was dark in the past and that was cloudy in the future. It almost
seemed as if the return of this much loved boy had changed his fears to
hopes. Athos was happy芒聙聰happy as he had never been before.

芒聙聹And you assisted and took part in this great battle, Bragelonne!芒聙聺
cried the former musketeer.

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And it was a fierce one?芒聙聺

芒聙聹His highness the prince charged eleven times in person.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is a great commander, Bragelonne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is a hero, sir. I did not lose sight of him for an instant. Oh! how
fine it is to be called Cond脙漏 and to be so worthy of such a name!芒聙聺

芒聙聹He was calm and radiant, was he not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹As calm as at parade, radiant as at a _f脙陋te_. When we went up to the
enemy it was slowly; we were forbidden to draw first and we were
marching toward the Spaniards, who were on a height with lowered
muskets. When we arrived about thirty paces from them the prince turned
around to the soldiers: 芒聙聵Comrades,芒聙聶 he said, 芒聙聵you are about to suffer a
furious discharge; but after that you will make short work with those
fellows.芒聙聶 There was such dead silence that friends and enemies could
have heard these words; then raising his sword, 芒聙聵Sound trumpets!芒聙聶 he
cried.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, very good; you will do as much when the opportunity occurs, will
you, Raoul?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know not, sir, but I thought it really very fine and grand!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Were you afraid, Raoul?芒聙聺 asked the count.

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 replied the young man na脙炉vely; 芒聙聹I felt a great chill at my
heart, and at the word 芒聙聵fire,芒聙聶 which resounded in Spanish from the
enemy芒聙聶s ranks, I closed my eyes and thought of you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In honest truth, Raoul?芒聙聺 said Athos, pressing his hand.

芒聙聹Yes, sir; at that instant there was such a rataplan of musketry that
one might have imagined the infernal regions had opened. Those who were
not killed felt the heat of the flames. I opened my eyes, astonished to
find myself alive and even unhurt; a third of the squadron were lying
on the ground, wounded, dead or dying. At that moment I encountered the
eye of the prince. I had but one thought and that was that he was
observing me. I spurred on and found myself in the enemy芒聙聶s ranks.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And the prince was pleased with you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He told me so, at least, sir, when he desired me to return to Paris
with Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon, who was charged to carry the news to the
queen and to bring the colors we had taken. 芒聙聵Go,芒聙聶 said he; 芒聙聵the enemy
will not rally for fifteen days and until that time I have no need of
your service. Go and see those whom you love and who love you, and tell
my sister De Longueville that I thank her for the present that she made
me of you.芒聙聶 And I came, sir,芒聙聺 added Raoul, gazing at the count with a
smile of real affection, 芒聙聹for I thought you would be glad to see me
again.芒聙聺

Athos drew the young man toward him and pressed his lips to his brow,
as he would have done to a young daughter.

芒聙聹And now, Raoul,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹you are launched; you have dukes for
friends, a marshal of France for godfather, a prince of the blood as
commander, and on the day of your return you have been received by two
queens; it is not so bad for a novice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, suddenly, 芒聙聹you recall something, which, in my
haste to relate my exploits, I had forgotten; it is that there was with
Her Majesty the Queen of England, a gentleman who, when I pronounced
your name, uttered a cry of surprise and joy; he said he was a friend
of yours, asked your address, and is coming to see you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is his name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I did not venture to ask, sir; he spoke elegantly, although I thought
from his accent he was an Englishman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Athos, leaning down his head as if to remember who it could
be. Then, when he raised it again, he was struck by the presence of a

compassionate air.

芒聙聹Lord de Winter!芒聙聺 exclaimed the count.

芒聙聹Athos, my friend!芒聙聺

And the two gentlemen were for an instant locked in each other芒聙聶s arms;
then Athos, looking into his friend芒聙聶s face and taking him by both
hands, said:

芒聙聹What ails you, my lord? you appear as unhappy as I am the reverse.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, truly, dear friend; and I may even say the sight of you increases
my dismay.芒聙聺

And De Winter glancing around him, Raoul quickly understood that the
two friends wished to be alone and he therefore left the room

芒聙聹Come, now that we are alone,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹let us talk of yourself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Whilst we are alone let us speak of ourselves,芒聙聺 replied De Winter. 芒聙聹He
is here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Milady芒聙聶s son.芒聙聺

Athos, again struck by this name, which seemed to pursue him like an
echo, hesitated for a moment, then slightly knitting his brows, he
calmly said:

芒聙聹I know it, Grimaud met him between Bethune and Arras and then came
here to warn me of his presence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Does Grimaud know him, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; but he was present at the deathbed of a man who knew him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The headsman of Bethune?芒聙聺 exclaimed De Winter.

芒聙聹You know about that?芒聙聺 cried Athos, astonished.

芒聙聹He has just left me,芒聙聺 replied De Winter, 芒聙聹after telling me all. Ah! my
friend! what a horrible scene! Why did we not destroy the child with
the mother?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What need you fear?芒聙聺 said Athos, recovering from the instinctive fear
he had at first experienced, by the aid of reason; 芒聙聹are we not men
accustomed to defend ourselves? Is this young man an assassin by
profession芒聙聰a murderer in cold blood? He has killed the executioner of
Bethune in an access of passion, but now his fury is assuaged.芒聙聺

De Winter smiled sorrowfully and shook his head.

芒聙聹Do you not know the race?芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Pooh!芒聙聺 said Athos, trying to smile in his turn. 芒聙聹It must have lost its
ferocity in the second generation. Besides, my friend, Providence has
warned us, that we may be on our guard. All we can now do is to wait.
Let us wait; and, as I said before, let us speak of yourself. What
brings you to Paris?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Affairs of importance which you shall know later. But what is this
that I hear from Her Majesty the Queen of England? Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan
sides with Mazarin! Pardon my frankness, dear friend. I neither hate
nor blame the cardinal, and your opinions will be held ever sacred by
me. But do you happen to belong to him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹is in the service; he is a
soldier and obeys all constitutional authority. Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan is
not rich and has need of his position as lieutenant to enable him to
live. Millionaires like yourself, my lord, are rare in France.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas!芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹I am at this moment as poor as he is, if not
poorer. But to return to our subject.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, you wish to know if I am of Mazarin芒聙聶s party? No. Pardon my
frankness, too, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am obliged to you, count, for this pleasing intelligence! You make
me young and happy again by it. Ah! so you are not a Mazarinist?
Delightful! Indeed, you could not belong to him. But pardon me, are you
free? I mean to ask if you are married?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! as to that, no,芒聙聺 replied Athos, laughing.

芒聙聹Because that young man, so handsome, so elegant, so polished芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is a child I have adopted and who does not even know who was his
father.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well; you are always the same, Athos, great and generous. Are you

芒聙聹Add Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, my lord. We still remain four friends devoted
to each other; but when it becomes a question of serving the cardinal
or of fighting him, of being Mazarinists or Frondists, then we are only
two.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is Monsieur Aramis with D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 asked Lord de Winter.

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹Monsieur Aramis does me the honor to share my
opinions.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Could you put me in communication with your witty and agreeable

芒聙聹He has become an abb脙漏, that is all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You alarm me; his profession must have made him renounce any great
undertakings.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the contrary,芒聙聺 said Athos, smiling, 芒聙聹he has never been so much a
musketeer as since he became an abb脙漏, and you will find him a veritable
soldier.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Could you engage to bring him to me to-morrow morning at ten o芒聙聶clock,
on the Pont du Louvre?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, oh!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, smiling, 芒聙聹you have a duel in prospect.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, count, and a splendid duel, too; a duel in which I hope you will
take your part.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where are we to go, my lord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To Her Majesty the Queen of England, who has desired me to present you
to her.芒聙聺

芒聙聹This is an enigma,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹but it matters not; since you know
the solution of it I ask no further. Will your lordship do me the honor
to sup with me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thanks, count, no,芒聙聺 replied De Winter. 芒聙聹I own to you that that young
man芒聙聶s visit has subdued my appetite and probably will rob me of my
sleep. What undertaking can have brought him to Paris? It was not to
meet me that he came, for he was ignorant of my journey. This young man
terrifies me, my lord; there lies in him a sanguinary predisposition.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What occupies him in England?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is one of Cromwell芒聙聶s most enthusiastic disciples.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what attached him to the cause? His father and mother were
Catholics, I believe?芒聙聺

芒聙聹His hatred of the king, who deprived him of his estates and forbade
him to bear the name of De Winter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what name does he now bear?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A Puritan, yet disguised as a monk he travels alone in France.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you say as a monk?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It was thus, and by mere accident芒聙聰may God pardon me if I
blaspheme芒聙聰that he heard the confession of the executioner of Bethune.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then I understand it all! he has been sent by Cromwell to Mazarin, and
the queen guessed rightly; we have been forestalled. Everything is
clear to me now. Adieu, count, till to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But the night is dark,芒聙聺 said Athos, perceiving that Lord de Winter
seemed more uneasy than he wished to appear; 芒聙聹and you have no servant.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have Tony, a safe if simple youth.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Halloo, there, Grimaud, Olivain, and Blaisois! call the viscount and
take the musket with you.芒聙聺

Blaisois was the tall youth, half groom, half peasant, whom we saw at
the Ch脙垄teau de Bragelonne, whom Athos had christened by the name of his
province.

芒聙聹Viscount,芒聙聺 said Athos to Raoul, as he entered, 芒聙聹you will conduct my

芒聙聹Oh! count,芒聙聺 said De Winter, 芒聙聹for whom do you take me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For a stranger who does not know Paris,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and to whom the
viscount will show the way.芒聙聺

De Winter shook him by the hand.

芒聙聹Grimaud,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹put yourself at the head of the troop and
beware of the monk.芒聙聺

Grimaud shuddered, and nodding, awaited the departure, regarding the
butt of his musket with silent eloquence. Then obeying the orders given

hand and the musket in the other, until it reached De Winter芒聙聶s inn,
when pounding on the portal with his fist, he bowed to my lord and
faced about without a word.

The same order was followed in returning, nor did Grimaud芒聙聶s searching
glance discover anything of a suspicious appearance, save a dark
shadow, as it were, in ambuscade, at the corner of the Rue Gu脙漏n脙漏gaud
and of the Quai. He fancied, also, that in going he had already
observed the street watcher who had attracted his attention. He pushed
on toward him, but before he could reach it the shadow had disappeared
into an alley, into which Grimaud deemed it scarcely prudent to pursue
it.

The next day, on awaking, the count perceived Raoul by his bedside. The
young man was already dressed and was reading a new book by M.
Chapelain.

芒聙聹Already up, Raoul?芒聙聺 exclaimed the count.

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 replied Raoul, with slight hesitation; 芒聙聹I did not sleep
well.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You, Raoul, not sleep well! then you must have something on your
mind!芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Sir, you will perhaps think that I am in a great hurry to leave you
when I have only just arrived, but芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have you only two days of leave, Raoul?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the contrary, sir, I have ten; nor is it to the camp I wish to go.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where, then?芒聙聺 said Athos, smiling, 芒聙聹if it be not a secret. You are now
almost a man, since you have made your first passage of arms, and have
acquired the right to go where you will without consulting me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Never, sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, 芒聙聹as long as I possess the happiness of having
you for a protector, shall I deem I have the right of freeing myself
from a guardianship so valuable to me. I have, however, a wish to go
and pass a day at Blois. You look at me and you are going to laugh at
me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, on the contrary, I am not inclined to laugh,芒聙聺 said Athos,
suppressing a sigh. 芒聙聹You wish to see Blois again; it is but natural.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then you permit me to go, you are not angry in your heart?芒聙聺 exclaimed
Raoul, joyously.

芒聙聹Certainly; and why should I regret what gives you pleasure?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! how kind you are,芒聙聺 exclaimed the young man, pressing his
guardian芒聙聶s hand; 芒聙聹and I can set out immediately?芒聙聺

芒聙聹When you like, Raoul.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Raoul, as he turned to leave the room, 芒聙聹I have thought of
one thing, and that is about the Duchess of Chevreuse, who was so kind

芒聙聹And you ought to thank her, Raoul. Well, try the Hotel de Luynes,

芒聙聹Both, sir?芒聙聺 asked Raoul, astonished.

芒聙聹Both.芒聙聺

Raoul went out, and when Athos heard his young, joyous voice calling to

芒聙聹It is very soon to leave me,芒聙聺 he thought, 芒聙聹but he follows the common
custom. Nature has made us thus; she makes the young look ever forward,
not behind. He certainly likes the child, but will he love me less as
his affection grows for _her?_芒聙聺

And Athos confessed to himself that, he was unprepared for so prompt a
departure; but Raoul was so happy that this reflection effaced
everything else from the consideration of his guardian.

Everything was ready at ten o芒聙聶clock for the departure, and as Athos was
watching Raoul mount, a groom rode up from the Duchess de Chevreuse. He
was charged to tell the Comte de la F脙篓re, that she had learned of the
return of her youthful _prot脙漏g脙漏_, and also the manner he had conducted
himself on the field, and she added that she should be very glad to
offer him her congratulations.

芒聙聹Tell her grace,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹that the viscount has just mounted
his horse to proceed to the Hotel de Luynes.芒聙聺

Then, with renewed instructions to Grimaud, Athos signified to Raoul
that he could set out, and ended by reflecting that it was perhaps
better that Raoul should be away from Paris at that moment.




Chapter XLII.
Another Queen in Want of Help.


Athos had not failed to send early to Aramis and had given his letter
to Blaisois, the only serving-man whom he had left. Blaisois found
Bazin donning his beadle芒聙聶s gown, his services being required that day
at Notre Dame.

Athos had desired Blaisois to try to speak to Aramis himself. Blaisois,
a tall, simple youth, who understood nothing but what he was expressly
told, asked, therefore for the Abb脙漏 d芒聙聶Herblay, and in spite of Bazin芒聙聶s
assurances that his master was not at home, he persisted in such a
manner as to put Bazin into a passion. Blaisois seeing Bazin in
clerical guise, was a little discomposed at his denials and wanted to
pass at all risks, believing too, that the man with whom he had to do
was endowed with the virtues of his cloth, namely, patience and
Christian charity.

But Bazin, still the servant of a musketeer, when once the blood
mounted to his fat cheeks, seized a broomstick and began belaboring
Blaisois, saying:

芒聙聹You have insulted the church, my friend, you have insulted the
church!芒聙聺

At this moment Aramis, aroused by this unusual disturbance, cautiously
opened the door of his room; and Blaisois, looking reproachfully at the
Cerberus, drew the letter from his pocket and presented it to Aramis.

芒聙聹From the Comte de la F脙篓re,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹All right.芒聙聺 And he retired
into his room without even asking the cause of so much noise.

Blaisois returned disconsolate to the Hotel of the Grand Roi
Charlemagne and when Athos inquired if his commission was executed, he
related his adventure.

芒聙聹You foolish fellow!芒聙聺 said Athos, laughing. 芒聙聹And you did not tell him
that you came from me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir.芒聙聺

At ten o芒聙聶clock Athos, with his habitual exactitude, was waiting on the
Pont du Louvre and was almost immediately joined by Lord de Winter.

They waited ten minutes and then his lordship began to fear Aramis was
not coming to join them.

芒聙聹Patience,芒聙聺 said Athos, whose eyes were fixed in the direction of the
Rue du Bac, 芒聙聹patience; I see an abb脙漏 cuffing a man, then bowing to a
woman; it must be Aramis.芒聙聺

It was indeed Aramis. Having run against a young shopkeeper who was
gaping at the crows and who had splashed him, Aramis with one blow of
his fist had distanced him ten paces.

At this moment one of his penitents passed, and as she was young and
pretty Aramis took off his cap to her with his most gracious smile.

A most affectionate greeting, as one can well believe took place
between him and Lord de Winter.

芒聙聹Where are we going?芒聙聺 inquired Aramis; 芒聙聹are we going to fight,
perchance? I carry no sword this morning and cannot return home to
procure one.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Lord de Winter, 芒聙聹we are going to pay a visit to Her Majesty
the Queen of England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, very well,芒聙聺 replied Aramis; then bending his face down to Athos芒聙聶s
ear, 芒聙聹what is the object of this visit?芒聙聺 continued he.

芒聙聹Nay, I know not; some evidence required from us, perhaps.芒聙聺

芒聙聹May it not be about that cursed affair?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, 芒聙聹in which case
I do not greatly care to go, for it will be to pocket a lecture; and
since it is my function to give them to others I am rather averse to
receiving them myself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If it were so,芒聙聺 answered Athos, 芒聙聹we should not be taken there by Lord
de Winter, for he would come in for his share; he was one of us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You芒聙聶re right; yes, let us go.芒聙聺

On arriving at the Louvre Lord de Winter entered first; indeed, there
was but one porter there to receive them at the gate.

It was impossible in daylight for the impoverished state of the
habitation grudging charity had conceded to an unfortunate queen to
pass unnoticed by Athos, Aramis, and even the Englishman. Large rooms,
completely stripped of furniture, bare walls upon which, here and
there, shone the old gold moldings which had resisted time and neglect,
windows with broken panes (impossible to close), no carpets, neither
guards nor servants: this is what first met the eyes of Athos, to which
he, touching his companion芒聙聶s elbow, directed his attention by his

芒聙聹Mazarin is better lodged,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Mazarin is almost king,芒聙聺 answered Athos; 芒聙聹Madame Henrietta is almost
no longer queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If you would condescend to be clever, Athos,芒聙聺 observed Aramis, 芒聙聹I
really do think you would be wittier than poor Monsieur de Voiture.芒聙聺

Athos smiled.

The queen appeared to be impatiently expecting them, for at the first

芒聙聹Enter. You are welcome, gentlemen,芒聙聺 she said.

The gentlemen entered and remained standing, but at a motion from the

furious; the sight of such royal misery exasperated him and his eyes
examined every new trace of poverty that presented itself.

芒聙聹You are examining the luxury I enjoy,芒聙聺 said the queen, glancing sadly
around her.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied Aramis, 芒聙聹I must ask your pardon, but I know not how

芒聙聹Monsieur Aramis is not an officer?芒聙聺 asked the queen of Lord de Winter.

芒聙聹That gentleman is the Abb脙漏 d芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聺 replied he.

Aramis blushed. 芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I am an abb脙漏, it is true, but I am
so against my will. I never had a vocation for the bands; my cassock is
fastened by one button only, and I am always ready to become a
musketeer once more. This morning, being ignorant that I should have

芒聙聹The Abb脙漏 d芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聺 resumed De Winter, 芒聙聹is one of those gallant

芒聙聹And this gentleman is that noble Comte de la F脙篓re, whose high
reputation is so well known to your majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹a few years ago I had around me ushers,
treasures, armies; and by the lifting of a finger all these were busied

in order to accomplish a plan which is dearer to me than life I have
only Lord de Winter, the friend of twenty years, and you, gentlemen,
whom I see for the first time and whom I know but as my countrymen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is enough,芒聙聺 said Athos, bowing low, 芒聙聹if the lives of three men can

芒聙聹I thank you, gentlemen. But hear me,芒聙聺 continued she. 芒聙聹I am not only
the most miserable of queens, but the most unhappy of mothers, the most
wretched of wives. My children, two of them, at least, the Duke of York
and the Princess Elizabeth, are far away from me, exposed to the blows
of the ambitious and our foes; my husband, the king, is leading in
England so wretched an existence that it is no exaggeration to aver
that he seeks death as a thing to be desired. Hold! gentlemen, here is
the letter conveyed to me by Lord de Winter. Read it.芒聙聺

Obeying the queen, Athos read aloud the letter which we have already
seen, in which King Charles demanded to know whether the hospitality of
France would be accorded him.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 asked Athos, when he had closed the letter.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹it has been refused.芒聙聺

The two friends exchanged a smile of contempt.

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹what is to be done? I have the honor to inquire
from your majesty what you desire Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay and myself to do
in your service. We are ready.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, sir, you have a noble heart!芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, with a burst of

芒聙聹But you, sir?芒聙聺 said the queen to Aramis.

芒聙聹I, madame,芒聙聺 replied he, 芒聙聹follow Monsieur de la F脙篓re wherever he leads,
even were it on to death, without demanding wherefore; but when it

with all the grace of former days, 芒聙聹I precede the count.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹since it is thus, and since

every day; surrounded by the Scotch, whom he distrusts, although he be
himself a Scotchman. Since Lord de Winter left him I am distracted,

Go to England, join the king, be his friends, protectors, march to

reward you, I believe that word would offend you芒聙聰but to love you as a

And the queen raised her eyes solemnly upward.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹when must we set out?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You consent then?芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, joyfully.

芒聙聹Yes, madame; only it seems to me that your majesty goes too far in

芒聙聹Oh, sirs,芒聙聺 said the queen, moved even to tears, 芒聙聹this is the first

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹I have only to ask your majesty芒聙聶s prayers.芒聙聺

The queen held out her hand, which they kissed, and she said in a low

芒聙聹If you need money, my lord, separate the jewels I have given you;

The queen had two letters ready, one written by herself, the other by

should they be separated by chance they might make themselves known to

At the foot of the staircase De Winter stopped.

芒聙聹Not to arouse suspicions, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹go your way and I will

The three gentlemen then shook hands, Lord de Winter taking the Rue

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Aramis, when they were alone, 芒聙聹what do you think of this

芒聙聹Bad,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹very bad.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But you received it with enthusiasm.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As I shall ever receive the defense of a great principle, my dear
D芒聙聶Herblay. Monarchs are only strong by the assistance of the

芒聙聹We shall be murdered there,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹I hate the English芒聙聰they are

芒聙聹Oh! that is of the first importance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Attend to it, then, at once.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Unfortunately, it is a thing that I can芒聙聶t perform at any time I

芒聙聹About that time.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But, my dear fellow, those are things that bear postponement and you

They then separated. Athos went to pay a visit to Madame de Vend脙麓me,

芒聙聹Dear Friend,芒聙聰I am about to set off with Aramis on important business.

At the hour agreed upon Aramis arrived; he was dressed as an officer

芒聙聹By-the-bye,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I think that we are decidedly wrong to depart

When supper was over Blaisois entered. 芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹here is

芒聙聹And I set off without waiting for one, but he called me back and gave

Athos opened it and began by drawing forth a little note, written in

芒聙聹My dear Count,芒聙聰When one travels, and especially for three months, one

芒聙聹As to what you say about not seeing you again, I believe not a word of

芒聙聹It is unnecessary to say that from the day I saw Raoul I loved him;

芒聙聹Your

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹P.S.芒聙聰Be it well understood that the fifty louis which I send are

Athos smiled, and his fine eye was dimmed by a tear. D芒聙聶Artagnan, who

芒聙聹When you like; but have you no groom?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; that idiot Bazin had the folly to make himself verger, as you

芒聙聹Very well, take Blaisois, with whom I know not what to do, since I

芒聙聹Willingly,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

At this moment Grimaud appeared at the door. 芒聙聹Ready,芒聙聺 said he, with his
usual curtness.

芒聙聹Let us go, then,芒聙聺 said Athos.

The two friends mounted, as did their servants. At the corner of the

芒聙聹Oh, sir!芒聙聺 exclaimed he, 芒聙聹thank Heaven I have arrived in time. Monsieur

芒聙聹Wait,芒聙聺 said Bazin, striking a flint, and setting afire a twisted

芒聙聹My dear D芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聰I learned from D芒聙聶Artagnan who has embraced me on
the part of the Comte de la F脙篓re and yourself, that you are setting out

ch脙垄teaux; at Bracieux alone, I have twenty thousand francs in gold. So,

芒聙聹I address you, because you know, that although I esteem him from my

芒聙聹I am, as I trust you do not doubt, your devoted

芒聙聹Du Vallon de Bracieux de Pierrefonds.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹what do you say to that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I say, my dear D芒聙聶Herblay, that it is almost sacrilege to distrust

The division being made by the light of Bazin芒聙聶s taper, the two friends
continued their road and a quarter of an hour later they had joined De
Winter at the Porte Saint Denis.




Chapter XLIII.
In which it is proved that first Impulses are oftentimes the best.


The three gentlemen took the road to Picardy, a road so well known to
them and which recalled to Athos and Aramis some of the most
picturesque adventures of their youth.

芒聙聹If Mousqueton were with us,芒聙聺 observed Athos, on reaching the spot
where they had had a dispute with the paviers, 芒聙聹how he would tremble at
passing this! Do you remember, Aramis, that it was here he received
that famous bullet wound?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By my faith, 芒聙聶twould be excusable in him to tremble,芒聙聺 replied Aramis,
芒聙聹for even I feel a shudder at the recollection; hold, just above that
tree is the little spot where I thought I was killed.芒聙聺

It was soon time for Grimaud to recall the past. Arriving before the
inn at which his master and himself had made such an enormous repast,
he approached Athos and said, showing him the airhole of the cellar:

芒聙聹Sausages!芒聙聺

Athos began to laugh, for this juvenile escapade of his appeared to be
as amusing as if some one had related it of another person.

At last, after traveling two days and a night, they arrived at Boulogne
toward the evening, favored by magnificent weather. Boulogne was a
strong position, then almost a deserted town, built entirely on the
heights; what is now called the lower town did not then exist.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said De Winter, on reaching the gate of the town, 芒聙聹let us
do here as at Paris芒聙聰let us separate to avoid suspicion. I know an inn,
little frequented, but of which the host is entirely devoted to me. I
will go there, where I expect to find letters, and you go to the first
tavern in the town, to L芒聙聶Ep脙漏e du Grand Henri for instance, refresh
yourselves, and in two hours be upon the jetty; our boat is waiting for
us there.芒聙聺

The matter being thus decided, the two friends found, about two hundred
paces further, the tavern indicated. Their horses were fed, but not
unsaddled; the grooms supped, for it was already late, and their two
masters, impatient to return, appointed a place of meeting with them on
the jetty and desired them on no account to exchange a word with any
one. It is needless to say that this caution concerned Blaisois
alone芒聙聰long enough since it had been a useless one to Grimaud.

Athos and Aramis walked down toward the port. From their dress, covered
with dust, and from a certain easy manner by means of which a man
accustomed to travel is always recognizable, the two friends excited
the attention of a few promenaders. There was more especially one upon
whom their arrival had produced a decided impression. This man, whom
they had noticed from the first for the same reason they had themselves
been remarked by others, was walking in a listless way up and down the
jetty. From the moment he perceived them he did not cease to look at
them and seemed to burn with the wish to speak to them.

On reaching the jetty Athos and Aramis stopped to look at a little boat
made fast to a pile and ready rigged as if waiting to start.

芒聙聹That is doubtless our boat,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 replied Aramis, 芒聙聹and the sloop out there making ready to sail
must be that which is to take us to our destination; now,芒聙聺 continued
he, 芒聙聹if only De Winter does not keep us waiting. It is not at all
amusing here; there is not a single woman passing.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹we are overheard.芒聙聺

In truth, the walker, who, during the observations of the two friends,
had passed and repassed behind them several times, stopped at the name
of De Winter; but as his face betrayed no emotion at mention of this
name, it might have been by chance he stood so still.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the man, who was young and pale, bowing with ease and
courtesy, 芒聙聹pardon my curiosity, but I see you come from Paris, or at
least that you are strangers at Boulogne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We come from Paris, yes,芒聙聺 replied Athos, with the same courtesy; 芒聙聹what
is there we can do for you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said the young man, 芒聙聹will you be so good as to tell me if it be
true that Cardinal Mazarin is no longer minister?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is a strange question,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹He is and he is not,芒聙聺 replied Athos; 芒聙聹that is to say, he is dismissed
by one-half of France, but by intrigues and promises he makes the other
half sustain him; you will perceive that this may last a long time.芒聙聺

芒聙聹However, sir,芒聙聺 said the stranger, 芒聙聹he has neither fled nor is in
prison?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, not at this moment at least.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sirs, accept my thanks for your politeness,芒聙聺 said the young man,
retreating.

芒聙聹What do you think of that interrogator?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹I think he is either a dull provincial person or a spy in search of
information.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you replied to him with that notion?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing warranted me to answer him otherwise; he was polite to me and
I was so to him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But if he be a spy芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you think a spy would be about here? We are not living in the
time of Cardinal Richelieu, who would have closed the ports on bare
suspicion.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It matters not; you were wrong to reply to him as you did,芒聙聺 continued
Aramis, following with his eyes the young man, now vanishing behind the
cliffs.

芒聙聹And you,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹you forget that you committed a very different
kind of imprudence in pronouncing Lord de Winter芒聙聶s name. Did you not
see that at that name the young man stopped?芒聙聺

芒聙聹More reason, then, when he spoke to you, for sending him about his
business.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A quarrel?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹And since when have you become afraid of a quarrel?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am always afraid of a quarrel when I am expected at any place and
when such a quarrel might possibly prevent my reaching it. Besides, let
me own something to you. I am anxious to see that young man nearer.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And wherefore?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Aramis, you will certainly laugh at me, you will say that I am always
repeating the same thing, you will call me the most timorous of
visionaries; but to whom do you see a resemblance in that young man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In beauty or on the contrary?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, laughing.

芒聙聹In ugliness, in so far as a man can resemble a woman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! Egad!芒聙聺 cried Aramis, 芒聙聹you set me thinking. No, in truth you are no
visionary, my dear friend, and now I think of it芒聙聰you芒聙聰yes, i芒聙聶faith,
you芒聙聶re right芒聙聰those delicate, yet firm-set lips, those eyes which seem
always at the command of the intellect and never of the heart! Yes, it
is one of Milady芒聙聶s bastards!芒聙聺

芒聙聹You laugh Aramis.芒聙聺

芒聙聹From habit, that is all. I swear to you, I like no better than
yourself to meet that viper in my path.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! here is De Winter coming,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Good! one thing now is only awanting and that is, that our grooms
should not keep us waiting.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹I see them about twenty paces behind my lord. I
recognize Grimaud by his long legs and his determined slouch. Tony
carries our muskets.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then we set sail to-night?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, glancing toward the west,
where the sun had left a single golden cloud, which, dipping into the
ocean, appeared by degrees to be extinguished.

芒聙聹Probably,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹_Diable!_芒聙聺 resumed Aramis, 芒聙聹I have little fancy for the sea by day,
still less at night; the sounds of wind and wave, the frightful
movements of the vessel; I confess I prefer the convent of Noisy.芒聙聺

Athos smiled sadly, for it was evident that he was thinking of other
things as he listened to his friend and moved toward De Winter.

芒聙聹What ails our friend?芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹he resembles one of Dante芒聙聶s
damned, whose neck Apollyon has dislocated and who are ever looking at
their heels. What the devil makes him glower thus behind him?芒聙聺

When De Winter perceived them, in his turn he advanced toward them with
surprising rapidity.

芒聙聹What is the matter, my lord?芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and what puts you out of
breath thus?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing,芒聙聺 replied De Winter; 芒聙聹nothing; and yet in passing the heights
it seemed to me芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺 and he again turned round.

Athos glanced at Aramis.

芒聙聹But let us go,芒聙聺 continued De Winter; 芒聙聹let us be off; the boat must be
waiting for us and there is our sloop at anchor芒聙聰do you see it there? I
wish I were on board already,芒聙聺 and he looked back again.

芒聙聹He has seen him,芒聙聺 said Athos, in a low tone, to Aramis.

They had reached the ladder which led to the boat. De Winter made the
grooms who carried the arms and the porters with the luggage descend

At this moment Athos perceived a man walking on the seashore parallel
to the jetty, and hastening his steps, as if to reach the other side of
the port, scarcely twenty steps from the place of embarking. He fancied
in the darkness that he recognized the young man who had questioned

of the young man. The latter, to make a short cut, had appeared on a
sluice.

芒聙聹He certainly bodes us no good,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹but let us embark; once
out at sea, let him come.芒聙聺

And Athos sprang into the boat, which was immediately pushed off and
which soon sped seawards under the efforts of four stalwart rowers.

But the young man had begun to follow, or rather to advance before the
boat. She was obliged to pass between the point of the jetty,
surmounted by a beacon just lighted, and a rock which jutted out. They
saw him in the distance climbing the rock in order to look down upon
the boat as it passed.

芒聙聹Ay, but,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹that young fellow is decidedly a spy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Which is the young man?芒聙聺 asked De Winter, turning around.

芒聙聹He who followed us and spoke to us awaits us there; behold!芒聙聺

De Winter turned and followed the direction of Aramis芒聙聶s finger. The
beacon bathed with light the little strait through which they were
about to pass and the rock where the young man stood with bare head and
crossed arms.

芒聙聹It is he!芒聙聺 exclaimed De Winter, seizing the arm of Athos; 芒聙聹it is he! I
thought I recognized him and I was not mistaken.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Whom do you mean?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹Milady芒聙聶s son,芒聙聺 replied Athos.

芒聙聹The monk!芒聙聺 exclaimed Grimaud.

The young man heard these words and bent so forward over the rock that
one might have supposed he was about to precipitate himself from it.

芒聙聹Yes, it is I, my uncle芒聙聰I, the son of Milady芒聙聰I, the monk芒聙聰I, the
secretary and friend of Cromwell芒聙聰_I know you now_, both you and your
companions.芒聙聺

In that boat sat three men, unquestionably brave, whose courage no man
would have dared dispute; nevertheless, at that voice, that accent and
those gestures, they felt a chill access of terror cramp their veins.
As for Grimaud, his hair stood on end and drops of sweat ran down his
brow.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed Aramis, 芒聙聹that is the nephew, the monk, and the son of
Milady, as he says himself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas, yes,芒聙聺 murmured De Winter.

芒聙聹Then wait,芒聙聺 said Aramis; and with the terrible coolness which on
important occasions he showed, he took one of the muskets from Tony,
shouldered and aimed it at the young man, who stood, like the accusing
angel, upon the rock.

芒聙聹Fire!芒聙聺 cried Grimaud, unconsciously.

Athos threw himself on the muzzle of the gun and arrested the shot
which was about to be fired.

芒聙聹The devil take you,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹I had him so well at the point of
my gun I should have sent a ball into his breast.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is enough to have killed the mother,芒聙聺 said Athos, hoarsely.

芒聙聹The mother was a wretch, who struck at us all and at those dear to
us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but the son has done us no harm.芒聙聺

Grimaud, who had risen to watch the effect of the shot, fell back
hopeless, wringing his hands.

The young man burst into a laugh.

芒聙聹Ah, it is certainly you!芒聙聺 he cried. 芒聙聹I know you even better now.芒聙聺

His mocking laugh and threatening words passed over their heads,
carried by the breeze, until lost in the depths of the horizon. Aramis
shuddered.

芒聙聹Be calm,芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, 芒聙聹for Heaven芒聙聶s sake! have we ceased to be
men?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹but that fellow is a fiend; and ask the uncle
whether I was wrong to rid him of his dear nephew.芒聙聺

De Winter only replied by a groan.

芒聙聹It was all up with him,芒聙聺 continued Aramis; 芒聙聹ah I much fear that with
all your wisdom such mercy yet will prove supernal folly.芒聙聺

Athos took Lord de Winter芒聙聶s hand and tried to turn the conversation.

芒聙聹When shall we land in England?芒聙聺 he asked; but De Winter seemed not to
hear his words and made no reply.

芒聙聹Hold, Athos,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹perhaps there is yet time. See if he is
still in the same place.芒聙聺

Athos turned around with an effort; the sight of the young man was
evidently painful to him, and there he still was, in fact, on the rock,
the beacon shedding around him, as it were, a doubtful aureole.

芒聙聹Decidedly, Aramis,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹I think I was wrong not to let you
fire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hold your tongue,芒聙聺 replied Aramis; 芒聙聹you would make me weep, if such a
thing were possible.芒聙聺

At this moment they were hailed by a voice from the sloop and a few
seconds later men, servants and baggage were aboard. The captain was
only waiting for his passengers; hardly had they put foot on deck ere
her head was turned towards Hastings, where they were to disembark. At
this instant the three friends turned, in spite of themselves, a last
look on the rock, upon the menacing figure which pursued them and now
stood out with a distinctness still. Then a voice reached them once
more, sending this threat: 芒聙聹To our next meeting, sirs, in England.芒聙聺




Chapter XLIV.
Te Deum for the Victory of Lens.


The bustle which had been observed by Henrietta Maria and for which she
had vainly sought to discover a reason, was occasioned by the battle of
Lens, announced by the prince芒聙聶s messenger, the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon, who
had taken such a noble part in the engagement; he was, besides, charged
to hang five and twenty flags, taken from the Lorraine party, as well
as from the Spaniards, upon the arches of Notre Dame.

Such news was decisive; it destroyed, in favor of the court, the
struggle commenced with parliament. The motive given for all the taxes
summarily imposed and to which the parliament had made opposition, was
the necessity of sustaining the honor of France and the uncertain hope
of beating the enemy. Now, since the affair of Nordlingen, they had
experienced nothing but reverses; the parliament had a plea for calling
Mazarin to account for imaginary victories, always promised, ever
deferred; but this time there really had been fighting, a triumph and a
complete one. And this all knew so well that it was a double victory
for the court, a victory at home and abroad; so that even when the
young king learned the news he exclaimed, 芒聙聹Ah, gentlemen of the
parliament, we shall see what you will say now!芒聙聺 Upon which the queen
had pressed the royal child to her heart, whose haughty and unruly
sentiments were in such harmony with her own. A council was called on
the same evening, but nothing transpired of what had been decided on.
It was only known that on the following Sunday a _Te Deum_ would be
sung at Notre Dame in honor of the victory of Lens.

The following Sunday, then, the Parisians arose with joy; at that
period a _Te Deum_ was a grand affair; this kind of ceremony had not
then been abused and it produced a great effect. The shops were
deserted, houses closed; every one wished to see the young king with
his mother, and the famous Cardinal Mazarin whom they hated so much
that no one wished to be deprived of his presence. Moreover, great
liberty prevailed throughout the immense crowd; every opinion was
openly expressed and chorused, so to speak, of coming insurrection, as
the thousand bells of all the Paris churches rang out the _Te Deum_.
The police belonging to the city being formed by the city itself,
nothing threatening presented itself to disturb this concert of
universal hatred or freeze the frequent scoffs of slanderous lips.

Nevertheless, at eight o芒聙聶clock in the morning the regiment of the
queen芒聙聶s guards, commanded by Guitant, under whom was his nephew
Comminges, marched publicly, preceded by drums and trumpets, filing off
from the Palais Royal as far as Notre Dame, a man脜聯uvre which the
Parisians witnessed tranquilly, delighted as they were with military
music and brilliant uniforms.

Friquet had put on his Sunday clothes, under the pretext of having a
swollen face which he had managed to simulate by introducing a handful
of cherry kernels into one side of his mouth, and had procured a whole
holiday from Bazin. On leaving Bazin, Friquet started off to the Palais
Royal, where he arrived at the moment of the turning out of the
regiment of guards; and as he had only gone there for the enjoyment of
seeing it and hearing the music, he took his place at their head,
beating the drum on two pieces of slate and passing from that exercise
to that of the trumpet, which he counterfeited quite naturally with his
mouth in a manner which had more than once called forth the praises of
amateurs of imitative harmony.

This amusement lasted from the Barri脙篓re des Sergens to the place of
Notre Dame, and Friquet found in it very real enjoyment; but when at
last the regiment separated, penetrated the heart of the city and
placed itself at the extremity of the Rue Saint Christophe, near the
Rue Cocatrix, in which Broussel lived, then Friquet remembered that he
had not had breakfast; and after thinking in which direction he had
better turn his steps in order to accomplish this important act of the
day, he reflected deeply and decided that Councillor Broussel should
bear the cost of this repast.

In consequence he took to his heels, arrived breathlessly at the
councillor芒聙聶s door, and knocked violently.

His mother, the councillor芒聙聶s old servant, opened it.

芒聙聹What doest thou here, good-for-nothing?芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹and why art thou
not at Notre Dame?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have been there, mother,芒聙聺 said Friquet, 芒聙聹but I saw things happen of
which Master Broussel ought to be warned, and so with Monsieur Bazin芒聙聶s
permission芒聙聰you know, mother, Monsieur Bazin, the verger芒聙聰I came to speak
to Monsieur Broussel.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what hast thou to say, boy, to Monsieur Broussel?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wish to tell him,芒聙聺 replied Friquet, screaming with all his might,
芒聙聹that there is a whole regiment of guards coming this way. And as I
hear everywhere that at the court they are ill-disposed to him, I wish
to warn him, that he may be on his guard.芒聙聺

Broussel heard the scream of the young oddity, and, enchanted with this
excess of zeal, came down to the first floor, for he was, in truth,
working in his room on the second.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹friend, what matters the regiment of guards to us,
and art thou not mad to make such a disturbance? Knowest thou not that
it is the custom of these soldiers to act thus and that it is usual for
the regiment to form themselves into two solid walls when the king goes
by?芒聙聺

Friquet counterfeited surprise, and twisting his new cap around in his
fingers, said:

芒聙聹It is not astonishing for you to know it, Monsieur Broussel, who knows
everything; but as for me, by holy truth, I did not know it and I
thought I would give you good advice; you must not be angry with me for
that, Monsieur Broussel.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the contrary, my boy, on the contrary, I am pleased with your zeal.
Dame Nanette, look for those apricots which Madame de Longueville sent
to us yesterday from Noisy and give half a dozen of them to your son,
with a crust of new bread.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, thank you, sir, thank you, Monsieur Broussel,芒聙聺 said Friquet; 芒聙聹I am
so fond of apricots!芒聙聺

Broussel then proceeded to his wife芒聙聶s room and asked for breakfast; it
was nine o芒聙聶clock. The councillor placed himself at the window; the
street was completely deserted, but in the distance was heard, like the
noise of the tide rushing in, the deep hum of the populous waves
increasing now around Notre Dame.

This noise redoubled when D芒聙聶Artagnan, with a company of musketeers,
placed himself at the gates of Notre Dame to secure the service of the
church. He had instructed Porthos to profit by this opportunity to see
the ceremony; and Porthos, in full dress, mounted his finest horse,
taking the part of supernumerary musketeer, as D芒聙聶Artagnan had so often
done formerly. The sergeant of this company, a veteran of the Spanish
wars, had recognized Porthos, his old companion, and very soon all
those who served under him were placed in possession of startling facts
concerning the honor of the ancient musketeers of Tr脙漏ville. Porthos had
not only been well received by the company, but he was moreover looked
on with great admiration.

At ten o芒聙聶clock the guns of the Louvre announced the departure of the
king, and then a movement, similar to that of trees in a stormy wind
that bend and writhe with agitated tops, ran though the multitude,
which was compressed behind the immovable muskets of the guard. At last
the king appeared with the queen in a gilded chariot. Ten other
carriages followed, containing the ladies of honor, the officers of the
royal household, and the court.

芒聙聹God save the king!芒聙聺 was the cry in every direction; the young monarch
gravely put his head out of the window, looked sufficiently grateful
and even bowed; at which the cries of the multitude were renewed.

Just as the court was settling down in the cathedral, a carriage,
bearing the arms of Comminges, quitted the line of the court carriages
and proceeded slowly to the end of the Rue Saint Christophe, now
entirely deserted. When it arrived there, four guards and a police
officer, who accompanied it, mounted into the heavy machine and closed
the shutters; then through an opening cautiously made, the policeman
began to watch the length of the Rue Cocatrix, as if he was waiting for
some one.

All the world was occupied with the ceremony, so that neither the
chariot nor the precautions taken by those who were within it had been
observed. Friquet, whose eye, ever on the alert, could alone have
discovered them, had gone to devour his apricots upon the entablature
of a house in the square of Notre Dame. Thence he saw the king, the
queen and Monsieur Mazarin, and heard the mass as well as if he had
been on duty.

Toward the end of the service, the queen, seeing Comminges standing
near her, waiting for a confirmation of the order she had given him
before quitting the Louvre, said in a whisper:

芒聙聹Go, Comminges, and may God aid you!芒聙聺

Comminges immediately left the church and entered the Rue Saint
Christophe. Friquet, seeing this fine officer thus walk away, followed
by two guards, amused himself by pursuing them and did this so much the
more gladly as the ceremony ended at that instant and the king
remounted his carriage.

Hardly had the police officer observed Comminges at the end of the Rue
Cocatrix when he said one word to the coachman, who at once put his
vehicle into motion and drove up before Broussel芒聙聶s door. Comminges
knocked at the door at the same moment, and Friquet was waiting behind
Comminges until the door should be opened.

芒聙聹What dost thou there, rascal?芒聙聺 asked Comminges.

芒聙聹I want to go into Master Broussel芒聙聶s house, captain,芒聙聺 replied Friquet,
in that wheedling way the 芒聙聹gamins芒聙聺 of Paris know so well how to assume
when necessary.

芒聙聹And on what floor does he live?芒聙聺 asked Comminges.

芒聙聹In the whole house,芒聙聺 said Friquet; 芒聙聹the house belongs to him; he
occupies the second floor when he works and descends to the first to
take his meals; he must be at dinner now; it is noon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 said Comminges.

At this moment the door was opened, and having questioned the servant
the officer learned that Master Broussel was at home and at dinner.

Broussel was seated at the table with his family, having his wife
opposite to him, his two daughters by his side, and his son, Louvi脙篓res,
whom we have already seen when the accident happened to the
councillor芒聙聰an accident from which he had quite recovered芒聙聰at the bottom
of the table. The worthy man, restored to perfect health, was tasting
the fine fruit which Madame de Longueville had sent to him.

At sight of the officer Broussel was somewhat moved, but seeing him bow
politely he rose and bowed also. Still, in spite of this reciprocal
politeness, the countenances of the women betrayed a certain amount of
uneasiness; Louvi脙篓res became very pale and waited impatiently for the
officer to explain himself.

芒聙聹Upon what?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹Look here,芒聙聺 resumed D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹here is Monsieur de Bragelonne, who
determined at all risks to aid the arrest of Broussel and whom I had
great difficulty to prevent defending Monsieur de Comminges.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹and his guardian, what would he have said
to that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you hear?芒聙聺 interrupted D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹become a Frondist, my friend,
belong to the Fronde, and remember that I fill the count芒聙聶s place in
everything;芒聙聺 and he jingled his money.

芒聙聹Will you come?芒聙聺 said he to Porthos.

芒聙聹Where?芒聙聺 asked Porthos, filling a second glass of wine.

芒聙聹To present our respects to the cardinal.芒聙聺

Porthos swallowed the second glass with the same grace with which he
had imbibed the first, took his beaver and followed D芒聙聶Artagnan. As for
Raoul, he remained bewildered with what he had seen, having been
forbidden by D芒聙聶Artagnan to leave the room until the tumult was over.




Chapter XLV.
The Beggar of St. Eustache.


D芒聙聶Artagnan had calculated that in not going at once to the Palais Royal
he would give Comminges time to arrive before him, and consequently to
make the cardinal acquainted with the eminent services which he,
D芒聙聶Artagnan, and his friend had rendered to the queen芒聙聶s party in the
morning.

They were indeed admirably received by Mazarin, who paid them numerous
compliments, and announced that they were more than half on their way
to obtain what they desired, namely, D芒聙聶Artagnan his captaincy, Porthos
his barony.

D芒聙聶Artagnan would have preferred money in hand to all that fine talk,
for he knew well that to Mazarin it was easy to promise and hard to
perform. But, though he held the cardinal芒聙聶s promises as of little
worth, he affected to be completely satisfied, for he was unwilling to
discourage Porthos.

Whilst the two friends were with the cardinal, the queen sent for him.
Mazarin, thinking that it would be the means of increasing the zeal of
his two defenders if he procured them personal thanks from the queen,
motioned them to follow him. D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos pointed to their
dusty and torn dresses, but the cardinal shook his head.

芒聙聹Those costumes,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹are of more worth than most of those which
you will see on the backs of the queen芒聙聶s courtiers; they are costumes
of battle.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos obeyed. The court of Anne of Austria was full of
gayety and animation; for, after having gained a victory over the
Spaniard, it had just gained another over the people. Broussel had been
conducted out of Paris without further resistance, and was at this time
in the prison of Saint Germain; while Blancmesnil, who was arrested at
the same time, but whose arrest had been made without difficulty or
noise, was safe in the Castle of Vincennes.

Comminges was near the queen, who was questioning him upon the details
of his expedition, and every one was listening to his account, when
D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos were perceived at the door, behind the cardinal.

芒聙聹Ah, madame,芒聙聺 said Comminges, hastening to D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹here is one who
can tell you better than myself, for he was my protector. Without him I
should probably at this moment be a dead fish in the nets at Saint
Cloud, for it was a question of nothing less than throwing me into the
river. Speak, D芒聙聶Artagnan, speak.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan had been a hundred times in the same room with the queen
since he had become lieutenant of the musketeers, but her majesty had
never once spoken to him.

芒聙聹Well, sir,芒聙聺 at last said Anne of Austria, 芒聙聹you are silent, after
rendering such a service?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I have nought to say, save that my life
is ever at your majesty芒聙聶s service, and that I shall only be happy the
day I lose it for you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know that, sir; I have known that,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹a long time;
therefore I am delighted to be able thus publicly to mark my gratitude
and my esteem.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Permit me, madame,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹to reserve a portion for my
friend; like myself芒聙聺 (he laid an emphasis on these words) 芒聙聹an ancient
musketeer of the company of Tr脙漏ville; he has done wonders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹His name?芒聙聺 asked the queen.

芒聙聹In the regiment,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹he is called Porthos芒聙聺 (the queen
started), 芒聙聹but his true name is the Chevalier du Vallon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹De Bracieux de Pierrefonds,芒聙聺 added Porthos.

芒聙聹These names are too numerous for me to remember them all, and I will
content myself with the first,芒聙聺 said the queen, graciously. Porthos
bowed. At this moment the coadjutor was announced; a cry of surprise
ran through the royal assemblage. Although the coadjutor had preached
that same morning it was well known that he leaned much to the side of
the Fronde; and Mazarin, in requesting the archbishop of Paris to make
his nephew preach, had evidently had the intention of administering to
Monsieur de Retz one of those Italian kicks he so much enjoyed giving.

The fact was, in leaving Notre Dame the coadjutor had learned the event
of the day. Although almost engaged to the leaders of the Fronde he had
not gone so far but that retreat was possible should the court offer
him the advantages for which he was ambitious and to which the
coadjutorship was but a stepping-stone. Monsieur de Retz wished to
become archbishop in his uncle芒聙聶s place, and cardinal, like Mazarin; and
the popular party could with difficulty accord him favors so entirely
royal. He therefore hastened to the palace to congratulate the queen on
the battle of Lens, determined beforehand to act with or against the
court, as his congratulations were well or ill received.

The coadjutor possessed, perhaps, as much wit as all those put together
who were assembled at the court to laugh at him. His speech, therefore,
was so well turned, that in spite of the great wish felt by the
courtiers to laugh, they could find no point on which to vent their
ridicule. He concluded by saying that he placed his feeble influence at
her majesty芒聙聶s command.

During the whole time he was speaking, the queen appeared to be well
pleased with the coadjutor芒聙聶s harangue; but terminating as it did with
such a phrase, the only one which could be caught at by the jokers,
Anne turned around and directed a glance toward her favorites, which
announced that she delivered up the coadjutor to their tender mercies.
Immediately the wits of the court plunged into satire. Nogent-Beautin,
the fool of the court, exclaimed that 芒聙聹the queen was very happy to have
the succor of religion at such a moment.芒聙聺 This caused a universal burst
of laughter. The Count de Villeroy said that 芒聙聹he did not know how any
fear could be entertained for a moment, when the court had, to defend
itself against the parliament and the citizens of Paris, his holiness
the coadjutor, who by a signal could raise an army of curates, church
porters and vergers.芒聙聺

The Mar脙漏chal de la Meilleraie added that in case the coadjutor should
appear on the field of battle it would be a pity that he should not be
distinguished in the m脙陋l脙漏e by wearing a red hat, as Henry IV. had been
distinguished by his white plume at the battle of Ivry.

During this storm, Gondy, who had it in his power to make it most
unpleasant for the jesters, remained calm and stern. The queen at last
asked him if he had anything to add to the fine discourse he had just
made to her.

芒聙聹Yes, madame,芒聙聺 replied the coadjutor; 芒聙聹I have to beg you to reflect
twice ere you cause a civil war in the kingdom.芒聙聺

The queen turned her back and the laughing recommenced.

The coadjutor bowed and left the palace, casting upon the cardinal such
a glance as is best understood by mortal foes. That glance was so sharp
that it penetrated the heart of Mazarin, who, reading in it a
declaration of war, seized D芒聙聶Artagnan by the arm and said:

芒聙聹If occasion requires, monsieur, you will remember that man who has
just gone out, will you not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord,芒聙聺 he replied. Then, turning toward Porthos, 芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺
said he, 芒聙聹this has a bad look. I dislike these quarrels among men of
the church.芒聙聺

Gondy withdrew, distributing benedictions on his way, and finding a
malicious satisfaction in causing the adherents of his foes to
prostrate themselves at his feet.

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 he murmured, as he left the threshold of the palace: 芒聙聹ungrateful
court! faithless court! cowardly court! I will teach you how to laugh
to-morrow芒聙聰but in another manner.芒聙聺

But whilst they were indulging in extravagant joy at the Palais Royal,
to increase the hilarity of the queen, Mazarin, a man of sense, and
whose fear, moreover, gave him foresight, lost no time in making idle
and dangerous jokes; he went out after the coadjutor, settled his
account, locked up his gold, and had confidential workmen to contrive
hiding places in his walls.

On his return home the coadjutor was informed that a young man had come
in after his departure and was waiting for him; he started with delight
when, on demanding the name of this young man, he learned that it was
Louvi脙篓res. He hastened to his cabinet. Broussel芒聙聶s son was there, still
furious, and still bearing bloody marks of his struggle with the king芒聙聶s
officers. The only precaution he had taken in coming to the
archbishopric was to leave his arquebuse in the hands of a friend.

The coadjutor went to him and held out his hand. The young man gazed at
him as if he would have read the secret of his heart.

芒聙聹My dear Monsieur Louvi脙篓res,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹believe me, I am
truly concerned for the misfortune which has happened to you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is that true, and do you speak seriously?芒聙聺 asked Louvi脙篓res.

芒聙聹From the depth of my heart,芒聙聺 said Gondy.

芒聙聹In that case, my lord, the time for words has passed and the hour for
action is at hand; my lord, in three days, if you wish it, my father
will be out of prison and in six months you may be cardinal.芒聙聺

The coadjutor started.

芒聙聹Oh! let us speak frankly,芒聙聺 continued Louvi脙篓res, 芒聙聹and act in a
straightforward manner. Thirty thousand crowns in alms is not given, as
you have done for the last six months, out of pure Christian charity;

of genius and you know your worth. As for me, I hate the court and have

people, of whom you can dispose, and I will bring you the citizens and

It was now the coadjutor芒聙聶s turn to fix his piercing eyes on Louvi脙篓res.

芒聙聹But, Monsieur Louvi脙篓res, are you aware that it is simply civil war you
are proposing to me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have been preparing long enough, my lord, for it to be welcome to

芒聙聹Never mind,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor; 芒聙聹you must be well aware that this
requires reflection.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And how many hours of reflection do you ask?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Twelve hours, sir; is it too long?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is now noon; at midnight I will be at your house.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If I should not be in, wait for me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good! at midnight, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹At midnight, my dear Monsieur Louvi脙篓res.芒聙聺

When once more alone Gondy sent to summon all the curates with whom he
had any connection to his house. Two hours later, thirty officiating

芒聙聹Simply this,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor. 芒聙聹You are the directors of all
consciences. Well, undermine in them the miserable prejudice of respect

Three curates remained芒聙聰those of St. Merri, St. Sulpice and St.
Eustache. The others withdrew.

芒聙聹You think, then, that you can help me more efficaciously than your

芒聙聹We hope so,芒聙聺 answered the curates.

芒聙聹Let us hear. Monsieur de St. Merri, you begin.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, I have in my parish a man who might be of the greatest use to
you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who and what is this man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A shopkeeper in the Rue des Lombards, who has great influence upon the

芒聙聹What is his name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is named Planchet, who himself also caused a rising about six weeks

芒聙聹And can you find him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope so. I think he has not been arrested, and as I am his wife芒聙聶s

芒聙聹Very well, sir, find this man, and when you have found him bring him

芒聙聹We will be with you at six o芒聙聶clock, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, my dear curate, and may God assist you!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you, sir?芒聙聺 continued Gondy, turning to the curate of St. Sulpice.

芒聙聹I, my lord,芒聙聺 said the latter, 芒聙聹I know a man who has rendered great
services to a very popular prince and who would make an excellent

芒聙聹I know him also, but unfortunately he is not in Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord, he has been for three days at the Rue Cassette.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And wherefore has he not been to see me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He was told芒聙聰my lord will pardon me芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly, speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That your lordship was about to treat with the court.芒聙聺

Gondy bit his lips.

芒聙聹They are mistaken; bring him here at eight o芒聙聶clock, sir, and may
Heaven bless you as I bless you!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now 芒聙聶tis your turn,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, turning to the last that

芒聙聹Better, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹_Diable!_ think what a solemn engagement you are making; one has

芒聙聹I offer you a beggar, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! ah!芒聙聺 said Gondy, reflecting, 芒聙聹you are right, sir; some one who
could raise the legion of paupers who choke up the crossings of Paris;

芒聙聹Exactly your man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bravo! and the man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A plain and simple beggar, as I have said, my lord, who asks for alms,
as he gives holy water; a practice he has carried on for six years on
