芒聙聹And you say that he has a great influence over his compeers?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you aware, my lord, that mendacity is an organized body, a kind of
association of those who have nothing against those who have
everything; an association in which every one takes his share; one that
elects a leader?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, I have heard it said,芒聙聺 replied the coadjutor.

芒聙聹Well, the man whom I offer you is a general syndic.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what do you know of him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing, my lord, except that he is tormented with remorse.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What makes you think so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the twenty-eighth of every month he makes me say a mass for the
repose of the soul of one who died a violent death; yesterday I said
this mass again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And his name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Maillard; but I do not think it is his right one.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And think you that we should find him at this hour at his post?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us go and see your beggar, sir, and if he is such as you describe
him, you are right芒聙聰it will be you who have discovered the true
treasure.芒聙聺

Gondy dressed himself as an officer, put on a felt cap with a red
feather, hung on a long sword, buckled spurs to his boots, wrapped
himself in an ample cloak and followed the curate.

The coadjutor and his companion passed through all the streets lying
between the archbishopric and the St. Eustache Church, watching
carefully to ascertain the popular feeling. The people were in an
excited mood, but, like a swarm of frightened bees, seemed not to know
at what point to concentrate; and it was very evident that if leaders
of the people were not provided all this agitation would pass off in
idle buzzing.

On arriving at the Rue des Prouvaires, the curate pointed toward the
square before the church.

芒聙聹Stop!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹there he is at his post.芒聙聺

Gondy looked at the spot indicated and perceived a beggar seated in a
chair and leaning against one of the moldings; a little basin was near
him and he held a holy water brush in his hand.

芒聙聹Is it by permission that he remains there?芒聙聺 asked Gondy.

芒聙聹No, my lord; these places are bought. I believe this man paid his
predecessor a hundred pistoles for his.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The rascal is rich, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Some of those men sometimes die worth twenty thousand and twenty-five
and thirty thousand francs and sometimes more.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hum!芒聙聺 said Gondy, laughing; 芒聙聹I was not aware my alms were so well
invested.芒聙聺

In the meantime they were advancing toward the square, and the moment

He was a man between sixty-six and sixty-eight years of age, little,
rather stout, with gray hair and light eyes. His countenance denoted
the struggle between two opposite principles芒聙聰a wicked nature, subdued
by determination, perhaps by repentance.

He started on seeing the cavalier with the curate. The latter and the
coadjutor touched the brush with the tips of their fingers and made the
sign of the cross; the coadjutor threw a piece of money into the hat,
which was on the ground.

芒聙聹Maillard,芒聙聺 began the curate, 芒聙聹this gentleman and I have come to talk
with you a little.芒聙聺

芒聙聹With me!芒聙聺 said the mendicant; 芒聙聹it is a great honor for a poor

There was an ironical tone in his voice which he could not quite

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 continued the curate, apparently accustomed to this tone, 芒聙聹yes,

heard said by people going in and out of the church.芒聙聺

The mendicant shook his head.

芒聙聹These are melancholy doings, your reverence, which always fall again
upon the poor. As to what is said, everybody is discontented, everybody
complains, but 芒聙聵everybody芒聙聶 means 芒聙聵nobody.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Explain yourself, my good friend,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor.

芒聙聹I mean that all these cries, all these complaints, these curses,

lightning will not strike until there is a hand to guide it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 said Gondy, 芒聙聹you seem to be a clever and a thoughtful man;
are you disposed to take a part in a little civil war, should we have

personal influence and the influence you have acquired over your

芒聙聹Yes, sir, provided this war were approved of by the church and would
advance the end I wish to attain芒聙聰I mean, the remission of my sins.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The war will not only be approved of, but directed by the church. As

芒聙聹Consider, Maillard,芒聙聺 said the curate, 芒聙聹that I have recommended you to

芒聙聹I know, monsieur le cur脙漏,芒聙聺 said the beggar, 芒聙聹that you have always been

芒聙聹And do you think your power as great with the fraternity as monsieur

芒聙聹I think they have some esteem for me,芒聙聺 said the mendicant with pride,
芒聙聹and that not only will they obey me, but wherever I go they will

芒聙聹And could you count on fifty resolute men, good, unemployed, but

芒聙聹I think,芒聙聺 said the beggar, 芒聙聹I can undertake things more difficult and

芒聙聹I will undertake to throw up fifty, and when the day comes, to defend

芒聙聹I芒聙聶faith!芒聙聺 exclaimed Gondy, 芒聙聹you speak with a certainty that gives me
pleasure; and since monsieur le cur脙漏 can answer for you芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹I answer for him,芒聙聺 said the curate.

芒聙聹Here is a bag containing five hundred pistoles in gold; make all your
arrangements, and tell me where I shall be able to find you this
evening at ten o芒聙聶clock.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It must be on some elevated place, whence a given signal may be seen
in every part of Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Shall I give you a line for the vicar of St. Jacques de la Boucherie?
he will let you into the rooms in his tower,芒聙聺 said the curate.

芒聙聹Capital,芒聙聺 answered the mendicant.

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹this evening, at ten o芒聙聶clock, and if I am
pleased with you another bag of five hundred pistoles will be at your
disposal.芒聙聺

The eyes of the mendicant dashed with cupidity, but he quickly
suppressed his emotion.

芒聙聹This evening, sir,芒聙聺 he replied, 芒聙聹all will be ready.芒聙聺




Chapter XLVI.
The Tower of St. Jacques de la Boucherie.


At a quarter to six o芒聙聶clock, Monsieur de Gondy, having finished his
business, returned to the archiepiscopal palace.

At six o芒聙聶clock the curate of St. Merri was announced.

The coadjutor glanced rapidly behind and saw that he was followed by
another man. The curate then entered, followed by Planchet.

芒聙聹Your holiness,芒聙聺 said the curate, 芒聙聹here is the person of whom I had the
honor to speak to you.芒聙聺

Planchet saluted in the manner of one accustomed to fine houses.

芒聙聹And you are disposed to serve the cause of the people?芒聙聺 asked Gondy.

芒聙聹Most undoubtedly,芒聙聺 said Planchet. 芒聙聹I am a Frondist from my heart. You
see in me, such as I am, a person sentenced to be hung.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And on what account?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I rescued from the hands of Mazarin芒聙聶s police a noble lord whom they
were conducting back to the Bastile, where he had been for five years.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Will you name him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, you know him well, my lord芒聙聰it is Count de Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! really, yes,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹I have heard this affair
mentioned. You raised the whole district, so they told me!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very nearly,芒聙聺 replied Planchet, with a self-satisfied air.

芒聙聹And your business is芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹That of a confectioner, in the Rue des Lombards.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Explain to me how it happens that, following so peaceful a business,
you had such warlike inclinations.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why does my lord, belonging to the church, now receive me in the dress
of an officer, with a sword at his side and spurs to his boots?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not badly answered, i芒聙聶faith,芒聙聺 said Gondy, laughing; 芒聙聹but I have, you
must know, always had, in spite of my bands, warlike inclinations.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, my lord, before I became a confectioner I myself was three years
sergeant in the Piedmontese regiment, and before I became sergeant I
was for eighteen months the servant of Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The lieutenant of musketeers?芒聙聺 asked Gondy.

芒聙聹Himself, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But he is said to be a furious Mazarinist.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Phew!芒聙聺 whistled Planchet.

芒聙聹What do you mean by that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing, my lord; Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan belongs to the service; Monsieur
d芒聙聶Artagnan makes it his business to defend the cardinal, who pays him,
as much as we make it ours, we citizens, to attack him, whom he robs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are an intelligent fellow, my friend; can we count upon you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You may count upon me, my lord, provided you want to make a complete
upheaval of the city.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis that exactly. How many men, think you, you could collect together
to-night?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two hundred muskets and five hundred halberds.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let there be only one man in every district who can do as much and by
to-morrow we shall have quite a powerful army. Are you disposed to obey
Count de Rochefort?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I would follow him to hell, and that is saying not a little, as I
believe him entirely capable of the descent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bravo!芒聙聺

芒聙聹By what sign to-morrow shall we be able to distinguish friends from
foes?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Every Frondist must put a knot of straw in his hat.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good! Give the watchword.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you want money?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Money never comes amiss at any time, my lord; if one has it not, one
must do without it; with it, matters go on much better and more
rapidly.芒聙聺

Gondy went to a box and drew forth a bag.

芒聙聹Here are five hundred pistoles,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹and if the action goes off
well you may reckon upon a similar sum to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will give a faithful account of the sum to your lordship,芒聙聺 said
Planchet, putting the bag under his arm.

芒聙聹That is right; I recommend the cardinal to your attention.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Make your mind easy, he is in good hands.芒聙聺

Planchet went out, the curate remaining for a moment.

芒聙聹Are you satisfied, my lord?芒聙聺 he asked.

芒聙聹Yes; he appears to be a resolute fellow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, he will do more than he has promised.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He will do wonders then.芒聙聺

The curate rejoined Planchet, who was waiting for him on the stairs.
Ten minutes later the curate of St. Sulpice was announced. As soon as
the door of Gondy芒聙聶s study was opened a man rushed in. It was the Count
de Rochefort.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis you, then, my dear count,芒聙聺 cried Gondy, offering his hand.

芒聙聹You have made up your mind at last, my lord?芒聙聺 said Rochefort.

芒聙聹It has been made up a long time,芒聙聺 said Gondy.

芒聙聹Let us say no more on the subject; you tell me so, I believe you.
Well, we are going to give a ball to Mazarin.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And when will the dance begin?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The invitations are given for this evening,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹but
the violins will not begin to play until to-morrow morning.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You may reckon upon me and upon fifty soldiers which the Chevalier
d芒聙聶Humi脙篓res has promised me whenever I need them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Upon fifty soldiers?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, he is making recruits and he will lend them to me; if any are
missing when the _f脙陋te_ is over, I shall replace them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good, my dear Rochefort; but that is not all. What have you done with
Monsieur de Beaufort?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is in Vend脙麓me, where he will wait until I write to him to return to
Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Write to him; now芒聙聶s the time.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are sure of your enterprise?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but he must make haste; for hardly will the people of Paris have
revolted before we shall have a score of princes begging to lead them.
If he defers he will find the place of honor taken.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Shall I send word to him as coming from you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes certainly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Shall I tell him that he can count on you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the end.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you will leave the command to him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of the war, yes, but in politics芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹You must know it is not his element.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He must leave me to negotiate for my cardinal芒聙聶s hat in my own
fashion.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You care about it, then, so much?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Since they force me to wear a hat of a form which does not become me,芒聙聺
said Gondy, 芒聙聹I wish at least that the hat should be red.芒聙聺

芒聙聹One must not dispute matters of taste and colors,芒聙聺 said Rochefort,
laughing. 芒聙聹I answer for his consent.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How soon can he be here?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In five days.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let him come and he will find a change, I will answer for it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Therefore, go and collect your fifty men and hold yourself in
readiness.芒聙聺

芒聙聹For what?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is there any signal for the general rally?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A knot of straw in the hat.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very good. Adieu, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, my dear Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, Monsieur Mazarin, Monsieur Mazarin,芒聙聺 said Rochefort, leading off
his curate, who had not found an opportunity of uttering a single word
during the foregoing dialogue, 芒聙聹you will see whether I am too old to be
a man of action.芒聙聺

It was half-past nine o芒聙聶clock and the coadjutor required half an hour
to go from the archbishop芒聙聶s palace to the tower of St. Jacques de la
Boucherie. He remarked that a light was burning in one of the highest
windows of the tower. 芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹our syndic is at his post.芒聙聺

He knocked and the door was opened. The vicar himself awaited him,
conducted him to the top of the tower, and when there pointed to a
little door, placed the light which he had brought with him in a corner
of the wall, that the coadjutor might be able to find it on his return,
and went down again. Although the key was in the door the coadjutor
knocked.

芒聙聹Come in,芒聙聺 said a voice which he recognized as that of the mendicant,
whom he found lying on a kind of truckle bed. He rose on the entrance
of the coadjutor, and at that moment ten o芒聙聶clock struck.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Gondy, 芒聙聹have you kept your word with me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not exactly,芒聙聺 replied the mendicant.

芒聙聹How is that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You asked me for five hundred men, did you not? Well, I have ten
thousand for you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are not boasting?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you wish for a proof?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

There were three candles alight, each of which burnt before a window,
one looking upon the city, the other upon the Palais Royal, and a third
upon the Rue Saint Denis.

The man went silently to each of the candles and blew them out one
after the other.

芒聙聹What are you doing?芒聙聺 asked the coadjutor.

芒聙聹I have given the signal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹For what?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For the barricades. When you leave this you will behold my men at
work. Only take care you do not break your legs in stumbling over some
chain or your neck by falling in a hole.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good! there is your money, the same sum as that you have received
already. Now remember that you are a general and do not go and drink.芒聙聺

芒聙聹For twenty years I have tasted nothing but water.芒聙聺

The man took the bag from the hands of the coadjutor, who heard the
sound of his fingers counting and handling the gold pieces.

芒聙聹Ah! ah!芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹you are avaricious, my good fellow.芒聙聺

The mendicant sighed and threw down the bag.

芒聙聹Must I always be the same?芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹and shall I never succeed in
overcoming the old leaven? Oh, misery, oh, vanity!芒聙聺

芒聙聹You take it, however.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but I make hereby a vow in your presence, to employ all that
remains to me in pious works.芒聙聺

His face was pale and drawn, like that of a man who had just undergone
some inward struggle.

芒聙聹Singular man!芒聙聺 muttered Gondy, taking his hat to go away; but on
turning around he saw the beggar between him and the door. His first
idea was that this man intended to do him some harm, but on the
contrary he saw him fall on his knees before him with his hands
clasped.

芒聙聹Your blessing, your holiness, before you go, I beseech you!芒聙聺 he cried.

芒聙聹Your holiness!芒聙聺 said Gondy; 芒聙聹my friend, you take me for some one
else.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, your holiness, I take you for what you are, that is to say, the
coadjutor; I recognized you at the first glance.芒聙聺

Gondy smiled. 芒聙聹And you want my blessing?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Yes, I have need of it.芒聙聺

The mendicant uttered these words in a tone of such humility, such
earnest repentance, that Gondy placed his hand upon him and gave him
his benediction with all the unction of which he was capable.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said Gondy, 芒聙聹there is a communion between us. I have blessed you
and you are sacred to me. Come, have you committed some crime, pursued
by human justice, from which I can protect you?芒聙聺

The beggar shook his head. 芒聙聹The crime which I have committed, my lord,
has no call upon human justice, and you can only deliver me from it by
blessing me frequently, as you have just done.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, be candid,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, 芒聙聹you have not all your life
followed the trade which you do now?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, my lord. I have pursued it for six years only.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And previously, where were you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In the Bastile.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And before you went to the Bastile?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will tell you, my lord, on the day when you are willing to hear my
confession.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good! At whatsoever hour of the day or night you may present yourself,
remember that I shall be ready to give you absolution.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank you, my lord,芒聙聺 said the mendicant in a hoarse voice. 芒聙聹But I am
not yet ready to receive it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well. Adieu.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, your holiness,芒聙聺 said the mendicant, opening the door and
bending low before the prelate.




Chapter XLVII.
The Riot.


It was about eleven o芒聙聶clock at night. Gondy had not walked a hundred
steps ere he perceived the strange change which had been made in the
streets of Paris.

The whole city seemed peopled with fantastic beings; silent shadows
were seen unpaving the streets and others dragging and upsetting great
wagons, whilst others again dug ditches large enough to ingulf whole
regiments of horsemen. These active beings flitted here and there like
so many demons completing some unknown labor; these were the beggars of
the Court of Miracles芒聙聰the agents of the giver of holy water in the
Square of Saint Eustache, preparing barricades for the morrow.

Gondy gazed on these deeds of darkness, on these nocturnal laborers,
with a kind of fear; he asked himself, if, after having called forth
these foul creatures from their dens, he should have the power of
making them retire again. He felt almost inclined to cross himself when
one of these beings happened to approach him. He reached the Rue Saint
Honor脙漏 and went up it toward the Rue de la Ferronnerie; there the
aspect changed; here it was the tradesmen who were running from shop to
shop; their doors seemed closed like their shutters, but they were only
pushed to in such a manner as to open and allow the men, who seemed
fearful of showing what they carried, to enter, closing immediately.
These men were shopkeepers, who had arms to lend to those who had none.

One individual went from door to door, bending under the weight of
swords, guns, muskets and every kind of weapon, which he deposited as
fast as he could. By the light of a lantern the coadjutor recognized
Planchet.

The coadjutor proceeded onward to the quay by way of the Rue de la
Monnaie; there he found groups of bourgeois clad in black cloaks or
gray, according as they belonged to the upper or lower bourgeoisie.
They were standing motionless, while single men passed from one group
to another. All these cloaks, gray or black, were raised behind by the
point of a sword, or before by the barrel of an arquebuse or a musket.

On reaching the Pont Neuf the coadjutor found it strictly guarded and a
man approached him.

芒聙聹Who are you?芒聙聺 asked the man. 芒聙聹I do not know you for one of us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then it is because you do not know your friends, my dear Monsieur
Louvi脙篓res,芒聙聺 said the coadjutor, raising his hat.

Louvi脙篓res recognized him and bowed.

Gondy continued his way and went as far as the Tour de Nesle. There he
saw a lengthy chain of people gliding under the walls. They might be
said to be a procession of ghosts, for they were all wrapped in white
cloaks. When they reached a certain spot these men appeared to be
annihilated, one after the other, as if the earth had opened under
their feet. Gondy, edged into a corner, saw them vanish from the first
until the last but one. The last raised his eyes, to ascertain,
doubtless, that neither his companions nor himself had been watched,
and, in spite of the darkness, he perceived Gondy. He walked straight
up to him and placed a pistol to his throat.

芒聙聹Halloo! Monsieur de Rochefort,芒聙聺 said Gondy, laughing, 芒聙聹are you a boy
to play with firearms?芒聙聺

Rochefort recognized the voice.

芒聙聹Ah, it is you, my lord!芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹The very same. What people are you leading thus into the bowels of the
earth?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My fifty recruits from the Chevalier d芒聙聶Humi脙篓res, who are destined to
enter the light cavalry and who have only received as yet for their
equipment their white cloaks.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And where are you going?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the house of one of my friends, a sculptor, only we enter by the
trap through which he lets down his marble.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very good,芒聙聺 said Gondy, shaking Rochefort by the hand, who descended
in his turn and closed the trap after him.

It was now one o芒聙聶clock in the morning and the coadjutor returned home.
He opened a window and leaned out to listen. A strange,
incomprehensible, unearthly sound seemed to pervade the whole city; one
felt that something unusual and terrible was happening in all the
streets, now dark as ocean芒聙聶s most unfathomable caves. From time to time
a dull sound was heard, like that of a rising tempest or a billow of
the sea; but nothing clear, nothing distinct, nothing intelligible; it
was like those mysterious subterraneous noises that precede an
earthquake.

The work of revolt continued the whole night thus. The next morning, on
awaking, Paris seemed to be startled at her own appearance. It was like
a besieged town. Armed men, shouldering muskets, watched over the
barricades with menacing looks; words of command, patrols, arrests,
executions, even, were encountered at every step. Those bearing plumed
hats and gold swords were stopped and made to cry, 芒聙聹Long live
Broussel!芒聙聺 芒聙聹Down with Mazarin!芒聙聺 and whoever refused to comply with this
ceremony was hooted at, spat upon and even beaten. They had not yet
begun to slay, but it was well felt that the inclination to do so was
not wanting.

The barricades had been pushed as far as the Palais Royal. From the Rue
de Bons Enfants to that of the Ferronnerie, from the Rue Saint
Thomas-du-Louvre to the Pont Neuf, from the Rue Richelieu to the Porte
Saint Honor脙漏, there were more than ten thousand armed men; those who
were at the front hurled defiance at the impassive sentinels of the
regiment of guards posted around the Palais Royal, the gates of which
were closed behind them, a precaution which made their situation
precarious. Among these thousands moved, in bands numbering from one
hundred to two hundred, pale and haggard men, clothed in rags, who bore
a sort of standard on which was inscribed these words: 芒聙聹BEHOLD THE
MISERY OF THE PEOPLE!芒聙聺 Wherever these men passed, frenzied cries were
heard; and there were so many of these bands that the cries were to be
heard in all directions.

The astonishment of Mazarin and of Anne of Austria was great when it
was announced to them that the city, which the previous evening they
had left entirely tranquil, had awakened to such feverish commotion;
nor would either the one or the other believe the reports that were
brought to them, declaring they would rather rely on the evidence of
their own eyes and ears. Then a window was opened and when they saw and
heard they were convinced.

Mazarin shrugged his shoulders and pretended to despise the populace;
but he turned visibly pale and ran to his closet, trembling all over,
locked up his gold and jewels in his caskets and put his finest
diamonds on his fingers. As for the queen, furious, and left to her own
guidance, she went for the Mar脙漏chal de la Meilleraie and desired him to
take as many men as he pleased and to go and see what was the meaning
of this pleasantry.

The marshal was ordinarily very adventurous and was wont to hesitate at
nothing; and he had that lofty contempt for the populace which army
officers usually profess. He took a hundred and fifty men and attempted
to go out by the Pont du Louvre, but there he met Rochefort and his
fifty horsemen, attended by more than five hundred men. The marshal
made no attempt to force that barrier and returned up the quay. But at
Pont Neuf he found Louvi脙篓res and his bourgeois. This time the marshal
charged, but he was welcomed by musket shots, while stones fell like
hail from all the windows. He left there three men.

He beat a retreat toward the market, but there he met Planchet with his
halberdiers; their halberds were leveled at him threateningly. He
attempted to ride over those gray cloaks, but the gray cloaks held
their ground and the marshal retired toward the Rue Saint Honor脙漏,
leaving four of his guards dead on the field of battle.

The marshal then entered the Rue Saint Honor脙漏, but there he was opposed
by the barricades of the mendicant of Saint Eustache. They were
guarded, not only by armed men, but even by women and children. Master
Friquet, the owner of a pistol and of a sword which Louvi脙篓res had given
him, had organized a company of rogues like himself and was making a
tremendous racket.

The marshal thought this barrier not so well fortified as the others
and determined to break through it. He dismounted twenty men to make a
breach in the barricade, whilst he and others, remaining on their
horses, were to protect the assailants. The twenty men marched straight
toward the barrier, but from behind the beams, from among the
wagon-wheels and from the heights of the rocks a terrible fusillade
burst forth and at the same time Planchet芒聙聶s halberdiers appeared at the
corner of the Cemetery of the Innocents, and Louvi脙篓res芒聙聶s bourgeois at
the corner of the Rue de la Monnaie.

The Mar脙漏chal de la Meilleraie was caught between two fires, but he was
brave and made up his mind to die where he was. He returned blow for
blow and cries of pain began to be heard in the crowd. The guards, more
skillful, did greater execution; but the bourgeois, more numerous,
overwhelmed them with a veritable hurricane of iron. Men fell around
him as they had fallen at Rocroy or at Lerida. Fontrailles, his
aide-de-camp, had an arm broken; his horse had received a bullet in his
neck and he had difficulty in controlling him, maddened by pain. In
short, he had reached that supreme moment when the bravest feel a
shudder in their veins, when suddenly, in the direction of the Rue de
l芒聙聶Arbre-Sec, the crowd opened, crying: 芒聙聹Long live the coadjutor!芒聙聺 and
Gondy, in surplice and cloak, appeared, moving tranquilly in the midst
of the fusillade and bestowing his benedictions to the right and left,
as undisturbed as if he were leading a procession of the _F脙陋te Dieu_.

All fell to their knees. The marshal recognized him and hastened to
meet him.

芒聙聹Get me out of this, in Heaven芒聙聶s name!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹or I shall leave my
carcass here and those of all my men.芒聙聺

A great tumult arose, in the midst of which even the noise of thunder
could not have been heard. Gondy raised his hand and demanded silence.
All were still.

芒聙聹My children,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹this is the Mar脙漏chal de la Meilleraie, as to

芒聙聹_Morbleu!_芒聙聺 cried the latter, 芒聙聹I should say that I do pledge myself to

芒聙聹He gives you his word of honor,芒聙聺 said Gondy.

The marshal raised his hand in token of assent.

芒聙聹Long live the coadjutor!芒聙聺 cried the crowd. Some voices even added:
芒聙聹Long live the marshal!芒聙聺 But all took up the cry in chorus: 芒聙聹Down with
Mazarin!芒聙聺

The crowd gave place, the barricade was opened, and the marshal, with

bandits, some of them making a presence of beating drums and others
imitating the sound of the trumpet. It was almost a triumphal
procession; only, behind the guards the barricades were closed again.
The marshal bit his fingers.

In the meantime, as we have said, Mazarin was in his closet, putting

芒聙聹Speak, speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聵Down with Mazarin!芒聙聶 I芒聙聶faith, the treasonable word is out.芒聙聺

Mazarin smiled, but became very pale.

芒聙聹And you did cry?芒聙聺 he asked.

芒聙聹I芒聙聶faith, no,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I was not in voice; Monsieur du Vallon
has a cold and did not cry either. Then, my lord芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Look at my hat and cloak.芒聙聺

And D芒聙聶Artagnan displayed four gunshot holes in his cloak and two in his

At this moment the tumult was heard nearer.

芒聙聹See what is going on, Monsieur D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said he.

D芒聙聶Artagnan went to the window with his habitual composure. 芒聙聹Oho!芒聙聺 said

He stopped at the end of the corridor and said:

芒聙聹We do not give ourselves twice over,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹we have given

芒聙聹Very well, then,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹enter this cabinet and wait till I

And turning off he entered the drawing-room by another door.




Chapter XLVIII.
The Riot becomes a Revolution.


The closet into which D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos had been ushered was
separated from the drawing-room where the queen was by tapestried
curtains only, and this thin partition enabled them to hear all that
passed in the adjoining room, whilst the aperture between the two
hangings, small as it was, permitted them to see.

The queen was standing in the room, pale with anger; her self-control,
however, was so great that it might have been imagined that she was
calm. Comminges, Villequier and Guitant were behind her and the women
again were behind the men. The Chancellor Sequier, who twenty years
previously had persecuted her so ruthlessly, stood before her, relating
how his carriage had been smashed, how he had been pursued and had
rushed into the Hotel d芒聙聶O芒聙聰芒聙聰, that the hotel was immediately invaded,
pillaged and devastated; happily he had time to reach a closet hidden
behind tapestry, in which he was secreted by an old woman, together
with his brother, the Bishop of Meaux. Then the danger was so imminent,
the rioters came so near, uttering such threats, that the chancellor
thought his last hour had come and confessed himself to his brother
priest, so as to be all ready to die in case he was discovered.
Fortunately, however, he had not been taken; the people, believing that
he had escaped by some back entrance, retired and left him at liberty
to retreat. Then, disguised in the clothes of the Marquis d芒聙聶O芒聙聰芒聙聰, he had
left the hotel, stumbling over the bodies of an officer and two guards
who had been killed whilst defending the street door.

During the recital Mazarin entered and glided noiselessly up to the
queen to listen.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said the queen, when the chancellor had finished speaking;
芒聙聹what do you think of it all?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I think that matters look very gloomy, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what step would you propose to me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I _could_ propose one to your majesty, but I dare not.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You may, you may, sir,芒聙聺 said the queen with a bitter smile; 芒聙聹you were
not so timid once.芒聙聺

The chancellor reddened and stammered some words.

芒聙聹It is not a question of the past, but of the present,芒聙聺 said the queen;
芒聙聹you said you could give me advice芒聙聰what is it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said the chancellor, hesitating, 芒聙聹it would be to release
Broussel.芒聙聺

The queen, although already pale, became visibly paler and her face was

芒聙聹Release Broussel!芒聙聺 she cried, 芒聙聹never!芒聙聺

At this moment steps were heard in the ante-room and without any

芒聙聹Ah, there you are, mar脙漏chal,芒聙聺 cried Anne of Austria joyfully. 芒聙聹I trust

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied the mar脙漏chal, 芒聙聹I have left three men on the Pont

芒聙聹Yes, madame; but I only came back on one condition芒聙聰that I would

The irony with which these words were uttered did not escape the

芒聙聹Pardon, madame,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I am not a lawyer, I am a mere soldier, and

The queen smiled.

芒聙聹Well, then, madame, yes, I did feel fear; and though I have been

(芒聙聹Bravo,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan in a whisper to Porthos; 芒聙聹well answered.芒聙聺)

芒聙聹Never!芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹never!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your majesty is mistress,芒聙聺 said La Meilleraie, retreating a few steps.

芒聙聹To give your majesty芒聙聶s reply to those who await it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Stay, mar脙漏chal; I will not appear to parley with rebels.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, I have pledged my word, and unless you order me to be arrested

Anne of Austria芒聙聶s eyes shot glances of fire.

The fiery Spanish woman spoke in a tone so imperative that the mar脙漏chal

(D芒聙聶Artagnan turned to Porthos. 芒聙聹How will this end?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹We shall soon see,芒聙聺 said Porthos, in his tranquil way.)

芒聙聹Well, sir,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹what is your opinion of this riot?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your majesty wishes, then芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

The coadjutor looked at the queen, who replied by a sign in the

芒聙聹Were I in her majesty芒聙聶s place,芒聙聺 said Gondy, coldly, 芒聙聹I should not

The color mounted to the queen芒聙聶s face; her fine blue eyes seemed to

芒聙聹Give up Broussel!芒聙聺 she cried; 芒聙聹fine counsel, indeed. Upon my word! one

Gondy remained firm, and the abuse of the day seemed to glide over his

Mazarin, according to his custom, was thinking much and saying little.

芒聙聹Ho! ho!芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹good advice, advice of a friend. I, too, would give

芒聙聹If you yield him dead, all will indeed be at an end, my lord, but

(芒聙聹This is a council of state,芒聙聺 D芒聙聶Artagnan remarked to Porthos; 芒聙聹but we

芒聙聹At the Saint Gervais bastion,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹There and elsewhere.芒聙聺)

The coadjutor let the storm pass over his head and resumed, still with

芒聙聹Madame, if the opinion I have submitted to you does not please you it

芒聙聹Thus, then, it is your opinion,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria, with a sneer

芒聙聹Yes, madame,芒聙聺 replied the coadjutor, gravely.

芒聙聹But if I am to believe you, sir, the people seem to have thrown off

芒聙聹Yes; but fortunately we have no Oliver Cromwell in France,芒聙聺 replied

Every one shuddered and there was a moment of silence, during which the

(芒聙聹Porthos,芒聙聺 murmured D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹look well at that priest.芒聙聺

Porthos looked at D芒聙聶Artagnan in astonishment. Evidently he did not

芒聙聹Your majesty,芒聙聺 continued the coadjutor, pitilessly, 芒聙聹is about to take

violent and such as will still further exasperate the rioters.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case, you, monsieur le coadjuteur, who have such power over
them and are at the same time friendly to us,芒聙聺 said the queen,
ironically, 芒聙聹will quiet them by bestowing your blessing upon them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Perhaps it will be too late,芒聙聺 said Gondy, still unmoved; 芒聙聹perhaps I
shall have lost all influence; while by giving up Broussel your majesty
will strike at the root of the sedition and will gain the right to
punish severely any revival of the revolt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have I not, then, that right?芒聙聺 cried the queen.

芒聙聹If you have it, use it,芒聙聺 replied Gondy.

(芒聙聹_Peste!_芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to Porthos. 芒聙聹There is a man after my own
heart. Oh! if he were minister and I were his D芒聙聶Artagnan, instead of

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Porthos.)

The queen made a sign for every one, except Mazarin, to quit the room;
and Gondy bowed, as if to leave with the rest.

芒聙聹Stay, sir,芒聙聺 said Anne to him.

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 thought Gondy, 芒聙聹she is going to yield.芒聙聺

(芒聙聹She is going to have him killed,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to Porthos, 芒聙聹but at

芒聙聹And I, too,芒聙聺 said Porthos.)

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 muttered Mazarin, sitting down, 芒聙聹we shall soon see something
startling.芒聙聺

The queen芒聙聶s eyes followed the retreating figures and when the last had

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 cried Anne, 芒聙聹to humble myself thus! Am I, or am I not, the queen?

And she sprang toward Gondy, whom assuredly at that moment she hated

(芒聙聹He were a dead man芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹if there were still a Vitry at

This rough appeal, marked by the eloquence which characterized Mazarin

The queen, on her part, thus chided, softened immediately and sat down,

芒聙聹Ah! how much you have need of me, cunning rogue!芒聙聺 thought Gondy.

(芒聙聹And what will he promise us?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹_Peste_, if he is

芒聙聹For you? I will give you the baton of the Mar脙漏chal de la Meilleraie,

芒聙聹And I,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹think that in such a case other embankments

Gondy looked at Mazarin, astonished, and Mazarin approached the queen

Gondy smiled, the queen芒聙聶s color rose and Mazarin grew even paler.

芒聙聹What is that again?芒聙聺 he asked.

At this moment Comminges rushed into the room.

芒聙聹Pardon, your majesty,芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹but the people have dashed the

芒聙聹Listen, madame,芒聙聺 said Gondy.

The moaning of waves, the noise of thunder, the roaring of a volcano,
cannot be compared with the tempest of cries heard at that moment.

芒聙聹What are my commands?芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹Yes, for time presses.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How many men have you about the Palais Royal?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Six hundred.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Place a hundred around the king and with the remainder sweep away this

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 cried Mazarin, 芒聙聹what are you about?芒聙聺

Comminges went out with a soldier芒聙聶s passive obedience.

At this moment a monstrous battering was heard. One of the gates began
to yield.

芒聙聹Oh! madame,芒聙聺 cried Mazarin, 芒聙聹you have ruined us all芒聙聰the king, yourself

At this cry from the soul of the frightened cardinal, Anne became

芒聙聹It is too late,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, tearing his hair, 芒聙聹too late!芒聙聺

The gale had given way. Hoarse shouts were heard from the excited mob.
D芒聙聶Artagnan put his hand to his sword, motioning to Porthos to follow

芒聙聹Save the queen!芒聙聺 cried Mazarin to the coadjutor.

Gondy sprang to the window and threw it open; he recognized Louvi脙篓res

芒聙聹Not a step further,芒聙聺 he shouted, 芒聙聹the queen is signing!芒聙聺

The queen fell into a chair, took the pen and signed.

The people, kept back by Louvi脙篓res, had not made another step forward;
but the awful murmuring, which indicates an angry people, continued.

The queen had written, 芒聙聹The keeper of the prison at Saint Germain will

The coadjutor, whose eyes devoured her slightest movements, seized the

芒聙聹This is the order,芒聙聺 he said.

All Paris seemed to shout with joy, and then the air resounded with the

芒聙聹Long live the queen!芒聙聺 cried De Gondy; but the cries which replied to

芒聙聹And now that you have obtained what you want, go,芒聙聺 said she, 芒聙聹Monsieur
de Gondy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Whenever her majesty has need of me,芒聙聺 replied the coadjutor, bowing,
芒聙聹her majesty knows I am at her command.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, cursed priest!芒聙聺 cried Anne, when he had retired, stretching out

Mazarin wished to approach her. 芒聙聹Leave me!芒聙聺 she exclaimed; 芒聙聹you are not

芒聙聹It is you who are not a woman,芒聙聺 muttered Mazarin.

Then, after a moment of reverie, he remembered where he had left

The closet was empty.

At the queen芒聙聶s last word, D芒聙聶Artagnan had dragged Porthos into the

芒聙聹Why did you leave the closet, Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 asked the

芒聙聹Because,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹the queen desired every one to leave
and I thought that this command was intended for us as well as for the

芒聙聹And you have been here since芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹About a quarter of an hour,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, motioning to Porthos not

Mazarin saw the sign and remained convinced that D芒聙聶Artagnan had seen

芒聙聹Decidedly, Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, you are the man I have been seeking.

musketeers with his most gracious smile, he re-entered his closet more

though by enchantment.




Chapter XLIX.
Misfortune refreshes the Memory.


Anne of Austria returned to her oratory, furious.

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 she cried, wringing her beautiful hands, 芒聙聹What! the people have
seen Monsieur de Cond脙漏, a prince of the blood royal, arrested by my
mother-in-law, Maria de M脙漏dicis; they saw my mother-in-law, their
former regent, expelled by the cardinal; they saw Monsieur de Vend脙麓me,
that is to say, the son of Henry IV., a prisoner at Vincennes; and
whilst these great personages were imprisoned, insulted and threatened,
they said nothing; and now for a Broussel芒聙聰good God! what, then, is to
become of royalty?芒聙聺

The queen unconsciously touched here upon the exciting question. The
people had made no demonstration for the princes, but they had risen
for Broussel; they were taking the part of a plebeian and in defending
Broussel they instinctively felt they were defending themselves.

During this time Mazarin walked up and down the study, glancing from
time to time at his beautiful Venetian mirror, starred in every
direction. 芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹it is sad, I know well, to be forced to
yield thus; but, pshaw! we shall have our revenge. What matters it
about Broussel芒聙聰it is a name, not a thing.芒聙聺

Mazarin, clever politician as he was, was for once mistaken; Broussel
was a thing, not a name.

The next morning, therefore, when Broussel made his entrance into Paris
in a large carriage, having his son Louvi脙篓res at his side and Friquet
behind the vehicle, the people threw themselves in his way and cries of
芒聙聹Long live Broussel!芒聙聺 芒聙聹Long live our father!芒聙聺 resounded from all parts
and was death to Mazarin芒聙聶s ears; and the cardinal芒聙聶s spies brought bad
news from every direction, which greatly agitated the minister, but was
calmly received by the queen. The latter seemed to be maturing in her
mind some great stroke, a fact which increased the uneasiness of the
cardinal, who knew the proud princess and dreaded much the
determination of Anne of Austria.

The coadjutor returned to parliament more a monarch than king, queen,
and cardinal, all three together. By his advice a decree from
parliament summoned the citizens to lay down their arms and demolish
the barricades. They now knew that it required but one hour to take up
arms again and one night to reconstruct the barricades.

Rochefort had returned to the Chevalier d芒聙聶Humi脙篓res his fifty horsemen,
less two, missing at roll call. But the chevalier was himself at heart
a Frondist and would hear nothing said of compensation.

The mendicant had gone to his old place on the steps of Saint Eustache
and was again distributing holy water with one hand and asking alms
with the other. No one could suspect that those two hands had been
engaged with others in drawing out from the social edifice the keystone
of royalty.

Louvi脙篓res was proud and satisfied; he had taken revenge on Mazarin and
had aided in his father芒聙聶s deliverance from prison. His name had been
mentioned as a name of terror at the Palais Royal. Laughingly he said
to the councillor, restored to his family:

芒聙聹Do you think, father, that if now I should ask for a company the queen
would give it to me?芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan profited by this interval of calm to send away Raoul, whom
he had great difficulty in keeping shut up during the riot, and who
wished positively to strike a blow for one party or the other. Raoul
had offered some opposition at first; but D芒聙聶Artagnan made use of the
Comte de la F脙篓re芒聙聶s name, and after paying a visit to Madame de
Chevreuse, Raoul started to rejoin the army.

Rochefort alone was dissatisfied with the termination of affairs. He
had written to the Duc de Beaufort to come and the duke was about to
arrive, and he would find Paris tranquil. He went to the coadjutor to
consult with him whether it would not be better to send word to the
duke to stop on the road, but Gondy reflected for a moment, and then
said:

芒聙聹Let him continue his journey.芒聙聺

芒聙聹All is not then over?芒聙聺 asked Rochefort.

芒聙聹My dear count, we have only just begun.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What induces you to think so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The knowledge that I have of the queen芒聙聶s heart; she will not rest
contented beaten.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is she, then, preparing for a stroke?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, let us see what you know.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know that she has written to the prince to return in haste from the

芒聙聹Ah! ha!芒聙聺 said Rochefort, 芒聙聹you are right. We must let Monsieur de

In fact, the evening after this conversation the report was circulated

confidence had unveiled the sinister project of the queen.

Even on the night of the prince芒聙聶s return, some citizens, bolder than

芒聙聹Why do we not take the king and place him in the Hotel de Ville? It is

would imbibe national principles and love his people.芒聙聺

That night the question was secretly agitated and on the morrow the
gray and black cloaks, the patrols of armed shop-people, and the bands
of mendicants reappeared.

The queen had passed the night in lonely conference with the prince,

At five o芒聙聶clock Anne went to the cardinal芒聙聶s room. If she had not yet

The queen was in a morning gown, but it became her still; for, like

remaining ever beautiful; nevertheless, this morning she looked

芒聙聹What is the matter, madame?芒聙聺 said Mazarin, uneasily. 芒聙聹You seem

芒聙聹Yes, Giulio,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹proud and happy; for I have found the means

芒聙聹You are a great politician, my queen,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹let us hear the

芒聙聹You know,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹that they want to take the king away from

芒聙聹Alas! yes, and to hang me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They shall not have the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nor hang me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Listen. I want to carry off my son from them, with yourself. I wish

芒聙聹And who is this third person?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur le Prince.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He has come, then, as they told me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Last evening.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you have seen him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He has just left me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And will he aid this project?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The plan is his own.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And Paris?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He will starve it out and force it to surrender at discretion.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The plan is not wanting in grandeur; I see but one impediment.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Impossibility.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A senseless word. Nothing is impossible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On paper.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In execution. We have money?芒聙聺

芒聙聹A little,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, trembling, lest Anne should ask to draw upon

芒聙聹Troops?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Five or six thousand men.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Courage?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Plenty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then the thing is easy. Oh! do think of it, Giulio! Paris, this odious

芒聙聹Charming! charming!芒聙聺 said Mazarin. 芒聙聹I can imagine the effect, I do not

芒聙聹I will find the means myself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are aware it will be war, civil war, furious, devouring,

芒聙聹Oh! yes, yes, war,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria. 芒聙聹Yes, I will reduce this

Paris!; I芒聙聰I detest, I loathe it!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very fine, Anne. You are now sanguinary; but take care. We are not in

芒聙聹You laugh.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faintly. It is dangerous to go to war with a nation. Look at your
brother monarch, Charles I. He is badly off, very badly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are in France, and I am Spanish.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So much the worse; I had much rather you were French and myself also;
they would hate us both less.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nevertheless, you consent?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, if the thing be possible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is; it is I who tell you so; make preparations for departure.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I! I am always prepared to go, only, as you know, I never do go, and
perhaps shall go this time as little as before.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In short, if I go, will you go too?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will try.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You torment me, Giulio, with your fears; and what are you afraid of,
then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of many things.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What are they?芒聙聺

Mazarin芒聙聶s face, smiling as it was, became clouded.

芒聙聹Anne,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹you are but a woman and as a woman you may insult men
at your ease, knowing that you can do it with impunity. You accuse me
of fear; I have not so much as you have, since I do not fly as you do.
Against whom do they cry out? is it against you or against myself? Whom
would they hang, yourself or me? Well, I can weather the storm芒聙聰I, whom,
notwithstanding, you tax with fear芒聙聰not with bravado, that is not my
way; but I am firm. Imitate me. Make less hubbub and think more deeply.
You cry very loud, you end by doing nothing; you talk of flying芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

Mazarin shrugged his shoulders and taking the queen芒聙聶s hand led her to
the window.

芒聙聹Look!芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 said the queen, blinded by her obstinacy.

芒聙聹Well, what do you see from this window? If I am not mistaken those are
citizens, helmeted and mailed, armed with good muskets, as in the time
of the League, and whose eyes are so intently fixed on this window that
they will see you if you raise that curtain much; and now come to the
other side芒聙聰what do you see? Creatures of the people, armed with
halberds, guarding your doors. You will see the same at every opening
from this palace to which I should lead you. Your doors are guarded,
the airholes of your cellars are guarded, and I could say to you, as
that good La Ramee said to me of the Duc de Beaufort, you must be
either bird or mouse to get out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He did get out, nevertheless.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you think of escaping in the same way?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am a prisoner, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹_Parbleu!_芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹I have been proving it to you this last
hour.芒聙聺

And he quietly resumed his dispatch at the place where he had been

Anne, trembling with anger and scarlet with humiliation, left the room,

wept; then suddenly struck with an idea:

芒聙聹I am saved!芒聙聺 she exclaimed, rising; 芒聙聹oh, yes! yes! I know a man who
will find the means of taking me from Paris, a man I have too long

expression of joy, 芒聙聹Ungrateful woman that I am, for twenty years I have
forgotten this man, whom I ought to have made a mar脙漏chal of France. My
mother-in-law expended gold, caresses, dignities on Concini, who ruined
her; the king made Vitry mar脙漏chal of France for an assassination: while
I have left in obscurity, in poverty, the noble D芒聙聶Artagnan, who saved
me!芒聙聺

And running to a table, on which were paper, pens and ink, she hastily
began to write.




Chapter L.
The Interview.


It had been D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s practice, ever since the riots, to sleep in
the same room as Porthos, and on this eventful morning he was still

sky and was raining gold pieces into his hat, which he held out till it
was overflowing with pistoles. As for Porthos, he dreamed that the
panels of his carriage were not capacious enough to contain the
armorial bearings he had ordered to be painted on them. They were both

brought a letter for D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹From whom?芒聙聺 asked the Gascon.

芒聙聹From the queen,芒聙聺 replied the servant.

芒聙聹Ho!芒聙聺 said Porthos, raising himself in his bed; 芒聙聹what does she say?芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan requested the servant to wait in the next room and when the

芒聙聹Friend Porthos,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, handing the letter to him, 芒聙聹this

Porthos took the letter and with a trembling voice read the following

芒聙聹The queen wishes to speak to Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, who must follow the

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 exclaimed Porthos; 芒聙聹I see nothing in that very extraordinary.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But I see much that is very extraordinary in it,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan.
芒聙聹It is evident, by their sending for me, that matters are becoming
complicated. Just reflect a little what an agitation the queen芒聙聶s mind
must be in for her to have remembered me after twenty years.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is true,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Sharpen your sword, baron, load your pistols, and give some corn to

芒聙聹But, stop; do you think it can be a trap that they are laying for us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹If it is a snare,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I shall scent it out, be
assured. If Mazarin is an Italian, I am a Gascon.芒聙聺

And D芒聙聶Artagnan dressed himself in an instant.

Whilst Porthos, still in bed, was hooking on his cloak for him, a

芒聙聹Come in,芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan; and another servant entered.

芒聙聹From His Eminence, Cardinal Mazarin,芒聙聺 presenting a letter.

D芒聙聶Artagnan looked at Porthos.

芒聙聹A complicated affair,芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹where will you begin?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is arranged capitally; his eminence expects me in half an hour.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, turning to the servant, 芒聙聹tell his
eminence that in half an hour I shall be at his command.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I do not think it, I am certain of it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Quick, quick, D芒聙聶Artagnan. Remember that the queen awaits you, and

D芒聙聶Artagnan summoned Anne of Austria芒聙聶s servant and signified that he was

The servant conducted him by the Rue des Petits Champs and turning to

The silence of the oratory was at last disturbed by the slight rustling

芒聙聹No, madame,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹No, madame, I was ignorant of that,芒聙聺 said the musketeer.

Anne understood this covert reproach and was more moved than irritated

芒聙聹All that you tell me of those by whom I am surrounded, Monsieur

芒聙聹I will do everything your majesty commands,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan.

The queen reflected for a moment and then, seeing the cautious demeanor

芒聙聹Perhaps you like repose?芒聙聺 she said.

芒聙聹I do not know, for I have never had it, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have you any friends?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I had three, two of whom have left Paris, to go I know not where. One

芒聙聹Very good,芒聙聺 said the queen; 芒聙聹you and your friend are worth an army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What am I to do, madame?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Return at five o芒聙聶clock and I will tell you; but do not breathe to a

芒聙聹No, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Swear it upon the cross.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, I have never been false to my word; when I say I will not do a

The queen, although astonished at this language, to which she was not

芒聙聹Has the queen any further commands for me now?芒聙聺 asked D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹No, sir,芒聙聺 replied Anne of Austria, 芒聙聹and you may retire until the time

D芒聙聶Artagnan bowed and went out.

芒聙聹_Diable!_芒聙聺 he exclaimed when the door was shut, 芒聙聹they seem to have the

Then, as the half hour had already glided by, he crossed the gallery

Bernouin introduced him.

芒聙聹I come for your commands, my lord,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Nobody, but I know it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I regret infinitely to tell you, my lord, that you are mistaken,芒聙聺

芒聙聹I opened the door of the ante-room myself and I saw you enter at the

芒聙聹Because I was shown up the private stairs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know not; it must have been a mistake.芒聙聺

Mazarin was aware that it was not easy to make D芒聙聶Artagnan reveal

芒聙聹Let us speak of my affairs,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹since you will tell me

芒聙聹My life has been passed on the high road.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing but an order from a superior would retain me in Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well. Here is a letter, which must be taken to its address.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To its address, my lord? But it has none.芒聙聺

In fact, the side of the letter opposite the seal was blank.

芒聙聹I must tell you,芒聙聺 resumed Mazarin, 芒聙聹that it is in a double envelope.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, my lord, and I will set out at once. There is one thing only which

芒聙聹What for?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Merely to say these words: 芒聙聵I am going to send Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan

Mazarin returned, pale, and evidently thoughtful. He seated himself at

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 thought the Gascon; 芒聙聹he looks displeased. Can it be with me? He

The Gascon, with his vivid imagination, had already seen the advantage

芒聙聹You were right,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹my dear Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, and you cannot

D芒聙聶Artagnan obeyed, and Mazarin ascertained that the seal was intact.

芒聙聹I shall want you this evening,芒聙聺 he said 芒聙聹Return in two hours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do not be uneasy,芒聙聺 said Mazarin; 芒聙聹it is the same.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I fancied it was so.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan bowed, and thought: 芒聙聹Both at the same hour; both commands

芒聙聹You are thoughtful,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, uneasily.

芒聙聹Yes, I was thinking whether we ought to come armed or not.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Armed to the teeth!芒聙聺 replied Mazarin.

芒聙聹Very well, my lord; it shall be so.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan saluted, went out and hastened to repeat to his friend

happiness.




Chapter LI.
The Flight.


When D芒聙聶Artagnan returned to the Palais Royal at five o芒聙聶clock, it
presented, in spite of the excitement which reigned in the town, a
spectacle of the greatest rejoicing. Nor was that surprising. The queen
had restored Broussel and Blancmesnil to the people and had therefore
nothing to fear, since the people had nothing more just then to ask
for. The return, also, of the conqueror of Lens was the pretext for
giving a grand banquet. The princes and princesses were invited and
their carriages had crowded the court since noon; then after dinner the
queen was to have a play in her apartment. Anne of Austria had never
appeared more brilliant than on that day芒聙聰radiant with grace and wit.
Mazarin disappeared as they rose from table. He found D芒聙聶Artagnan
waiting for him already at his post in the ante-room.

The cardinal advanced to him with a smile and taking him by the hand

芒聙聹My dear M. d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said the minister, sitting down, 芒聙聹I am about

芒聙聹I hope,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, bowing, 芒聙聹that you give it, my lord, without

芒聙聹More worthy than any one in Paris my dear friend; therefore I apply to

芒聙聹Explain yourself, my lord, I am listening.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The queen has resolved to make a little excursion with the king to

芒聙聹Aha!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that is to say, the queen wishes to leave

芒聙聹A woman芒聙聶s caprice芒聙聰you understand.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, I understand perfectly,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹It was for this she summoned you this morning and that she told you to

芒聙聹Was it worth while to wish me to swear this morning that I would

women! whether queens or not, they are always the same.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you disapprove of this journey, my dear M. d芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 asked

芒聙聹Because you shrug your shoulders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord, and I feel the whole responsibility of such a charge.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You accept, however?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I always accept.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you think the thing possible?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Everything is possible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Shall you be attacked on the road?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Probably.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what will you do in that case?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall pass through those who attack me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And suppose you cannot pass through them?芒聙聺

芒聙聹So much the worse for them; I shall pass over them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you will place the king and queen in safety also, at Saint

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹On your life?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On my life.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are a hero, my friend,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, gazing at the musketeer with

Chapter LII.
The Carriage of Monsieur le Coadjuteur.


Instead of returning, then, by the Saint Honor脙漏 gate, D芒聙聶Artagnan, who
had time before him, walked around and re-entered by the Porte
Richelieu. He was approached to be examined, and when it was discovered
by his plumed hat and his laced coat, that he was an officer of the
musketeers, he was surrounded, with the intention of making him cry,
芒聙聹Down with Mazarin!芒聙聺 The demonstration did not fail to make him uneasy
at first; but when he discovered what it meant, he shouted it in such a
voice that even the most exacting were satisfied. He walked down the
Rue Richelieu, meditating how he should carry off the queen in her
turn, for to take her in a carriage bearing the arms of France was not
to be thought of, when he perceived an equipage standing at the door of
the hotel belonging to Madame de Gu脙漏m脙漏n脙漏e.

He was struck by a sudden idea.

芒聙聹Ah, _pardieu!_芒聙聺 he exclaimed; 芒聙聹that would be fair play.芒聙聺

And approaching the carriage, he examined the arms on the panels and
the livery of the coachman on his box. This scrutiny was so much the
more easy, the coachman being sound asleep.

芒聙聹It is, in truth, monsieur le coadjuteur芒聙聶s carriage,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan;
芒聙聹upon my honor I begin to think that Heaven favors us.芒聙聺

He mounted noiselessly into the chariot and pulled the silk cord which
was attached to the coachman芒聙聶s little finger.

芒聙聹To the Palais Royal,芒聙聺 he called out.

The coachman awoke with a start and drove off in the direction he was
desired, never doubting but that the order had come from his master.
The porter at the palace was about to close the gates, but seeing such
a handsome equipage he fancied that it was some visit of importance and
the carriage was allowed to pass and to stop beneath the porch. It was
then only the coachman perceived the grooms were not behind the
vehicle; he fancied monsieur le coadjuteur had sent them back, and
without dropping the reins he sprang from his box to open the door.
D芒聙聶Artagnan, in his turn, sprang to the ground, and just at the moment
when the coachman, alarmed at not seeing his master, fell back a step,
he seized him by his collar with the left, whilst with the right hand
he placed the muzzle of a pistol at his breast.

芒聙聹Pronounce one single word,芒聙聺 muttered D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹and you are a dead
man.芒聙聺

The coachman perceived at once, by the expression of the man who thus
addressed him, that he had fallen into a trap, and he remained with his
mouth wide open and his eyes portentously staring.

Two musketeers were pacing the court, to whom D芒聙聶Artagnan called by
their names.

芒聙聹Monsieur de Belli脙篓re,芒聙聺 said he to one of them, 芒聙聹do me the favor to
take the reins from the hands of this worthy man, mount upon the box
and drive to the door of the private stair, and wait for me there; it
is an affair of importance on the service of the king.芒聙聺

The musketeer, who knew that his lieutenant was incapable of jesting
with regard to the service, obeyed without a word, although he thought
the order strange. Then turning toward the second musketeer, D芒聙聶Artagnan
said:

芒聙聹Monsieur du Verger, help me to place this man in a place of safety.芒聙聺

The musketeer, thinking that his lieutenant had just arrested some
prince in disguise, bowed, and drawing his sword, signified that he was
ready. D芒聙聶Artagnan mounted the staircase, followed by his prisoner, who
in his turn was followed by the soldier, and entered Mazarin芒聙聶s
ante-room. Bernouin was waiting there, impatient for news of his
master.

芒聙聹Well, sir?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Everything goes on capitally, my dear Monsieur Bernouin, but here is a
man whom I must beg you to put in a safe place.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where, then, sir?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where you like, provided that the place which you shall choose has
iron shutters secured by padlocks and a door that can be locked.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We have that, sir,芒聙聺 replied Bernouin; and the poor coachman was
conducted to a closet, the windows of which were barred and which
looked very much like a prison.

芒聙聹And now, my good friend,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to him, 芒聙聹I must invite you
to deprive yourself, for my sake, of your hat and cloak.芒聙聺

The coachman, as we can well understand, made no resistance; in fact,
he was so astonished at what had happened to him that he stammered and
reeled like a drunken man; D芒聙聶Artagnan deposited his clothes under the
arm of one of the valets.

芒聙聹And now, Monsieur du Verger,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹shut yourself up with this man
until Monsieur Bernouin returns to open the door. The duty will be
tolerably long and not very amusing, I know; but,芒聙聺 added he, seriously,
芒聙聹you understand, it is on the king芒聙聶s service.芒聙聺

芒聙聹At your command, lieutenant,芒聙聺 replied the musketeer, who saw the
business was a serious one.

芒聙聹By-the-bye,芒聙聺 continued D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹should this man attempt to fly or
to call out, pass your sword through his body.芒聙聺

The musketeer signified by a nod that these commands should be obeyed
to the letter, and D芒聙聶Artagnan went out, followed by Bernouin. Midnight
struck.

芒聙聹Lead me into the queen芒聙聶s oratory,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹announce to her I
am here, and put this parcel, with a well-loaded musket, under the seat
of the carriage which is waiting at the foot of the private stair.芒聙聺

Bernouin conducted D芒聙聶Artagnan to the oratory, where he sat down
pensively. Everything had gone on as usual at the Palais Royal. As we
said before, by ten o芒聙聶clock almost all the guests had dispersed; those
who were to fly with the court had the word of command and they were
each severally desired to be from twelve o芒聙聶clock to one at Cours la
Reine.

At ten o芒聙聶clock Anne of Austria had entered the king芒聙聶s room. Monsieur
had just retired, and the youthful Louis, remaining the last, was
amusing himself by placing some lead soldiers in a line of battle, a
game which delighted him much. Two royal pages were playing with him.

芒聙聹Laporte,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹it is time for his majesty to go to bed.芒聙聺

The king asked to remain up, having, he said, no wish to sleep; but the
queen was firm.

芒聙聹Are you not going to-morrow morning at six o芒聙聶clock, Louis, to bathe at
Conflans? I think you wished to do so of your own accord?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are right, madame,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹and I am ready to retire to my
room when you have kissed me. Laporte, give the light to Monsieur the
Chevalier de Coislin.芒聙聺

The queen touched with her lips the white, smooth brow the royal child
presented to her with a gravity which already partook of etiquette.

芒聙聹Go to sleep soon, Louis,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹for you must be awakened
very early.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will do my best to obey you, madame,芒聙聺 said the youthful king, 芒聙聹but I
have no inclination to sleep.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Laporte,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria, in an undertone, 芒聙聹find some very dull
book to read to his majesty, but do not undress yourself.芒聙聺

The king went out, accompanied by the Chevalier de Coislin, bearing the
candlestick, and then the queen returned to her own apartment. Her
ladies芒聙聰that is to say Madame de Bregy, Mademoiselle de Beaumont, Madame
de Motteville, and Socratine, her sister, so called on account of her
sense芒聙聰had just brought into her dressing-room the remains of the
dinner, on which, according to her usual custom, she supped. The queen
then gave her orders, spoke of a banquet which the Marquis de
Villequier was to give to her on the day after the morrow, indicated
the persons she would admit to the honor of partaking of it, announced
another visit on the following day to Val-de-Grace, where she intended
to pay her devotions, and gave her commands to her senior valet to
accompany her. When the ladies had finished their supper the queen
feigned extreme fatigue and passed into her bedroom. Madame de
Motteville, who was on especial duty that evening, followed to aid and
undress her. The queen then began to read, and after conversing with
her affectionately for a few minutes, dismissed her.

It was at this moment D芒聙聶Artagnan entered the courtyard of the palace,
in the coadjutor芒聙聶s carriage, and a few seconds later the carriages of
the ladies-in-waiting drove out and the gates were shut after them.

A few minutes after twelve o芒聙聶clock Bernouin knocked at the queen芒聙聶s
bedroom door, having come by the cardinal芒聙聶s secret corridor. Anne of
Austria opened the door to him herself. She was dressed, that is to
say, in _dishabille_, wrapped in a long, warm dressing-gown.

芒聙聹It is you, Bernouin,芒聙聺 she said. 芒聙聹Is Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan there?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame, in your oratory. He is waiting till your majesty is
ready.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am. Go and tell Laporte to wake and dress the king, and then pass on
to the Mar脙漏chal de Villeroy and summon him to me.芒聙聺

Bernouin bowed and retired.

The queen entered her oratory, which was lighted by a single lamp of
Venetian crystal, She saw D芒聙聶Artagnan, who stood expecting her.

芒聙聹Is it you?芒聙聺 she said.

芒聙聹Yes, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you ready?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And his eminence, the cardinal?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Has got off without any accident. He is awaiting your majesty at Cours
la Reine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But in what carriage do we start?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have provided for everything; a carriage below is waiting for your
majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us go to the king.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan bowed and followed the queen. The young Louis was already

commands.芒聙聺

The bedclothes were thrown back, exposing the king芒聙聶s bed linen, which
was so worn that here and there holes could be seen. It was one of the
results of Mazarin芒聙聶s niggardliness.

The queen entered and D芒聙聶Artagnan remained at the door. As soon as the

Anne then motioned to D芒聙聶Artagnan to approach, and he obeyed.

芒聙聹My son,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria, pointing to the musketeer, calm,

one of those ancient heroes of whom you like so much to hear from my
women. Remember his name well and look at him well, that his face may
not be forgotten, for this evening he is going to render us a great

The young king looked at the officer with his large-formed eye, and
repeated:

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is it, my son.芒聙聺

The young king slowly raised his little hand and held it out to the
musketeer; the latter bent on his knee and kissed it.

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 repeated Louis; 芒聙聹very well, madame.芒聙聺

At this moment they were startled by a noise as if a tumult were
approaching.

芒聙聹What is that?芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen.

芒聙聹Oh, oh!芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, straining both at the same time his quick
ear and his intelligent glance, 芒聙聹it is the murmur of the populace in

芒聙聹We must fly,芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹Your majesty has given me the control of this business; we had better

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will answer for everything.芒聙聺

Nothing is so catching as confidence. The queen, full of energy and
courage, was quickly alive to these two virtues in others.

芒聙聹Do as you like,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹I rely upon you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Will your majesty permit me to give orders in your name throughout
this business?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Command, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do the people want this time?芒聙聺 demanded the king.

芒聙聹We are about to ascertain, sire,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, as he rapidly
left the room.

The tumult continued to increase and seemed to surround the Palais
Royal entirely. Cries were heard from the interior, of which they could
not comprehend the sense. It was evident that there was clamor and
sedition.

The king, half dressed, the queen and Laporte remained each in the same
state and almost in the same place, where they were listening and
waiting. Comminges, who was on guard that night at the Palais Royal,
ran in. He had about two hundred men in the courtyards and stables, and
he placed them at the queen芒聙聶s disposal.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 asked Anne of Austria, when D芒聙聶Artagnan reappeared, 芒聙聹what does
it mean?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It means, madame, that the report has spread that the queen has left
the Palais Royal, carrying off the king, and the people ask to have
proof to the contrary, or threaten to demolish the Palais Royal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, this time it is too much!芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, 芒聙聹and I will prove
to them I have not left.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan saw from the expression of the queen芒聙聶s face that she was
about to issue some violent command. He approached her and said in a
low voice:

芒聙聹Has your majesty still confidence in me?芒聙聺

This voice startled her. 芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 she replied, 芒聙聹every confidence;
speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Will the queen deign to follow my advice?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Speak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let your majesty dismiss M. de Comminges and desire him to shut
himself up with his men in the guardhouse and in the stables.芒聙聺

Comminges glanced at D芒聙聶Artagnan with the envious look with which every

芒聙聹You hear, Comminges?芒聙聺 said the queen.

D芒聙聶Artagnan went up to him; with his usual quickness he caught the

芒聙聹Monsieur de Comminges,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹pardon me; we both are servants of

Comminges bowed and left.

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to himself, 芒聙聹I have got one more enemy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said the queen, addressing D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹what is to be done?
for you hear that, instead of becoming calmer, the noise increases.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹the people want to see the king and they
must see him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! _must_ see him! Where芒聙聰on the balcony?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not at all, madame, but here, sleeping in his bed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, your majesty,芒聙聺 exclaimed Laporte, 芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan is right.芒聙聺

The queen became thoughtful and smiled, like a woman to whom duplicity
is no stranger.

芒聙聹Without doubt,芒聙聺 she murmured.

芒聙聹Monsieur Laporte,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹go and announce to the people
through the grating that they are going to be satisfied and that in

芒聙聹But not every one; a deputation of two or four people.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Every one, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But reflect, they will keep us here till daybreak.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It shall take but a quarter of an hour, I answer for everything,

and timid as lambs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, Laporte,芒聙聺 said the queen.

The young king approached his mother and said, 芒聙聹Why do as these people
ask?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It must be so, my son,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria.

芒聙聹But if they say, 芒聙聵it must be芒聙聶 to me, am I no longer king?芒聙聺

The queen remained silent.

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹will your majesty permit me to ask you a

Louis XIV. turned around, astonished that any one should dare to

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Does your majesty remember, when playing in the park of Fontainebleau,

芒聙聹Yes, certainly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, this noise of thunder, however much your majesty may have
wished to continue playing, has said, 芒聙聵go in, sire. You must do so.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly, sir; but they tell me that the noise of thunder is the

芒聙聹Well then, sire,芒聙聺 continued D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹listen to the noise of the

In truth at that moment a terrible murmur was wafted to them by the

芒聙聹Hold, sire,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹they have just told the people that you

The queen looked with surprise at this strange man, whose brilliant
courage made him the equal of the bravest, and who was, by his fine and

Laporte entered.

芒聙聹Well, Laporte?芒聙聺 asked the queen.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 he replied, 芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s prediction has been

to be opened and in five minutes they will be here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Laporte,芒聙聺 said the queen, 芒聙聹suppose you put one of your sons in the

芒聙聹If your majesty desires it,芒聙聺 said Laporte, 芒聙聹my sons, like myself, are
at the queen芒聙聶s service.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not at all,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹should one of them know his majesty and

芒聙聹You are right, sir, always right,芒聙聺 said Anne of Austria. 芒聙聹Laporte,

Laporte placed the king, dressed as he was, in the bed and then covered

芒聙聹Pretend to sleep, Louis,芒聙聺 said she.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹but I do not wish to be touched by any of those

芒聙聹Sire, I am here,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹and I give you my word, that if a

芒聙聹And now what is to be done?芒聙聺 asked the queen, 芒聙聹for I hear them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur Laporte, go to them and again recommend silence. Madame, wait

Laporte went out; the queen remained standing near the hangings, whilst
D芒聙聶Artagnan glided behind the curtains.

Then the heavy and collected steps of a multitude of men were heard,
and the queen herself raised the tapestry hangings and put her finger
on her lips.

On seeing the queen, the men stopped short, respectfully.

芒聙聹Enter, gentlemen, enter,芒聙聺 said the queen.

There was then amongst that crowd a moment芒聙聶s hesitation, which looked
like shame. They had expected resistance, they had expected to be
thwarted, to have to force the gates, to overturn the guards. The gates
had opened of themselves, and the king, ostensibly at least, had no
other guard at his bed-head but his mother. The foremost of them
stammered and attempted to fall back.

芒聙聹Enter, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said Laporte, 芒聙聹since the queen desires you so to
do.芒聙聺

Then one more bold than the rest ventured to pass the door and to
advance on tiptoe. This example was imitated by the rest, until the
room filled silently, as if these men had been the humblest, most
devoted courtiers. Far beyond the door the heads of those who were not
able to enter could be seen, all craning to their utmost height to try
and see.

D芒聙聶Artagnan saw it all through an opening he had made in the curtain,
and in the very first man who entered he recognized Planchet.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said the queen to him, thinking he was the leader of the band,
芒聙聹you wished to see the king and therefore I determined to show him to
you myself. Approach and look at him and say if we have the appearance
of people who wish to run away.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, certainly,芒聙聺 replied Planchet, rather astonished at the unexpected
honor conferred upon him.

芒聙聹You will say, then, to my good and faithful Parisians,芒聙聺 continued
Anne, with a smile, the expression of which did not deceive D芒聙聶Artagnan,
芒聙聹that you have seen the king in bed, asleep, and the queen also ready
to retire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall tell them, madame, and those who accompany me will say the
same thing; but芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what?芒聙聺 asked Anne of Austria.

芒聙聹Will your majesty pardon me,芒聙聺 said Planchet, 芒聙聹but is it really the
king who is lying there?芒聙聺

Anne of Austria started. 芒聙聹If,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹there is one among you who
knows the king, let him approach and say whether it is really his
majesty lying there.芒聙聺

A man wrapped in a cloak, in the folds of which his face was hidden,
approached and leaned over the bed and looked.

For one second, D芒聙聶Artagnan thought the man had some evil design and he
