Chapter LIII.
How D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos earned by selling Straw.


Mazarin was desirous of setting out instantly for Saint Germain, but
the queen declared that she should wait for the people whom she had
appointed to meet her. However, she offered the cardinal Laporte芒聙聶s
place, which he accepted and went from one carriage to the other.

It was not without foundation that a report of the king芒聙聶s intention to
leave Paris by night had been circulated. Ten or twelve persons had
been in the secret since six o芒聙聶clock, and howsoever great their
prudence might be, they could not issue the necessary orders for the
departure without suspicion being generated. Besides, each individual
had one or two others for whom he was interested; and as there could be
no doubt but that the queen was leaving Paris full of terrible projects
of vengeance, every one had warned parents and friends of what was
about to transpire; so that the news of the approaching exit ran like a
train of lighted gunpowder along the streets.

The first carriage which arrived after that of the queen was that of

There was no more to be had in Saint Germain. This foraging did not
occupy more than half an hour. Mousqueton, duly instructed, was put at

D芒聙聶Artagnan, taking with him three trusses of straw, returned to the

D芒聙聶Artagnan gave up his bed to any one who wanted it, making it over

D芒聙聶Artagnan had taken the precaution to set apart four trusses for his

Mousqueton, who knew nothing of what was going on in the ch脙垄teau,

Porthos was snoring most musically when some one touched him on the

No sooner had he turned his back than the officer laid himself down in

Chapter LIV.
In which we hear Tidings of Aramis.


D芒聙聶Artagnan went straight to the stables; day was just dawning. He found

The camp had entered Saint Omer; the friends made a little detour and

And they set out for Boulogne, where toward evening they arrived, their
horses flecked with foam and dark with perspiration.

At ten steps from the place where they halted was a young man in black,
who seemed waiting for some one, and who, from the moment he saw them
enter the town, never took his eyes off them.

D芒聙聶Artagnan approached him, and seeing him stare so fixedly, said:

芒聙聹Well, friend! I don芒聙聶t like people to quiz me!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said the young man, 芒聙聹do you not come from Paris, if you please?芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan thought it was some gossip who wanted news from the capital.

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 he said, in a softened tone.

芒聙聹Are you not going to put up at the 芒聙聵Arms of England芒聙聶?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you not charged with a mission from his eminence, Cardinal
Mazarin?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case, I am the man you have to do with. I am M. Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹the man I am warned against by Athos.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 thought Porthos, 芒聙聹the man Aramis wants me to strangle.芒聙聺

They both looked searchingly at the young man, who misunderstood the
meaning of that inquisition.

芒聙聹Do you doubt my word?芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹In that case I can give you proofs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹and we place ourselves at your orders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, gentlemen,芒聙聺 resumed Mordaunt, 芒聙聹we must set out without delay,
to-day is the last day granted me by the cardinal. My ship is ready,
and had you not come I must have set off without you, for General
Cromwell expects my return impatiently.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So!芒聙聺 thought the lieutenant, 芒聙聹芒聙聶tis to General Cromwell that our
dispatches are addressed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have you no letter for him?芒聙聺 asked the young man.

芒聙聹I have one, the seal of which I am not to break till I reach London;
but since you tell me to whom it is addressed, 芒聙聶tis useless to wait
till then.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan tore open the envelope of the letter. It was directed to
芒聙聹Monsieur Oliver Cromwell, General of the Army of the English Nation.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹a singular commission.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who is this Monsieur Oliver Cromwell?芒聙聺 inquired Porthos.

芒聙聹Formerly a brewer,芒聙聺 replied the Gascon.

芒聙聹Perhaps Mazarin wishes to make a speculation in beer, as we did in
straw,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Come, come, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, impatiently, 芒聙聹let us depart.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 exclaimed Porthos 芒聙聹without supper? Cannot Monsieur Cromwell
wait a little?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but I?芒聙聺 said Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Well, you,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹what then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I cannot wait.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! as to you, that is not my concern, and I shall sup either with or
without your permission.芒聙聺

The young man芒聙聶s eyes kindled in secret, but he restrained himself.

芒聙聹Monsieur,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹you must excuse famished travelers.
Besides, our supper can芒聙聶t delay you much. We will hasten on to the inn;
you will meanwhile proceed on foot to the harbor. We will take a bite
and shall be there as soon as you are.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Just as you please, gentlemen, provided we set sail,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹The name of your ship?芒聙聺 inquired D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹The _Standard_.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well; in half an hour we shall be on board.芒聙聺

And the friends, spurring on their horses, rode to the hotel, the 芒聙聹Arms
of England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you say of that young man?芒聙聺 asked D芒聙聶Artagnan, as they hurried
along.

芒聙聹I say that he doesn芒聙聶t suit me at all,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹and that I feel
a strong itching to follow Aramis芒聙聶s advice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By no means, my dear Porthos; that man is a messenger of General
Cromwell; it would insure for us a poor reception, I imagine, should it
be announced to him that we had twisted the neck of his confidant.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nevertheless,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I have always noticed that Aramis gives
good advice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Listen,芒聙聺 returned D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹when our embassy is finished芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺

芒聙聹If it brings us back to France芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, we shall see.芒聙聺

At that moment the two friends reached the hotel, 芒聙聹Arms of England,芒聙聺
where they supped with hearty appetite and then at once proceeded to
the port.

There they found a brig ready to set sail, upon the deck of which they
recognized Mordaunt walking up and down impatiently.

芒聙聹It is singular,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, whilst the boat was taking them to
the _Standard_, 芒聙聹it is astonishing how that young man resembles some
one I must have known, but who it was I cannot yet remember.芒聙聺

A few minutes later they were on board, but the embarkation of the
horses was a longer matter than that of the men, and it was eight
o芒聙聶clock before they raised anchor.

The young man stamped impatiently and ordered all sail to be spread.

Porthos, completely used up by three nights without sleep and a journey
of seventy leagues on horseback, retired to his cabin and went to
sleep.

D芒聙聶Artagnan, overcoming his repugnance to Mordaunt, walked with him upon
the deck and invented a hundred stories to make him talk.

Mousqueton was seasick.




Chapter LV.
The Scotchman.


And now our readers must leave the _Standard_ to sail peaceably, not
toward London, where D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos believed they were going,
but to Durham, whither Mordaunt had been ordered to repair by the
letter he had received during his sojourn at Boulogne, and accompany us
to the royalist camp, on this side of the Tyne, near Newcastle.

There, placed between two rivers on the borders of Scotland, but still
on English soil, the tents of a little army extended. It was midnight.
Some Highlanders were listlessly keeping watch. The moon, which was
partially obscured by heavy clouds, now and then lit up the muskets of
the sentinels, or silvered the walls, the roofs, and the spires of the
town that Charles I. had just surrendered to the parliamentary troops,
whilst Oxford and Newark still held out for him in the hopes of coming
to some arrangement.

At one of the extremities of the camp, near an immense tent, in which
the Scottish officers were holding a kind of council, presided over by
Lord Leven, their commander, a man attired as a cavalier lay sleeping
on the turf, his right hand extended over his sword.

About fifty paces off, another man, also appareled as a cavalier, was
talking to a Scotch sentinel, and, though a foreigner, he seemed to
understand without much difficulty the answers given in the broad
Perthshire dialect.

As the town clock of Newcastle struck one the sleeper awoke, and with
all the gestures of a man rousing himself out of deep sleep he looked
attentively about him; perceiving that he was alone he rose and making
a little circuit passed close to the cavalier who was speaking to the
sentinel. The former had no doubt finished his questions, for a moment
later he said good-night and carelessly followed the same path taken by
the first cavalier.

In the shadow of a tent the former was awaiting him.

芒聙聹Well, my dear friend?芒聙聺 said he, in as pure French as has ever been
uttered between Rouen and Tours.

芒聙聹Well, my friend, there is not a moment to lose; we must let the king
know immediately.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, what is the matter?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It would take too long to tell you, besides, you will hear it all
directly and the least word dropped here might ruin all. We must go and
find Lord Winter.芒聙聺

They both set off to the other end of the camp, but as it did not cover
more than a surface of five hundred feet they quickly arrived at the
tent they were looking for.

芒聙聹Tony, is your master sleeping?芒聙聺 said one of the two cavaliers to a
servant who was lying in the outer compartment, which served as a kind
of ante-room.

芒聙聹No, monsieur le comte,芒聙聺 answered the servant, 芒聙聹I think not; or at
least he has not long been so, for he was pacing up and down for more
than two hours after he left the king, and the sound of his footsteps
has only ceased during the last ten minutes. However, you may look and
see,芒聙聺 added the lackey, raising the curtained entrance of the tent.

Lord Winter was seated near an aperture, arranged as a window to let in
the night air, his eyes mechanically following the course of the moon,
intermittently veiled, as we before observed, by heavy clouds. The two
friends approached Winter, who, with his head on his hands, was gazing
at the heavens; he did not hear them enter and remained in the same
attitude till he felt a hand upon his shoulder.

He turned around, recognized Athos and Aramis and held out his hand to
them.

芒聙聹Have you observed,芒聙聺 said he to them, 芒聙聹what a blood-red color the moon
has to-night?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied Athos; 芒聙聹I thought it looked much the same as usual.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Look, again, chevalier,芒聙聺 returned Lord Winter.

芒聙聹I must own,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹I am like the Comte de la F脙篓re芒聙聰I can see
nothing remarkable about it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹in a position so precarious as ours we must
examine the earth and not the heavens. Have you studied our Scotch
troops and have you confidence in them?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Scotch?芒聙聺 inquired Winter. 芒聙聹What Scotch?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ours, egad!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos. 芒聙聹Those in whom the king has
confided芒聙聰Lord Leven芒聙聶s Highlanders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Winter, then he paused; 芒聙聹but tell me, can you not perceive
the russet tint which marks the heavens?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not the least in the world,芒聙聺 said Aramis and Athos at once.

芒聙聹Tell me,芒聙聺 continued Winter, always possessed by the same idea, 芒聙聹is
there not a tradition in France that Henry IV., the evening before the
day he was assassinated, when he was playing at chess with M. de
Bassompiere, saw clots of blood upon the chessboard?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and the mar脙漏chal has often told me so himself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then it was so,芒聙聺 murmured Winter, 芒聙聹and the next day Henry IV. was
killed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what has this vision of Henry IV. to do with you, my lord?芒聙聺
inquired Aramis.

芒聙聹Nothing; and indeed I am mad to trouble you with such things, when
your coming to my tent at such an hour announces that you are the
bearers of important news.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, my lord,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹I wish to speak to the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the king! but the king is asleep.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have something important to reveal to him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Can it not be put off till to-morrow?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He must know it this moment, and perhaps it is already too late.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, then,芒聙聺 said Lord Winter.

Lord Winter芒聙聶s tent was pitched by the side of the royal marquee, a kind
of corridor communicating between the two. This corridor was guarded,
not by a sentinel, but by a confidential servant, through whom, in case
of urgency, Charles could communicate instantly with his faithful
subject.

芒聙聹These gentlemen are with me,芒聙聺 said Winter.

The lackey bowed and let them pass. As he had said, on a camp bed,
dressed in his black doublet, booted, unbelted, with his felt hat
beside him, lay the king, overcome by sleep and fatigue. They advanced,
and Athos, who was the first to enter, gazed a moment in silence on
that pale and noble face, framed in its long and now untidy, matted
hair, the blue veins showing through the transparent temples, his eyes
seemingly swollen by tears.

Athos sighed deeply; the sigh woke the king, so lightly did he sleep.

He opened his eyes.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said he, raising himself on his elbow, 芒聙聹is it you, Comte de la
F脙篓re?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sire,芒聙聺 replied Athos.

芒聙聹You watch while I sleep and you have come to bring me some news?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas, sire,芒聙聺 answered Athos, 芒聙聹your majesty has guessed aright.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is bad news?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Never mind; the messenger is welcome. You never come to me without
conferring pleasure. You whose devotion recognizes neither country nor
misfortune, you who are sent to me by Henrietta; whatever news you
bring, speak out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire, Cromwell has arrived this night at Newcastle.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed the king, 芒聙聹to fight?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sire, but to buy your majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What did you say?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I said, sire, that four hundred thousand pounds are owing to the
Scottish army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹For unpaid wages; yes, I know it. For the last year my faithful
Highlanders have fought for honor alone.芒聙聺

Athos smiled.

芒聙聹Well, sir, though honor is a fine thing, they are tired of fighting
for it, and to-night they have sold you for two hundred thousand
pounds芒聙聰that is to say, for half what is owing them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Impossible!芒聙聺 cried the king, 芒聙聹the Scotch sell their king for two
hundred thousand pounds! And who is the Judas who has concluded this
infamous bargain?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lord Leven.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you certain of it, sir?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I heard it with my own ears.芒聙聺

The king sighed deeply, as if his heart would break, and then buried
his face in his hands.

芒聙聹Oh! the Scotch,芒聙聺 he exclaimed, 芒聙聹the Scotch I called 芒聙聵my faithful,芒聙聶 to
whom I trusted myself when I could have fled to Oxford! the Scotch, my
brothers! But are you well assured, sir?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lying behind the tent of Lord Leven, I raised it and saw all, heard
all!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And when is this to be consummated?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To-day芒聙聰this morning; so your majesty must perceive there is no time to
lose!芒聙聺

芒聙聹To do what? since you say I am sold.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To cross the Tyne, reach Scotland and rejoin Lord Montrose, who will
not sell you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what shall I do in Scotland? A war of partisans, unworthy of a
king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The example of Robert Bruce will absolve you, sire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, no! I have fought too long; they have sold me, they shall give me
up, and the eternal shame of treble treason shall fall on their heads.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹perhaps a king should act thus, but not a husband
and a father. I have come in the name of your wife and daughter and of
the children you have still in London, and I say to you, 芒聙聵Live,
sire,芒聙聶芒聙聰it is the will of Heaven.芒聙聺

The king raised himself, buckled on his belt, and passing his
handkerchief over his moist forehead, said:

芒聙聹Well, what is to be done?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire, have you in the army one regiment on which you can implicitly
rely?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Winter,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹do you believe in the fidelity of yours?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire, they are but men, and men are become both weak and wicked. I
will not answer for them. I would confide my life to them, but I should
hesitate ere I trusted them with your majesty芒聙聶s.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹since you have not a regiment, we are three
devoted men. It is enough. Let your majesty mount on horseback and
place yourself in the midst of us; we will cross the Tyne, reach
Scotland, and you will be saved.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is this your counsel also, Winter?芒聙聺 inquired the king.

芒聙聹Yes, sire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And yours, Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As you wish, then. Winter, give the necessary orders.芒聙聺

Winter then left the tent; in the meantime the king finished his
toilet. The first rays of daybreak penetrated the aperture of the tent
as Winter re-entered it.

芒聙聹All is ready, sire,芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹For us, also?芒聙聺 inquired Athos.

芒聙聹Grimaud and Blaisois are holding your horses, ready saddled.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case,芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, 芒聙聹let us not lose an instant, but set
off.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 added the king.

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹will not your majesty acquaint some of your
friends of this?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Friends!芒聙聺 answered Charles, sadly, 芒聙聹I have but three芒聙聰one of twenty
years, who has never forgotten me, and two of a week芒聙聶s standing, whom I
shall never forget. Come, gentlemen, come!芒聙聺

The king quitted his tent and found his horse ready waiting for him. It
was a chestnut that the king had ridden for three years and of which he
was very fond.

The horse neighed with pleasure at seeing him.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹I was unjust; here is a creature that loves me.
You at least will be faithful to me, Arthur.芒聙聺

The horse, as if it understood these words, bent its red nostrils
toward the king芒聙聶s face, and parting his lips displayed all its teeth,
as if with pleasure.

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 said the king, caressing it with his hand, 芒聙聹yes, my Arthur,
thou art a fond and faithful creature.芒聙聺

After this little scene Charles threw himself into the saddle, and
turning to Athos, Aramis and Winter, said:

芒聙聹Now, gentlemen, I am at your service.芒聙聺

But Athos was standing with his eyes fixed on a black line which
bordered the banks of the Tyne and seemed to extend double the length
of the camp.

芒聙聹What is that line?芒聙聺 cried Athos, whose vision was still rather
obscured by the uncertain shades and demi-tints of daybreak. 芒聙聹What is
that line? I did not observe it yesterday.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It must be the fog rising from the river,芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹Sire, it is something more opaque than the fog.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Indeed!芒聙聺 said Winter, 芒聙聹it appears to me like a bar of red color.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the enemy, who have made a sortie from Newcastle and are
surrounding us!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos.

芒聙聹The enemy!芒聙聺 cried the king.

芒聙聹Yes, the enemy. It is too late. Stop a moment; does not that sunbeam
yonder, just by the side of the town, glitter on the Ironsides?芒聙聺

This was the name given the cuirassiers, whom Cromwell had made his
body-guard.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹we shall soon see whether my Highlanders have
betrayed me or not.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What are you going to do?芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos.

芒聙聹To give them the order to charge, and run down these miserable
rebels.芒聙聺

And the king, putting spurs to his horse, set off to the tent of Lord
Leven.

芒聙聹Follow him,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Come!芒聙聺 exclaimed Aramis.

芒聙聹Is the king wounded?芒聙聺 cried Lord Winter. 芒聙聹I see spots of blood on the
ground.芒聙聺 And he set off to follow the two friends.

He was stopped by Athos.

芒聙聹Go and call out your regiment,芒聙聺 said he; 芒聙聹I can foresee that we shall
have need of it directly.芒聙聺

Winter turned his horse and the two friends rode on. It had taken but
two minutes for the king to reach the tent of the Scottish commander;
he dismounted and entered.

The general was there, surrounded by the more prominent chiefs.

芒聙聹The king!芒聙聺 they exclaimed, as all rose in bewilderment.

Charles was indeed in the midst of them, his hat on his head, his brows
bent, striking his boot with his riding whip.

芒聙聹Yes, gentlemen, the king in person, the king who has come to ask for
some account of what has happened.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is the matter, sire?芒聙聺 exclaimed Lord Leven.

芒聙聹It is this, sir,芒聙聺 said the king, angrily, 芒聙聹that General Cromwell has
reached Newcastle; that you knew it and I was not informed of it; that
the enemy have left the town and are now closing the passages of the
Tyne against us; that our sentinels have seen this movement and I have
been left unacquainted with it; that, by an infamous treaty you have
sold me for two hundred thousand pounds to Parliament. Of this treaty,
at least, I have been warned. This is the matter, gentlemen; answer and
exculpate yourselves, for I stand here to accuse you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Lord Leven, with hesitation, 芒聙聹sire, your majesty has been
deceived by false reports.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My own eyes have seen the enemy extend itself between myself and
Scotland; and I can almost say that with my own ears I have heard the
clauses of the treaty debated.芒聙聺

The Scotch chieftains looked at each other in their turn with frowning
brows.

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 murmured Lord Leven, crushed by shame, 芒聙聹sire, we are ready to
give you every proof of our fidelity.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I ask but one,芒聙聺 said the king; 芒聙聹put the army in battle array and face
the enemy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That cannot be, sire,芒聙聺 said the earl.

芒聙聹How, cannot be? What hinders it?芒聙聺 exclaimed the king.

芒聙聹Your majesty is well aware that there is a truce between us and the
English army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And if there is a truce the English army has broken it by quitting the
town, contrary to the agreement which kept it there. Now, I tell you,
you must pass with me through this army across to Scotland, and if you
refuse you may choose betwixt two names, which the contempt of all
honest men will brand you with芒聙聰you are either cowards or traitors!芒聙聺

The eyes of the Scotch flashed fire; and, as often happens on such
occasions, from shame they passed to effrontery and two heads of clans
advanced upon the king.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said they, 芒聙聹we have promised to deliver Scotland and England
from him who for the last five-and-twenty years has sucked the blood
and gold of Scotland and England. We have promised and we will keep our
promise. Charles Stuart, you are our prisoner.芒聙聺

And both extended their hands as if to seize the king, but before they
could touch him with the tips of their fingers, both had fallen, one
dead, the other stunned.

Aramis had passed his sword through the body of the first and Athos had
knocked down the other with the butt end of his pistol.

Then, as Lord Leven and the other chieftains recoiled before this
unexpected rescue, which seemed to come from Heaven for the prince they
already thought was their prisoner, Athos and Aramis dragged the king
from the perjured assembly into which he had so imprudently ventured,
and throwing themselves on horseback all three returned at full gallop
to the royal tent.

On their road they perceived Lord Winter marching at the head of his
regiment. The king motioned him to accompany them.




Chapter LVI.
The Avenger.


They all four entered the tent; they had no plan ready芒聙聰they must think
of one.

The king threw himself into an arm-chair. 芒聙聹I am lost,芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹No, sire,芒聙聺 replied Athos. 芒聙聹You are only betrayed.芒聙聺

The king sighed deeply.

芒聙聹Betrayed! yes betrayed by the Scotch, amongst whom I was born, whom I
have always loved better than the English. Oh, traitors that ye are!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹this is not a moment for recrimination, but a time
to show yourself a king and a gentleman. Up, sire! up! for you have
here at least three men who will not betray you. Ah! if we had been
five!芒聙聺 murmured Athos, thinking of D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos.

芒聙聹What do you say?芒聙聺 inquired Charles, rising.

芒聙聹I say, sire, that there is now but one way open. Lord Winter answers
for his regiment, or at least very nearly so芒聙聰we will not split straws
about words芒聙聰let him place himself at the head of his men, we will place
ourselves at the side of your majesty, and we will mow a swath through
Cromwell芒聙聶s army and reach Scotland.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There is another method,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹Let one of us put on the dress
and mount the king芒聙聶s horse. Whilst they pursue him the king might
escape.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is good advice,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and if the king will do one of us the
honor we shall be truly grateful to him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you think of this counsel, Winter?芒聙聺 asked the king, looking
with admiration at these two men, whose chief idea seemed to be how
they could take on their shoulders all the dangers that assailed him.

芒聙聹I think the only chance of saving your majesty has just been proposed
by Monsieur d芒聙聶Herblay. I humbly entreat your majesty to choose quickly,
for we have not an instant to lose.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But if I accept, it is death, or at least imprisonment, for him who
takes my place.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He will have had the glory of having saved his king,芒聙聺 cried Winter.

The king looked at his old friend with tears in his eyes; undid the
Order of the Saint Esprit which he wore, to honor the two Frenchmen who
were with him, and passed it around Winter芒聙聶s neck, who received on his
knees this striking proof of his sovereign芒聙聶s confidence and friendship.

芒聙聹It is right,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹he has served your majesty longer than we
have.芒聙聺

The king overheard these words and turned around with tears in his
eyes.

芒聙聹Wait a moment, sir,芒聙聺 said he; 芒聙聹I have an order for each of you also.芒聙聺

He turned to a closet where his own orders were locked up, and took out
two ribbons of the Order of the Garter.

芒聙聹These cannot be for us,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Why not, sir?芒聙聺 asked Charles.

芒聙聹Such are for royalty, and we are simple commoners.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Speak not of crowns. I shall not find amongst them such great hearts
as yours. No, no, you do yourselves injustice; but I am here to do you
justice. On your knees, count.芒聙聺

Athos knelt down and the king passed the ribbon down from left to right
as usual, raised his sword, and instead of pronouncing the customary
formula, 芒聙聹I make you a knight. Be brave, faithful and loyal,芒聙聺 he said,
芒聙聹You are brave, faithful and loyal. I knight you, monsieur le comte.芒聙聺

Then turning to Aramis, he said:

芒聙聹It is now your turn, monsieur le chevalier.芒聙聺

The same ceremony recommenced, with the same words, whilst Winter
unlaced his leather cuirass, that he might disguise himself like the
king. Charles, having proceeded with Aramis as with Athos, embraced
them both.

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Winter, who in this trying emergency felt all his strength
and energy fire up, 芒聙聹we are ready.芒聙聺

The king looked at the three gentlemen. 芒聙聹Then we must fly!芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Flying through an army, sire,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹in all countries in the
world is called _charging_.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then I shall die, sword in hand,芒聙聺 said Charles. 芒聙聹Monsieur le comte,
monsieur le chevalier, if ever I am king芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire, you have already done us more honor than simple gentlemen could
ever aspire to, therefore gratitude is on our side. But we must not
lose time. We have already wasted too much.芒聙聺

The king again shook hands with all three, exchanged hats with Winter
and went out.

Winter芒聙聶s regiment was ranged on some high ground above the camp. The
king, followed by the three friends, turned his steps that way. The
Scotch camp seemed as if at last awakened; the soldiers had come out of
their tents and taken up their station in battle array.

芒聙聹Do you see that?芒聙聺 said the king. 芒聙聹Perhaps they are penitent and
preparing to march.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If they are penitent,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹let them follow us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹what shall we do?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us examine the enemy芒聙聶s army.芒聙聺

At the same instant the eyes of the little group were fixed on the same
line which at daybreak they had mistaken for fog and which the morning
sun now plainly showed was an army in order of battle. The air was soft
and clear, as it generally is at that early hour of the morning. The
regiments, the standards, and even the colors of the horses and
uniforms were now clearly distinct.

On the summit of a rising ground, a little in advance of the enemy,
appeared a short and heavy looking man; this man was surrounded by
officers. He turned a spyglass toward the little group amongst which
the king stood.

芒聙聹Does this man know your majesty personally?芒聙聺 inquired Aramis.

Charles smiled.

芒聙聹That man is Cromwell,芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹Then draw down your hat, sire, that he may not discover the
substitution.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹how much time we have lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹give the word and let us start.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Will you not give it, sire?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹No; I make you my lieutenant-general,芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹Listen, then, Lord Winter. Proceed, sire, I beg. What we are going to
say does not concern your majesty.芒聙聺

The king, smiling, turned a few steps back.

芒聙聹This is what I propose to do,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹We will divide our
regiments into two squadrons. You will put yourself at the head of the
first. We and his majesty will lead the second. If no obstacle occurs
we will both charge together, force the enemy芒聙聶s line and throw
ourselves into the Tyne, which we must cross, either by fording or
swimming; if, on the contrary, any repulse should take place, you and
your men must fight to the last man, whilst we and the king proceed on
our road. Once arrived at the brink of the river, should we even find
them three ranks deep, as long as you and your regiment do your duty,
we will look to the rest.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To horse!芒聙聺 said Lord Winter.

芒聙聹To horse!芒聙聺 re-echoed Athos; 芒聙聹everything is arranged and decided.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Now, gentlemen,芒聙聺 cried the king, 芒聙聹forward! and rally to the old cry of
France, 芒聙聵Montjoy and St. Denis!芒聙聶 The war cry of England is too often in
the mouths of traitors.芒聙聺

They mounted芒聙聰the king on Winter芒聙聶s horse and Winter on that of the king;
then Winter took his place at the head of the first squadron, and the
king, with Athos on his right and Aramis on his left, at the head of
the second.

The Scotch army stood motionless and silent, seized with shame at sight
of these preparations.

Some of the chieftains left the ranks and broke their swords in two.

芒聙聹There,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹that consoles me; they are not all traitors.芒聙聺

At this moment Winter芒聙聶s voice was raised with the cry of 芒聙聹Forward!芒聙聺

The first squadron moved off; the second followed, and descended from
the plateau. A regiment of cuirassiers, nearly equal as to numbers,
issued from behind the hill and came full gallop toward it.

The king pointed this out.

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹we foresaw this; and if Lord Winter芒聙聶s men but do
their duty, we are saved, instead of lost.芒聙聺

At this moment they heard above all the galloping and neighing of the
horses Winter芒聙聶s voice crying out:

芒聙聹Sword in hand!芒聙聺

At these words every sword was drawn, and glittered in the air like
lightning.

芒聙聹Now, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the king in his turn, excited by this sight,
芒聙聹come, gentlemen, sword in hand!芒聙聺

But Aramis and Athos were the only ones to obey this command and the
king芒聙聶s example.

芒聙聹We are betrayed,芒聙聺 said the king in a low voice.

芒聙聹Wait a moment,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹perhaps they do not recognize your
majesty芒聙聶s voice, and await the order of their captain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have they not heard that of their colonel? But look! look!芒聙聺 cried the
king, drawing up his horse with a sudden jerk, which threw it on its
haunches, and seizing the bridle of Athos芒聙聶s horse.

芒聙聹Ah, cowards! traitors!芒聙聺 screamed Lord Winter, whose voice they heard,
whilst his men, quitting their ranks, dispersed all over the plain.

About fifteen men were ranged around him and awaited the charge of
Cromwell芒聙聶s cuirassiers.

芒聙聹Let us go and die with them!芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹Let us go,芒聙聺 said Athos and Aramis.

芒聙聹All faithful hearts with me!芒聙聺 cried out Winter.

This voice was heard by the two friends, who set off, full gallop.

芒聙聹No quarter!芒聙聺 cried a voice in French, answering to that of Winter,
which made them tremble.

As for Winter, at the sound of that voice he turned pale, and was, as
it were, petrified.

It was the voice of a cavalier mounted on a magnificent black horse,
who was charging at the head of the English regiment, of which, in his
ardor, he was ten steps in advance.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis he!芒聙聺 murmured Winter, his eyes glazed and he allowed his sword to
fall to his side.

芒聙聹The king! the king!芒聙聺 cried out several voices, deceived by the blue
ribbon and chestnut horse of Winter; 芒聙聹take him alive.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No! it is not the king!芒聙聺 exclaimed the cavalier. 芒聙聹Lord Winter, you are
not the king; you are my uncle.芒聙聺

At the same moment Mordaunt, for it was he, leveled his pistol at
Winter; it went off and the ball entered the heart of the old cavalier,
who with one bound on his saddle fell back into the arms of Athos,
murmuring: 芒聙聹He is avenged!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Think of my mother!芒聙聺 shouted Mordaunt, as his horse plunged and darted
off at full gallop.

芒聙聹Wretch!芒聙聺 exclaimed Aramis, raising his pistol as he passed by him; but
the powder flashed in the pan and it did not go off.

At this moment the whole regiment came up and they fell upon the few
men who had held out, surrounding the two Frenchmen. Athos, after
making sure that Lord Winter was really dead, let fall the corpse and
said:

芒聙聹Come, Aramis, now for the honor of France!芒聙聺 and the two Englishmen who
were nearest to them fell, mortally wounded.

At the same moment a fearful 芒聙聹hurrah!芒聙聺 rent the air and thirty blades
glittered about their heads.

Suddenly a man sprang out of the English ranks, fell upon Athos, twined
arms of steel around him, and tearing his sword from him, said in his
ear:

芒聙聹Silence! yield芒聙聰you yield to me, do you not?芒聙聺

A giant had seized also Aramis芒聙聶s two wrists, who struggled in vain to
release himself from this formidable grasp.

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Art芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, whilst the Gascon covered his mouth with his
hand.

芒聙聹I am your prisoner,芒聙聺 said Aramis, giving up his sword to Porthos.

芒聙聹Fire, fire!芒聙聺 cried Mordaunt, returning to the group surrounding the
two friends.

芒聙聹And wherefore fire?芒聙聺 said the colonel; 芒聙聹every one has yielded.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the son of Milady,芒聙聺 said Athos to D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹I recognize him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the monk,芒聙聺 whispered Porthos to Aramis.

芒聙聹I know it.芒聙聺

And now the ranks began to open. D芒聙聶Artagnan held the bridle of Athos芒聙聶s
horse and Porthos that of Aramis. Both of them attempted to lead his
prisoner off the battle-field.

This movement revealed the spot where Winter芒聙聶s body had fallen.
Mordaunt had found it out and was gazing on his dead relative with an
expression of malignant hatred.

Athos, though now cool and collected, put his hand to his belt, where
his loaded pistols yet remained.

芒聙聹What are you about?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Let me kill him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are all four lost, if by the least gesture you discover that you
recognize him.芒聙聺

Then turning to the young man he exclaimed:

芒聙聹A fine prize! a fine prize, friend Mordaunt; we have both myself and
Monsieur du Vallon, taken two Knights of the Garter, nothing less.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, looking at Athos and Aramis with bloodshot eyes,
芒聙聹these are Frenchmen, I imagine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I芒聙聶faith, I don芒聙聶t know. Are you French, sir?芒聙聺 said he to Athos.

芒聙聹I am,芒聙聺 replied the latter, gravely.

芒聙聹Very well, my dear sir, you are the prisoner of a fellow countryman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But the king芒聙聰where is the king?芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, anxiously.

D芒聙聶Artagnan vigorously seized his prisoner芒聙聶s hand, saying:

芒聙聹Eh! the king? We have secured him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹through an infamous act of treason.芒聙聺

Porthos pressed his friend芒聙聶s hand and said to him:

芒聙聹Yes, sir, all is fair in war, stratagem as well as force; look
yonder!芒聙聺

At this instant the squadron, that ought to have protected Charles芒聙聶s
retreat, was advancing to meet the English regiments. The king, who was
entirely surrounded, walked alone in a great empty space. He appeared
calm, but it was evidently not without a mighty effort. Drops of
perspiration trickled down his face, and from time to time he put a
handkerchief to his mouth to wipe away the blood that rilled from it.

芒聙聹Behold Nebuchadnezzar!芒聙聺 exclaimed an old Puritan soldier, whose eyes
flashed at the sight of the man they called the tyrant.

芒聙聹Do you call him Nebuchadnezzar?芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, with a terrible smile;
芒聙聹no, it is Charles the First, the king, the good King Charles, who
despoils his subjects to enrich himself.芒聙聺

Charles glanced a moment at the insolent creature who uttered this, but
did not recognize him. Nevertheless, the calm religious dignity of his
countenance abashed Mordaunt.

芒聙聹_Bon jour_, messieurs!芒聙聺 said the king to the two gentlemen who were
held by D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos. 芒聙聹The day has been unfortunate, but it
is not your fault, thank God! But where is my old friend Winter?芒聙聺

The two gentlemen turned away their heads in silence.

芒聙聹In Strafford芒聙聶s company,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, tauntingly.

Charles shuddered. The demon had known how to wound him. The
remembrance of Strafford was a source of lasting remorse to him, the
shadow that haunted him by day and night. The king looked around him.
He saw a corpse at his feet. It was Winter芒聙聶s. He uttered not a word,
nor shed a tear, but a deadly pallor spread over his face; he knelt
down on the ground, raised Winter芒聙聶s head, and unfastening the Order of
the Saint Esprit, placed it on his own breast.

芒聙聹Lord Winter is killed, then?芒聙聺 inquired D芒聙聶Artagnan, fixing his eyes on
the corpse.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹by his own nephew.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, he was the first of us to go; peace be to him! he was an honest
man,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Charles Stuart,芒聙聺 said the colonel of the English regiment, approaching
the king, who had just put on the insignia of royalty, 芒聙聹do you yield
yourself a prisoner?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Colonel Tomlison,芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹kings cannot yield; the man alone
submits to force.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your sword.芒聙聺

The king drew his sword and broke it on his knee.

At this moment a horse without a rider, covered with foam, his nostrils
extended and eyes all fire, galloped up, and recognizing his master,
stopped and neighed with pleasure; it was Arthur.

The king smiled, patted it with his hand and jumped lightly into the
saddle.

芒聙聹Now, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹conduct me where you will.芒聙聺

Turning back again, he said, 芒聙聹I thought I saw Winter move; if he still
lives, by all you hold most sacred, do not abandon him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Never fear, King Charles,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹the bullet pierced his
heart.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do not breathe a word nor make the least sign to me or Porthos,芒聙聺 said
D芒聙聶Artagnan to Athos and Aramis, 芒聙聹that you recognize this man, for
Milady is not dead; her soul lives in the body of this demon.芒聙聺

The detachment now moved toward the town with the royal captive; but on
the road an aide-de-camp, from Cromwell, sent orders that Colonel
Tomlison should conduct him to Holdenby Castle.

At the same time couriers started in every direction over England and
Europe to announce that Charles Stuart was the prisoner of Oliver
Cromwell.




Chapter LVII.
Oliver Cromwell.


Have you been to the general?芒聙聺 said Mordaunt to D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos;
芒聙聹you know he sent for you after the action.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We want first to put our prisoners in a place of safety,芒聙聺 replied
D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Do you know, sir, these gentlemen are each of them worth
fifteen hundred pounds?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, be assured,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, looking at them with an expression he
vainly endeavoured to soften, 芒聙聹my soldiers will guard them, and guard
them well, I promise you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall take better care of them myself,芒聙聺 answered D芒聙聶Artagnan;
芒聙聹besides, all they require is a good room, with sentinels, or their
simple parole that they will not attempt escape. I will go and see
about that, and then we shall have the honor of presenting ourselves to
the general and receiving his commands for his eminence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You think of starting at once, then?芒聙聺 inquired Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Our mission is ended, and there is nothing more to detain us now but
the good pleasure of the great man to whom we were sent.芒聙聺

The young man bit his lips and whispered to his sergeant:

芒聙聹You will follow these men and not lose sight of them; when you have
discovered where they lodge, come and await me at the town gate.芒聙聺

The sergeant made a sign of comprehension.

Instead of following the knot of prisoners that were being taken into
the town, Mordaunt turned his steps toward the rising ground from
whence Cromwell had witnessed the battle and on which he had just had
his tent pitched.

Cromwell had given orders that no one was to be allowed admission; but
the sentinel, who knew that Mordaunt was one of the most confidential
friends of the general, thought the order did not extend to the young
man. Mordaunt, therefore, raised the canvas, and saw Cromwell seated
before a table, his head buried in his hands, his back being turned.

Whether he heard Mordaunt or not as he entered, Cromwell did not move.
Mordaunt remained standing near the door. At last, after a few moments,
Cromwell raised his head, and, as if he divined that some one was
there, turned slowly around.

芒聙聹I said I wished to be alone,芒聙聺 he exclaimed, on seeing the young man.

芒聙聹They thought this order did not concern me, sir; nevertheless, if you
wish it, I am ready to go.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! is it you, Mordaunt?芒聙聺 said Cromwell, the cloud passing away from
his face; 芒聙聹since you are here, it is well; you may remain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I come to congratulate you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To congratulate me芒聙聰what for?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On the capture of Charles Stuart. You are now master of England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was much more really so two hours ago.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How so, general?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because England had need of me to take the tyrant, and now the tyrant
is taken. Have you seen him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺 said Mordaunt.

芒聙聹What is his bearing?芒聙聺

Mordaunt hesitated; but it seemed as though he was constrained to tell
the truth.

芒聙聹Calm and dignified,芒聙聺 said he.

芒聙聹What did he say?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Some parting words to his friends.芒聙聺

芒聙聹His friends!芒聙聺 murmured Cromwell. 芒聙聹_Has_ he any friends?芒聙聺 Then he added
aloud, 芒聙聹Did he make any resistance?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, with the exception of two or three friends every one deserted
him; he had no means of resistance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To whom did he give up his sword?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He did not give it up; he broke it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He did well; but instead of breaking it, he might have used it to
still more advantage.芒聙聺

There was a momentary pause.

芒聙聹I heard that the colonel of the regiment that escorted Charles was
killed,芒聙聺 said Cromwell, staring very fixedly at Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Yes, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By whom?芒聙聺 inquired Cromwell.

芒聙聹By me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What was his name?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lord Winter.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your uncle?芒聙聺 exclaimed Cromwell.

芒聙聹My uncle,芒聙聺 answered Mordaunt; 芒聙聹but traitors to England are no longer
members of my family.芒聙聺

Cromwell observed the young man a moment in silence, then, with that
profound melancholy Shakespeare describes so well:

芒聙聹Mordaunt,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you are a terrible servant.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When the Lord commands,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹His commands are not to be
disputed. Abraham raised the knife against Isaac, and Isaac was his
son.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Cromwell, 芒聙聹but the Lord did not suffer that sacrifice to be
accomplished.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have looked around me,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹and I have seen neither goat
nor kid caught among the bushes of the plain.芒聙聺

Cromwell bowed. 芒聙聹You are strong among the strong, Mordaunt,芒聙聺 he said;
芒聙聹and the Frenchmen, how did they behave?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Most fearlessly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes,芒聙聺 murmured Cromwell; 芒聙聹the French fight well; and if my glass
was good and I mistake not, they were foremost in the fight.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They were,芒聙聺 replied Mordaunt.

芒聙聹After you, however,芒聙聺 said Cromwell.

芒聙聹It was the fault of their horses, not theirs.芒聙聺

Another pause.

芒聙聹And the Scotch?芒聙聺

芒聙聹They kept their word and never stirred,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Wretched men!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Their officers wish to see you, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have no time to see them. Are they paid?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, to-night.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let them be off and return to their own country, there to hide their
shame, if its hills are high enough; I have nothing more to do with
them nor they with me. And now go, Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Before I go,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹I have some questions and a favor to ask
you, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A favor from me?芒聙聺

Mordaunt bowed.

芒聙聹I come to you, my leader, my head, my father, and I ask you, master,
are you contented with me?芒聙聺

Cromwell looked at him with astonishment. The young man remained
immovable.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Cromwell; 芒聙聹you have done, since I knew you, not only your
duty, but more than your duty; you have been a faithful friend, a
cautious negotiator, a brave soldier.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you remember, sir it was my idea, the Scotch treaty, for giving up
the king?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, the idea was yours. I had no such contempt for men before.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Was I not a good ambassador in France?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, for Mazarin has granted what I desire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have I not always fought for your glory and interests?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Too ardently, perhaps; it is what I have just reproached you for. But
what is the meaning of all these questions?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To tell you, my lord, that the moment has now arrived when, with a
single word, you may recompense all these services.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 said Oliver, with a slight curl of his lip, 芒聙聹I forgot that every
service merits some reward and that up to this moment you have not been
paid.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, I can take my pay at this moment, to the full extent of my
wishes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How is that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I have the payment under my hand; I almost possess it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is it? Have they offered you money? Do you wish a step, or some
place in the government?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, will you grant me my request?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let us hear what it is, first.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, when you have told me to obey an order did I ever answer, 芒聙聵Let me
see that order芒聙聶?芒聙聺

芒聙聹If, however, your wish should be one impossible to fulfill?芒聙聺

芒聙聹When you have cherished a wish and have charged me with its
fulfillment, have I ever replied, 芒聙聵It is impossible芒聙聶?芒聙聺

芒聙聹But a request preferred with so much preparation芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, do not fear, sir,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, with apparent simplicity: 芒聙聹it
will not ruin you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 said Cromwell, 芒聙聹I promise, as far as lies in my power, to
grant your request; proceed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, two prisoners were taken this morning, will you let me have
them?芒聙聺

芒聙聹For their ransom? have they then offered a large one?芒聙聺 inquired
Cromwell.

芒聙聹On the contrary, I think they are poor, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They are friends of yours, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 exclaimed Mordaunt, 芒聙聹they are friends, dear friends of
mine, and I would lay down my life for them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well, Mordaunt,芒聙聺 exclaimed Cromwell, pleased at having his
opinion of the young man raised once more; 芒聙聹I will give them to you; I
will not even ask who they are; do as you like with them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank you, sir!芒聙聺 exclaimed Mordaunt, 芒聙聹thank you; my life is always at
your service, and should I lose it I should still owe you something;
thank you; you have indeed repaid me munificently for my services.芒聙聺

He threw himself at the feet of Cromwell, and in spite of the efforts
of the Puritan general, who did not like this almost kingly homage, he
took his hand and kissed it.

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 said Cromwell, arresting him for a moment as he arose; 芒聙聹is
there nothing more you wish? neither gold nor rank?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have given me all you can give me, and from to-day your debt is
paid.芒聙聺

And Mordaunt darted out of the general芒聙聶s tent, his heart beating and
his eyes sparkling with joy.

Cromwell gazed a moment after him.

芒聙聹He has slain his uncle!芒聙聺 he murmured. 芒聙聹Alas! what are my servants?
Possibly this one, who asks nothing or seems to ask nothing, has asked
more in the eyes of Heaven than those who tax the country and steal the
bread of the poor. Nobody serves me for nothing. Charles, who is my
prisoner, may still have friends, but I have none!芒聙聺

And with a deep sigh he again sank into the reverie that had been
interrupted by Mordaunt.




Chapter LVIII.
Jesus Seigneur.


Whilst Mordaunt was making his way to Cromwell芒聙聶s tent, D芒聙聶Artagnan and
Porthos had brought their prisoners to the house which had been
assigned to them as their dwelling at Newcastle.

The order given by Mordaunt to the sergeant had been heard by
D芒聙聶Artagnan, who accordingly, by an expressive glance, warned Athos and
Aramis to exercise extreme caution. The prisoners, therefore, had
remained silent as they marched along in company with their
conquerors芒聙聰which they could do with the less difficulty since each of
them had occupation enough in answering his own thoughts.

It would be impossible to describe Mousqueton芒聙聶s astonishment when from
the threshold of the door he saw the four friends approaching, followed
by a sergeant with a dozen men. He rubbed his eyes, doubting if he
really saw before him Athos and Aramis; and forced at last to yield to
evidence, he was on the point of breaking forth in exclamations when he
encountered a glance from the eyes of Porthos, the repressive force of
which he was not inclined to dispute.

Mousqueton remained glued to the door, awaiting the explanation of this
strange occurrence. What upset him completely was that the four friends
seemed to have no acquaintance with one another.

The house to which D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos conducted Athos and Aramis
was the one assigned to them by General Cromwell and of which they had
taken possession on the previous evening. It was at the corner of two
streets and had in the rear, bordering on the side street, stables and
a sort of garden. The windows on the ground floor, according to a
custom in provincial villages, were barred, so that they strongly
resembled the windows of a prison.

The two friends made the prisoners enter the house first, whilst they
stood at the door, desiring Mousqueton to take the four horses to the
stable.

芒聙聹Why don芒聙聶t we go in with them?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹We must first see what the sergeant wishes us to do,芒聙聺 replied
D芒聙聶Artagnan.

The sergeant and his men took possession of the little garden.

D芒聙聶Artagnan asked them what they wished and why they had taken that
position.

芒聙聹We have had orders,芒聙聺 answered the man, 芒聙聹to help you in taking care of
your prisoners.芒聙聺

There could be no fault to find with this arrangement; on the contrary,
it seemed to be a delicate attention, to be gratefully received;
D芒聙聶Artagnan, therefore, thanked the man and gave him a crown piece to
drink to General Cromwell芒聙聶s health.

The sergeant answered that Puritans never drank, and put the crown
piece in his pocket.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹what a fearful day, my dear D芒聙聶Artagnan!芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! a fearful day, when to-day we find our friends?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; but under what circumstances?芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true that our position is an awkward one; but let us go in and
see more clearly what is to be done.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Things look black enough,芒聙聺 replied Porthos; 芒聙聹I understand now why
Aramis advised me to strangle that horrible Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Silence!芒聙聺 cried the Gascon; 芒聙聹do not utter that name.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 argued Porthos, 芒聙聹I speak French and they are all English.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan looked at Porthos with that air of wonder which a cunning
man cannot help feeling at displays of crass stupidity.

But as Porthos on his side could not comprehend his astonishment, he
merely pushed him indoors, saying, 芒聙聹Let us go in.芒聙聺

They found Athos in profound despondency; Aramis looked first at
Porthos and then at D芒聙聶Artagnan, without speaking, but the latter
understood his meaningful look.

芒聙聹You want to know how we came here? 芒聙聶Tis easily guessed. Mazarin sent
us with a letter to General Cromwell.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But how came you to fall into company with Mordaunt, whom I bade you
distrust?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹And whom I advised you to strangle, Porthos,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Mazarin again. Cromwell had sent him to Mazarin. Mazarin sent us to
Cromwell. There is a certain fatality in it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, you are right, D芒聙聶Artagnan, a fatality that will separate and ruin
us! So, my dear Aramis, say no more about it and let us prepare to
submit to destiny.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Zounds! on the contrary, let us speak about it; for it was agreed
among us, once for all, that we should always hold together, though
engaged on opposing sides.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 added Athos, 芒聙聹I now ask you, D芒聙聶Artagnan, what side you are on?
Ah! behold for what end the wretched Mazarin has made use of you. Do
you know in what crime you are to-day engaged? In the capture of a
king, his degradation and his murder.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! oh!芒聙聺 cried Porthos, 芒聙聹do you think so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are exaggerating, Athos; we are not so far gone as that,芒聙聺 replied
the lieutenant.

芒聙聹Good heavens! we are on the very eve of it. I say, why is the king
taken prisoner? Those who wish to respect him as a master would not buy
him as a slave. Do you think it is to replace him on the throne that
Cromwell has paid for him two hundred thousand pounds sterling? They
will kill him, you may be sure of it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t maintain the contrary,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹But what芒聙聶s that to
us? I am here because I am a soldier and have to obey orders芒聙聰I have
taken an oath to obey, and I do obey; but you who have taken no such
oath, why are you here and what cause do you represent?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That most sacred in the world,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹the cause of misfortune,
of religion, royalty. A friend, a wife, a daughter, have done us the
honor to call us to their aid. We have served them to the best of our
poor means, and God will recompense the will, forgive the want of
power. You may see matters differently, D芒聙聶Artagnan, and think
otherwise. I will not attempt to argue with you, but I blame you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Heyday!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹what matters it to me, after all, if
Cromwell, who芒聙聶s an Englishman, revolts against his king, who is a
Scotchman? I am myself a Frenchman. I have nothing to do with these
things芒聙聰why hold me responsible?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Because all gentlemen are brothers, because you are a gentleman,
because the kings of all countries are the first among gentlemen,
because the blind populace, ungrateful and brutal, always takes
pleasure in pulling down what is above them. And you, you, D芒聙聶Artagnan,
a man sprung from the ancient nobility of France, bearing an honorable
name, carrying a good sword, have helped to give up a king to
beersellers, shopkeepers, and wagoners. Ah! D芒聙聶Artagnan! perhaps you
have done your duty as a soldier, but as a gentleman, I say that you
are very culpable.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan was chewing the stalk of a flower, unable to reply and
thoroughly uncomfortable; for when turned from the eyes of Athos he
encountered those of Aramis.

芒聙聹And you, Porthos,芒聙聺 continued the count, as if in consideration for
D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s embarrassment, 芒聙聹you, the best heart, the best friend, the
best soldier that I know芒聙聰you, with a soul that makes you worthy of a
birth on the steps of a throne, and who, sooner or later, must receive
your reward from an intelligent king芒聙聰you, my dear Porthos, you, a
gentleman in manners, in tastes and in courage, you are as culpable as
D芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

Porthos blushed, but with pleasure rather than with confusion; and yet,
bowing his head, as if humiliated, he said:

芒聙聹Yes, yes, my dear count, I feel that you are right.芒聙聺

Athos arose.

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 he said, stretching out his hand to D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹come, don芒聙聶t be
sullen, my dear son, for I have said all this to you, if not in the
tone, at least with the feelings of a father. It would have been easier
to me merely to have thanked you for preserving my life and not to have
uttered a word of all this.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Doubtless, doubtless, Athos. But here it is: you have sentiments, the
devil knows what, such as every one can芒聙聶t entertain. Who could suppose
that a sensible man could leave his house, France, his ward芒聙聰a charming
youth, for we saw him in the camp芒聙聰to fly to the aid of a rotten,
worm-eaten royalty, which is going to crumble one of these days like an
old hovel. The sentiments you air are certainly fine, so fine that they
are superhuman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹However that may be, D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 replied Athos, without falling into
the snare which his Gascon friend had prepared for him by an appeal to
his parental love, 芒聙聹however that may be, you know in the bottom of your
heart that it is true; but I am wrong to dispute with my master.
D芒聙聶Artagnan, I am your prisoner芒聙聰treat me as such.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! _pardieu!_芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹you know you will not be my prisoner
very long.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹they will doubtless treat us like the prisoners of
the Philipghauts.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And how were they treated?芒聙聺 asked D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Why,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹one-half were hanged and the other half were
shot.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, I,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan 芒聙聹I answer that while there remains a drop of
blood in my veins you will be neither hanged nor shot. _Sang Diou!_ let
them come on! Besides芒聙聰do you see that door, Athos?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; what then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, you can go out by that door whenever you please; for from this
moment you are free as the air.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I recognize you there, my brave D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 replied Athos; 芒聙聹but you
are no longer our masters. That door is guarded, D芒聙聶Artagnan; you know
that.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well, you will force it,芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹There are only a dozen
men at the most.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That would be nothing for us four; it is too much for us two. No,
divided as we now are, we must perish. See the fatal example: on the
Vendomois road, D芒聙聶Artagnan, you so brave, and you, Porthos, so valiant
and so strong芒聙聰you were beaten; to-day Aramis and I are beaten in our
turn. Now that never happened to us when we were four together. Let us
die, then, as De Winter has died; as for me, I will fly only on
condition that we all fly together.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Impossible,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹we are under Mazarin芒聙聶s orders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know it and I have nothing more to say; my arguments lead to
nothing; doubtless they are bad, since they have not determined minds
so just as yours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Besides,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹had they taken effect it would be still better
not to compromise two excellent friends like D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos. Be
assured, gentlemen, we shall do you honor in our dying. As for myself,
I shall be proud to face the bullets, or even the rope, in company with
you, Athos; for you have never seemed to me so grand as you are
to-day.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan said nothing, but, after having gnawed the flower stalk, he
began to bite his nails. At last:

芒聙聹Do you imagine,芒聙聺 he resumed, 芒聙聹that they mean to kill you? And
wherefore should they do so? What interest have they in your death?
Moreover, you are _our_ prisoners.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Fool!芒聙聺 cried Aramis; 芒聙聹knowest thou not, then, Mordaunt? I have but
exchanged with him one look, yet that look convinced me that we were
doomed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The truth is, I芒聙聶m very sorry that I did not strangle him as you
advised me,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Eh! I make no account of the harm Mordaunt can do!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan.
芒聙聹_Cap de Diou!_ if he troubles me too much I will crush him, the
insect! Do not fly, then. It is useless; for I swear to you that you
are as safe here as you were twenty years, ago芒聙聰you, Athos, in the Rue
Ferou, and you, Aramis, in the Rue de Vaugirard.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Stop,芒聙聺 cried Athos, extending his hand to one of the grated windows by
which the room was lighted; 芒聙聹you will soon know what to expect, for
here he is.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Mordaunt.芒聙聺

In fact, looking at the place to which Athos pointed, D芒聙聶Artagnan saw a
cavalier coming toward the house at full gallop.

It was Mordaunt.

D芒聙聶Artagnan rushed out of the room.

Porthos wanted to follow him.

芒聙聹Stay,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹and do not come till you hear me drum my
fingers on the door.芒聙聺

When Mordaunt arrived opposite the house he saw D芒聙聶Artagnan on the
threshold and the soldiers lying on the grass here and there, with
their arms.

芒聙聹Halloo!芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹are the prisoners still there?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 answered the sergeant, uncovering.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis well; order four men to conduct them to my lodging.芒聙聺

Four men prepared to do so.

芒聙聹What is it?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, with that jeering manner which our

Mordaunt芒聙聶s very lips grew white with rage.

This look of Porthos芒聙聶s made the Gascon regret that he had summoned the

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹a quarter of an hour to go to the tent, a

芒聙聹All right.芒聙聺

He then proceeded, with his usual calm gait, to the stable and went

At the corner of the street he met Mousqueton and took him with him.

D芒聙聶Artagnan, meantime, went into the house, whistling a tune which he

芒聙聹My dear Athos, I have reflected on your arguments and I am convinced.

He put it into his pocket. The two friends were perfectly stupefied.

芒聙聹Well, pray, is there anything to be so surprised at?芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹I was

The two friends went near him.

芒聙聹Do you see that street? There are the horses. Go out by the door, turn

The soldiers were playing or sleeping; two of them were singing in a

D芒聙聶Artagnan called the sergeant. 芒聙聹My dear friend, General Cromwell has

The sergeant made a sign, as much as to say he did not understand

芒聙聹In a moment.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You didn芒聙聶t forget my purse?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; be easy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good.芒聙聺

Porthos and Mousqueton each took a horse by the bridle and proceeded to

Then D芒聙聶Artagnan, being alone, struck a light and lighted a small bit of

The soldiers, whom he was nearly trampling, ran away.

芒聙聹Help! help!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹stop芒聙聰my horse has the staggers.芒聙聺

In an instant the horse芒聙聶s eyes grew bloodshot and he was white with

芒聙聹Help!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹What! will you let me be killed? _J脙漏sus

No sooner had he uttered this cry than the door opened and Athos and

芒聙聹The prisoners are escaping! the prisoners are escaping!芒聙聺 cried the

芒聙聹Stop! stop!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, giving rein to his famous steed, who,

芒聙聹Stop! stop!芒聙聺 cried the soldiers, and ran for their arms.

But the prisoners were in their saddles and lost no time hastening to

In the middle of the street they saw Grimaud and Blaisois, who were

They passed through the gate like apparitions, without the guards

All this time the soldiers were calling out, 芒聙聹Stop! stop!芒聙聺 and the
sergeant, who began to see that he was the victim of an artifice, was

芒聙聹The prisoners!芒聙聺 he exclaimed, jumping off his horse.

The sergeant had not the courage to reply; he showed him the open door,
the empty room. Mordaunt darted to the steps, understood all, uttered a
cry, as if his very heart was pierced, and fell fainting on the stone
steps.




Chapter LIX.
Noble Natures never lose Courage, nor good Stomachs their Appetites.


The little troop, without looking behind them or exchanging a word,
fled at a rapid gallop, fording a little stream, of which none of them
knew the name, and leaving on their left a town which Athos declared to
be Durham. At last they came in sight of a small wood, and spurring
their horses afresh, rode in its direction.

As soon as they had disappeared behind a green curtain sufficiently
thick to conceal them from the sight of any one who might be in pursuit
they drew up to hold a council together. The two grooms held the
horses, that they might take a little rest without being unsaddled, and
Grimaud was posted as sentinel.

芒聙聹Come, first of all,芒聙聺 said Athos to D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹my friend, that I may
shake hands with you芒聙聰you, our rescuer芒聙聰you, the true hero of us all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Athos is right芒聙聰you have my adoration,芒聙聺 said Aramis, in his turn
pressing his hand. 芒聙聹To what are you not equal, with your superior
intelligence, infallible eye, your arm of iron and your enterprising
mind!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹that is all well, I accept for Porthos and

The two friends, recalled by D芒聙聶Artagnan to what was also due to

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹it is not our plan to run anywhere and like

芒聙聹What are we going to do, i芒聙聶faith? It is not very difficult to say.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell us, then, D芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are going to reach the nearest seaport, unite our little resources,
hire a vessel and return to France. As for me I will give my last sou
for it. Life is the greatest treasure, and speaking candidly, ours
hangs by a thread.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you say to this, Du Vallon?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I am entirely of D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s opinion; this is a

芒聙聹You are quite decided, then, to leave it?芒聙聺 asked Athos of D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Egad! I don芒聙聶t see what is to keep me here.芒聙聺

A glance was exchanged between Athos and Aramis.

芒聙聹Go, then, my friends,芒聙聺 said the former, sighing.

芒聙聹How, go then?芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Let us go, you mean?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Leave you!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, quite bewildered at this unexpected

芒聙聹Bah!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹why separate, since we are all together?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because you can and ought to return to France; your mission is

芒聙聹Your mission is not accomplished?芒聙聺 exclaimed D芒聙聶Artagnan, looking in

芒聙聹No, my friend,芒聙聺 replied Athos, in his gentle but decided voice, 芒聙聹we

芒聙聹To save the king?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, looking at Aramis as he had looked

Aramis contented himself by making a sign with his head.

D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s countenance took an expression of the deepest compassion;
he began to think he had to do with madmen.

芒聙聹You cannot be speaking seriously, Athos!芒聙聺 said he; 芒聙聹the king is

芒聙聹芒聙聵Princes must be knocked on the head.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹we have promised.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So you know beforehand you must perish!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹We fear so, and our only regret is to die so far from both of _you_.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What will you do in a foreign land, an enemy芒聙聶s country?芒聙聺

芒聙聹And did you promise the queen,芒聙聺 resumed D芒聙聶Artagnan, petulantly, 芒聙聹to
storm the Tower of London, to kill a hundred thousand soldiers, to

Heaven芒聙聶s name, my dear Athos, do not make a useless sacrifice. When I
see you merely, you look like a reasonable being; when you speak, I
seem to have to do with a madman. Come, Porthos, join me; say frankly,
what do you think of this business?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Nothing good,芒聙聺 replied Porthos.

芒聙聹Come,芒聙聺 continued D芒聙聶Artagnan, who, irritated that instead of listening

芒聙聹Friend,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹our resolution is irrevocable.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then you have some other motive unknown to us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, at last, furious, 芒聙聹very well, since you

芒聙聹Only reflect, my good fellow,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹it is but dying rather

芒聙聹Pooh! a little sooner or a little later, it isn芒聙聶t worth quarreling

And the four friends, guided by Mousqueton, took up the way toward the

now, as Athos had said, all four once more united and of single mind.




Chapter LX.
Respect to Fallen Majesty.


As our fugitives approached the house, they found the ground cut up, as
if a considerable body of horsemen had preceded them. Before the door
the traces were yet more apparent; these horsemen, whoever they might
be, had halted there.

芒聙聹Egad!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹it芒聙聶s quite clear that the king and his
escort have been by here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹in that case they have eaten everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bah!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹they will have left a chicken, at least.芒聙聺 He
dismounted and knocked on the door. There was no response.

He pushed open the door and found the first room empty and deserted.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 cried Porthos.

芒聙聹I can see nobody,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Aha!芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Blood!芒聙聺

At this word the three friends leaped from their horses and entered.
D芒聙聶Artagnan had already opened the door of the second room, and from the
expression of his face it was clear that he there beheld some
extraordinary object.

The three friends drew near and discovered a young man stretched on the
ground, bathed in a pool of blood. It was evident that he had attempted
to regain his bed, but had not had sufficient strength to do so.

Athos, who imagined that he saw him move, was the first to go up to
him.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 inquired D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Well, if he is dead,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹he has not been so long, for he is
still warm. But no, his heart is beating. Ho, there, my friend!芒聙聺

The wounded man heaved a sigh. D芒聙聶Artagnan took some water in the hollow
of his hand and threw it upon his face. The man opened his eyes, made
an effort to raise his head, and fell back again. The wound was in the
top of his skull and blood was flawing copiously.

Aramis dipped a cloth into some water and applied it to the gash. Again
the wounded man opened his eyes and looked in astonishment at these
strangers, who appeared to pity him.

芒聙聹You are among friends,芒聙聺 said Athos, in English; 芒聙聹so cheer up, and tell

芒聙聹The king,芒聙聺 muttered the wounded man, 芒聙聹the king is a prisoner.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have seen him?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, in the same language.

The man made no reply.

芒聙聹Make your mind easy,芒聙聺 resumed Athos, 芒聙聹we are all faithful servants of
his majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is what you tell me true?芒聙聺 asked the wounded man.

芒聙聹On our honor as gentlemen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then I may tell you all. I am brother to Parry, his majesty芒聙聶s lackey.芒聙聺

Athos and Aramis remembered that this was the name by which De Winter
had called the man they had found in the passage of the king芒聙聶s tent.

芒聙聹We know him,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹he never left the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, that is he. Well, he thought of me, when he saw the king was
taken, and as they were passing before the house he begged in the
king芒聙聶s name that they would stop, as the king was hungry. They brought
him into this room and placed sentinels at the doors and windows. Parry
knew this room, as he had often been to see me when the king was at
Newcastle. He knew that there was a trap-door communicating with a
cellar, from which one could get into the orchard. He made a sign,
which I understood, but the king芒聙聶s guards must have noticed it and held
themselves on guard. I went out as if to fetch wood, passed through the
subterranean passage into the cellar, and whilst Parry was gently
bolting the door, pushed up the board and beckoned to the king to
follow me. Alas! he would not. But Parry clasped his hands and implored
him, and at last he agreed. I went on first, fortunately. The king was
a few steps behind me, when suddenly I saw something rise up in front
of me like a huge shadow. I wanted to cry out to warn the king, but
that very moment I felt a blow as if the house was falling on my head,
and fell insensible. When I came to myself again, I was stretched in
the same place. I dragged myself as far as the yard. The king and his
escort were no longer there. I spent perhaps an hour in coming from the
yard to this place; then my strength gave out and I fainted again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now how are you feeling?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very ill,芒聙聺 replied the wounded man.

芒聙聹Can we do anything for you?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Help to put me on the bed; I think I shall feel better there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have you any one to depend on for assistance?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My wife is at Durham and may return at any moment. But you芒聙聰is there
nothing that you want?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We came here with the intention of asking for something to eat.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas, they have taken everything; there isn芒聙聶t a morsel of bread in the

芒聙聹You hear, D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹we shall have to look elsewhere

芒聙聹It is all one to me now,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I am no longer hungry.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faith! neither am I,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

They carried the man to his bed and called Grimaud to dress the wound.
In the service of the four friends Grimaud had had so frequent occasion

In the meantime the fugitives had returned to the first room, where

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹we know how the matter stands. The king and his

Athos did not reply; he reflected.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹let us take the opposite direction; if we follow

芒聙聹What do you think, D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 asked Athos. 芒聙聹Do you agree with

芒聙聹Not at all,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹I am precisely of the contrary

Athos芒聙聶s eyes shone with joy.

芒聙聹Join the escort!芒聙聺 cried Aramis.

芒聙聹Let D芒聙聶Artagnan speak,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹you know he always has wise advice

芒聙聹Good, my friend, good!芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹It is excellent advice. I was

芒聙聹That, then, is your opinion?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹Yes. They will think we are trying to leave England and will search

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹I understand.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I, however, do not understand,芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹But no matter; since it

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹shall we not be suspected by Colonel Harrison?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Egad!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹he芒聙聶s just the man I count upon. Colonel

Athos smiled. 芒聙聹You are the best companion that I know, D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 he

This was, as we have seen, the term which Athos applied to D芒聙聶Artagnan
in his more expansive moods.

At this moment Grimaud came in. He had stanched the wound and the man

The four friends took leave of him and asked if they could deliver any

芒聙聹Tell him,芒聙聺 answered the brave man, 芒聙聹to let the king know that they

his majesty is concerned for me and blames himself for my death.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Be easy,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹he will know all before night.芒聙聺

The little troop recommenced their march, and at the end of two hours
perceived a considerable body of horsemen about half a league ahead.

芒聙聹My dear friends,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹give your swords to Monsieur

It was not long before they joined the escort. The king was riding in
front, surrounded by troopers, and when he saw Athos and Aramis a glow
of pleasure lighted his pale cheeks.

D芒聙聶Artagnan passed to the head of the column, and leaving his friends
under the guard of Porthos, went straight to Harrison, who recognized
him as having met him at Cromwell芒聙聶s and received him as politely as a
man of his breeding and disposition could. It turned out as D芒聙聶Artagnan
had foreseen. The colonel neither had nor could have any suspicion.

They halted for the king to dine. This time, however, due precautions
were taken to prevent any attempt at escape. In the large room of the
hotel a small table was placed for him and a large one for the
officers.

芒聙聹Will you dine with me?芒聙聺 asked Harrison of D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Gad, I should be very happy, but I have my companion, Monsieur du
Vallon, and the two prisoners, whom I cannot leave. Let us manage it
better. Have a table set for us in a corner and send us whatever you
like from yours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 answered Harrison.

The matter was arranged as D芒聙聶Artagnan had suggested, and when he
returned he found the king already seated at his little table, where
Parry waited on him, Harrison and his officers sitting together at
another table, and, in a corner, places reserved for himself and his
companions.

The table at which the Puritan officers were seated was round, and
whether by chance or coarse intention, Harrison sat with his back to
the king.

The king saw the four gentlemen come in, but appeared to take no notice
of them.

They sat down in such a manner as to turn their backs on nobody. The

Chapter LXI.
D芒聙聶Artagnan hits on a Plan.


As night closed in they arrived at Thirsk. The four friends appeared to
be entire strangers to one another and indifferent to the precautions
taken for guarding the king. They withdrew to a private house, and as
they had reason every moment to fear for their safety, they occupied
but one room and provided an exit, which might be useful in case of an
attack. The lackeys were sent to their several posts, except that
Grimaud lay on a truss of straw across the doorway.

Chapter LXII.
London.


As soon as the noise of the hoofs was lost in the distance D芒聙聶Artagnan

芒聙聹To prevent recognition,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Our clothes have a cut
