芒聙聹But can you find your man?芒聙聺 said Aramis to Athos.

芒聙聹Oh! to be sure, yes. He lives at the Bedford Tavern, Greenhall Street.

芒聙聹I wish we were already there,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹and my advice is that

芒聙聹Come on, then,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹for unless I am mistaken in my

The friends urged on their horses and arrived, in fact, at about five

Athos was right. He went direct to the Bedford Tavern, and the host,
who recognized him, was delighted to see him again with such a numerous
and promising company.

Though it was scarcely daylight our four travelers found the town in a
great bustle, owing to the reported approach of Harrison and the king.

The plan of changing their clothes was unanimously adopted. The
landlord sent out for every description of garment, as if he wanted to
fit up his wardrobe. Athos chose a black coat, which gave him the
appearance of a respectable citizen. Aramis, not wishing to part with
his sword, selected a dark-blue cloak of a military cut. Porthos was
seduced by a wine-colored doublet and sea-green breeches. D芒聙聶Artagnan,
who had fixed on his color beforehand, had only to select the shade,
and looked in his chestnut suit exactly like a retired sugar dealer.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹for the actual man. We must cut off our hair,
that the populace may not insult us. As we no longer wear the sword of
the gentleman we may as well have the head of the Puritan. This, as you
know, is the important point of distinction between the Covenanter and
the Cavalier.芒聙聺

After some discussion this was agreed to and Mousqueton played the
_r脙麓le_ of barber.

芒聙聹We look hideous,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹And smack of the Puritan to a frightful extent,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹My head feels actually cold,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹As for me, I feel anxious to preach a sermon,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹that we cannot even recognize one another and have
therefore no fear of others recognizing us, let us go and see the
king芒聙聶s entrance.芒聙聺

They had not been long in the crowd before loud cries announced the
king芒聙聶s arrival. A carriage had been sent to meet him, and the gigantic
Porthos, who stood a head above the entire rabble, soon announced that
he saw the royal equipage approaching. D芒聙聶Artagnan raised himself on
tiptoe, and as the carriage passed, saw Harrison at one window and
Mordaunt at the other.

The next day, Athos, leaning out of his window, which looked upon the
most populous part of the city, heard the Act of Parliament, which
summoned the ex-king, Charles I., to the bar, publicly cried.

芒聙聹Parliament indeed!芒聙聺 cried Athos. 芒聙聹Parliament can never have passed
such an act as that.芒聙聺

At this moment the landlord came in.

芒聙聹Did parliament pass this act?芒聙聺 Athos asked of him in English.

芒聙聹Yes, my lord, the pure parliament.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you mean by 芒聙聵the pure parliament芒聙聶? Are there, then, two
parliaments?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 D芒聙聶Artagnan interrupted, 芒聙聹as I don芒聙聶t understand English and
we all understand Spanish, have the kindness to speak to us in that
language, which, since it is your own, you must find pleasure in using
when you have the chance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! excellent!芒聙聺 said Aramis.

As to Porthos, all his attention was concentrated on the allurements of
the breakfast table.

芒聙聹You were asking, then?芒聙聺 said the host in Spanish.

芒聙聹I asked,芒聙聺 said Athos, in the same language, 芒聙聹if there are two
parliaments, a pure and an impure?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, how extraordinary!芒聙聺 said Porthos, slowly raising his head and
looking at his friends with an air of astonishment, 芒聙聹I understand
English, then! I understand what you say!芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is because we are talking Spanish, my dear friend,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Oh, the devil!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I am sorry for that; it would have been
one language more.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When I speak of the pure parliament,芒聙聺 resumed the host, 芒聙聹I mean the
one which Colonel Bridge has weeded.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! really,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹these people are very ingenious. When I
go back to France I must suggest some such convenient course to
Cardinal Mazarin and the coadjutor. One of them will weed the
parliament in the name of the court, and the other in the name of the
people; and then there won芒聙聶t be any parliament at all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And who is this Colonel Bridge?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, 芒聙聹and how does he go to
work to weed the parliament?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Colonel Bridge,芒聙聺 replied the Spaniard, 芒聙聹is a retired wagoner, a man of
much sense, who made one valuable observation whilst driving his team,
namely, that where there happened to be a stone on the road, it was
much easier to remove the stone than try and make the wheel pass over
it. Now, of two hundred and fifty-one members who composed the
parliament, there were one hundred and ninety-one who were in the way
and might have upset his political wagon. He took them up, just as he
formerly used to take up the stones from the road, and threw them out
of the house.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Neat,芒聙聺 remarked D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Very!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And all these one hundred and ninety-one were Royalists?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Without doubt, se脙卤or; and you understand that they would have saved
the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To be sure,芒聙聺 said Porthos, with majestic common sense; 芒聙聹they were in
the majority.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you think,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹he will consent to appear before such a
tribunal?芒聙聺

芒聙聹He will be forced to do so,芒聙聺 smiled the Spaniard.

芒聙聹Now, Athos!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹do you begin to believe that it芒聙聶s a
ruined cause, and that what with your Harrisons, Joyces, Bridges and
Cromwells, we shall never get the upper hand?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The king will be delivered at the tribunal,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹the very
silence of his supporters indicates that they are at work.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan shrugged his shoulders.

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹if they dare to condemn their king, it can only be
to exile or imprisonment.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan whistled a little air of incredulity.

芒聙聹We shall see,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹for we shall go to the sittings, I
presume.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You will not have long to wait,芒聙聺 said the landlord; 芒聙聹they begin
to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So, then, they drew up the indictments before the king was taken?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Of course,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹they began the day he was sold.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you know,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹that it was our friend Mordaunt who made,

芒聙聹And you know,芒聙聺 added D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that whenever I catch him I will
kill him, this Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I, too,芒聙聺 exclaimed Porthos.

芒聙聹And I, too,芒聙聺 added Aramis.

芒聙聹Touching unanimity!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹which well becomes good
citizens like us. Let us take a turn around the town and imbibe a
little fog.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹芒聙聶twill be at least a little change from beer.芒聙聺




Chapter LXIII.
The Trial.


The next morning King Charles I. was haled by a strong guard before the
high court which was to judge him. All London was crowding to the doors
of the house. The throng was terrific, and it was not till after much
pushing and some fighting that our friends reached their destination.
When they did so they found the three lower rows of benches already
occupied; but being anxious not to be too conspicuous, all, with the
exception of Porthos, who had a fancy to display his red doublet, were
quite satisfied with their places, the more so as chance had brought
them to the centre of their row, so that they were exactly opposite the
arm-chair prepared for the royal prisoner.

Toward eleven o芒聙聶clock the king entered the hall, surrounded by guards,
but wearing his head covered, and with a calm expression turned to
every side with a look of complete assurance, as if he were there to
preside at an assembly of submissive subjects, rather than to meet the
accusations of a rebel court.

The judges, proud of having a monarch to humiliate, evidently prepared
to enjoy the right they had arrogated to themselves, and sent an
officer to inform the king that it was customary for the accused to
uncover his head.

Charles, without replying a single word, turned his head in another
direction and pulled his felt hat over it. Then when the officer was
gone he sat down in the arm-chair opposite the president and struck his
boots with a little cane which he carried in his hand. Parry, who
accompanied him, stood behind him.

D芒聙聶Artagnan was looking at Athos, whose face betrayed all those emotions
which the king, possessing more self-control, had banished from his
own. This agitation in one so cold and calm as Athos, frightened him.

芒聙聹I hope,芒聙聺 he whispered to him, 芒聙聹that you will follow his majesty芒聙聶s
example and not get killed for your folly in this den.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Set your mind at rest,芒聙聺 replied Athos.

芒聙聹Aha!芒聙聺 continued D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹it is clear that they are afraid of
something or other; for look, the sentinels are being reinforced. They
had only halberds before, now they have muskets. The halberds were for
the audience in the rear; the muskets are for us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thirty, forty, fifty, sixty-five men,芒聙聺 said Porthos, counting the
reinforcements.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹but you forget the officer.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan grew pale with rage. He recognized Mordaunt, who with bare
sword was marshalling the musketeers behind the king and opposite the
benches.

芒聙聹Do you think they have recognized us?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹In that case
I should beat a retreat. I don芒聙聶t care to be shot in a box.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹he has not seen us. He sees no one but the king.
_Mon Dieu!_ how he stares at him, the insolent dog! Does he hate his
majesty as much as he does us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹_Pardi_,芒聙聺 answered Athos 芒聙聹we only carried off his mother; the king has
spoiled him of his name and property.芒聙聺

芒聙聹True,芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹but silence! the president is speaking to the
king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Stuart,芒聙聺 Bradshaw was saying, 芒聙聹listen to the roll call of your judges
and address to the court any observations you may have to make.芒聙聺

The king turned his head away, as if these words had not been intended
for him. Bradshaw waited, and as there was no reply there was a moment
of silence.

Out of the hundred and sixty-three members designated there were only
seventy-three present, for the rest, fearful of taking part in such an
act, had remained away.

When the name of Colonel Fairfax was called, one of those brief but
solemn silences ensued, which announced the absence of the members who
had no wish to take a personal part in the trial.

芒聙聹Colonel Fairfax,芒聙聺 repeated Bradshaw.

芒聙聹Fairfax,芒聙聺 answered a laughing voice, the silvery tone of which
betrayed it as that of a woman, 芒聙聹is not such a fool as to be here.芒聙聺

A loud laugh followed these words, pronounced with that boldness which
women draw from their own weakness芒聙聰a weakness which removes them beyond
the power of vengeance.

芒聙聹It is a woman芒聙聶s voice,芒聙聺 cried Aramis; 芒聙聹faith, I would give a good deal
if she is young and pretty.芒聙聺 And he mounted on the bench to try and get
a sight of her.

芒聙聹By my soul,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹she is charming. Look D芒聙聶Artagnan; everybody
is looking at her; and in spite of Bradshaw芒聙聶s gaze she has not turned
pale.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is Lady Fairfax herself,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Don芒聙聶t you remember,
Porthos, we saw her at General Cromwell芒聙聶s?芒聙聺

The roll call continued.

芒聙聹These rascals will adjourn when they find that they are not in
sufficient force,芒聙聺 said the Comte de la F脙篓re.

芒聙聹You don芒聙聶t know them. Athos, look at Mordaunt芒聙聶s smile. Is that the look
of a man whose victim is likely to escape him? Ah, cursed basilisk, it
will be a happy day for me when I can cross something more than a look
with you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The king is really very handsome,芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹and look, too,
though he is a prisoner, how carefully he is dressed. The feather in
his hat is worth at least five-and-twenty pistoles. Look at it,
Aramis.芒聙聺

The roll call finished, the president ordered them to read the act of
accusation. Athos turned pale. A second time he was disappointed in his
expectation. Notwithstanding the judges were so few the trial was to
continue; the king then, was condemned in advance.

芒聙聹I told you so, Athos,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, shrugging his shoulders. 芒聙聹Now
take your courage in both hands and hear what this gentleman in black
is going to say about his sovereign, with full license and privilege.芒聙聺

Never till then had a more brutal accusation or meaner insults
tarnished kingly majesty.

Charles listened with marked attention, passing over the insults,
noting the grievances, and, when hatred overflowed all bounds and the
accuser turned executioner beforehand, replying with a smile of lofty
scorn.

芒聙聹The fact is,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹if men are punished for imprudence and
triviality, this poor king deserves punishment. But it seems to me that
that which he is just now undergoing is hard enough.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In any case,芒聙聺 Aramis replied, 芒聙聹the punishment should fall not on the
king, but on his ministers; for the first article of the constitution
is, 芒聙聵The king can do no wrong.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹As for me,芒聙聺 thought Porthos, giving Mordaunt his whole attention,
芒聙聹were it not for breaking in on the majesty of the situation I would
leap down from the bench, reach Mordaunt in three bounds and strangle
him; I would then take him by the feet and knock the life out of these
wretched musketeers who parody the musketeers of France. Meantime,
D芒聙聶Artagnan, who is full of invention, would find some way to save the
king. I must speak to him about it.芒聙聺

As to Athos, his face aflame, his fists clinched, his lips bitten till
they bled, he sat there foaming with rage at that endless parliamentary
insult and that long enduring royal patience; the inflexible arm and
steadfast heart had given place to a trembling hand and a body shaken
by excitement.

At this moment the accuser concluded with these words: 芒聙聹The present
accusation is preferred by us in the name of the English people.芒聙聺

At these words there was a murmur along the benches, and a second
voice, not that of a woman, but a man芒聙聶s, stout and furious, thundered
behind D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹You lie!芒聙聺 it cried. 芒聙聹Nine-tenths of the English people are horrified
at what you say.芒聙聺

This voice was that of Athos, who, standing up with outstretched hand
and quite out of his mind, thus assailed the public accuser.

King, judges, spectators, all turned their eyes to the bench where the
four friends were seated. Mordaunt did the same and recognized the
gentleman, around whom the three other Frenchmen were standing, pale
and menacing. His eyes glittered with delight. He had discovered those
to whose death he had devoted his life. A movement of fury called to
his side some twenty of his musketeers, and pointing to the bench where
his enemies were: 芒聙聹Fire on that bench!芒聙聺 he cried.

But with the rapidity of thought D芒聙聶Artagnan seized Athos by the waist,
and followed by Porthos with Aramis, leaped down from the benches,
rushed into the passages, and flying down the staircase were lost in
the crowd without, while the muskets within were pointed on some three
thousand spectators, whose piteous cries and noisy alarm stopped the
impulse already given to bloodshed.

Charles also had recognized the four Frenchmen. He put one hand on his
heart to still its beating and the other over his eyes, that he might
not witness the slaying of his faithful friends.

Mordaunt, pale and trembling with anger, rushed from the hall sword in
hand, followed by six pikemen, pushing, inquiring and panting in the
crowd; and then, having found nothing, returned.

The tumult was indescribable. More than half an hour passed before any
one could make himself heard. The judges were looking for a new
outbreak from the benches. The spectators saw the muskets leveled at
them, and divided between fear and curiosity, remained noisy and
excited.

Quiet was at length restored.

芒聙聹What have you to say in your defense?芒聙聺 asked Bradshaw of the king.

Then rising, with his head still covered, in the tone of a judge rather
than a prisoner, Charles began.

芒聙聹Before questioning me,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹reply to my question. I was free at
Newcastle and had there concluded a treaty with both houses. Instead of
performing your part of this contract, as I performed mine, you bought
me from the Scotch, cheaply, I know, and that does honor to the
economic talent of your government. But because you have paid the price
of a slave, do you imagine that I have ceased to be your king? No. To
answer you would be to forget it. I shall only reply to you when you
have satisfied me of your right to question me. To answer you would be
to acknowledge you as my judges, and I only acknowledge you as my
executioners.芒聙聺 And in the middle of a deathlike silence, Charles, calm,
lofty, and with his head still covered, sat down again in his
arm-chair.

芒聙聹Why are not my Frenchmen here?芒聙聺 he murmured proudly and turning his
eyes to the benches where they had appeared for a moment; 芒聙聹they would
have seen that their friend was worthy of their defense while alive,
and of their tears when dead.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said the president, seeing that Charles was determined to
remain silent, 芒聙聹so be it. We will judge you in spite of your silence.
You are accused of treason, of abuse of power, and murder. The evidence
will support it. Go, and another sitting will accomplish what you have
postponed in this.芒聙聺

Charles rose and turned toward Parry, whom he saw pale and with his
temples dewed with moisture.

芒聙聹Well, my dear Parry,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹what is the matter, and what can
affect you in this manner?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, my king,芒聙聺 said Parry, with tears in his eyes and in a tone of
supplication, 芒聙聹do not look to the left as we leave the hall.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And why, Parry?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do not look, I implore you, my king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But what is the matter? Speak,芒聙聺 said Charles, attempting to look
across the hedge of guards which surrounded him.

芒聙聹It is芒聙聰but you will not look, will you?芒聙聰it is because they have had the
axe, with which criminals are executed, brought and placed there on the
table. The sight is hideous.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Fools,芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹do they take me for a coward, like themselves?
You have done well to warn me. Thank you, Parry.芒聙聺

When the moment arrived the king followed his guards out of the hall.
As he passed the table on which the axe was laid, he stopped, and
turning with a smile, said:

芒聙聹Ah! the axe, an ingenious device, and well worthy of those who know
not what a gentleman is; you frighten me not, executioner芒聙聶s axe,芒聙聺 added
he, touching it with the cane which he held in his hand, 芒聙聹and I strike
you now, waiting patiently and Christianly for you to return the blow.芒聙聺

And shrugging his shoulders with unaffected contempt he passed on. When
he reached the door a stream of people, who had been disappointed in
not being able to get into the house and to make amends had collected
to see him come out, stood on each side, as he passed, many among them
glaring on him with threatening looks.

芒聙聹How many people,芒聙聺 thought he, 芒聙聹and not one true friend.芒聙聺

And as he uttered these words of doubt and depression within his mind,
a voice beside him said:

芒聙聹Respect to fallen majesty.芒聙聺

The king turned quickly around, with tears in his eyes and heart. It
was an old soldier of the guards who could not see his king pass
captive before him without rendering him this final homage. But the
next moment the unfortunate man was nearly killed with heavy blows of
sword-hilts, and among those who set upon him the king recognized
Captain Groslow.

芒聙聹Alas!芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹that is a severe chastisement for a very
trifling fault.芒聙聺

He continued his walk, but he had scarcely gone a hundred paces, when a
furious fellow, leaning between two soldiers, spat in the king芒聙聶s face,
as once an infamous and accursed Jew spit in the face of Jesus of
Nazareth. Loud roars of laughter and sullen murmurs arose together. The
crowd opened and closed again, undulating like a stormy sea, and the
king imagined that he saw shining in the midst of this living wave the
bright eyes of Athos.

Charles wiped his face and said with a sad smile: 芒聙聹Poor wretch, for
half a crown he would do as much to his own father.芒聙聺

The king was not mistaken. Athos and his friends, again mingling with
the throng, were taking a last look at the martyr king.

When the soldier saluted Charles, Athos芒聙聶s heart bounded for joy; and
that unfortunate, on coming to himself, found ten guineas that the
French gentleman had slipped into his pocket. But when the cowardly
insulter spat in the face of the captive monarch Athos grasped his
dagger. But D芒聙聶Artagnan stopped his hand and in a hoarse voice cried,
芒聙聹Wait!芒聙聺

Athos stopped. D芒聙聶Artagnan, leaning on Athos, made a sign to Porthos and
Aramis to keep near them and then placed himself behind the man with
the bare arms, who was still laughing at his own vile pleasantry and
receiving the congratulations of several others.

The man took his way toward the city. The four friends followed him.
The man, who had the appearance of being a butcher, descended a little
steep and isolated street, looking on to the river, with two of his
friends. Arrived at the bank of the river the three men perceived that
they were followed, turned around, and looking insolently at the
Frenchmen, passed some jests from one to another.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t know English, Athos,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹but you know it and
will interpret for me.芒聙聺

Then quickening their steps they passed the three men, but turned back
immediately, and D芒聙聶Artagnan walked straight up to the butcher and
touching him on the chest with the tip of his finger, said to Athos:

芒聙聹Say this to him in English: 芒聙聵You are a coward. You have insulted a
defenseless man. You have befouled the face of your king. You must
die.芒聙聶芒聙聺

Athos, pale as a ghost, repeated these words to the man, who, seeing
the bodeful preparations that were making, put himself in an attitude
of defense. Aramis, at this movement, drew his sword.

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹no steel. Steel is for gentlemen.芒聙聺

And seizing the butcher by the throat:

芒聙聹Porthos,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹kill this fellow for me with a single blow.芒聙聺

Porthos raised his terrible fist, which whistled through the air like a
sling, and the portentous mass fell with a smothered crash on the
insulter芒聙聶s skull and crushed it. The man fell like an ox beneath the
poleaxe. His companions, horror-struck, could neither move nor cry out.

芒聙聹Tell them this, Athos,芒聙聺 resumed D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹thus shall all die who
forget that a captive man is sacred and that a captive king doubly
represents the Lord.芒聙聺

Athos repeated D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s words.

The fellows looked at the body of their companion, swimming in blood,
and then recovering voice and legs together, ran screaming off.

芒聙聹Justice is done,芒聙聺 said Porthos, wiping his forehead.

芒聙聹And now,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan to Athos, 芒聙聹entertain no further doubts about
me; I undertake all that concerns the king.芒聙聺




Chapter LXIV.
Whitehall.


The parliament condemned Charles to death, as might have been foreseen.
Political judgments are generally vain formalities, for the same
passions which give rise to the accusation ordain to the condemnation.
Such is the atrocious logic of revolutions.

Although our friends were expecting that condemnation, it filled them
with grief. D芒聙聶Artagnan, whose mind was never more fertile in resources
than in critical emergencies, swore again that he would try all
conceivable means to prevent the _d脙漏nouement_ of the bloody tragedy.
But by what means? As yet he could form no definite plan; all must
depend on circumstances. Meanwhile, it was necessary at all hazards, in
order to gain time, to put some obstacle in the way of the execution on
the following day芒聙聰the day appointed by the judges. The only way of
doing that was to cause the disappearance of the London executioner.
The headsman out of the way, the sentence could not be executed. True,
they could send for the headsman of the nearest town, but at least a
day would be gained, and a day might be sufficient for the rescue.
D芒聙聶Artagnan took upon himself that more than difficult task.

Another thing, not less essential, was to warn Charles Stuart of the
attempt to be made, so that he might assist his rescuers as much as
possible, or at least do nothing to thwart their efforts. Aramis
assumed that perilous charge. Charles Stuart had asked that Bishop
Juxon might be permitted to visit him. Mordaunt had called on the
bishop that very evening to apprise him of the religious desire
expressed by the king and also of Cromwell芒聙聶s permission. Aramis
determined to obtain from the bishop, through fear or by persuasion,
consent that he should enter in the bishop芒聙聶s place, and clad in his
sacerdotal robes, the prison at Whitehall.

Finally, Athos undertook to provide, in any event, the means of leaving
England芒聙聰in case either of failure or of success.

The night having come they made an appointment to meet at eleven
o芒聙聶clock at the hotel, and each started out to fulfill his dangerous
mission.

The palace of Whitehall was guarded by three regiments of cavalry and
by the fierce anxiety of Cromwell, who came and went or sent his
generals or his agents continually. Alone in his usual room, lighted by
two candles, the condemned monarch gazed sadly on the luxury of his
past greatness, just as at the last hour one sees the images of life
more mildly brilliant than of yore.

Parry had not quitted his master, and since his condemnation had not
ceased to weep. Charles, leaning on a table, was gazing at a medallion
of his wife and daughter; he was waiting first for Juxon, then for
martyrdom.

At times he thought of those brave French gentlemen who had appeared to
him from a distance of a hundred leagues fabulous and unreal, like the
forms that appear in dreams. In fact, he sometimes asked himself if all
that was happening to him was not a dream, or at least the delirium of
a fever. He rose and took a few steps as if to rouse himself from his
torpor and went as far as the window; he saw glittering below him the
muskets of the guards. He was thereupon constrained to admit that he
was indeed awake and that his bloody dream was real.

Charles returned in silence to his chair, rested his elbow on the
table, bowed his head upon his hand and reflected.

芒聙聹Alas!芒聙聺 he said to himself, 芒聙聹if I only had for a confessor one of those
lights of the church, whose soul has sounded all the mysteries of life,
all the littlenesses of greatness, perhaps his utterance would overawe
the voice that wails within my soul. But I shall have a priest of
vulgar mind, whose career and fortune I have ruined by my misfortune.
He will speak to me of God and death, as he has spoken to many another
dying man, not understanding that this one leaves his throne to an
usurper, his children to the cold contempt of public charity.芒聙聺

And he raised the medallion to his lips.

It was a dull, foggy night. A neighboring church clock slowly struck
the hour. The flickering light of the two candles showed fitful phantom
shadows in the lofty room. These were the ancestors of Charles,
standing back dimly in their tarnished frames.

An awful sadness enveloped the heart of Charles. He buried his brow in
his hands and thought of the world, so beautiful when one is about to
leave it; of the caresses of children, so pleasing and so sweet,
especially when one is parting from his children never to see them
again; then of his wife, the noble and courageous woman who had
sustained him to the last moment. He drew from his breast the diamond
cross and the star of the Garter which she had sent him by those
generous Frenchmen; he kissed it, and then, as he reflected, that she
would never again see those things till he lay cold and mutilated in
the tomb, there passed over him one of those icy shivers which may be
called forerunners of death.

Then, in that chamber which recalled to him so many royal souvenirs,
whither had come so many courtiers, the scene of so much flattering
homage, alone with a despairing servant, whose feeble soul could afford
no support to his own, the king at last yielded to sorrow, and his
courage sank to a level with that feebleness, those shadows, and that
wintry cold. That king, who was so grand, so sublime in the hour of
death, meeting his fate with a smile of resignation on his lips, now in
that gloomy hour wiped away a tear which had fallen on the table and
quivered on the gold embroidered cloth.

Suddenly the door opened, an ecclesiastic in episcopal robes entered,
followed by two guards, to whom the king waved an imperious gesture.
The guards retired; the room resumed its obscurity.

芒聙聹Juxon!芒聙聺 cried Charles, 芒聙聹Juxon, thank you, my last friend; you come at
a fitting moment.芒聙聺

The bishop looked anxiously at the man sobbing in the ingle-nook.

芒聙聹Come, Parry,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹cease your tears.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If it芒聙聶s Parry,芒聙聺 said the bishop, 芒聙聹I have nothing to fear; so allow me
to salute your majesty and to tell you who I am and for what I am
come.芒聙聺

At this sight and this voice Charles was about to cry out, when Aramis
placed his finger on his lips and bowed low to the king of England.

芒聙聹The chevalier!芒聙聺 murmured Charles.

芒聙聹Yes, sire,芒聙聺 interrupted Aramis, raising his voice, 芒聙聹Bishop Juxon, the
faithful knight of Christ, obedient to your majesty芒聙聶s wishes.芒聙聺

Charles clasped his hands, amazed and stupefied to find that these
foreigners, without other motive than that which their conscience
imposed on them, thus combated the will of a people and the destiny of
a king.

芒聙聹You!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you! how did you penetrate hither? If they recognize
you, you are lost.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Care not for me, sire; think only of yourself. You see, your friends
are wakeful. I know not what we shall do yet, but four determined men
can do much. Meanwhile, do not be surprised at anything that happens;
prepare yourself for every emergency.芒聙聺

Charles shook his head.

芒聙聹Do you know that I die to-morrow at ten o芒聙聶clock?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Something, your majesty, will happen between now and then to make the
execution impossible.芒聙聺

The king looked at Aramis with astonishment.

At this moment a strange noise, like the unloading of a cart, and
followed by a cry of pain, was heard beneath the window.

芒聙聹Do you hear?芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹I hear,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹but I understand neither the noise nor the cry
of pain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know not who can have uttered the cry,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹but the
noise is easily understood. Do you know that I am to be beheaded
outside this window? Well, these boards you hear unloaded are the posts
and planks to build my scaffold. Some workmen must have fallen
underneath them and been hurt.芒聙聺

Aramis shuddered in spite of himself.

芒聙聹You see,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹that it is useless for you to resist. I am
condemned; leave me to my death.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My king,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹they well may raise a scaffold, but they
cannot make an executioner.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you mean?芒聙聺 asked the king.

芒聙聹I mean that at this hour the headsman has been got out of the way by
force or persuasion. The scaffold will be ready by to-morrow, but the
headsman will be wanting and they will put it off till the day after
to-morrow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What then?芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹To-morrow night we shall rescue you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How can that be?芒聙聺 cried the king, whose face was lighted up, in spite
of himself, by a flash of joy.

芒聙聹Oh! sir,芒聙聺 cried Parry, 芒聙聹may you and yours be blessed!芒聙聺

芒聙聹How can it be?芒聙聺 repeated the king. 芒聙聹I must know, so that I may assist
you if there is any chance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I know nothing about it,芒聙聺 continued Aramis, 芒聙聹but the cleverest, the
bravest, the most devoted of us four said to me when I left him, 芒聙聵Tell
the king that to-morrow at ten o芒聙聶clock at night, we shall carry him
off.芒聙聶 He has said it and will do it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Tell me the name of that generous friend,芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹that I may

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan, sire, the same who had so nearly rescued you when Colonel
Harrison made his untimely entrance.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are, indeed, wonderful men,芒聙聺 said the king; 芒聙聹if such things had

芒聙聹Now, sire,芒聙聺 resumed Aramis, 芒聙聹listen to me. Do not forget for a single

芒聙聹Oh, chevalier!芒聙聺 cried the king, 芒聙聹what can I say to you? There is no
word, though it should come from the profoundest depth of my heart,

assure you, is a very small affair; but you will save a husband to his

a friend who will love you to his last sigh.芒聙聺

Aramis stooped to kiss the king芒聙聶s hand, but Charles clasped his and
pressed it to his heart.

At this moment a man entered, without even knocking at the door. Aramis
tried to withdraw his hand, but the king still held it. The man was one

Cromwell.

芒聙聹What do you want, sir?芒聙聺 said the king.

芒聙聹I desire to know if the confession of Charles Stuart is at an end?芒聙聺
said the stranger.

芒聙聹And what is it to you?芒聙聺 replied the king; 芒聙聹we are not of the same
religion.芒聙聺

芒聙聹All men are brothers,芒聙聺 said the Puritan. 芒聙聹One of my brothers is about

芒聙聹Bear with him,芒聙聺 whispered Aramis; 芒聙聹it is doubtless some spy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹After my reverend lord bishop,芒聙聺 said the king to the man, 芒聙聹I shall

The man retired, but not before examining the supposed Juxon with an

芒聙聹Chevalier,芒聙聺 said the king, when the door was closed, 芒聙聹I believe you
are right and that this man only came here with evil intentions. Take

芒聙聹I thank your majesty,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹but under these robes I have a
coat of mail, a pistol and a dagger.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, then, sir, and God keep you!芒聙聺

The king accompanied him to the door, where Aramis pronounced his
benediction upon him, and passing through the ante-rooms, filled with
soldiers, jumped into his carriage and drove to the bishop芒聙聶s palace.
Juxon was waiting for him impatiently.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 said he, on perceiving Aramis.

芒聙聹Everything has succeeded as I expected; spies, guards, satellites, all
took me for you, and the king blesses you while waiting for you to
bless him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹May God protect you, my son; for your example has given me at the same
time hope and courage.芒聙聺

Aramis resumed his own attire and left Juxon with the assurance that he
might again have recourse to him.

He had scarcely gone ten yards in the street when he perceived that he
was followed by a man, wrapped in a large cloak. He placed his hand on
his dagger and stopped. The man came straight toward him. It was
Porthos.

芒聙聹My dear friend,芒聙聺 cried Aramis.

芒聙聹You see, we had each our mission,芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹mine was to guard
you and I am doing so. Have you seen the king?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, and all goes well.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are to meet our friends at the hotel at eleven.芒聙聺

It was then striking half-past ten by St. Paul芒聙聶s.

Arrived at the hotel it was not long before Athos entered.

芒聙聹All芒聙聶s well,芒聙聺 he cried, as he entered; 芒聙聹I have hired a cedar wherry, as
light as a canoe, as easy on the wing as any swallow. It is waiting for
us at Greenwich, opposite the Isle of Dogs, manned by a captain and
four men, who for the sum of fifty pounds sterling will keep themselves
at our disposition three successive nights. Once on board we drop down
the Thames and in two hours are on the open sea. In case I am killed,
the captain芒聙聶s name is Roger and the skiff is called the _Lightning_. A
handkerchief, tied at the four corners, is to be the signal.芒聙聺

Next moment D芒聙聶Artagnan entered.

芒聙聹Empty your pockets,芒聙聺 said he; 芒聙聹I want a hundred pounds, and as for my
own芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺 and he emptied them inside out.

The sum was collected in a minute. D芒聙聶Artagnan ran out and returned
directly after.

芒聙聹There,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹it芒聙聶s done. Ough! and not without a deal of trouble,
too.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Has the executioner left London?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Ah, you see that plan was not sure enough; he might go out by one gate
and return by another.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where is he, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In the cellar.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The cellar芒聙聰what cellar?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Our landlord芒聙聶s, to be sure. Mousqueton is propped against the door and
here芒聙聶s the key.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bravo!芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹how did you manage it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Like everything else, with money; but it cost me dear.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How much?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Five hundred pounds.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And where did you get so much money?芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹Had you, then, that
sum?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The queen芒聙聶s famous diamond,芒聙聺 answered D芒聙聶Artagnan, with a sigh.

芒聙聹Ah, true,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹I recognized it on your finger.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You bought it back, then, from Monsieur des Essarts?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹Yes, but it was fated that I should not keep it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So, then, we are all right as regards the executioner,芒聙聺 said Athos;
芒聙聹but unfortunately every executioner has his assistant, his man, or

芒聙聹And this one had his,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹but, as good luck would have

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 cried Aramis, 芒聙聹that accounts for the cry I heard.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Probably,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹but as he is a thoughtful young man he

芒聙聹And what on earth are you going to do with it?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Can芒聙聶t you guess, my dear Athos? You, who speak English like John Bull

Athos uttered a cry of joy and admiration, ran to a closet and drew

they then left the hotel, Athos carrying a saw, Porthos a vise, Aramis

The letter from the executioner芒聙聶s assistant satisfied the master
carpenter that those were the men he expected.




Chapter LXV.
The Workmen.


Toward midnight Charles heard a great noise beneath his window. It
arose from blows of hammer and hatchet, clinking of pincers and
cranching of saws.

Lying dressed upon his bed, the noise awoke him with a start and found
a gloomy echo in his heart. He could not endure it, and sent Parry to
ask the sentinel to beg the workmen to strike more gently and not
disturb the last slumber of one who had been their king. The sentinel
was unwilling to leave his post, but allowed Parry to pass.

Arriving at the window Parry found an unfinished scaffold, over which
they were nailing a covering of black serge. Raised to the height of
twenty feet, so as to be on a level with the window, it had two lower
stories. Parry, odious as was this sight to him, sought for those among
some eight or ten workmen who were making the most noise; and fixed on
two men, who were loosening the last hooks of the iron balcony.

芒聙聹My friends,芒聙聺 said Parry, mounting the scaffold and standing beside
them, 芒聙聹would you work a little more quietly? The king wishes to get a
sleep.芒聙聺

One of the two, who was standing up, was of gigantic size and was
driving a pick with all his might into the wall, whilst the other,
kneeling beside him, was collecting the pieces of stone. The face of
the first was lost to Parry in the darkness; but as the second turned
around and placed his finger on his lips Parry started back in
amazement.

芒聙聹Very well, very well,芒聙聺 said the workman aloud, in excellent English.
芒聙聹Tell the king that if he sleeps badly to-night he will sleep better
to-morrow night.芒聙聺

These blunt words, so terrible if taken literally, were received by the
other workmen with a roar of laughter. But Parry withdrew, thinking he
was dreaming.

Charles was impatiently awaiting his return. At the moment he
re-entered, the sentinel who guarded the door put his head through the
opening, curious as to what the king was doing. The king was lying on
his bed, resting on his elbow. Parry closed the door and approaching
the king, his face radiant with joy:

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 he said, in a low voice, 芒聙聹do you know who these workmen are who
are making so much noise?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I? No; how would you have me know?芒聙聺

Parry bent his head and whispered to the king: 芒聙聹It is the Comte de la
F脙篓re and his friends.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Raising my scaffold!芒聙聺 cried the king, astounded.

芒聙聹Yes, and at the same time making a hole in the wall.芒聙聺

The king clasped his hands and raised his eyes to Heaven; then leaping
down from his bed he went to the window, and pulling aside the curtain
tried to distinguish the figures outside, but in vain.

Parry was not wrong. It was Athos he had recognized, and Porthos who
was boring a hole through the wall.

This hole communicated with a kind of loft芒聙聰the space between the floor
of the king芒聙聶s room and the ceiling of the one below it. Their plan was
to pass through the hole they were making into this loft and cut out
from below a piece of the flooring of the king芒聙聶s room, so as to form a
kind of trap-door.

Through this the king was to escape the next night, and, hidden by the
black covering of the scaffold, was to change his dress for that of a
workman, slip out with his deliverers, pass the sentinels, who would
suspect nothing, and so reach the skiff that was waiting for him at
Greenwich.

Day gilded the tops of the houses. The aperture was finished and Athos
passed through it, carrying the clothes destined for the king wrapped
in black cloth, and the tools with which he was to open a communication
with the king芒聙聶s room. He had only two hours芒聙聶 work to do to open
communication with the king and, according to the calculations of the
four friends, they had the entire day before them, since, the
executioner being absent, another must be sent for to Bristol.

D芒聙聶Artagnan returned to change his workman芒聙聶s clothes for his
chestnut-colored suit, and Porthos to put on his red doublet. As for
Aramis, he went off to the bishop芒聙聶s palace to see if he could possibly
pass in with Juxon to the king芒聙聶s presence. All three agreed to meet at
noon in Whitehall Place to see how things went on.

Before leaving the scaffold Aramis had approached the opening where
Athos was concealed to tell him that he was about to make an attempt to
gain another interview with the king.

芒聙聹Adieu, then, and be of good courage,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹Report to the king
the condition of affairs. Say to him that when he is alone it will help
us if he will knock on the floor, for then I can continue my work in
safety. Try, Aramis, to keep near the king. Speak loud, very loud, for
they will be listening at the door. If there is a sentinel within the
apartment, kill him without hesitation. If there are two, let Parry
kill one and you the other. If there are three, let yourself be slain,
but save the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Be easy,芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹I will take two poniards and give one to
Parry. Is that all?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, go; but urge the king strongly not to stand on false generosity.
While you are fighting if there is a fight, he must flee. The trap once
replaced over his head, you being on the trap, dead or alive, they will
need at least ten minutes to find the hole by which he has escaped. In
those ten minutes we shall have gained the road and the king will be
saved.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Everything shall be done as you say, Athos. Your hand, for perhaps we
shall not see each other again.芒聙聺

Athos put his arm around Aramis芒聙聶s neck and embraced him.

芒聙聹For you,芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹Now if I die, say to D芒聙聶Artagnan that I love him as
a son, and embrace him for me. Embrace also our good and brave Porthos.
Adieu.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹I am as sure now that the king will be saved as
I am sure that I clasp the most loyal hand in the world.芒聙聺

Aramis parted from Athos, went down from the scaffold in his turn and
took his way to the hotel, whistling the air of a song in praise of
Cromwell. He found the other two friends sitting at table before a good
fire, drinking a bottle of port and devouring a cold chicken. Porthos

revolving in his mind the most audacious plans.

Aramis related what had been agreed upon. D芒聙聶Artagnan approved with a
movement of the head and Porthos with his voice.

芒聙聹Bravo!芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹besides, we shall be there at the time of the
flight. What with D芒聙聶Artagnan, Grimaud and Mousqueton, we can manage to
dispatch eight of them. I say nothing about Blaisois, for he is only
fit to hold the horses. Two minutes a man makes four minutes.
Mousqueton will lose another, that芒聙聶s five; and in five minutes we shall
have galloped a quarter of a league.芒聙聺

Aramis swallowed a hasty mouthful, gulped a glass of wine and changed
his clothes.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m off to the bishop芒聙聶s. Take care of the executioner,
D芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹All right. Grimaud has relieved Mousqueton and has his foot on the
cellar door.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, don芒聙聶t be inactive.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Inactive, my dear fellow! Ask Porthos. I pass my life upon my legs.芒聙聺

Aramis again presented himself at the bishop芒聙聶s. Juxon consented the
more readily to take him with him, as he would require an assistant
priest in case the king should wish to communicate. Dressed as Aramis

former, more disguised by his pallor and sad countenance than his

the palace.

It was about nine o芒聙聶clock in the morning.

Nothing was changed. The ante-rooms were still full of soldiers, the
passages still lined by guards. The king was already sanguine, but when

pressed the hand of Aramis. The bishop affected to speak in a loud

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 said Aramis, speaking rapidly, 芒聙聹you are saved; the London
executioner has vanished. His assistant broke his leg last night
beneath your majesty芒聙聶s window芒聙聰the cry we heard was his芒聙聰and there is no
executioner nearer at hand than Bristol.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But the Comte de la F脙篓re?芒聙聺 asked the king.

芒聙聹Two feet below you; take the poker from the fireplace and strike three
times on the floor. He will answer you.芒聙聺

The king did so, and the moment after, three muffled knocks, answering
the given signal, sounded beneath the floor.

芒聙聹So,芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹he who knocks down there芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Is the Comte de la F脙篓re, sire,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹He is preparing a way
for your majesty to escape. Parry, for his part, will raise this slab
of marble and a passage will be opened.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, Juxon,芒聙聺 said the king, seizing the bishop芒聙聶s two hands in his own,
芒聙聹promise that you will pray all your life for this gentleman and for

who, wherever they may be, are on the watch for my safety.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sire,芒聙聺 replied Juxon, 芒聙聹you shall be obeyed.芒聙聺

Meanwhile, the miner underneath was heard working away incessantly,
when suddenly an unexpected noise resounded in the passage. Aramis

nearer. It was that of a number of men steadily approaching. The four

A parliamentary officer, clothed in black and with a gravity that
augured ill, entered, bowed to the king, and unfolding a parchment,
read the sentence, as is usually done to criminals before their
execution.

芒聙聹What is this?芒聙聺 said Aramis to Juxon.

Juxon replied with a sign which meant that he knew no more than Aramis
about it.

芒聙聹Then it is for to-day?芒聙聺 asked the king.

芒聙聹Was not your majesty warned that it was to take place this morning?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then I must die like a common criminal by the hand of the London

芒聙聹The London executioner has disappeared, your majesty, but a man has

A slight moisture on his brow was the only trace of emotion that

Charles perceived it and took his hand.

芒聙聹Come, my friend,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹courage.芒聙聺 Then he turned to the officer.
芒聙聹Sir, I am ready. There is but little reason why I should delay you.
Firstly, I wish to communicate; secondly, to embrace my children and
bid them farewell for the last time. Will this be permitted me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly,芒聙聺 replied the officer, and left the room.

Aramis dug his nails into his flesh and groaned aloud.

芒聙聹Oh! my lord bishop,芒聙聺 he cried, seizing Juxon芒聙聶s hands, 芒聙聹where is
Providence? where is Providence?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My son,芒聙聺 replied the bishop, with firmness, 芒聙聹you see Him not, because
the passions of the world conceal Him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My son,芒聙聺 said the king to Aramis, 芒聙聹do not take it so to heart. You ask
what God is doing. God beholds your devotion and my martyrdom, and

happening, and not to God. It is men who drive me to death; it is men
who make you weep.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sire,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹yes, you are right. It is men whom I should

芒聙聹Be seated, Juxon,芒聙聺 said the king, falling upon his knees. 芒聙聹I have now
to confess to you. Remain, sir,芒聙聺 he added to Aramis, who had moved to

said before all.芒聙聺

Juxon sat down, and the king, kneeling humbly before him, began his
confession.




Chapter LXVI.
Remember!


The mob had already assembled when the confession terminated. The
king芒聙聶s children next arrived芒聙聰the Princess Charlotte, a beautiful,
fair-haired child, with tears in her eyes, and the Duke of Gloucester,
a boy eight or nine years old, whose tearless eyes and curling lip
revealed a growing pride. He had wept all night long, but would not
show his grief before the people.

Charles芒聙聶s heart melted within him at the sight of those two children,
whom he had not seen for two years and whom he now met at the moment of
death. He turned to brush away a tear, and then, summoning up all his
firmness, drew his daughter toward him, recommending her to be pious
and resigned. Then he took the boy upon his knee.

芒聙聹My son,芒聙聺 he said to him, 芒聙聹you saw a great number of people in the
streets as you came here. These men are going to behead your father. Do
not forget that. Perhaps some day they will want to make you king,
instead of the Prince of Wales, or the Duke of York, your elder
brothers. But you are not the king, my son, and can never be so while
they are alive. Swear to me, then, never to let them put a crown upon
your head unless you have a legal right to the crown. For one
day芒聙聰listen, my son芒聙聰one day, if you do so, they will doom you to
destruction, head and crown, too, and then you will not be able to die
with a calm conscience, as I die. Swear, my son.芒聙聺

The child stretched out his little hand toward that of his father and
said, 芒聙聹I swear to your majesty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Henry,芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹call me your father.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Father,芒聙聺 replied the child, 芒聙聹I swear to you that they shall kill me
sooner than make me king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good, my child. Now kiss me; and you, too, Charlotte. Never forget
me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! never, never!芒聙聺 cried both the children, throwing their arms around
their father芒聙聶s neck.

芒聙聹Farewell,芒聙聺 said Charles, 芒聙聹farewell, my children. Take them away,
Juxon; their tears will deprive me of the courage to die.芒聙聺

Juxon led them away, and this time the doors were left open.

Meanwhile, Athos, in his concealment, waited in vain the signal to
recommence his work. Two long hours he waited in terrible inaction. A
deathlike silence reigned in the room above. At last he determined to
discover the cause of this stillness. He crept from his hole and stood,
hidden by the black drapery, beneath the scaffold. Peeping out from the
drapery, he could see the rows of halberdiers and musketeers around the
scaffold and the first ranks of the populace swaying and groaning like
the sea.

芒聙聹What is the matter, then?芒聙聺 he asked himself, trembling more than the
wind-swayed cloth he was holding back. 芒聙聹The people are hurrying on, the
soldiers under arms, and among the spectators I see D芒聙聶Artagnan. What is
he waiting for? What is he looking at? Good God! have they allowed the
headsman to escape?芒聙聺

Suddenly the dull beating of muffled drums filled the square. The sound
of heavy steps was heard above his head. The next moment the very
planks of the scaffold creaked with the weight of an advancing
procession, and the eager faces of the spectators confirmed what a last
hope at the bottom of his heart had prevented him till then believing.
At the same moment a well-known voice above him pronounced these words:

芒聙聹Colonel, I want to speak to the people.芒聙聺

Athos shuddered from head to foot. It was the king speaking on the
scaffold.

In fact, after taking a few drops of wine and a piece of bread,
Charles, weary of waiting for death, had suddenly decided to go to meet
it and had given the signal for movement. Then the two wings of the
window facing the square had been thrown open, and the people had seen
silently advancing from the interior of the vast chamber, first, a
masked man, who, carrying an axe in his hand, was recognized as the
executioner. He approached the block and laid his axe upon it. Behind
him, pale indeed, but marching with a firm step, was Charles Stuart,
who advanced between two priests, followed by a few superior officers
appointed to preside at the execution and attended by two files of
partisans who took their places on opposite sides of the scaffold.

The sight of the masked man gave rise to a prolonged sensation. Every
one was full of curiosity as to who that unknown executioner could be
who presented himself so opportunely to assure to the people the
promised spectacle, when the people believed it had been postponed
until the following day. All gazed at him searchingly.

But they could discern nothing but a man of middle height, dressed in
black, apparently of a certain age, for the end of a gray beard peeped
out from the bottom of the mask that hid his features.

The king芒聙聶s request had undoubtedly been acceded to by an affirmative
sign, for in firm, sonorous accents, which vibrated in the depths of
Athos芒聙聶s heart, the king began his speech, explaining his conduct and
counseling the welfare of the kingdom.

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 said Athos to himself, 芒聙聹is it indeed possible that I hear what I
hear and that I see what I see? Is it possible that God has abandoned
His representative on earth and left him to die thus miserably? And I
have not seen him! I have not said adieu to him!芒聙聺

A noise was heard like that the instrument of death would make if moved
upon the block.

芒聙聹Do not touch the axe,芒聙聺 said the king, and resumed his speech.

At the end of his speech the king looked tenderly around upon the
people. Then unfastening the diamond ornament which the queen had sent
him, he placed it in the hands of the priest who accompanied Juxon.
Then he drew from his breast a little cross set in diamonds, which,
like the order, had been the gift of Henrietta Maria.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said he to the priest, 芒聙聹I shall keep this cross in my hand till
the last moment. Take it from me when I am芒聙聰dead.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sire,芒聙聺 said a voice, which Athos recognized as that of Aramis.

He then took his hat from his head and threw it on the ground. One by
one he undid the buttons of his doublet, took it off and deposited it
by the side of his hat. Then, as it was cold, he asked for his gown,
which was brought to him.

All the preparations were made with a frightful calmness. One would
have thought the king was going to bed and not to his coffin.

芒聙聹Will these be in your way?芒聙聺 he said to the executioner, raising his
long locks; 芒聙聹if so, they can be tied up.芒聙聺

Charles accompanied these words with a look designed to penetrate the
mask of the unknown headsman. His calm, noble gaze forced the man to
turn away his head. But after the searching look of the king he
encountered the burning eyes of Aramis.

The king, seeing that he did not reply, repeated his question.

芒聙聹It will do,芒聙聺 replied the man, in a tremulous voice, 芒聙聹if you separate
them across the neck.芒聙聺

The king parted his hair with his hands, and looking at the block he
said:

芒聙聹This block is very low, is there no other to be had?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the usual block,芒聙聺 answered the man in the mask.

芒聙聹Do you think you can behead me with a single blow?芒聙聺 asked the king.

芒聙聹I hope so,芒聙聺 was the reply. There was something so strange in these
three words that everybody, except the king, shuddered.

芒聙聹I do not wish to be taken by surprise,芒聙聺 added the king. 芒聙聹I shall kneel
down to pray; do not strike then.芒聙聺

芒聙聹When shall I strike?芒聙聺

芒聙聹When I shall lay my head on the block and say 芒聙聵_Remember!_芒聙聶 then
strike boldly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the king to those around him, 芒聙聹I leave you to brave
the tempest; I go before you to a kingdom which knows no storms.
Farewell.芒聙聺

He looked at Aramis and made a special sign to him with his head.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 he continued, 芒聙聹withdraw a little and let me say my prayer, I
beseech you. You, also, stand aside,芒聙聺 he said to the masked man. 芒聙聹It is
only for a moment and I know that I belong to you; but remember that
you are not to strike till I give the signal.芒聙聺

Then he knelt down, made the sign of the cross, and lowering his face

芒聙聹Yes, your majesty,芒聙聺 he answered, trembling.

芒聙聹Faithful friend, noble heart!芒聙聺 said the king, 芒聙聹I should not have been
rescued. I have addressed my people and I have spoken to God; last of

芒聙聹Farewell, saintly, martyred majesty,芒聙聺 lisped Athos, chilled with

A moment芒聙聶s silence ensued and then, in a full, sonorous voice, the king
exclaimed: 芒聙聹_Remember!_芒聙聺

He had scarcely uttered the word when a heavy blow shook the scaffold
and where Athos stood immovable a warm drop fell upon his brow. He
reeled back with a shudder and the same moment the drops became a
crimson cataract.

Athos fell on his knees and remained some minutes as if bewildered or
stunned. At last he rose and taking his handkerchief steeped it in the
blood of the martyred king. Then as the crowd gradually dispersed he
leaped down, crept from behind the drapery, glided between two horses,
mingled with the crowd and was the first to arrive at the inn.

Having gained his room he raised his hand to his face, and observing
that his fingers were covered with the monarch芒聙聶s blood, fell down
insensible.




Chapter LXVII.
The Man in the Mask.


The snow was falling thick and icy. Aramis was the next to come in and
to discover Athos almost insensible. But at the first words he uttered
the comte roused himself from the kind of lethargy in which he had
sunk.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹beaten by fate!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Beaten!芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹Noble and unhappy king!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Are you wounded?芒聙聺 cried Aramis.

芒聙聹No, this is his blood.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where were you, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where you left me芒聙聰under the scaffold.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Did you see it all?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but I heard all. God preserve me from another such hour as I have
just passed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then you know that I did not leave him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I heard your voice up to the last moment.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Here is the order he gave me and the cross I took from his hand; he

芒聙聹Then here is a handkerchief to wrap them in,芒聙聺 replied Athos, drawing

芒聙聹And what,芒聙聺 he continued, 芒聙聹has been done with the poor body?芒聙聺

芒聙聹By order of Cromwell royal honors will be accorded to it. The doctors

芒聙聹Mockery,芒聙聺 muttered Athos, savagely; 芒聙聹royal honors to one whom they

芒聙聹Well, cheer up!芒聙聺 said a loud voice from the staircase, which Porthos

芒聙聹You are late, my dear Porthos.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, there were some people on the way who delayed me. The wretches

芒聙聹Have you seen D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We got separated in the crowd and I could not find him again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 said Athos, satirically, 芒聙聹I saw him. He was in the front row of

芒聙聹Ah Comte de la F脙篓re,芒聙聺 said a calm voice, though hoarse with running,

This reproof stung Athos to the heart, but as the impression produced

芒聙聹I am not calumniating you, my friend. They were anxious about you

So saying, he stretched out his hand, but the other pretended not to

芒聙聹Ugh! I am tired,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, sitting down.

芒聙聹Drink a glass of port,芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹it will refresh you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, let us drink,芒聙聺 said Athos, anxious to make it up by hobnobbing
with D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹let us drink and get away from this hateful country.
The felucca is waiting for us, you know; let us leave to-night, we have

芒聙聹You are in a hurry, sir count,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹But what would you have us to do here, now that the king is dead?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go, sir count,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, carelessly; 芒聙聹you see nothing to

Athos turned pale. Every reproach his friend uttered struck deeply in

芒聙聹Ah! you remain in London?芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Yes. And you?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hang it!芒聙聺 said Porthos, a little perplexed between the two, 芒聙聹I

芒聙聹Thanks, my worthy friend. So I have a little adventure to propose to

芒聙聹A man in a mask?芒聙聺 cried Athos. 芒聙聹You did not let the executioner

芒聙聹The executioner is still in the cellar, where, I presume, he has had

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 answered a voice from the depths of the earth.

芒聙聹Let out your prisoner. All is over.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹who is the wretch that has dared to raise his hand

芒聙聹An amateur headsman,芒聙聺 replied Aramis, 芒聙聹who however, does not handle

芒聙聹Did you not see his face?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹He wore a mask.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But you, Aramis, who were close to him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I could see nothing but a gray beard under the fringe of the mask.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then it must be a man of a certain age.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that matters little. When one puts on a mask,
it is not difficult to wear a beard under it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am sorry I did not follow him,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹Well, my dear Porthos,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that芒聙聶s the very thing it

Athos understood all now.

芒聙聹Well, while I was watching芒聙聰not the king, as monsieur le comte thinks,

The night was dark, snow was falling, the streets were silent and

芒聙聹Fatality!芒聙聺 he muttered.




Chapter LXVIII.
Cromwell芒聙聶s House.


It was, in fact, Mordaunt whom D芒聙聶Artagnan had followed, without knowing
it. On entering the house he had taken off his mask and imitation
beard, then, mounting a staircase, had opened a door, and in a room
lighted by a single lamp found himself face to face with a man seated
behind a desk.

This man was Cromwell.

Cromwell had two or three of these retreats in London, unknown except
to the most intimate of his friends. Mordaunt was among these.

芒聙聹It is you, Mordaunt,芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹You are late.芒聙聺

芒聙聹General, I wished to see the ceremony to the end, which delayed me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! I scarcely thought you were so curious as that.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am always curious to see the downfall of your honor芒聙聶s enemies, and
_he_ was not among the least of them. But you, general, were you not at

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Cromwell.

There was a moment芒聙聶s silence.

芒聙聹Have you had any account of it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹None. I have been here since the morning. I only know that there was a
conspiracy to rescue the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, you knew that?芒聙聺 said Mordaunt.

芒聙聹It matters little. Four men, disguised as workmen, were to get the
king out of prison and take him to Greenwich, where a vessel was
waiting.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And knowing all that, your honor remained here, far from the city,

芒聙聹Tranquil, yes,芒聙聺 replied Cromwell. 芒聙聹But who told you I was inactive?芒聙聺

芒聙聹But芒聙聰if the plot had succeeded?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I wished it to do so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I thought your excellence considered the death of Charles I. as a

芒聙聹Yes, his death; but it would have been more seemly not upon the

芒聙聹Why so?芒聙聺 asked Mordaunt.

Cromwell smiled. 芒聙聹Because it could have been said that I had had him

芒聙聹But if he had escaped?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Impossible; my precautions were taken.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And does your honor know the four men who undertook to rescue him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The four Frenchmen, of whom two were sent by the queen to her husband

芒聙聹And do you think Mazarin commissioned them to act as they have done?芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is possible. But he will not avow it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because they failed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your honor gave me two of these Frenchmen when they were only guilty

芒聙聹Take them,芒聙聺 said Cromwell.

Mordaunt bowed with a smile of triumphant ferocity.

芒聙聹Did the people shout at all?芒聙聺 Cromwell asked.

芒聙聹Very little, except 芒聙聵Long live Cromwell!芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where were you placed?芒聙聺

Mordaunt tried for a moment to read in the general芒聙聶s face if this was

芒聙聹I was so situated as to hear and see _everything_,芒聙聺 he answered.

It was now Cromwell芒聙聶s turn to look fixedly at Mordaunt, and Mordaunt to

芒聙聹It appears,芒聙聺 said Cromwell, 芒聙聹that this improvised executioner did his

Mordaunt remembered that Cromwell had told him he had had no detailed

芒聙聹In fact,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, with a calm voice and immovable countenance,

芒聙聹Do you think so, sir? He did not look like an executioner.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹it might have been some personal enemy of the

芒聙聹But if your honor knew this man?芒聙聺

芒聙聹And yet, without the man, the king would have been rescued.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They would have carried him to Greenwich,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹and put him on

Mordaunt bowed humbly. 芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you are a profound thinker and

芒聙聹This skiff might be of use to you for personal projects.芒聙聺

Cromwell turned as he was going.

芒聙聹Are you armed?芒聙聺 he asked.

芒聙聹I have my sword.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And no one waiting for you outside?芒聙聺

Cromwell placed his hand on a hidden handle and opened a door so well

It was just before this that Grimaud had perceived the two men seated

D芒聙聶Artagnan was the first to recover from his surprise.

Grimaud began to scale the wall again.

芒聙聹Keep guard at the other door, Athos and Aramis. Porthos and I will

The friends obeyed.

芒聙聹He is alone,芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

芒聙聹We did not see his companion come out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He may have gone by the other door.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is he doing?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Putting on his cloak and gloves.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He芒聙聶s ours,芒聙聺 muttered D芒聙聶Artagnan.

Porthos mechanically drew his dagger from the scabbard.

芒聙聹Put it up again, my friend,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹We must proceed in an
orderly manner.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 said Grimaud, 芒聙聹he is coming out. He has put out the lamp, I can

芒聙聹Get down then and quickly.芒聙聺

Grimaud leaped down. The snow deadened the noise of his fall.

芒聙聹Now go and tell Athos and Aramis to stand on each side of the door and

The next moment the door opened and Mordaunt appeared on the threshold,

Mordaunt had been talking to Cromwell.

Porthos came up behind, and unhooking the lamp on the staircase relit
that in the room. Athos and Aramis entered last and locked the door
behind them.

芒聙聹Oblige me by taking a seat,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, pushing a chair toward
Mordaunt, who sat down, pale but calm. Aramis, Porthos and D芒聙聶Artagnan
drew their chairs near him. Athos alone kept away and sat in the
furthest corner of the room, as if determined to be merely a spectator

hands in feverish impatience. Aramis bit his lips till the blood came.

D芒聙聶Artagnan alone was calm, at least in appearance.

芒聙聹Monsieur Mordaunt,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹since, after running after one another
so long, chance has at last brought us together, let us have a little
conversation, if you please.芒聙聺




Chapter LXIX.
Conversational.


Though Mordaunt had been so completely taken by surprise and had
mounted the stairs in such utter confusion, when once seated he
recovered himself, as it were, and prepared to seize any possible
opportunity of escape. His eye wandered to a long stout sword on his
flank and he instinctively slipped it around within reach of his right
hand.

D芒聙聶Artagnan was waiting for a reply to his remark and said nothing.
Aramis muttered to himself, 芒聙聹We shall hear nothing but the usual
commonplace things.芒聙聺

Porthos sucked his mustache, muttering, 芒聙聹A good deal of ceremony
to-night about crushing an adder.芒聙聺 Athos shrunk into his corner, pale
and motionless as a _bas-relief_.

The silence, however, could not last forever. So D芒聙聶Artagnan began:

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 he said, with desperate politeness, 芒聙聹it seems to me that you
change your costume almost as rapidly as I have seen the Italian
mummers do, whom the Cardinal Mazarin brought over from Bergamo and
whom he doubtless took you to see during your travels in France.芒聙聺

Mordaunt did not reply.

芒聙聹Just now,芒聙聺 D芒聙聶Artagnan continued, 芒聙聹you were disguised芒聙聰I mean to say,
attired芒聙聰as a murderer, and now芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now I look very much like a man who is going to be murdered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No sword is excellent enough to be of use against four swords and

芒聙聹Well, that is scarcely the question. I had the honor of asking you why

芒聙聹Because, remembering the scene at Armenti脙篓res, I thought I should find

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, in the calmest manner possible, 芒聙聹you are

芒聙聹Aha! It is a duel, then?芒聙聺 cried Mordaunt, as if disposed to reply at

Porthos rose, always ready for this kind of adventure.

芒聙聹Pardon me,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Do not let us do things in a hurry. We

芒聙聹All,芒聙聺 replied Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Then, my dear sir; I am convinced that these gentlemen return your

this芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹At your service, sir,芒聙聺 he continued. 芒聙聹My sword is shorter than yours,
it芒聙聶s true, but, bah! I think the arm will make up for the sword.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Halt!芒聙聺 cried Porthos coming forward. 芒聙聹I begin, and without any

芒聙聹Allow me, Porthos,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

Athos did not move. He might have been taken for a statue. Even his

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹you shall have your turn. Monsieur

Porthos and Aramis drew back, disappointed, and drawing his sword

芒聙聹Sir, I am waiting for you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And for my part, gentlemen, I admire you. You are disputing which

芒聙聹It is but fair,芒聙聺 said Porthos and Aramis, hoping he would choose one

Athos and D芒聙聶Artagnan said nothing, but their silence seemed to imply

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹I choose for my adversary the man who,

Athos sprang up, but after an instant of motionless silence he said, to

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, with a sneer, 芒聙聹there芒聙聶s one who is afraid.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let him have his say, D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said Athos, with a smile of sadness

芒聙聹As long as I don芒聙聶t fight with him it is the same to me with whom I

芒聙聹A good idea,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹At least that will conciliate us all,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹I should never have thought of that,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹and yet it is

Mordaunt sustained this new attack without wincing. He stood with his

Aramis went to Cromwell芒聙聶s desk, tore off three bits of paper of equal

Mordaunt put his hand into the hat, took out one of the three papers

芒聙聹Ah! serpent,芒聙聺 muttered D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I would give my chance of a

The Gascon uttered a cry of joy and turning to Mordaunt:

芒聙聹I hope, sir,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹you have no objection to make.芒聙聺

芒聙聹None, whatever,芒聙聺 replied the other, drawing his sword and resting the

The moment that D芒聙聶Artagnan saw that his wish was accomplished and his

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹take care of yourself, for I am not a

芒聙聹Nor I either.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So much the better; that sets my mind at rest. Defend yourself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹One minute,芒聙聺 said the young man. 芒聙聹Give me your word, gentlemen, that

芒聙聹It is for something else than that, I imagine,芒聙聺 muttered D芒聙聶Artagnan,

芒聙聹On the honor of gentlemen,芒聙聺 said Aramis and Porthos.

芒聙聹Yes, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹we must not leave this person the

This new attack made no impression on Mordaunt. The space was cleared,

D芒聙聶Artagnan was too good a swordsman to trifle with his opponent. He

芒聙聹Aha!芒聙聺 he cried with a smile of satisfaction.

And without losing a minute, thinking he saw an opening, he thrust his

This time it was Mordaunt who smiled.

芒聙聹Ah, sir,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹you have a wicked smile. It must have been

Mordaunt replied by trying his opponent芒聙聶s weapon with an amount of

Mordaunt rapidly sprang back a step.

芒聙聹Ah! you lose ground, you are turning? Well, as you please, I even gain

To this flow of words, not perhaps in the best taste, but

He smiled more and more sarcastically and his smile began to make the

芒聙聹Come, come,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹we must finish with this,芒聙聺 and in his

芒聙聹Ah, this time you cannot lose ground, my fine friend!芒聙聺 exclaimed

In a second D芒聙聶Artagnan had made three terrible thrusts at Mordaunt, all

Mordaunt, in fact, while defending himself, had man脜聯uvred so as to

The Gascon uttered a furious imprecation, which was answered by a wild

芒聙聹Help me, gentlemen,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹we must break in this door.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the devil in person!芒聙聺 said Aramis, hastening forward.

芒聙聹He escapes us,芒聙聺 growled Porthos, pushing his huge shoulder against the

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s a misfortune, to which his friend, the devil, treats us,芒聙聺 said

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s a piece of good fortune sent from Heaven,芒聙聺 said Athos, evidently

芒聙聹Really!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, abandoning the attempt to burst open the

芒聙聹Yes, you are right.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But where shall we go?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹To the hotel, to be sure, to get our baggage and horses; and from

So, suiting the action to the word, D芒聙聶Artagnan thrust the remnant of

Chapter LXX.
The Skiff 芒聙聹Lightning.芒聙聺


D芒聙聶Artagnan had judged correctly; Mordaunt felt that he had no time to
lose, and he lost none. He knew the rapidity of decision and action
that characterized his enemies and resolved to act with reference to
that. This time the musketeers had an adversary who was worthy of them.

After closing the door carefully behind him Mordaunt glided into the
subterranean passage, sheathing on the way his now useless sword, and
thus reached the neighboring house, where he paused to examine himself
and to take breath.

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹nothing, almost nothing芒聙聰scratches, nothing more; two
in the arm and one in the breast. The wounds that I make are better
than that芒聙聰witness the executioner of Bethune, my uncle and King
Charles. Now, not a second to lose, for a second lost will perhaps save
them. They must die芒聙聰die all together芒聙聰killed at one stroke by the
thunder of men in default of God芒聙聶s. They must disappear, broken,
scattered, annihilated. I will run, then, till my legs no longer serve,
till my heart bursts in my bosom but I will arrive before they do.芒聙聺

Mordaunt proceeded at a rapid pace to the nearest cavalry barracks,
about a quarter of a league distant. He made that quarter of a league
in four or five minutes. Arrived at the barracks he made himself known,
took the best horse in the stables, mounted and gained the high road. A
quarter of an hour later he was at Greenwich.

芒聙聹There is the port,芒聙聺 he murmured. 芒聙聹That dark point yonder is the Isle
of Dogs. Good! I am half an hour in advance of them, an hour, perhaps.
Fool that I was! I have almost killed myself by my needless haste.
Now,芒聙聺 he added, rising in the stirrups and looking about him, 芒聙聹which, I
wonder, is the _Lightning?_芒聙聺

At this moment, as if in reply to his words, a man lying on a coil of

handkerchief from his pocket, and tying a knot at each corner芒聙聰the
signal agreed upon芒聙聰waved it in the air and the man came up to him. He
was wrapped in a large rough cape, which concealed his form and partly

芒聙聹Do you wish to go on the water, sir?芒聙聺 said the sailor.

芒聙聹Yes, just so. Along the Isle of Dogs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And perhaps you have a preference for one boat more than another. You
would like one that sails as rapidly as芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lightning,芒聙聺 interrupted Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Then mine is the boat you want, sir. I芒聙聶m your man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I begin to think so, particularly if you have not forgotten a certain

芒聙聹Here it is, sir,芒聙聺 and the sailor took from his coat a handkerchief,

芒聙聹Good, quite right!芒聙聺 cried Mordaunt, springing off his horse. 芒聙聹There芒聙聶s

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 asked the sailor, 芒聙聹where are your companions? I thought there

芒聙聹Listen to me, sir. I芒聙聶m not the man you take me for; you are in Captain

芒聙聹Indeed, sir, I recognize you; you are Captain Mordaunt.芒聙聺

Mordaunt was startled.

芒聙聹Oh, fear nothing,芒聙聺 said the skipper, showing his face. 芒聙聹I am a
friend.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Captain Groslow!芒聙聺 cried Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Himself. The general remembered that I had formerly been a naval

芒聙聹Nothing.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I thought, perhaps, that the king芒聙聶s death芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Has only hastened their flight; in ten minutes they will perhaps be

芒聙聹What have you come for, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To embark with you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! ah! the general doubted my fidelity?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but I wish to have a share in my revenge. Haven芒聙聶t you some one who

Groslow whistled and a sailor appeared.

芒聙聹Patrick,芒聙聺 said Groslow, 芒聙聹take this horse to the stables of the nearest

The sailor departed without reply.

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹are you not afraid that they will recognize

芒聙聹There is no danger, dressed as I am in this pilot coat, on a night as

芒聙聹That is true,芒聙聺 said Mordaunt, 芒聙聹and they will be far from thinking of

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Five full casks?芒聙聺

芒聙聹And fifty empty ones.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are carrying port wine to Anvers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Excellent. Now take me aboard and return to your post, for they will

芒聙聹I am ready.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is important that none of your crew should see me.芒聙聺

They then went down to the Thames. A boat was fastened to the shore by
