芒聙聹They will have nothing to do, then at this end?芒聙聺 said Mordaunt.

芒聙聹Nothing at all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s a capital arrangement. Return to Greenwich and bring them here.
I shall hide myself in your cabin. You have a longboat?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That in which we came.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It appeared light and well constructed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Quite a canoe.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Fasten it to the poop with a rope; put the oars into it, so that it
may follow in the track and there will be nothing to do except to cut
the cord. Put a good supply of rum and biscuit in it for the seamen;
should the night happen to be stormy they will not be sorry to find
something to console themselves with.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Consider all this done. Do you wish to see the powder-room?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No. When you return I will set the fuse myself, but be careful to
conceal your face, so that you cannot be recognized by them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Never fear.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There芒聙聶s ten o芒聙聶clock striking at Greenwich.芒聙聺

Groslow, then, having given the sailor on duty an order to be on the
watch with more than usual vigilance, went down into the longboat and
soon reached Greenwich. The wind was chilly and the jetty was deserted,
as he approached it; but he had no sooner landed than he heard a noise
of horses galloping upon the paved road.

These horsemen were our friends, or rather, an avant garde, composed of
D芒聙聶Artagnan and Athos. As soon as they arrived at the spot where Groslow
stood they stopped, as if guessing that he was the man they wanted.
Athos alighted and calmly opened the handkerchief tied at each corner,
whilst D芒聙聶Artagnan, ever cautious, remained on horseback, one hand upon
his pistol, leaning forward watchfully.

On seeing the appointed signal, Groslow, who had at first crept behind
one of the cannons planted on that spot, walked straight up to the
gentlemen. He was so well wrapped up in his cloak that it would have
been impossible to see his face even if the night had not been so dark
as to render precaution superfluous; nevertheless, the keen glance of
Athos perceived at once it was not Rogers who stood before them.

芒聙聹What do you want with us?芒聙聺 he asked of Groslow.

芒聙聹I wish to inform you, my lord,芒聙聺 replied Groslow, with an Irish accent,
feigned of course, 芒聙聹that if you are looking for Captain Rogers you will
not find him. He fell down this morning and broke his leg. But I芒聙聶m his
cousin; he told me everything and desired me to watch instead of him,
and in his place to conduct, wherever they wished to go, the gentlemen
who should bring me a handkerchief tied at each corner, like that one
which you hold and one which I have in my pocket.芒聙聺

And he drew out the handkerchief.

芒聙聹Was that all he said?芒聙聺 inquired Athos.

芒聙聹No, my lord; he said you had engaged to pay seventy pounds if I landed
you safe and sound at Boulogne or any other port you choose in France.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you think of all this?芒聙聺 said Athos, in a low tone to
D芒聙聶Artagnan, after explaining to him in French what the sailor had said
in English.

芒聙聹It seems a likely story to _me_.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And to me, too.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Besides, we can but blow out his brains if he proves false,芒聙聺 said the
Gascon; 芒聙聹and you, Athos, you know something of everything and can be
our captain. I dare say you know how to navigate, should he fail us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My dear friend, you guess well. My father meant me for the navy and I
have some vague notions about navigation.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You see!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan.

They then summoned their friends, who, with Blaisois, Mousqueton and
Grimaud, promptly joined them, leaving Parry behind them, who was to
take back to London the horses of the gentlemen and of their lackeys,
which had been sold to the host in settlement of their account with
him. Thanks to this stroke of business the four friends were able to
take away with them a sum of money which, if not large, was sufficient
as a provision against delays and accidents.

Parry parted from his friends regretfully; they had proposed his going
with them to France, but he had straightway declined.

芒聙聹It is very simple,芒聙聺 Mousqueton had said; 芒聙聹he is thinking of Groslow.芒聙聺

It was Captain Groslow, the reader will remember, who had broken
Parry芒聙聶s head.

D芒聙聶Artagnan resumed immediately the attitude of distrust that was
habitual with him. He found the wharf too completely deserted, the
night too dark, the captain too accommodating. He had reported to
Aramis what had taken place, and Aramis, not less distrustful than he,
had increased his suspicions. A slight click of the tongue against his
teeth informed Athos of the Gascon芒聙聶s uneasiness.

芒聙聹We have no time now for suspicions,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹The boat is waiting
for us; come.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Besides,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹what prevents our being distrustful and going
aboard at the same time? We can watch the skipper.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And if he doesn芒聙聶t go straight I will crush him, that芒聙聶s all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well said, Porthos,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Let us go, then. You first,
Mousqueton,芒聙聺 and he stopped his friends, directing the valets to go

The three valets passed without accident. Athos followed them, then
Porthos, then Aramis. D芒聙聶Artagnan went last, still shaking his head.

芒聙聹What in the devil is the matter with you, my friend?芒聙聺 said Porthos.
芒聙聹Upon my word you would make C脙娄sar afraid.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The matter is,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹that I can see upon this pier
neither inspector nor sentinel nor exciseman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And you complain of that!芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹Everything goes as if in
flowery paths.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Everything goes too well, Porthos. But no matter; we must trust in
God.芒聙聺

As soon as the plank was withdrawn the captain took his place at the
tiller and made a sign to one of the sailors, who, boat-hook in hand,
began to push out from the labyrinth of boats in which they were
involved. The other sailor had already seated himself on the port side
and was ready to row. As soon as there was room for rowing, his
companion rejoined him and the boat began to move more rapidly.

芒聙聹At last we are off!芒聙聺 exclaimed Porthos.

芒聙聹Alas,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹we depart alone.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; but all four together and without a scratch; which is a
consolation.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are not yet at our destination,芒聙聺 observed the prudent D芒聙聶Artagnan;
芒聙聹beware of misadventure.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, my friend!芒聙聺 cried Porthos, 芒聙聹like the crows, you always bring bad
omens. Who could intercept us on such a night as this, pitch dark, when
one does not see more than twenty yards before one?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but to-morrow morning芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹To-morrow we shall be at Boulogne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope so, with all my heart,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹and I confess my
weakness. Yes, Athos, you may laugh, but as long as we were within

frightful discharge of musketry which would crush us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 said Porthos, with great wisdom, 芒聙聹that was impossible, for they
would have killed the captain and the sailors.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Bah! much Monsieur Mordaunt would care. You don芒聙聶t imagine he would
consider a little thing like that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹At any rate,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I am glad to hear D芒聙聶Artagnan admit that
he is afraid.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I not only confess it, but am proud of it,芒聙聺 returned the Gascon; 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m

芒聙聹The _Lightning_,芒聙聺 answered the captain, 芒聙聹our felucca.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So far, so good,芒聙聺 laughed Athos.

They went on board and the captain instantly conducted them to the
berth prepared for them芒聙聰a cabin which was to serve for all purposes and

orders to some one.

芒聙聹Stop a moment,芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹pray how many men have you on

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t understand,芒聙聺 was the reply.

芒聙聹Explain it, Athos.芒聙聺

Groslow, on the question being interpreted, answered, 芒聙聹Three, without
counting myself.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan understood, for while replying the captain had raised three
fingers. 芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 he exclaimed, 芒聙聹I begin to be more at my ease, however,

芒聙聹As for me,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I will see to the supper.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A very good idea, Porthos,芒聙聺 said the Gascon. 芒聙聹Athos lend me Grimaud,
who in the society of his friend Parry has perhaps picked up a little

芒聙聹Go, Grimaud,芒聙聺 said Athos.

D芒聙聶Artagnan, finding a lantern on the deck, took it up and with a pistol

the classic English oath, the only English words he knew), and so
saying he descended to the lower deck.

This was divided into three compartments芒聙聰one which was covered by the
floor of that room in which Athos, Porthos and Aramis were to pass the
night; the second was to serve as the sleeping-room for the servants,
the third, under the prow of the ship, was under the temporary cabin in
which Mordaunt was concealed.

芒聙聹Oho!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, as he went down the steps of the hatchway,
preceded by the lantern, 芒聙聹what a number of barrels! one would think one

his lantern on one of the casks.

The captain seemed inclined to go upon deck again, but controlling
himself he answered:

芒聙聹Port wine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! port wine! 芒聙聶tis a comfort,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹since we shall not
die of thirst. Are they all full?芒聙聺

Grimaud translated the question, and Groslow, who was wiping the
perspiration from off his forehead, answered:

芒聙聹Some full, others empty.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan struck the barrels with his hand, and having ascertained

芒聙聹Come along,芒聙聺 he said; and he went toward the door of the second
compartment.

芒聙聹Stop!芒聙聺 said the Englishman, 芒聙聹I have the key of that door;芒聙聺 and he
opened the door, with a trembling hand, into the second compartment,
where Mousqueton and Blaisois were preparing supper.

Here there was evidently nothing to seek or to apprehend and they
passed rapidly to examine the third compartment.

This was the room appropriated to the sailors. Two or three hammocks
hung upon the ceiling, a table and two benches composed the entire
furniture. D芒聙聶Artagnan picked up two or three old sails hung on the
walls, and meeting nothing to suspect, regained by the hatchway the
deck of the vessel.

芒聙聹And this room?芒聙聺 he asked, pointing to the captain芒聙聶s cabin.

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s my room,芒聙聺 replied Groslow.

芒聙聹Open the door.芒聙聺

The captain obeyed. D芒聙聶Artagnan stretched out his arm in which he held
the lantern, put his head in at the half opened door, and seeing that
the cabin was nothing better than a shed:

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹If there is an army on board it is not here that it
is hidden. Let us see what Porthos has found for supper.芒聙聺 And thanking
the captain, he regained the state cabin, where his friends were.

Porthos had found nothing, and with him fatigue had prevailed over
hunger. He had fallen asleep and was in a profound slumber when
D芒聙聶Artagnan returned. Athos and Aramis were beginning to close their
eyes, which they half opened when their companion came in again.

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹All is well; we may sleep tranquilly.芒聙聺

On this assurance the two friends fell asleep; and D芒聙聶Artagnan, who was
very weary, bade good-night to Grimaud and laid himself down in his
cloak, with naked sword at his side, in such a manner that his body
barricaded the passage, and it should be impossible to enter the room
without upsetting him.




Chapter LXXI.
Port Wine.


In ten minutes the masters slept; not so the servants芒聙聰-hungry, and more
thirsty than hungry.

Blaisois and Mousqueton set themselves to preparing their bed which
consisted of a plank and a valise. On a hanging table, which swung to
and fro with the rolling of the vessel, were a pot of beer and three
glasses.

芒聙聹This cursed rolling!芒聙聺 said Blaisois. 芒聙聹I know it will serve me as it
did when we came over.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And to think,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, 芒聙聹that we have nothing to fight
seasickness with but barley bread and hop beer. Pah!芒聙聺

芒聙聹But where is your wicker flask, Monsieur Mousqueton? Have you lost
it?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois.

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 replied Mousqueton, 芒聙聹Parry kept it. Those devilish Scotchmen are
always thirsty. And you, Grimaud,芒聙聺 he said to his companion, who had
just come in after his round with D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹are you thirsty?芒聙聺

芒聙聹As thirsty as a Scotchman!芒聙聺 was Grimaud芒聙聶s laconic reply.

And he sat down and began to cast up the accounts of his party, whose
money he managed.

芒聙聹Oh, lackadaisy! I芒聙聶m beginning to feel queer!芒聙聺 cried Blaisois.

芒聙聹If that芒聙聶s the case,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, with a learned air, 芒聙聹take some
nourishment.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you call that nourishment?芒聙聺 said Blaisois, pointing to the barley
bread and pot of beer upon the table.

芒聙聹Blaisois,芒聙聺 replied Mousqueton, 芒聙聹remember that bread is the true
nourishment of a Frenchman, who is not always able to get bread, ask
Grimaud.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but beer?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois sharply, 芒聙聹is that their true drink?芒聙聺

芒聙聹As to that,芒聙聺 answered Mousqueton, puzzled how to get out of the
difficulty, 芒聙聹I must confess that to me beer is as disagreeable as wine
is to the English.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! Monsieur Mousqueton! The English芒聙聰do they dislike wine?芒聙聺

芒聙聹They hate it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But I have seen them drink it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As a punishment. For example, an English prince died one day because
they had put him into a butt of Malmsey. I heard the Chevalier
d芒聙聶Herblay say so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The fool!芒聙聺 cried Blaisois, 芒聙聹I wish I had been in his place.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thou canst be,芒聙聺 said Grimaud, writing down his figures.

芒聙聹How?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois, 芒聙聹I can? Explain yourself.芒聙聺

Grimaud went on with his sum and cast up the whole.

芒聙聹Port,芒聙聺 he said, extending his hand in the direction of the first
compartment examined by D芒聙聶Artagnan and himself.

芒聙聹Eh? eh? ah? Those barrels I saw through the door?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Port!芒聙聺 replied Grimaud, beginning a fresh sum.

芒聙聹I have heard,芒聙聺 said Blaisois, 芒聙聹that port is a very good wine.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Excellent!芒聙聺 exclaimed Mousqueton, smacking his lips. 芒聙聹Excellent; there
is port wine in the cellar of Monsieur le Baron de Bracieux.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Suppose we ask these Englishmen to sell us a bottle,芒聙聺 said the honest
Blaisois.

芒聙聹Sell!芒聙聺 cried Mousqueton, about whom there was a remnant of his ancient
marauding character left. 芒聙聹One may well perceive, young man, that you
are inexperienced. Why buy what one can take?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Take!芒聙聺 said Blaisois; 芒聙聹covet the goods of your neighbor? That is
forbidden, it seems to me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where forbidden?芒聙聺 asked Mousqueton.

芒聙聹In the commandments of God, or of the church, I don芒聙聶t know which. I
only know it says, 芒聙聵Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor芒聙聶s goods, nor yet
his wife.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is a child芒聙聶s reason, Monsieur Blaisois,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton in his
most patronizing manner. 芒聙聹Yes, you talk like a child芒聙聰I repeat the word.
Where have you read in the Scriptures, I ask you, that the English are
your neighbors?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where, that is true,芒聙聺 said Blaisois; 芒聙聹at least, I can芒聙聶t now recall
it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹A child芒聙聶s reason芒聙聰I repeat it,芒聙聺 continued Mousqueton. 芒聙聹If you had been
ten years engaged in war, as Grimaud and I have been, my dear Blaisois,
you would know the difference there is between the goods of others and
the goods of enemies. Now an Englishman is an enemy; this port wine
belongs to the English, therefore it belongs to us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And our masters?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois, stupefied by this harangue,
delivered with an air of profound sagacity, 芒聙聹will they be of your
opinion?芒聙聺

Mousqueton smiled disdainfully.

芒聙聹I suppose that you think it necessary that I should disturb the repose
of these illustrious lords to say, 芒聙聵Gentlemen, your servant,
Mousqueton, is thirsty.芒聙聶 What does Monsieur Bracieux care, think you,
whether I am thirsty or not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis a very expensive wine,芒聙聺 said Blaisois, shaking his head.

芒聙聹Were it liquid gold, Monsieur Blaisois, our masters would not deny
themselves this wine. Know that Monsieur de Bracieux is rich enough to
drink a tun of port wine, even if obliged to pay a pistole for every
drop.芒聙聺 His manner became more and more lofty every instant; then he
arose and after finishing off the beer at one draught he advanced
majestically to the door of the compartment where the wine was. 芒聙聹Ah!
locked!芒聙聺 he exclaimed; 芒聙聹these devils of English, how suspicious they
are!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Locked!芒聙聺 said Blaisois; 芒聙聹ah! the deuce it is; unlucky, for my stomach
is getting more and more upset.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Locked!芒聙聺 repeated Mousqueton.

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 Blaisois ventured to say, 芒聙聹I have heard you relate, Monsieur
Mousqueton, that once on a time, at Chantilly, you fed your master and
yourself by taking partridges in a snare, carp with a line, and bottles
with a slipnoose.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Perfectly true; but there was an airhole in the cellar and the wine
was in bottles. I cannot throw the loop through this partition nor move
with a pack-thread a cask of wine which may perhaps weigh two hundred
pounds.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, but you can take out two or three boards of the partition,芒聙聺
answered Blaisois, 芒聙聹and make a hole in the cask with a gimlet.芒聙聺

Mousqueton opened his great round eyes to the utmost, astonished to
find in Blaisois qualities for which he did not give him credit.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹but where can I get a chisel to take the planks
out, a gimlet to pierce the cask?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Trousers,芒聙聺 said Grimaud, still squaring his accounts.

芒聙聹Ah, yes!芒聙聺 said Mousqueton.

Grimaud, in fact, was not only the accountant, but the armorer of the
party; and as he was a man full of forethought, these trousers,
carefully rolled up in his valise, contained every sort of tool for
immediate use.

Mousqueton, therefore, was soon provided with tools and he began his
task. In a few minutes he had extracted three boards. He tried to pass
his body through the aperture, but not being like the frog in the
fable, who thought he was larger than he really was, he found he must
take out three or four more before he could get through.

He sighed and set to work again.

Grimaud had now finished his accounts. He arose and stood near
Mousqueton.

芒聙聹I,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 said Mousqueton.

芒聙聹I can pass.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is true,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, glancing at his friend芒聙聶s long and thin
body, 芒聙聹you will pass easily.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And he knows the full casks,芒聙聺 said Blaisois, 芒聙聹for he has already been
in the hold with Monsieur le Chevalier d芒聙聶Artagnan. Let Monsieur Grimaud
go in, Monsieur Mouston.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I could go in as well as Grimaud,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, a little piqued.

芒聙聹Yes, but that would take too much time and I am thirsty. I am getting
more and more seasick.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Go in, then, Grimaud,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, handing him the beer pot and
gimlet.

芒聙聹Rinse the glasses,芒聙聺 said Grimaud. Then with a friendly gesture toward
Mousqueton, that he might forgive him for finishing an enterprise so
brilliantly begun by another, he glided like a serpent through the
opening and disappeared.

Blaisois was in a state of great excitement; he was in ecstasies. Of
all the exploits performed since their arrival in England by the
extraordinary men with whom he had the honor to be associated, this
seemed without question to be the most wonderful.

芒聙聹You are about to see,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, looking at Blaisois with an
expression of superiority which the latter did not even think of
questioning, 芒聙聹you are about to see, Blaisois, how we old soldiers drink
when we are thirsty.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My cloak,芒聙聺 said Grimaud, from the bottom of the hold.

芒聙聹What do you want?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois.

芒聙聹My cloak芒聙聰stop up the aperture with it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois.

芒聙聹Simpleton!芒聙聺 exclaimed Mousqueton; 芒聙聹suppose any one came into the

芒聙聹Ah, true,芒聙聺 cried Blaisois, with evident admiration; 芒聙聹but it will be

芒聙聹Grimaud always sees, dark or light, night as well as day,芒聙聺 answered
Mousqueton.

芒聙聹That is lucky,芒聙聺 said Blaisois. 芒聙聹As for me, when I have no candle I

芒聙聹That芒聙聶s because you haven芒聙聶t served,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton. 芒聙聹Had you been in
the army you would have been able to pick up a needle on the floor of a
closed oven. But hark! I think some one is coming.芒聙聺

Mousqueton made, with a low whistling sound, the sign of alarm well
known to the lackeys in the days of their youth, resumed his place at
the table and made a sign to Blaisois to follow his example.

Blaisois obeyed.

The door of their cabin was opened. Two men, wrapped in their cloaks,
appeared.

芒聙聹Oho!芒聙聺 said they, 芒聙聹not in bed at a quarter past eleven. That芒聙聶s against
all rules. In a quarter of an hour let every one be in bed and

These two men then went toward the compartment in which Grimaud was

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 cried Blaisois, 芒聙聹he is lost!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Grimaud芒聙聶s a cunning fellow,芒聙聺 murmured Mousqueton.

They waited for ten minutes, during which time no noise was heard that
might indicate that Grimaud was discovered, and at the expiration of

芒聙聹Shall we obey?芒聙聺 asked Blaisois. 芒聙聹All this looks suspicious.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They said a quarter of an hour. We still have five minutes,芒聙聺 replied

芒聙聹Suppose we warn the masters.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let芒聙聶s wait for Grimaud.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But perhaps they have killed him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Grimaud would have cried out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You know he is almost dumb.芒聙聺

芒聙聹We should have heard the blow, then.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But if he doesn芒聙聶t return?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Here he is.芒聙聺

At that very moment Grimaud drew back the cloak which hid the aperture

Both, however, cast an inquisitive glance into the tankard芒聙聰it was full
of gunpowder.

Convinced that the ship was full of powder instead of having a cargo of

him than he perceived that something extraordinary had taken place.
Imposing silence, Grimaud put out the little night lamp, then knelt

not to require play of feature to give it pith.

This was the gist of his strange story:

The first barrel that Grimaud had found on passing into the compartment
he struck芒聙聰it was empty. He passed on to another芒聙聰it, also, was empty,
but the third which he tried was, from the dull sound it gave out,
evidently full. At this point Grimaud stopped and was preparing to make
a hole with his gimlet, when he found a spigot; he therefore placed his
tankard under it and turned the spout; something, whatever it was the
cask contained, fell silently into the tankard.

Whilst he was thinking that he should first taste the liquor which the
tankard contained before taking it to his companions, the door of the
cellar opened and a man with a lantern in his hands and enveloped in a
cloak, came and stood just before the hogshead, behind which Grimaud,
on hearing him come in, instantly crept. This was Groslow. He was
accompanied by another man, who carried in his hand something long and
flexible rolled up, resembling a washing line. His face was hidden
under the wide brim of his hat. Grimaud, thinking that they had come,
as he had, to try the port wine, effaced himself behind his cask and
consoled himself with the reflection that if he were discovered the
crime was not a great one.

芒聙聹Have you the wick?芒聙聺 asked the one who carried the lantern.

芒聙聹Here it is,芒聙聺 answered the other.

At the voice of this last speaker, Grimaud started and felt a shudder
creeping through his very marrow. He rose gently, so that his head was
just above the round of the barrel, and under the large hat he
recognized the pale face of Mordaunt.

芒聙聹How long will this fuse burn?芒聙聺 asked this person.

芒聙聹About five minutes,芒聙聺 replied the captain.

That voice also was known to Grimaud. He looked from one to the other
and after Mordaunt he recognized Groslow.

芒聙聹Then tell the men to be in readiness芒聙聰don芒聙聶t tell them why now. When the
clock strikes a quarter after midnight collect your men. Get down into
the longboat.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That is, when I have lighted the match?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will undertake that. I wish to be sure of my revenge. Are the oars
in the boat?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Everything is ready.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis well.芒聙聺

Mordaunt knelt down and fastened one end of the train to the spigot, in
order that he might have nothing to do but to set it on fire at the
opposite end with the match.

He then arose.

芒聙聹You hear me芒聙聰at a quarter past midnight芒聙聰in fact, in twenty minutes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I understand all perfectly, sir,芒聙聺 replied Groslow; 芒聙聹but allow me to

芒聙聹My dear Groslow,芒聙聺 answered Mordaunt, 芒聙聹you know the French proverb,
芒聙聵Nothing one does not do one芒聙聶s self is ever well done.芒聙聶 I shall abide

Grimaud had heard all this, if he had not understood it. But what he
saw made good what he lacked in perfect comprehension of the language.
He had seen the two mortal enemies of the musketeers, had seen Mordaunt
adjust the fuse; he had heard the proverb, which Mordaunt had given in

powder.

Mordaunt went away with the captain. At the door he stopped to listen.

芒聙聹Do you hear how they sleep?芒聙聺 he asked.

In fact, Porthos could be heard snoring through the partition.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis God who gives them into our hands,芒聙聺 answered Groslow.

芒聙聹This time the devil himself shall not save them,芒聙聺 rejoined Mordaunt.

And they went out together.




Chapter LXXII.
End of the Port Wine Mystery.


Grimaud waited till he heard the bolt grind in the lock and when he was
satisfied that he was alone he slowly rose from his recumbent posture.

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 he said, wiping with his sleeve large drops of sweat from his

He made haste to pass out by the opening, still thinking himself in a

his dream was a fatal nightmare.

It may be imagined that D芒聙聶Artagnan listened to these details with

芒聙聹Athos is near you,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹warn him as I have warned you.芒聙聺

Aramis easily aroused Athos, whose sleep was light, like that of all

Then our Gascon, extending his arms, drew to him the heads of his three

芒聙聹Captain Groslow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Groslow!芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹the devil!

芒聙聹Who is this Groslow?芒聙聺 asked Porthos. 芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t remember him.芒聙聺

This time the musketeers did more than shudder芒聙聰they almost cried out.
Those invincible men were subject to a mysterious and fatal influence

芒聙聹What shall we do?芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹We must seize the felucca,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹And kill him,芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹The felucca is mined,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Those casks which I took for

芒聙聹You have some plan, then?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What is it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Give your orders,芒聙聺 said the three musketeers.

芒聙聹Very well; come this way.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan went toward a very small, low window, just large enough to

芒聙聹Stay here, if you like, but I warn you 芒聙聶twill be rather too warm

芒聙聹But we cannot swim to the shore.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The longboat is yonder, lashed to the felucca. We will take possession

芒聙聹A moment芒聙聶s delay,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹our servants?芒聙聺

Meantime the three friends were standing motionless before the awful

Those who have once beheld such a spectacle know that there is nothing
more solemn, more striking, than the raging sea, rolling, with its

芒聙聹Gracious Heaven, we are hesitating!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹if we hesitate

芒聙聹I do not hesitate, you know,芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 interposed Blaisois, 芒聙聹I warn you that I can only swim in

芒聙聹And I not at all,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton.

But D芒聙聶Artagnan had now slipped through the window.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 the Gascon answered; 芒聙聹Athos! you, who are a perfect being, bid

And after pressing the hand of Athos, D芒聙聶Artagnan chose a moment when

Athos followed him before the felucca rose again on the waves; the

D芒聙聶Artagnan swam to it and held it, suspending himself by this rope, his

In one second Athos joined him.

Then they saw, as the felucca turned, two other heads peeping, those of

芒聙聹I am uneasy about Blaisois,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹he can, he says, only swim

芒聙聹When people can swim at all they can swim anywhere. To the boat! to

芒聙聹Porthos is coming芒聙聰he swims like Leviathan.芒聙聺

In fact, Porthos did not appear; for a scene, half tragedy and half

芒聙聹Come, come!芒聙聺 said Porthos; 芒聙聹jump in.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But, monsieur,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton, 芒聙聹I can芒聙聶t swim; let me stay here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And me, too, monsieur,芒聙聺 said Blaisois.

芒聙聹I assure you, I shall be very much in the way in that little boat,芒聙聺
said Mousqueton.

芒聙聹And I know I shall drown before reaching it,芒聙聺 continued Blaisois.

芒聙聹Come along! I shall strangle you both if you don芒聙聶t get out,芒聙聺 said

A groan, stifled by the grasp of Porthos, was all the reply of poor

芒聙聹Now, Mousqueton,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I hope you don芒聙聶t mean to desert your

芒聙聹Ah, sir,芒聙聺 replied Mousqueton, his eyes filling with tears, 芒聙聹why did
you re-enter the army? We were all so happy in the Ch脙垄teau de

And without any other complaint, passive and obedient, either from true

Mousqueton looked upon himself as dead. But Porthos was not a man to
abandon an old servant, and when Mousqueton rose above the water, blind
as a new-born puppy, he found he was supported by the large hand of
Porthos and that he was thus enabled, without having occasion even to
move, to advance toward the cable with the dignity of a very triton.

In a few minutes Porthos had rejoined his companions, who were already
in the boat; but when, after they had all got in, it came to his turn,
there was great danger that in putting his huge leg over the edge of
the boat he would upset the little vessel. Athos was the last to enter.

芒聙聹Are you all here?芒聙聺 he asked.

芒聙聹Ah! have you your sword, Athos?芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Cut the cable, then.芒聙聺

Athos drew a sharp poniard from his belt and cut the cord. The felucca
went on, the boat continued stationary, rocked only by the swashing

芒聙聹Come, Athos!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, giving his hand to the count; 芒聙聹you are
going to see something curious,芒聙聺 added the Gascon.




Chapter LXIII.
Fatality.


Scarcely had D芒聙聶Artagnan uttered these words when a ringing and sudden
noise was heard resounding through the felucca, which had now become
dim in the obscurity of the night.

芒聙聹That, you may be sure,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹means something.芒聙聺

They then at the same instant perceived a large lantern carried on a
pole appear on the deck, defining the forms of shadows behind it.

Suddenly a terrible cry, a cry of despair, was wafted through space;
and as if the shrieks of anguish had driven away the clouds, the veil
which hid the moon was cleated away and the gray sails and dark shrouds
of the felucca were plainly visible beneath the silvery light.

Shadows ran, as if bewildered, to and fro on the vessel, and mournful
cries accompanied these delirious walkers. In the midst of these
screams they saw Mordaunt upon the poop with a torch in hand.

The agitated figures, apparently wild with terror, consisted of
Groslow, who at the hour fixed by Mordaunt had collected his men and
the sailors. Mordaunt, after having listened at the door of the cabin
to hear if the musketeers were still asleep, had gone down into the
cellar, convinced by their silence that they were all in a deep
slumber. Then he had run to the train, impetuous as a man who is
excited by revenge, and full of confidence, as are those whom God
blinds, he had set fire to the wick of nitre.

All this while Groslow and his men were assembled on deck.

芒聙聹Haul up the cable and draw the boat to us,芒聙聺 said Groslow.

One of the sailors got down the side of the ship, seized the cable, and
drew it; it came without the least resistance.

芒聙聹The cable is cut!芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹no boat!芒聙聺

芒聙聹How! no boat!芒聙聺 exclaimed Groslow; 芒聙聹it is impossible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true, however,芒聙聺 answered the sailor; 芒聙聹there芒聙聶s nothing in the wake
of the ship; besides, here芒聙聶s the end of the cable.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What芒聙聶s the matter?芒聙聺 cried Mordaunt, who, coming up out of the
hatchway, rushed to the stern, waving his torch.

芒聙聹Only that our enemies have escaped; they have cut the cord and gone
off with the boat.芒聙聺

Mordaunt bounded with one step to the cabin and kicked open the door.

芒聙聹Empty!芒聙聺 he exclaimed; 芒聙聹the infernal demons!芒聙聺

芒聙聹We must pursue them,芒聙聺 said Groslow, 芒聙聹they can芒聙聶t be gone far, and we
will sink them, passing over them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but the fire,芒聙聺 ejaculated Mordaunt; 芒聙聹I have lighted it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ten thousand devils!芒聙聺 cried Groslow, rushing to the hatchway; 芒聙聹perhaps
there is still time to save us.芒聙聺

Mordaunt answered only by a terrible laugh, threw his torch into the
sea and plunged in after it. The instant Groslow put his foot upon the
hatchway steps the ship opened like the crater of a volcano. A burst of
flame rose toward the skies with an explosion like that of a hundred
cannon; the air burned, ignited by flaming embers, then the frightful
lightning disappeared, the brands sank, one after another, into the
abyss, where they were extinguished, and save for a slight vibration in
the air, after a few minutes had elapsed one would have thought that
nothing had happened.

Only芒聙聰the felucca had disappeared from the surface of the sea and
Groslow and his three sailors were consumed.

The four friends saw all this芒聙聰not a single detail of this fearful scene
escaped them. At one moment, bathed as they were in a flood of
brilliant light, which illumined the sea for the space of a league,
they might each be seen, each by his own peculiar attitude and manner
expressing the awe which, even in their hearts of bronze, they could
not help experiencing. Soon a torrent of vivid sparks fell around
them芒聙聰then, at last, the volcano was extinguished芒聙聰then all was dark and
still芒聙聰the floating bark and heaving ocean.

They sat silent and dejected.

芒聙聹By Heaven!芒聙聺 at last said Athos, the first to speak, 芒聙聹by this time, I
think, all must be over.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Here, my lords! save me! help!芒聙聺 cried a voice, whose mournful accents,

All looked around; Athos himself stared.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis he! it is his voice!芒聙聺

All still remained silent, the eyes of all were turned in the direction
where the vessel had disappeared, endeavoring in vain to penetrate the
darkness. After a minute or two they were able to distinguish a man,
who approached them, swimming vigorously.

Athos extended his arm toward him, pointing him out to his companions.

芒聙聹Yes, yes, I see him well enough,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹He芒聙聰again!芒聙聺 cried Porthos, who was breathing like a blacksmith芒聙聶s

芒聙聹Oh, my God!芒聙聺 muttered Athos.

Aramis and D芒聙聶Artagnan whispered to each other.

Mordaunt made several strokes more, and raising his arm in sign of

The voice that implored aid was so piteous that it awakened pity in the
heart of Athos.

芒聙聹Poor fellow!芒聙聺 he exclaimed.

芒聙聹Oh! you will not abandon me! You will not leave me to perish! You will

D芒聙聶Artagnan, however, who had consulted with Aramis, spoke to the poor

Then, as if his strength failed him, he seemed unable to sustain

芒聙聶Twas the first time D芒聙聶Artagnan had ever refused a request from Athos.

Aramis calmly drew his sword, which he had carried between his teeth as

芒聙聹If he lays his hand on the boat芒聙聶s edge I will cut it off, regicide

D芒聙聶Artagnan began to be touched. The boat at this moment turned around,

which, in its turn, was soon effaced, alone indicated the spot where
these two had sunk.

Mute with horror, the three friends had remained open-mouthed, their

were, the beating of their hearts was audible. Porthos was the first

men was again obvious, and first were seen, rising above the waves, a
wisp of hair, then a pale face with open eyes, yet, nevertheless, the
eyes of death; then a body, which, after rising of itself even to the
waist above the sea, turned gently on its back, according to the
caprice of the waves, and floated.

In the bosom of this corpse was plunged a poniard, the gold hilt of
which shone in the moonbeams.

Chapter LXIV.
How Mousqueton had a Narrow Escape of being eaten.


A deep silence reigned for a long time in the boat after the fearful
scene described.

The moon, which had shone for a short time, disappeared behind the
clouds; every object was again plunged in the obscurity that is so
awful in the deserts and still more so in that liquid desert, the
ocean, and nothing was heard save the whistling of the west wind
driving along the tops of the crested billows.

Porthos was the first to speak.

芒聙聹I have seen,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹many dreadful things, but nothing that ever
agitated me so much as what I have just witnessed. Nevertheless, even
in my present state of perturbation, I protest that I feel happy. I
have a hundred pounds芒聙聶 weight less upon my chest. I breathe more
freely.芒聙聺 In fact, Porthos breathed so loud as to do credit to the free
play of his powerful lungs.

芒聙聹For my part,芒聙聺 observed Aramis, 芒聙聹I cannot say the same as you do,
Porthos. I am still terrified to such a degree that I scarcely believe

芒聙聹Oh! I feel easy,芒聙聺 replied Porthos. 芒聙聹The poniard was pointed at the

Mousqueton heaved a deep sigh.

us good swimmers, able enough to turn it over again, or if not, to hold

fisherman who will pick us up.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But should we not find any fisherman and should the wind shift to the

Mousqueton sighed again, more deeply than before.

芒聙聹I am cold, sir,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton.

芒聙聹Impossible! your body is covered with a coating of fat which preserves

芒聙聹Ah! sir, 芒聙聶tis this very coating of fat that makes me shiver.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Alas! your honor, in the library of the Ch脙垄teau of Bracieux there are

In a few moments, notwithstanding their wet clothes, the icy blast that

hours of repose the sleepers were aroused by Athos.

Dawn was shedding its pallid, placid glimmer on the purple ocean, when

芒聙聹A bark!芒聙聺 all cried together.

A quarter of an hour afterward the rowboat of this craft took them all

芒聙聹Egad! how strong one feels here!芒聙聺 said Porthos, almost burying his

芒聙聹I shall not trust to that,芒聙聺 said the lieutenant, 芒聙聹and I shall make for

* Sandy hills about Dunkirk, from which it derives its name.

The party followed him and soon disappeared with him behind the

芒聙聹Leave that to me芒聙聰I have my plan. He laughs best who laughs last.
Cromwell is mighty, Mazarin is tricky, but I would rather have to do
with them than with the late Monsieur Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹it is very pleasant to be able to say 芒聙聵the late
Monsieur Mordaunt.芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹My faith, yes,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹But we must be going.芒聙聺

The two immediately started across country toward the road to Paris,
followed by Mousqueton, who, after being too cold all night, at the end
of a quarter of an hour found himself too warm.




Chapter LXXV.
The Return.


During the six weeks that Athos and Aramis had been absent from France,
the Parisians, finding themselves one morning without either queen or
king, were greatly annoyed at being thus deserted, and the absence of
Mazarin, a thing so long desired, did not compensate for that of the
two august fugitives.

The first feeling that pervaded Paris on hearing of the flight to Saint
Germain, was that sort of affright which seizes children when they
awake in the night and find themselves alone. A deputation was
therefore sent to the queen to entreat her to return to Paris; but she
not only declined to receive the deputies, but sent an intimation by
Chancellor S脙漏guier, implying that if the parliament did not humble
itself before her majesty by negativing all the questions that had been
the cause of the quarrel, Paris would be besieged the very next day.

This threatening answer, unluckily for the court, produced quite a
different effect to that which was intended. It wounded the pride of
the parliament, which, supported by the citizens, replied by declaring
that Cardinal Mazarin was the cause of all the discontent; denounced
him as the enemy both of the king and the state, and ordered him to
retire from the court that same day and from France within a week
afterward; enjoining, in case of disobedience on his part, all the
subjects of the king to pursue and take him.

Mazarin being thus placed beyond the pale of the protection of the law,
preparations on both sides were commenced芒聙聰by the queen, to attack
Paris, by the citizens, to defend it. The latter were occupied in
breaking up the pavement and stretching chains across the streets,
when, headed by the coadjutor, appeared the Prince de Conti (the
brother of the Prince de Cond脙漏) and the Duc de Longueville, his
brother-in-law. This unexpected band of auxiliaries arrived in Paris on
the tenth of January and the Prince of Conti was named, but not until
after a stormy discussion, generalissimo of the army of the king, out
of Paris.

As for the Duc de Beaufort, he arrived from Vend脙麓me, according to the
annals of the day, bringing with him his high bearing and his long and
beautiful hair, qualifications which gained him the sovereignty of the
marketplaces.

The Parisian army had organized with the promptness characteristic of
the bourgeois whenever they are moved by any sentiment whatever to
disguise themselves as soldiers. On the nineteenth the impromptu army
had attempted a sortie, more to assure itself and others of its actual
existence than with any more serious intention. They carried a banner,
on which could be read this strange device: 芒聙聹_We are seeking our
king_.芒聙聺

The next following days were occupied in trivial movements which
resulted only in the carrying off of a few herds of cattle and the
burning of two or three houses.

That was still the situation of affairs up to the early days of
February. On the first day of that month our four companions had landed
at Boulogne, and, in two parties, had set out for Paris. Toward the end
of the fourth day of the journey Athos and Aramis reached Nanterre,
which place they cautiously passed by on the outskirts, fearing that
they might encounter some troop from the queen芒聙聶s army.

It was against his will that Athos took these precautions, but Aramis
had very judiciously reminded him that they had no right to be
imprudent, that they had been charged by King Charles with a supreme
and sacred mission, which, received at the foot of the scaffold, could
be accomplished only at the feet of Queen Henrietta. Upon that, Athos
yielded.

On reaching the capital Athos and Aramis found it in arms. The sentinel
at the gate refused even to let them pass, and called his sergeant.

The sergeant, with the air of importance which such people assume when
they are clad with military dignity, said:

芒聙聹Who are you, gentlemen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Two gentlemen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And where do you come from?芒聙聺

芒聙聹From London.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And what are you going to do in Paris?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We are going with a mission to Her Majesty, the Queen of England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, every one seems to be going to see the queen of England. We have
already at the station three gentlemen whose passports are under
examination, who are on their way to her majesty. Where are your
passports?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We have none; we left England, ignorant of the state of politics here,
having left Paris before the departure of the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 said the sergeant, with a cunning smile, 芒聙聹you are Mazarinists,
who are sent as spies.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My dear friend,芒聙聺 here Athos spoke, 芒聙聹rest assured, if we were
Mazarinists we should come well prepared with every sort of passport.
In your situation distrust those who are well provided with every
formality.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Enter the guardroom,芒聙聺 said the sergeant; 芒聙聹we will lay your case before
the commandant of the post.芒聙聺

The guardroom was filled with citizens and common people, some playing,
some drinking, some talking. In a corner, almost hidden from view, were
three gentlemen, who had preceded Athos and Aramis, and an officer was
examining their passports. The first impulse of these three, and of
those who last entered, was to cast an inquiring glance at each other.
The first arrivals wore long cloaks, in whose drapery they were
carefully enveloped; one of them, shorter than the rest, remained
pertinaciously in the background.

When the sergeant on entering the room announced that in all
probability he was bringing in two Mazarinists, it appeared to be the
unanimous opinion of the officers on guard that they ought not to pass.

芒聙聹Be it so,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹yet it is probable, on the contrary, that we
shall enter, because we seem to have to do with sensible people. There
seems to be only one thing to do, which is, to send our names to Her
Majesty the Queen of England, and if she engages to answer for us I
presume we shall be allowed to enter.芒聙聺

On hearing these words the shortest of the other three men seemed more
attentive than ever to what was going on, wrapping his cloak around him
more carefully than before.

芒聙聹Merciful goodness!芒聙聺 whispered Aramis to Athos, 芒聙聹did you see?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹The face of the shortest of those three gentlemen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He looked to me芒聙聰but 芒聙聶tis impossible.芒聙聺

At this instant the sergeant, who had been for his orders, returned,
and pointing to the three gentlemen in cloaks, said:

芒聙聹The passports are in order; let these three gentlemen pass.芒聙聺

The three gentlemen bowed and hastened to take advantage of this
permission.

Aramis looked after them, and as the last of them passed close to him
he pressed the hand of Athos.

芒聙聹What is the matter with you, my friend?芒聙聺 asked the latter.

芒聙聹I have芒聙聰doubtless I am dreaming; tell me, sir,芒聙聺 he said to the
sergeant, 芒聙聹do you know those three gentlemen who are just gone out?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Only by their passports; they are three Frondists, who are gone to
rejoin the Duc de Longueville.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis strange,芒聙聺 said Aramis, almost involuntarily; 芒聙聹I fancied that I
recognized Mazarin himself.芒聙聺

The sergeant burst into a fit of laughter.

芒聙聹He!芒聙聺 he cried; 芒聙聹he venture himself amongst us, to be hung! Not so
foolish as all that.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 muttered Athos, 芒聙聹I may be mistaken, I haven芒聙聶t the unerring eye of
D芒聙聶Artagnan.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who is speaking of Monsieur D芒聙聶Artagnan?芒聙聺 asked an officer who appeared
at that moment upon the threshold of the room.

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 cried Aramis and Athos, 芒聙聹what! _Planchet!_芒聙聺

芒聙聹Planchet,芒聙聺 added Grimaud; 芒聙聹Planchet, with a gorget, indeed!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, gentlemen!芒聙聺 cried Planchet, 芒聙聹so you are back again in Paris. Oh,
how happy you make us! no doubt you come to join the princes!芒聙聺

芒聙聹As thou seest, Planchet,芒聙聺 said Aramis, whilst Athos smiled on seeing
what important rank was held in the city militia by the former comrade
of Mousqueton, Bazin and Grimaud.

芒聙聹And Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, of whom you spoke just now, Monsieur
d芒聙聶Herblay; may I ask if you have any news of him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We parted from him four days ago and we have reason to believe that he
has reached Paris before us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir; I am sure he hasn芒聙聶t yet arrived. But then he may have stopped
at Saint Germain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t think so; we appointed to meet at La Chevrette.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was there this very day.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And had the pretty Madeleine no news?芒聙聺 asked Aramis, smiling.

芒聙聹No, sir, and it must be admitted that she seemed very anxious.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In fact,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹there is no time lost and we made our journey
quickly. Permit me, then, my dear Athos, without inquiring further
about our friend, to pay my respects to M. Planchet.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, monsieur le chevalier,芒聙聺 said Planchet, bowing.

芒聙聹Lieutenant?芒聙聺 asked Aramis.

芒聙聹Lieutenant, with a promise of becoming captain.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis capital; and pray, how did you acquire all these honors?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In the first place, gentlemen, you know that I was the means of
Monsieur de Rochefort芒聙聶s escape; well, I was very near being hung by
Mazarin and that made me more popular than ever.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So, owing to your popularity芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; thanks to something better. You know, gentlemen, that I served the
Piedmont regiment and had the honor of being a sergeant?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, one day when no one could drill a mob of citizens, who began to
march, some with the right foot, others with the left, I succeeded, I
did, in making them all begin with the same foot, and I was made
lieutenant on the spot.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So I presume,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹that you have a large number of the nobles

芒聙聹Certainly. There are the Prince de Conti, the Duc de Longueville, the
Duc de Beaufort, the Duc de Bouillon, the Mar脙漏chal de la Mothe, the
Marquis de Sevign脙漏, and I don芒聙聶t know who, for my part.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And the Vicomte Raoul de Bragelonne?芒聙聺 inquired Athos, in a tremulous
voice. 芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan told me that he had recommended him to your care, in
parting.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, count; nor have I lost sight of him for a single instant since.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said Athos in a tone of delight, 芒聙聹he is well? no accident has
happened to him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹None, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And he lives?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Still at the Hotel of the Great Charlemagne.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And passes his time?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sometimes with the queen of England, sometimes with Madame de
Chevreuse. He and the Count de Guiche are like each other芒聙聶s shadows.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thanks, Planchet, thanks!芒聙聺 cried Athos, extending his hand to the
lieutenant.

芒聙聹Oh, sir!芒聙聺 Planchet only touched the tips of the count芒聙聶s fingers.

芒聙聹Well, what are you doing, count芒聙聰to a former lackey?

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹he has given me news of Raoul.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And now, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said Planchet, who had not heard what they were
saying, 芒聙聹what do you intend to do?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Re-enter Paris, if you will let us, my good Planchet.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Let you, sir? Now, as ever, I am nothing but your servant.芒聙聺 Then
turning to his men:

芒聙聹Allow these gentlemen to pass,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹they are friends of the Duc
de Beaufort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Long live the Duc de Beaufort!芒聙聺 cried the sentinels.

The sergeant drew near to Planchet.

芒聙聹What! without passports?芒聙聺 he murmured.

芒聙聹Without passports,芒聙聺 said Planchet.

芒聙聹Take notice, captain,芒聙聺 he continued, giving Planchet his expected
title, 芒聙聹take notice that one of the three men who just now went out
from here told me privately to distrust these gentlemen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I,芒聙聺 said Planchet, with dignity, 芒聙聹I know them and I answer for
them.芒聙聺

As he said this, he pressed Grimaud芒聙聶s hand, who seemed honored by the
distinction.

芒聙聹Farewell till we meet again,芒聙聺 said Aramis, as they took leave of
Planchet; 芒聙聹if anything happens to us we shall blame you for it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Planchet, 芒聙聹I am in all things at your service.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That fellow is no fool,芒聙聺 said Aramis, as he got on his horse.

芒聙聹How should he be?芒聙聺 replied Athos, whilst mounting also, 芒聙聹seeing he was
used so long to brush _your_ hats.芒聙聺




Chapter LXXVI.
The Ambassadors.


The two friends rode rapidly down the declivity of the Faubourg, but on
arriving at the bottom were surprised to find that the streets of Paris
had become rivers, and the open places lakes; after the great rains
which fell in January the Seine had overflowed its banks and the river
inundated half the capital. The two gentlemen were obliged, therefore,
to get off their horses and take a boat; and in that strange manner
they approached the Louvre.

Night had closed in, and Paris, seen thus, by the light of lanterns
flickering on the pools of water, crowded with ferry-boats of every
kind, including those that glittered with the armed patrols, with the
watchword, passing from post to post芒聙聰Paris presented such an aspect as
to strongly seize the senses of Aramis, a man most susceptible to
warlike impressions.

They reached the queen芒聙聶s apartments, but were compelled to stop in the
ante-chamber, since her majesty was at that moment giving audience to
gentlemen bringing her news from England.

芒聙聹We, too,芒聙聺 said Athos, to the footman who had given him that answer,
芒聙聹not only bring news from England, but have just come from there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What? then, are your names, gentlemen?芒聙聺

芒聙聹The Comte de la F脙篓re and the Chevalier d芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Ah! in that case, gentlemen,芒聙聺 said the footman, on hearing the names
which the queen had so often pronounced with hope, 芒聙聹in that case it is
another thing, and I think her majesty will pardon me for not keeping
you here a moment. Please follow me,芒聙聺 and he went on before, followed
by Athos and Aramis.

On arriving at the door of the room where the queen was receiving he
made a sign for them to wait and opening the door:

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I hope your majesty will forgive me for disobeying
your orders, when you learn that the gentlemen I have come to announce
are the Comte de la F脙篓re and the Chevalier d芒聙聶Herblay.芒聙聺

On hearing those two names the queen uttered a cry of joy, which the
two gentlemen heard.

芒聙聹Poor queen!芒聙聺 murmured Athos.

芒聙聹Oh, let them come in! let them come in,芒聙聺 cried the young princess,
bounding to the door.

The poor child was constant in her attendance on her mother and sought
by her filial attentions to make her forget the absence of her two sons
and her other daughter.

芒聙聹Come in, gentlemen,芒聙聺 repeated the princess, opening the door herself.

The queen was seated on a fauteuil and before her were standing two or
three gentlemen, and among them the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon, the brother of
the nobleman killed eight or nine years previously in a duel on account
of Madame de Longueville, on the Place Royale. All these gentlemen had
been noticed by Athos and Aramis in the guardhouse, and when the two
friends were announced they started and exchanged some words in a low
tone. 芒聙聹Well, sirs!芒聙聺 cried the queen, on perceiving the two friends,
芒聙聹you have come, faithful friends! But the royal couriers have been more
expeditious than you, and here are Monsieur de Flamarens and Monsieur
de Ch脙垄tillon, who bring me from Her Majesty the Queen Anne of Austria,
the very latest intelligence.芒聙聺

Aramis and Athos were astounded by the calmness, even the gayety of the
queen芒聙聶s manner.

芒聙聹Go on with your recital, sirs,芒聙聺 said the queen, turning to the Duc de
Ch脙垄tillon. 芒聙聹You said that His Majesty, King Charles, my august consort,
had been condemned to death by a majority of his subjects!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame,芒聙聺 Ch脙垄tillon stammered out.

Athos and Aramis were more and more astonished.

芒聙聹And that being conducted to the scaffold,芒聙聺 resumed the queen芒聙聰芒聙聹oh, my
lord! oh, my king!芒聙聰and that being led to the scaffold he had been saved
by an indignant people.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Just so madame,芒聙聺 replied Ch脙垄tillon, in so low a voice that though the
two friends were listening eagerly they could hardly hear this
affirmation.

The queen clasped her hands in enthusiastic gratitude, whilst her
daughter threw her arms around her mother芒聙聶s neck and kissed her芒聙聰her own
eyes streaming with tears.

芒聙聹Now, madame, nothing remains to me except to proffer my respectful
homage,芒聙聺 said Ch脙垄tillon, who felt confused and ashamed beneath the
stern gaze of Athos.

芒聙聹One moment, yes,芒聙聺 answered the queen. 芒聙聹One moment芒聙聰I beg芒聙聰for here are
the Chevalier d芒聙聶Herblay and the Comte de la F脙篓re, just arrived from

listening; conceal nothing, gloss over nothing. Since his majesty still
lives, since the honor of the throne is safe, everything else is a

Athos turned pale and laid his hand on his heart.

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, who remarked this movement and his
paleness. 芒聙聹Speak, sir! I beg you to do so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I beg you to excuse me, madame; I wish to add nothing to the recital
of these gentlemen until they perceive themselves that they have

芒聙聹Mistaken!芒聙聺 cried the queen, almost suffocated by emotion; 芒聙聹mistaken!

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 interposed Monsieur de Flamarens to Athos, 芒聙聹if we are mistaken

芒聙聹With the queen, sir?芒聙聺 replied Athos, in his calm, vibrating voice.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 murmured Flamarens, lowering his eyes.

Athos sighed deeply.

芒聙聹Or rather, sir,芒聙聺 said Aramis, with his peculiar irritating politeness,
芒聙聹the error of the person who was with you when we met you in the

Ch脙垄tillon and Flamarens started.

芒聙聹Explain yourself, count!芒聙聺 cried the queen, whose anxiety grew greater
every moment. 芒聙聹On your brow I read despair芒聙聰your lips falter ere you

芒聙聹Lord!芒聙聺 ejaculated the young princess, falling on her knees, 芒聙聹have

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Ch脙垄tillon, 芒聙聹if you bring bad tidings it will be cruel in

Aramis went so close to Ch脙垄tillon as almost to touch him.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said he, with compressed lips and flashing eyes, 芒聙聹you have not
the presumption to instruct the Comte de la F脙篓re and myself what we

During this brief altercation Athos, with his hands on his heart, his

芒聙聹Madame, princes芒聙聰who by nature are above other men芒聙聰receive from Heaven
courage to support greater misfortunes than those of lower rank, for
their hearts are elevated as their fortunes. We ought not, therefore, I
think, to act toward a queen so illustrious as your majesty as we
should act toward a woman of our lowlier condition. Queen, destined as
you are to endure every sorrow on this earth, hear the result of our
unhappy mission.芒聙聺

Athos, kneeling down before the queen, trembling and very cold, drew
from his bosom, inclosed in the same case, the order set in diamonds
which the queen had given to Lord de Winter and the wedding ring which
Charles I. before his death had placed in the hands of Aramis. Since
the moment he had first received these two mementoes Athos had never
parted with them.

He opened the case and offered them to the queen with deep and silent
anguish.

The queen stretched out her hand, seized the ring, pressed it
convulsively to her lips芒聙聰and without being able to breathe a sigh, to
give vent to a sob, she extended her arms, became deadly pale, and fell
senseless in the arms of her attendants and her daughter.

Athos kissed the hem of the robe of the widowed queen and rising, with
a dignity that made a deep impression on those around:

芒聙聹I, the Comte de la F脙篓re, a gentleman who has never deceived any human
being, swear before God and before this unhappy queen, that all that
was possible to save the king of England was done whilst we were on
English ground. Now, chevalier,芒聙聺 he added, turning to Aramis, 芒聙聹let us
go. Our duty is fulfilled.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Not yet.芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹we have still a word to say to these
gentlemen.芒聙聺

And turning to Ch脙垄tillon: 芒聙聹Sir, be so good as not to go away without
giving me an opportunity to tell you something I cannot say before the
queen.芒聙聺

Ch脙垄tillon bowed in token of assent and they all went out, stopping at
the window of a gallery on the ground floor.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹you allowed yourself just now to treat us in a

Ch脙垄tillon and Flamarens looked at each other. There was such irony in

芒聙聹They芒聙聰did you not see what they did?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No.芒聙聺

Athos curled his lip with disdain.

Athos smiled.

It was then agreed that they were to go first to Monsieur de Bouillon,

They re-entered the boat which had brought them to the Louvre and

But Raoul was not at the H脙麓tel du Grand Charlemagne. He had received a
message from the prince, to whom he had hastened with Olivain the

Chapter LXXVII.
The three Lieutenants of the Generalissimo.


The night was dark, but still the town resounded with those noises that
disclose a city in a state of siege. Athos and Aramis did not proceed a
hundred steps without being stopped by sentinels placed before the
barricades, who demanded the watchword; and on their saying that they
were going to Monsieur de Bouillon on a mission of importance a guide
was given them under pretext of conducting them, but in fact as a spy
over their movements.

On arriving at the H脙麓tel de Bouillon they came across a little troop of
three cavaliers, who seemed to know every possible password; for they
walked without either guide or escort, and on arriving at the
barricades had nothing to do but to speak to those who guarded them,
who instantly let them pass with evident deference, due probably to
their high birth.

On seeing them Athos and Aramis stood still.

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 cried Aramis, 芒聙聹do you see, count?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Who do these three cavaliers appear to you to be?芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you think, Aramis?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, they are our men.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are not mistaken; I recognize Monsieur de Flamarens.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I, Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As to the cavalier in the brown cloak芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is the cardinal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In person.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How the devil do they venture so near the H脙麓tel de Bouillon?芒聙聺

Athos smiled, but did not reply. Five minutes afterward they knocked at
the prince芒聙聶s door.

This door was guarded by a sentinel and there was also a guard placed

lieutenant.

Monsieur de Bouillon had the gout, but notwithstanding his illness,

He was in bed, but surrounded with all the paraphernalia of war.
Everywhere were swords, pistols, cuirasses, and arquebuses, and it was
plain that as soon as his gout was better Monsieur de Bouillon would
give a pretty tangle to the enemies of the parliament to unravel.
Meanwhile, to his great regret, as he said, he was obliged to keep his
bed.

芒聙聹Ah, gentlemen,芒聙聺 he cried, as the two friends entered, 芒聙聹you are very

芒聙聹He is dead, my lord!芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Dead on the scaffold; condemned by parliament.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Impossible!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And executed in our presence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What, then, has Monsieur de Flamarens been telling me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Flamarens?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, he has just gone out.芒聙聺

Athos smiled. 芒聙聹With two companions?芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹With two companions, yes,芒聙聺 replied the duke. Then he added with a

芒聙聹Why, yes, I think so芒聙聰in the street,芒聙聺 said Athos; and he looked

When they reached the street, Aramis said:

De Flamarens and De Ch脙垄tillon continued on their way and Athos and

At this Athos and Aramis could not help exchanging not only a look but

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹we are satisfied; we came here only to express

芒聙聹My most faithful friends, gentlemen, my most faithful friends; you

芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos.芒聙聺

Athos and Aramis bowed and went out.

芒聙聹My dear Athos,芒聙聺 cried Aramis, 芒聙聹I think you consented to accompany me

芒聙聹Wait a little, Aramis; it will be time for you to perceive my motive

They directed their horses to the city. On arriving at the cradle from

Thus did Athos and Aramis make their appearance in the ante-chamber,

芒聙聹Good heavens!芒聙聺 said Aramis to Athos, 芒聙聹does the coadjutor intend to

Bazin, who was crossing the ante-chamber majestically in his clerical

芒聙聹A truce to compliments,芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹we want to see the coadjutor,

芒聙聹Certainly, sir芒聙聰it is not such lords as you are who are allowed to wait

And they hastened to quit the palace, followed by Bazin, who was lavish

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Athos, when Aramis and he were in the boat again, 芒聙聹are you

What had especially been observed by the two friends was the little

attention of all Europe.

In fact, aside from a poor widow and a royal orphan who wept in the

The two friends made an appointment for ten o芒聙聶clock on the following

At ten o芒聙聶clock the next day they met again. Athos had been out since

芒聙聹Well, have you any news?芒聙聺 Athos asked.

芒聙聹Nothing. No one has seen D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos has not appeared. Have

芒聙聹Nothing.芒聙聺

芒聙聹The devil!芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Add to that D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s rapidity in action and that he is not the man

芒聙聹He expected, you will remember, to be here on the fifth.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you think should be done,芒聙聺 asked Athos, 芒聙聹if we have no news of
