芒聙聹_Pardieu!_ we must go and look for them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹All right,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹But Raoul?芒聙聺 said Aramis.

A light cloud passed over the count芒聙聶s face.

芒聙聹Raoul gives me much uneasiness,芒聙聺 he said. 芒聙聹He received yesterday a
message from the Prince de Cond脙漏; he went to meet him at Saint Cloud
and has not returned.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Have you seen Madame de Chevreuse?芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was not at home. And you, Aramis, you were going, I think, to
visit Madame de Longueville.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I did go there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺

芒聙聹She was no longer there, but she had left her new address.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where was she?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Guess; I give you a thousand chances.芒聙聺

芒聙聹How should I know where the most beautiful and active of the Frondists
was at midnight? for I presume it was when you left me that you went to
visit her.芒聙聺

芒聙聹At the H脙麓tel de Ville, my dear fellow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! at the Hotel de Ville? Has she, then, been appointed provost of
merchants?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; but she has become queen of Paris, _ad interim_, and since she
could not venture at once to establish herself in the Palais Royal or
the Tuileries, she is installed at the H脙麓tel de Ville, where she is on
the point of giving an heir or an heiress to that dear duke.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You didn芒聙聶t tell me of that, Aramis.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Really? It was my forgetfulness then; pardon me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Now,芒聙聺 asked Athos, 芒聙聹what are we to do with ourselves till evening?
Here we are without occupation, it seems to me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You forget, my friend, that we have work cut out for us in the
direction of Charenton; I hope to see Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon, whom I芒聙聶ve
hated for a long time, there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why have you hated him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because he is the brother of Coligny.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, true! he who presumed to be a rival of yours, for which he was
severely punished; that ought to satisfy you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聵Yes, but it does not; I am rancorous芒聙聰the only stigma that proves me
to be a churchman. Do you understand? You understand that you are in no
way obliged to go with me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, now,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹you are joking.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case, my dear friend, if you are resolved to accompany me
there is no time to lose; the drum beats; I observed cannon on the
road; I saw the citizens in order of battle on the Place of the H脙麓tel
de Ville; certainly the fight will be in the direction of Charenton, as
the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon said.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I supposed,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹that last night芒聙聶s conferences would modify
those warlike arrangements.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No doubt; but they will fight, none the less, if only to mask the
conferences.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Poor creatures!芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹who are going to be killed, in order
that Monsieur de Bouillon may have his estate at Sedan restored to him,
that the reversion of the admiralty may be given to the Duc de
Beaufort, and that the coadjutor may be made a cardinal.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, come, dear Athos, confess that you would not be so philosophical
if your Raoul were to be involved in this affair.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Perhaps you speak the truth, Aramis.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, let us go, then, where the fighting is, for that is the most
likely place to meet with D芒聙聶Artagnan, Porthos, and possibly even Raoul.
Stop, there are a fine body of citizens passing; quite attractive, by
Jupiter! and their captain芒聙聰see! he has the true military style.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What, ho!芒聙聺 said Grimaud.

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Planchet, sir.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Lieutenant yesterday,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹captain to-day, colonel,
doubtless, to-morrow; in a fortnight the fellow will be marshal of
France.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Question him about the fight,芒聙聺 said Athos.

Planchet, prouder than ever of his new duties, deigned to explain to
the two gentlemen that he was ordered to take up his position on the
Place Royale with two hundred men, forming the rear of the army of
Paris, and to march on Charenton when necessary.

芒聙聹This day will be a warm one,芒聙聺 said Planchet, in a warlike tone.

芒聙聹No doubt,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹but it is far from here to the enemy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, the distance will be diminished,芒聙聺 said a subordinate.

Aramis saluted, then turning toward Athos:

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t care to camp on the Place Royale with all these people,芒聙聺 he
said. 芒聙聹Shall we go forward? We shall see better what is going on.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And then Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon will not come to the Place Royale to
look for you. Come, then, my friend, we will go forward.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Haven芒聙聶t you something to say to Monsieur de Flamarens on your own
account?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹I have made a resolution never to draw my
sword save when it is absolutely necessary.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And how long ago was that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹When I last drew my poniard.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! Good! another souvenir of Monsieur Mordaunt. Well, my friend,
nothing now is lacking except that you should feel remorse for having
killed that fellow.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hush!芒聙聺 said Athos, putting a finger on his lips, with the sad smile
peculiar to him; 芒聙聹let us talk no more of Mordaunt芒聙聰it will bring bad
luck.芒聙聺 And Athos set forward toward Charenton, followed closely by
Aramis.




Chapter LXXVIII.
The Battle of Charenton.


As Athos and Aramis proceeded, and passed different companies on the
road, they became aware that they were arriving near the field of
battle.

芒聙聹Ah! my friend!芒聙聺 cried Athos, suddenly, 芒聙聹where have you brought us? I
fancy I perceive around us faces of different officers in the royal
army; is not that the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon himself coming toward us with
his brigadiers?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good-day, sirs,芒聙聺 said the duke, advancing; 芒聙聹you are puzzled by what
you see here, but one word will explain everything. There is now a
truce and a conference. The prince, Monsieur de Retz, the Duc de
Beaufort, the Duc de Bouillon, are talking over public affairs. Now one
of two things must happen: either matters will not be arranged, or they
will be arranged, in which last case I shall be relieved of my command
and we shall still meet again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹you speak to the point. Allow me to ask you a
question: Where are the plenipotentiaries?芒聙聺

芒聙聹At Charenton, in the second house on the right on entering from the
direction of Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And was this conference arranged beforehand?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, gentlemen, it seems to be the result of certain propositions which
Mazarin made last night to the Parisians.芒聙聺

Athos and Aramis exchanged smiles; for they well knew what those
propositions were, to whom they had been made and who had made them.

芒聙聹And that house in which the plenipotentiaries are,芒聙聺 asked Athos,
芒聙聹belongs to芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹To Monsieur de Chanleu, who commands your troops at Charenton. I say
your troops, for I presume that you gentlemen are Frondeurs?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, almost,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹We are for the king and the princes,芒聙聺 added Athos.

芒聙聹We must understand each other,芒聙聺 said the duke. 芒聙聹The king is with us
and his generals are the Duke of Orl脙漏ans and the Prince de Cond脙漏,
although I must add 芒聙聶tis almost impossible now to know to which party
any one belongs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 answered Athos, 芒聙聹but his right place is in our ranks, with the
Prince de Conti, De Beaufort, D芒聙聶Elbeuf, and De Bouillon; but, sir,
supposing that the conference is broken off芒聙聰are you going to try to
take Charenton?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Such are my orders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir, since you command the cavalry芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Pardon me, I am commander-in-chief.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So much the better. You must know all your officers芒聙聰I mean those more
distinguished.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, yes, very nearly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Will you then kindly tell me if you have in your command the Chevalier
d芒聙聶Artagnan, lieutenant in the musketeers?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, he is not with us; he left Paris more than six weeks ago and
is believed to have gone on a mission to England.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I knew that, but I supposed he had returned.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir; no one has seen him. I can answer positively on that point,
for the musketeers belong to our forces and Monsieur de Cambon, the
substitute for Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, still holds his place.芒聙聺

The two friends looked at each other.

芒聙聹You see,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹It is strange,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹It is absolutely certain that some misfortune has happened to them on
the way.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If we have no news of them this evening, to-morrow we must start.芒聙聺

Athos nodded affirmatively, then turning:

芒聙聹And Monsieur de Bragelonne, a young man fifteen years of age, attached
to the Prince de Cond脙漏芒聙聰has he the honor of being known to you?芒聙聺
diffident in allowing the sarcastic Aramis to perceive how strong were
his paternal feelings.

芒聙聹Yes, surely, he came with the prince; a charming young man; he is one
of your friends then, monsieur le comte?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir,芒聙聺 answered Athos, agitated; 芒聙聹so much so that I wish to see
him if possible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Quite possible, sir; do me the favor to accompany me and I will
conduct you to headquarters.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Halloo, there!芒聙聺 cried Aramis, turning around; 芒聙聹what a noise behind
us!芒聙聺

芒聙聹A body of cavaliers is coming toward us,芒聙聺 said Ch脙垄tillon.

芒聙聹I recognize the coadjutor by his Frondist hat.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I the Duc de Beaufort by his white plume of ostrich feathers.芒聙聺

芒聙聹They are coming, full gallop; the prince is with them芒聙聰ah! he is
leaving them!芒聙聺

芒聙聹They are beating the rappel!芒聙聺 cried Ch脙垄tillon; 芒聙聹we must discover what
is going on.芒聙聺

In fact, they saw the soldiers running to their arms; the trumpets
sounded; the drums beat; the Duc de Beaufort drew his sword. On his
side the prince sounded a rappel and all the officers of the royalist
army, mingling momentarily with the Parisian troops, ran to him.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 cried Ch脙垄tillon, 芒聙聹the truce is broken, that is evident;
they are going to fight; go, then, into Charenton, for I shall begin in
a short time芒聙聰there芒聙聶s a signal from the prince!芒聙聺

The cornet of a troop had in fact just raised the standard of the
prince.

芒聙聹Farewell, till the next time we meet,芒聙聺 cried Ch脙垄tillon, and he set
off, full gallop.

Athos and Aramis turned also and went to salute the coadjutor and the
Duc de Beaufort. As to the Duc de Bouillon, he had such a fit of gout
as obliged him to return to Paris in a litter; but his place was well
filled by the Duc d芒聙聶Elbeuf and his four sons, ranged around him like a
staff. Meantime, between Charenton and the royal army was left a space
which looked ready to serve as a last resting place for the dead.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 cried the coadjutor, tightening his sash, which he wore,
after the fashion of the ancient military prelates, over his
archiepiscopal simar, 芒聙聹there芒聙聶s the enemy approaching. Let us save them
half of their journey.芒聙聺

And without caring whether he were followed or not he set off; his
regiment, which bore the name of the regiment of Corinth, from the name
of his archbishopric, darted after him and began the fight. Monsieur de
Beaufort sent his cavalry, toward Etampes and Monsieur de Chanleu, who
defended the place, was ready to resist an assault, or if the enemy
were repulsed, to attempt a sortie.

The battle soon became general and the coadjutor performed miracles of
valor. His proper vocation had always been the sword and he was
delighted whenever he could draw it from the scabbard, no matter for
whom or against whom.

Chanleu, whose fire at one time repulsed the royal regiment, thought
that the moment was come to pursue it; but it was reformed and led
again to the charge by the Duc de Ch脙垄tillon in person. This charge was
so fierce, so skillfully conducted, that Chanleu was almost surrounded.
He commanded a retreat, which began, step by step, foot by foot;
unhappily, in an instant he fell, mortally wounded. De Ch脙垄tillon saw
him fall and announced it in a loud voice to his men, which raised
their spirits and completely disheartened their enemies, so that every
man thought only of his own safety and tried to gain the trenches,
where the coadjutor was trying to reform his disorganized regiment.

Suddenly a squadron of cavalry galloped up to encounter the royal
troops, who were entering, _p脙陋le-m脙陋le_, the intrenchments with the
fugitives. Athos and Aramis charged at the head of their squadrons;
Aramis with sword and pistol in his hands, Athos with his sword in his
scabbard, his pistol in his saddle-bags; calm and cool as if on the
parade, except that his noble and beautiful countenance became sad as
he saw slaughtered so many men who were sacrificed on the one side to
the obstinacy of royalty and on the other to the personal rancor of the
princes. Aramis, on the contrary, struck right and left and was almost
delirious with excitement. His bright eyes kindled, and his mouth, so
finely formed, assumed a wicked smile; every blow he aimed was sure,
and his pistol finished the deed芒聙聰annihilated the wounded wretch who
tried to rise again.

On the opposite side two cavaliers, one covered with a gilt cuirass,
the other wearing simply a buff doublet, from which fell the sleeves of
a vest of blue velvet, charged in front. The cavalier in the gilt
cuirass fell upon Aramis and struck a blow that Aramis parried with his
wonted skill.

芒聙聹Ah! 芒聙聶tis you, Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon,芒聙聺 cried the chevalier; 芒聙聹welcome to
you芒聙聰I expected you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I hope I have not made you wait too long, sir,芒聙聺 said the duke; 芒聙聹at all
events, here I am.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon,芒聙聺 cried Aramis, taking from his saddle-bags a
second pistol, 芒聙聹I think if your pistols have been discharged you are a
dead man.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank God, sir, they are not!芒聙聺

And the duke, pointing his pistol at Aramis, fired. But Aramis bent his
head the instant he saw the duke芒聙聶s finger press the trigger and the
ball passed without touching him.

芒聙聹Oh! you芒聙聶ve missed me,芒聙聺 cried Aramis, 芒聙聹but I swear to Heaven! I will
not miss you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If I give you time!芒聙聺 cried the duke, spurring on his horse and rushing
upon him with his drawn sword.

Aramis awaited him with that terrible smile which was peculiar to him
on such occasions, and Athos, who saw the duke advancing toward Aramis
with the rapidity of lightning, was just going to cry out, 芒聙聹Fire! fire,
then!芒聙聺 when the shot was fired. De Ch脙垄tillon opened his arms and fell
back on the crupper of his horse.

The ball had entered his breast through a notch in the cuirass.

芒聙聹I am a dead man,芒聙聺 he said, and fell from his horse to the ground.

芒聙聹I told you this, I am now grieved I have kept my word. Can I be of any
use to you?芒聙聺

Ch脙垄tillon made a sign with his hand and Aramis was about to dismount
when he received a violent shock; 芒聙聶twas a thrust from a sword, but his
cuirass turned aside the blow.

He turned around and seized his new antagonist by the wrist, when he
started back, exclaiming, 芒聙聹Raoul!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Raoul?芒聙聺 cried Athos.

The young man recognized at the same instant the voices of his father
and the Chevalier d芒聙聶Herblay; two officers in the Parisian forces rushed
at that instant on Raoul, but Aramis protected him with his sword.

芒聙聹My prisoner!芒聙聺 he cried.

Athos took his son芒聙聶s horse by the bridle and led him forth out of the
_m脙陋l脙漏e_.

At this crisis of the battle, the prince, who had been seconding De
Ch脙垄tillon in the second line, appeared in the midst of the fight; his
eagle eye made him known and his blows proclaimed the hero.

On seeing him, the regiment of Corinth, which the coadjutor had not
been able to reorganize in spite of all his efforts, threw itself into
the midst of the Parisian forces, put them into confusion and
re-entered Charenton flying. The coadjutor, dragged along with his
fugitive forces, passed near the group formed by Athos, Raoul and
Aramis. Aramis could not in his jealousy avoid being pleased at the
coadjutor芒聙聶s misfortune, and was about to utter some _bon mot_ more
witty than correct, when Athos stopped him.

芒聙聹On, on!芒聙聺 he cried, 芒聙聹this is no moment for compliments; or rather,
back, for the battle seems to be lost by the Frondeurs.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is a matter of indifference to me,芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹I came here only
to meet De Ch脙垄tillon; I have met him, I am contented; 芒聙聶tis something to
have met De Ch脙垄tillon in a duel!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And besides, we have a prisoner,芒聙聺 said Athos, pointing to Raoul.

The three cavaliers continued their road on full gallop.

芒聙聹What were you doing in the battle, my friend?芒聙聺 inquired Athos of the
youth; 芒聙聹芒聙聶twas not your right place, I think, as you were not equipped
for an engagement!芒聙聺

芒聙聹I had no intention of fighting to-day, sir; I was charged, indeed,
with a mission to the cardinal and had set out for Rueil, when, seeing
Monsieur de Ch脙垄tillon charge, an invincible desire possessed me to
charge at his side. It was then that he told me two cavaliers of the
Parisian army were seeking me and named the Comte de la F脙篓re.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What! you knew we were there and yet wished to kill your friend the
chevalier?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I did not recognize the chevalier in armor, sir!芒聙聺 said Raoul,
blushing; 芒聙聹though I might have known him by his skill and coolness in
danger.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Thank you for the compliment, my young friend,芒聙聺 replied Aramis, 芒聙聹we
can see from whom you learned courtesy. Then you were going to Rueil?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes! I have a despatch from the prince to his eminence.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You must still deliver it,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹No false generosity, count! the fate of our friends, to say nothing of
our own, is perhaps in that very despatch.芒聙聺

芒聙聹This young man must not, however, fail in his duty,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹In the first place, count, this youth is our prisoner; you seem to
forget that. What I propose to do is fair in war; the vanquished must
not be dainty in the choice of means. Give me the despatch, Raoul.芒聙聺

The young man hesitated and looked at Athos as if seeking to read in
his eyes a rule of conduct.

芒聙聹Give him the despatch, Raoul! you are the chevalier芒聙聶s prisoner.芒聙聺

Raoul gave it up reluctantly; Aramis instantly seized and read it.

芒聙聹You,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you, who are so trusting, read and reflect that there
is something in this letter important for us to see.芒聙聺

Athos took the letter, frowning, but an idea that he should find
something in this letter about D芒聙聶Artagnan conquered his unwillingness
to read it.

芒聙聹My lord, I shall send this evening to your eminence in order to
reinforce the troop of Monsieur de Comminges, the ten men you demand.
They are good soldiers, fit to confront the two violent adversaries
whose address and resolution your eminence is fearful of.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 cried Athos.

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹what think you about these two enemies whom it
requires, besides Comminges芒聙聶s troop, ten good soldiers to confront; are
they not as like as two drops of water to D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We芒聙聶ll search Paris all day long,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹and if we have no news
this evening we will return to the road to Picardy; and I feel no doubt
that, thanks to D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s ready invention, we shall then find some
clew which will solve our doubts.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, let us search Paris and especially inquire of Planchet if he has
yet heard from his former master.芒聙聺

芒聙聹That poor Planchet! You speak of him very much at your ease, Aramis;
he has probably been killed. All those fighting citizens went out to
battle and they have been massacred.芒聙聺

It was, then, with a sentiment of uneasiness whether Planchet, who
alone could give them information, was alive or dead, that the friends
returned to the Place Royale; to their great surprise they found the
citizens still encamped there, drinking and bantering each other,
although, doubtless, mourned by their families, who thought they were
at Charenton in the thickest of the fighting.

Athos and Aramis again questioned Planchet, but he had seen nothing of
D芒聙聶Artagnan; they wished to take Planchet with them, but he could not
leave his troop, who at five o芒聙聶clock returned home, saying that they
were returning from the battle, whereas they had never lost sight of
the bronze equestrian statue of Louis XIII.




Chapter LXXIX.
The Road to Picardy.


On leaving Paris, Athos and Aramis well knew that they would be
encountering great danger; but we know that for men like these there
could be no question of danger. Besides, they felt that the
_d脙漏nouement_ of this second Odyssey was at hand and that there remained
but a single effort to make.

Besides, there was no tranquillity in Paris itself. Provisions began to
fail, and whenever one of the Prince de Conti芒聙聶s generals wished to gain
more influence he got up a little popular tumult, which he put down
again, and thus for the moment gained a superiority over his
colleagues.

In one of these risings, the Duc de Beaufort pillaged the house and
library of Mazarin, in order to give the populace, as he put it,
something to gnaw at. Athos and Aramis left Paris after this
_coup-d芒聙聶脙漏tat_, which took place on the very evening of the day in which
the Parisians had been beaten at Charenton.

They quitted Paris, beholding it abandoned to extreme want, bordering
on famine; agitated by fear, torn by faction. Parisians and Frondeurs
as they were, the two friends expected to find the same misery, the
same fears, the same intrigue in the enemy芒聙聶s camp; but what was their
surprise, after passing Saint Denis, to hear that at Saint Germain
people were singing and laughing, and leading generally cheerful lives.
The two gentlemen traveled by byways in order not to encounter the
Mazarinists scattered about the Isle of France, and also to escape the
Frondeurs, who were in possession of Normandy and who never failed to
conduct captives to the Duc de Longueville, in order that he might
ascertain whether they were friends or foes. Having escaped these
dangers, they returned by the main road to Boulogne, at Abbeville, and
followed it step by step, examining every track.

Nevertheless, they were still in a state of uncertainty. Several inns
were visited by them, several innkeepers questioned, without a single
clew being given to guide their inquiries, when at Montreuil Athos felt
upon the table that something rough was touching his delicate fingers.
He turned up the cloth and found these hieroglyphics carved upon the
wood with a knife:

芒聙聹Port.... D芒聙聶Art.... 2d February.芒聙聺

芒聙聹This is capital!芒聙聺 said Athos to Aramis, 芒聙聹we were to have slept here,
but we cannot芒聙聰we must push on.芒聙聺 They rode forward and reached
Abbeville. There the great number of inns puzzled them; they could not
go to all; how could they guess in which those whom they were seeking
had stayed?

芒聙聹Trust me,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹do not expect to find anything in Abbeville.
If we had only been looking for Porthos, Porthos would have stationed
himself in one of the finest hotels and we could easily have traced
him. But D芒聙聶Artagnan is devoid of such weaknesses. Porthos would have
found it very difficult even to make him see that he was dying of
hunger; he has gone on his road as inexorable as fate and we must seek
him somewhere else.芒聙聺

They continued their route. It had now become a weary and almost
hopeless task, and had it not been for the threefold motives of honor,
friendship and gratitude, implanted in their hearts, our two travelers
would have given up many a time their rides over the sand, their
interrogatories of the peasantry and their close inspection of faces.

They proceeded thus to P脙漏ronne.

Athos began to despair. His noble nature felt that their ignorance was
a sort of reflection upon them. They had not looked carefully enough
for their lost friends. They had not shown sufficient pertinacity in
their inquiries. They were willing and ready to retrace their steps,
when, in crossing the suburb which leads to the gates of the town, upon
a white wall which was at the corner of a street turning around the
rampart, Athos cast his eyes upon a drawing in black chalk, which
represented, with the awkwardness of a first attempt, two cavaliers
riding furiously; one of them carried a roll of paper on which were
written these words: 芒聙聹They are following us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, 芒聙聹here it is, as clear as day; pursued as he was,
D芒聙聶Artagnan would not have tarried here five minutes had he been pressed
very closely, which gives us hopes that he may have succeeded in
escaping.芒聙聺

Aramis shook his head.

芒聙聹Had he escaped we should either have seen him or have heard him spoken
of.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are right, Aramis, let us travel on.芒聙聺

To describe the impatience and anxiety of these two friends would be
impossible. Uneasiness took possession of the tender, constant heart of
Athos, and fearful forecasts were the torment of the impulsive Aramis.
They galloped on for two or three hours as furiously as the cavaliers
on the wall. All at once, in a narrow pass, they perceived that the
road was partially barricaded by an enormous stone. It had evidently
been rolled across the pass by some arm of giant strength.

Aramis stopped.

芒聙聹Oh!芒聙聺 he said, looking at the stone, 芒聙聹this is the work of either
Hercules or Porthos. Let us get down, count, and examine this rock.芒聙聺

They both alighted. The stone had been brought with the evident
intention of barricading the road, but some one having perceived the
obstacle had partially turned it aside.

With the assistance of Blaisois and Grimaud the friends succeeded in
turning the stone over. Upon the side next the ground were scratched
the following words:

芒聙聹Eight of the light dragoons are pursuing us. If we reach Compi脙篓gne we
shall stop at the Peacock. It is kept by a friend of ours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹At last we have something definite,芒聙聺 said Athos; 芒聙聹let us go to the
Peacock.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 answered Aramis, 芒聙聹but if we are to get there we must rest our
horses, for they are almost broken-winded.芒聙聺

Aramis was right; they stopped at the first tavern and made each horse
swallow a double quantity of corn steeped in wine; they gave them three
hours芒聙聶 rest and then set off again. The men themselves were almost dead
with fatigue, but hope supported them.

In six hours they reached Compi脙篓gne and alighted at the Peacock. The
host proved to be a worthy man, as bald as a Chinaman. They asked him
if some time ago he had not received in his house two gentlemen who
were pursued by dragoons; without answering he went out and brought in
the blade of a rapier.

芒聙聹Do you know that?芒聙聺 he asked.

Athos merely glanced at it.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s sword,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Does it belong to the smaller or to the larger of the two?芒聙聺 asked the
host.

芒聙聹To the smaller.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I see that you are the friends of these gentlemen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, what has happened to them?芒聙聺

芒聙聹They were pursued by eight of the light dragoons, who rode into the
courtyard before they had time to close the gate.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Eight!芒聙聺 said Aramis; 芒聙聹it surprises me that two such heroes as Porthos
and D芒聙聶Artagnan should have allowed themselves to be arrested by eight

芒聙聹The eight men would doubtless have failed had they not been assisted
by twenty soldiers of the regiment of Italians in the king芒聙聶s service,
who are in garrison in this town so that your friends were overpowered

芒聙聹Arrested, were they?芒聙聺 inquired Athos; 芒聙聹is it known why?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, sir, they were carried off instantly, and had not even time to

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis still a consolation that they were not wounded,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Where were they taken?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Toward the town of Louvres,芒聙聺 was the reply.

The two friends having agreed to leave Blaisois and Grimaud at
Compi脙篓gne with the horses, resolved to take post horses; and having
snatched a hasty dinner they continued their journey to Louvres. Here
they found only one inn, in which was consumed a liqueur which
preserves its reputation to our time and which is still made in that
town.

芒聙聹Let us alight here,芒聙聺 said Athos. 芒聙聹D芒聙聶Artagnan will not have let slip an
opportunity of drinking a glass of this liqueur, and at the same time
leaving some trace of himself.芒聙聺

They went into the town and asked for two glasses of liqueur, at the
counter芒聙聰as their friends must have done before them. The counter was
covered with a plate of pewter; upon this plate was written with the
point of a large pin: 芒聙聹Rueil... D..芒聙聺

芒聙聹They went to Rueil,芒聙聺 cried Aramis.

芒聙聹Let us go to Rueil,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹It is to throw ourselves into the wolf芒聙聶s jaws,芒聙聺 said Aramis.

芒聙聹Had I been as great a friend of Jonah as I am of D芒聙聶Artagnan I should
have followed him even into the inside of the whale itself; and you
would have done the same, Aramis.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly芒聙聰but you make me out better than I am, dear count. Had I been
alone I should scarcely have gone to Rueil without great caution. But
where you go, I go.芒聙聺

They then set off for Rueil. Here the deputies of the parliament had
just arrived, in order to enter upon those famous conferences which
were to last three weeks, and produced eventually that shameful peace,
at the conclusion of which the prince was arrested. Rueil was crowded
with advocates, presidents and councillors, who came from the
Parisians, and, on the side of the court, with officers and guards; it
was therefore easy, in the midst of this confusion, to remain as
unobserved as any one might wish; besides, the conferences implied a
truce, and to arrest two gentlemen, even Frondeurs, at this time, would
have been an attack on the rights of the people.

The two friends mingled with the crowd and fancied that every one was
occupied with the same thought that tormented them. They expected to
hear some mention made of D芒聙聶Artagnan or of Porthos, but every one was
engrossed by articles and reforms. It was the advice of Athos to go
straight to the minister.

芒聙聹My friend,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹take care; our safety lies in our obscurity.
If we were to make ourselves known we should be sent to rejoin our
friends in some deep ditch, from which the devil himself could not take
us out. Let us try not to find them out by accident, but from our
notions. Arrested at Compi脙篓gne, they have been carried to Rueil; at
Rueil they have been questioned by the cardinal, who has either kept

are not dead, for the death of D芒聙聶Artagnan would make a sensation. As

that they are at Rueil. But what ails you? You are pale.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is this,芒聙聺 answered Athos, with a trembling voice.

芒聙聹I remember that at the Castle of Rueil the Cardinal Richelieu had some
horrible 芒聙聵oubliettes芒聙聶 constructed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh! never fear,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹Richelieu was a gentleman, our equal in
birth, our superior in position. He could, like the king, touch the
greatest of us on the head, and touching them make such heads shake on
their shoulders. But Mazarin is a low-born rogue, who can at the most
take us by the collar, like an archer. Be calm芒聙聰for I am sure that
D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos are at Rueil, alive and well.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 resumed Athos, 芒聙聹I recur to my first proposal. I know no better
means than to act with candor. I shall seek, not Mazarin, but the
queen, and say to her, 芒聙聵Madame, restore to us your two servants and our
two friends.芒聙聶芒聙聺

Aramis shook his head.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis a last resource, but let us not employ it till it is imperatively
called for; let us rather persevere in our researches.芒聙聺

They continued their inquiries and at last met with a light dragoon who
had formed one of the guard which had escorted D芒聙聶Artagnan to Rueil.

Athos, however, perpetually recurred to his proposed interview with the
queen.

芒聙聹In order to see the queen,芒聙聺 said Aramis, 芒聙聹we must first see the
cardinal; and when we have seen the cardinal芒聙聰remember what I tell you,
Athos芒聙聰we shall be reunited to our friends, but not in the way you wish.
Now, that way of joining them is not very attractive to me, I confess.
Let us act in freedom, that we may act well and quickly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I shall go,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹to the queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then,芒聙聺 answered Aramis, 芒聙聹pray tell me a day or two beforehand,

芒聙聹To whom?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Zounds! how do I know? perhaps to Madame de Longueville. She is
all-powerful yonder; she will help me. But send me word should you be
arrested, for then I will return directly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why do you not take your chance and be arrested with me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, I thank you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Should we, by being arrested, be all four together again, we should
not, I am not sure, be twenty-four hours in prison without getting

芒聙聹My friend, since I killed Ch脙垄tillon, adored of the ladies of Saint
Germain, I am too great a celebrity not to fear a prison doubly. The
queen is likely to follow Mazarin芒聙聶s counsels and to have me tried.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do you think she loves this Italian so much as they say she does?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Did she not love an Englishman?芒聙聺

芒聙聹My friend, she is a woman.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No, no, you are deceived芒聙聰she is a queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Dear friend, I shall sacrifice myself and go and see Anne of Austria.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, Athos, I am going to raise an army.芒聙聺

芒聙聹For what purpose?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To come back and besiege Rueil.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Where shall we meet again?芒聙聺

芒聙聹At the foot of the cardinal芒聙聶s gallows.芒聙聺

The two friends departed芒聙聰Aramis to return to Paris, Athos to take
measures preparatory to an interview with the queen.




Chapter LXXX.
The Gratitude of Anne of Austria.


Athos found much less difficulty than he had expected in obtaining an
audience of Anne of Austria. It was granted, and was to take place
after her morning芒聙聶s 芒聙聹levee,芒聙聺 at which, in accordance with his rights of
birth, he was entitled to be present. A vast crowd filled the
apartments of Saint Germain. Anne had never at the Louvre had so large
a court; but this crowd represented chiefly the second class of
nobility, while the Prince de Conti, the Duc de Beaufort and the
coadjutor assembled around them the first nobility of France.

The greatest possible gayety prevailed at court. The particular
characteristic of this was that more songs were made than cannons fired
during its continuance. The court made songs on the Parisians and the
Parisians on the court; and the casualties, though not mortal, were
painful, as are all wounds inflicted by the weapon of ridicule.

In the midst of this seeming hilarity, nevertheless, people芒聙聶s minds
were uneasy. Was Mazarin to remain the favorite and minister of the
queen? Was he to be carried back by the wind which had blown him there?
Every one hoped so, so that the minister felt that all around him,
beneath the homage of the courtiers, lay a fund of hatred, ill
disguised by fear and interest. He felt ill at ease and at a loss what
to do.

Cond脙漏 himself, whilst fighting for him, lost no opportunity of
ridiculing, of humbling him. The queen, on whom he threw himself as
sole support, seemed to him now not much to be relied upon.

When the hour appointed for the audience arrived Athos was obliged to
stay until the queen, who was waited upon by a new deputation from
Paris, had consulted with her minister as to the propriety and manner
of receiving them. All were fully engrossed with the affairs of the
day; Athos could not therefore have chosen a more inauspicious moment
to speak of his friends芒聙聰poor atoms, lost in that raging whirlwind.

But Athos was a man of inflexible determination; he firmly adhered to a
purpose once formed, when it seemed to him to spring from conscience
and to be prompted by a sense of duty. He insisted on being introduced,
saying that although he was not a deputy from Monsieur de Conti, or
Monsieur de Beaufort, or Monsieur de Bouillon, or Monsieur d芒聙聶Elbeuf, or
the coadjutor, or Madame de Longueville, or Broussel, or the
Parliament, and although he had come on his own private account, he
nevertheless had things to say to her majesty of the utmost importance.

The conference being finished, the queen summoned him to her cabinet.

Athos was introduced and announced by name. It was a name that too
often resounded in her majesty芒聙聶s ears and too often vibrated in her
heart for Anne of Austria not to recognize it; yet she remained
impassive, looking at him with that fixed stare which is tolerated only
in women who are queens, either by the power of beauty or by the right
of birth.

芒聙聹It is then a service which you propose to render us, count?芒聙聺 asked
Anne of Austria, after a moment芒聙聶s silence.

芒聙聹Yes, madame, another service,芒聙聺 said Athos, shocked that the queen did
not seem to recognize him.

Athos had a noble heart, and made, therefore, but a poor courtier.

Anne frowned. Mazarin, who was sitting at a table folding up papers, as
if he had only been a secretary of state, looked up.

芒聙聹Speak,芒聙聺 said the queen.

Mazarin turned again to his papers.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 resumed Athos, 芒聙聹two of my friends, named D芒聙聶Artagnan and
Monsieur du Vallon, sent to England by the cardinal, suddenly
disappeared when they set foot on the shores of France; no one knows
what has become of them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹I address myself, therefore, first to the benevolence of your majesty,
that I may know what has become of my friends, reserving to myself, if
necessary, the right of appealing hereafter to your justice.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Anne, with a degree of haughtiness which to certain
persons became impertinence, 芒聙聹this is the reason that you trouble me in
the midst of so many absorbing concerns! an affair for the police!
Well, sir, you ought to know that we no longer have a police, since we
are no longer at Paris.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I think your majesty will have no need to apply to the police to know
where my friends are, but that if you will deign to interrogate the
cardinal he can reply without any further inquiry than into his own
recollections.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But, God forgive me!芒聙聺 cried Anne, with that disdainful curl of the
lips peculiar to her, 芒聙聹I believe that you are yourself interrogating.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame, here I have a right to do so, for it concerns Monsieur
d芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聰-d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 he repeated, in such a manner as to bow the
regal brow with recollections of the weak and erring woman.

The cardinal saw that it was now high time to come to the assistance of
Anne.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I can tell you what is at present unknown to her
majesty. These individuals are under arrest. They disobeyed orders.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I beg of your majesty, then,芒聙聺 said Athos, calmly and not replying to

芒聙聹What you ask is merely an affair of discipline and does not concern

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan never made such an answer as that when the service

He was going toward the door when Mazarin stopped him.

芒聙聹You, too, have been in England, sir?芒聙聺 he said, making a sign to the
queen, who was evidently going to issue a severe order.

芒聙聹I was a witness of the last hours of Charles I. Poor king! culpable,

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Anne to Mazarin, with an accent from which daily habits of

芒聙聹I wish to do, madame, all that your majesty pleases.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Do what Monsieur de la F脙篓re requests; that is your name, is it not,

芒聙聹I have another name, madame芒聙聰I am called Athos.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, with a smile, 芒聙聹you may rest easy; your wishes

芒聙聹You hear, sir?芒聙聺 said the queen.

芒聙聹Yes, madame, I expected nothing less from the justice of your majesty.
May I not go and see my friends?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, sir, you shall see them. But, 脙聽propos, you belong to the Fronde,

芒聙聹Madame, I serve the king.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, in your own way.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My way is the way of all gentlemen, and I know only one way,芒聙聺 answered
Athos, haughtily.

芒聙聹Go, sir, then,芒聙聺 said the queen; 芒聙聹you have obtained what you wish and

Scarcely, however, had the tapestry closed behind Athos when she said

芒聙聹Cardinal, desire them to arrest that insolent fellow before he leaves

芒聙聹Your majesty,芒聙聺 answered Mazarin, 芒聙聹desires me to do only what I was
going to ask you to let me do. These bravoes who resuscitate in our

Athos was not altogether the queen芒聙聶s dupe, but he was not a man to run

his friends again. He waited, then, in the ante-chamber with

He walked to the window and looked into the court. He saw the
deputation from the Parisians enter it; they were coming to assign the

A very imposing escort awaited them without the gates.

Athos was looking on attentively, when some one touched him softly on

芒聙聹Ah! Monsieur de Comminges,芒聙聺 he said.

芒聙聹Yes, count, and charged with a commission for which I beg of you to

芒聙聹What is it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Be so good as to give me up your sword, count.芒聙聺

Athos smiled and opened the window.

芒聙聹Aramis!芒聙聺 he cried.

A gentleman turned around. Athos fancied he had seen him among the
crowd. It was Aramis. He bowed with great friendship to the count.

芒聙聹Aramis,芒聙聺 cried Athos, 芒聙聹I am arrested.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good,芒聙聺 replied Aramis, calmly.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Athos, turning to Comminges and giving him politely his
sword by the hilt, 芒聙聹here is my sword; have the kindness to keep it

ancestor by King Francis I. In his time they armed gentlemen, not
disarmed them. Now, whither do you conduct me?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Into my room first,芒聙聺 replied Comminges; 芒聙聹the queen will ultimately
decide your place of domicile.芒聙聺

Athos followed Comminges without saying a single word.




Chapter LXXXI.
Cardinal Mazarin as King.


The arrest produced no sensation, indeed was almost unknown, and
scarcely interrupted the course of events. To the deputation it was
formally announced that the queen would receive it.

Accordingly, it was admitted to the presence of Anne, who, silent and
lofty as ever, listened to the speeches and complaints of the deputies;
but when they had finished their harangues not one of them could say,
so calm remained her face, whether or no she had heard them.

On the other hand, Mazarin, present at that audience, heard very well
what those deputies demanded. It was purely and simply his removal, in
terms clear and precise.

The discourse being finished, the queen remained silent.

芒聙聹Gentlemen,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹I join with you in supplicating the queen
to put an end to the miseries of her subjects. I have done all in my
power to ameliorate them and yet the belief of the public, you say, is
that they proceed from me, an unhappy foreigner, who has been unable to
please the French. Alas! I have never been understood, and no wonder. I
succeeded a man of the most sublime genius that ever upheld the sceptre
of France. The memory of Richelieu annihilates me. In vain芒聙聰were I an
ambitious man芒聙聰should I struggle against such remembrances as he has
left; but that I am not ambitious I am going to prove to you. I own
myself conquered. I shall obey the wishes of the people. If Paris has
injuries to complain of, who has not some wrongs to be redressed? Paris
has been sufficiently punished; enough blood has flowed, enough misery
has humbled a town deprived of its king and of justice. 芒聙聶Tis not for
me, a private individual, to disunite a queen from her kingdom. Since
you demand my resignation, I retire.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 said Aramis, in his neighbor芒聙聶s ear, 芒聙聹the conferences are over.
There is nothing to do but to send Monsieur Mazarin to the most distant
frontier and to take care that he does not return even by that, nor any
other entrance into France.芒聙聺

芒聙聹One instant, sir,芒聙聺 said the man in a gown, whom he addressed; 芒聙聹a
plague on芒聙聶t! how fast you go! one may soon see that you芒聙聶re a soldier.
There芒聙聶s the article of remunerations and indemnifications to be
discussed and set to rights.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Chancellor,芒聙聺 said the queen, turning to S脙漏guier, our old acquaintance,
芒聙聹you will open the conferences. They can take place at Rueil. The
cardinal has said several things which have agitated me, therefore I
will not speak more fully now. As to his going or staying, I feel too
much gratitude to the cardinal not to leave him free in all his
actions; he shall do what he wishes to do.芒聙聺

A transient pallor overspread the speaking countenance of the prime
minister; he looked at the queen with anxiety. Her face was so
passionless, that he, as every one else present, was incapable of
reading her thoughts.

芒聙聹But,芒聙聺 added the queen, 芒聙聹in awaiting the cardinal芒聙聶s decision let there
be, if you please, a reference to the king only.芒聙聺

The deputies bowed and left the room.

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 exclaimed the queen, when the last of them had quitted the
apartment, 芒聙聹you would yield to these limbs of the law芒聙聰these advocates?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To promote your majesty芒聙聶s welfare, madame,芒聙聺 replied Mazarin, fixing
his penetrating eyes on the queen, 芒聙聹there is no sacrifice that I would
not make.芒聙聺

Anne dropped her head and fell into one of those reveries so habitual
with her. A recollection of Athos came into her mind. His fearless
deportment, his words, so firm, yet dignified, the shades which by one
word he had evoked, recalled to her the past in all its intoxication of
poetry and romance, youth, beauty, the _脙漏clat_ of love at twenty years
of age, the bloody death of Buckingham, the only man whom she had ever
really loved, and the heroism of those obscure champions who had saved
her from the double hatred of Richelieu and the king.

Mazarin looked at her, and whilst she deemed herself alone and freed
from the world of enemies who sought to spy into her secret thoughts,
he read her thoughts in her countenance, as one sees in a transparent
lake clouds pass芒聙聰reflections, like thoughts, of the heavens.

芒聙聹Must we, then,芒聙聺 asked Anne of Austria, 芒聙聹yield to the storm, buy peace,
and patiently and piously await better times?芒聙聺

Mazarin smiled sarcastically at this speech, which showed that she had
taken the minister芒聙聶s proposal seriously.

Anne芒聙聶s head was bent down芒聙聰she had not seen the Italian芒聙聶s smile; but
finding that her question elicited no reply she looked up.

芒聙聹Well, you do not answer, cardinal, what do you think about it?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I am thinking, madame, of the allusion made by that insolent
gentleman, whom you have caused to be arrested, to the Duke of
Buckingham芒聙聰to him whom you allowed to be assassinated芒聙聰to the Duchess de
Chevreuse, whom you suffered to be exiled芒聙聰to the Duc de Beaufort, whom
you imprisoned; but if he made allusion to me it was because he is
ignorant of the relation in which I stand to you.芒聙聺

Anne drew up, as she always did, when anything touched her pride. She
blushed, and that she might not answer, clasped her beautiful hands
till her sharp nails almost pierced them.

芒聙聹That man has sagacity, honor and wit, not to mention likewise that he
is a man of undoubted resolution. You know something about him, do you
not, madame? I shall tell him, therefore, and in doing so I shall
confer a personal favor on him, how he is mistaken in regard to me.
What is proposed to me would be, in fact, almost an abdication, and an
abdication requires reflection.芒聙聺

芒聙聹An abdication?芒聙聺 repeated Anne; 芒聙聹I thought, sir, that it was kings
alone who _abdicated!_芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 replied Mazarin, 芒聙聹and am I not almost a king芒聙聰king, indeed, of
France? Thrown over the foot of the royal bed, my simar, madame, looks
not unlike the mantle worn by kings.芒聙聺

This was one of the humiliations which Mazarin made Anne undergo more
frequently than any other, and one that bowed her head with shame.
Queen Elizabeth and Catherine II. of Russia are the only two monarchs
of their set on record who were at once sovereigns and lovers. Anne of
Austria looked with a sort of terror at the threatening aspect of the
cardinal芒聙聰his physiognomy in such moments was not destitute of a certain
grandeur.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 she replied, 芒聙聹did I not say, and did you not hear me say to
those people, that you should do as you pleased?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case,芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹I think it must please me best to
remain; not only on account of my own interest, but for your safety.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Remain, then, sir; nothing can be more agreeable to me; only do not
allow me to be insulted.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are referring to the demands of the rebels and to the tone in
which they stated them? Patience! They have selected a field of battle
on which I am an abler general than they芒聙聰that of a conference. No, we
shall beat them by merely temporizing. They want food already. They
will be ten times worse off in a week.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, yes! Good heavens! I know it will end in that way; but it is not
they who taunt me with the most wounding reproaches, but芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹I understand; you mean to allude to the recollections perpetually
revived by these three gentlemen. However, we have them safe in prison,
and they are just sufficiently culpable for us to keep them in prison
as long as we find it convenient. One only is still not in our power
and braves us. But, devil take him! we shall soon succeed in sending
him to join his boon companions. We have accomplished more difficult
things than that. In the first place I have as a precaution shut up at
Rueil, near me, under my own eyes, within reach of my hand, the two
most intractable ones. To-day the third will be there also.芒聙聺

芒聙聹As long as they are in prison all will be well,芒聙聺 said Anne, 芒聙聹but one
of these days they will get out.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, if your majesty releases them.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah!芒聙聺 exclaimed Anne, following the train of her own thoughts on such
occasions, 芒聙聹one regrets Paris!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why so?芒聙聺

芒聙聹On account of the Bastile, sir, which is so strong and so secure.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame, these conferences will bring us peace; when we have peace we
shall regain Paris; with Paris, the Bastile, and our four bullies shall
rot therein.芒聙聺

Anne frowned slightly when Mazarin, in taking leave, kissed her hand.

Mazarin, after this half humble, half gallant attention, went away.
Anne followed him with her eyes, and as he withdrew, at every step he
took, a disdainful smile was seen playing, then gradually burst upon
her lips.

芒聙聹I once,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹despised the love of a cardinal who never said 芒聙聵I
_shall_ do,芒聙聶 but, 芒聙聵I _have_ done so and so.芒聙聶 That man knew of retreats
more secure than Rueil, darker and more silent even than the Bastile.
Degenerate world!芒聙聺




Chapter LXXXII.
Precautions.


After quitting Anne, Mazarin took the road to Rueil, where he usually
resided; in those times of disturbance he went about with numerous
followers and often disguised himself. In military dress he was,
indeed, as we have stated, a very handsome man.

In the court of the old Ch脙垄teau of Saint Germain he entered his coach,
and reached the Seine at Chatou. The prince had supplied him with fifty
light horse, not so much by way of guard as to show the deputies how
readily the queen芒聙聶s generals dispersed their troops and to prove that
they might be safely scattered at pleasure. Athos, on horseback,
without his sword and kept in sight by Comminges, followed the cardinal
in silence. Grimaud, finding that his master had been arrested, fell
back into the ranks near Aramis, without saying a word and as if
nothing had happened.

Grimaud had, indeed, during twenty-two years of service, seen his
master extricate himself from so many difficulties that nothing less
than Athos芒聙聶s imminent death was likely to make him uneasy.

At the branching off of the road toward Paris, Aramis, who had followed
in the cardinal芒聙聶s suite, turned back. Mazarin went to the right hand
and Aramis could see the prisoner disappear at the turning of the
avenue. Athos, at the same moment, moved by a similar impulse, looked
back also. The two friends exchanged a simple inclination of the head
and Aramis put his finger to his hat, as if to bow, Athos alone
comprehending by that signal that he had some project in his head.

Ten minutes afterward Mazarin entered the court of that ch脙垄teau which
his predecessor had built for him at Rueil; as he alighted, Comminges
approached him.

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 he asked, 芒聙聹where does your eminence wish Monsieur Comte de
la F脙篓re to be lodged?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In the pavilion of the orangery, of course, in front of the pavilion
where the guard is. I wish every respect to be shown the count,
although he is the prisoner of her majesty the queen.芒聙聺

芒聙聹My lord,芒聙聺 answered Comminges, 芒聙聹he begs to be taken to the place where
Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan is confined芒聙聰that is, in the hunting lodge, opposite
the orangery.芒聙聺

Mazarin thought for an instant.

Comminges saw that he was undecided.

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis a very strong post,芒聙聺 he resumed, 芒聙聹and we have forty good men,
tried soldiers, having no connection with Frondeurs nor any interest in
the Fronde.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If we put these three men together, Monsieur Comminges,芒聙聺 said Mazarin,
芒聙聹we must double the guard, and we are not rich enough in fighting men
to commit such acts of prodigality.芒聙聺

Comminges smiled; Mazarin read and construed that smile.

芒聙聹You do not know these men, Monsieur Comminges, but I know them, first
personally, also by hearsay. I sent them to carry aid to King Charles
and they performed prodigies to save him; had it not been for an
adverse destiny, that beloved monarch would this day have been among
us.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But since they served your eminence so well, why are they, my lord
cardinal, in prison?芒聙聺

芒聙聹In prison?芒聙聺 said Mazarin, 芒聙聹and when has Rueil been a prison?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ever since there were prisoners in it,芒聙聺 answered Comminges.

芒聙聹These gentlemen, Comminges, are not prisoners,芒聙聺 returned Mazarin, with
his ironical smile, 芒聙聹only guests; but guests so precious that I have
put a grating before each of their windows and bolts to their doors,
that they may not refuse to continue my visitors. So much do I esteem
them that I am going to make the Comte de la F脙篓re a visit, that I may
converse with him _t脙陋te-脙聽-t脙陋te_, and that we may not be disturbed at
our interview you must conduct him, as I said before, to the pavilion
of the orangery; that, you know, is my daily promenade. Well, while
taking my walk I will call on him and we will talk. Although he
professes to be my enemy I have sympathy for him, and if he is
reasonable perhaps we shall arrange matters.芒聙聺

Comminges bowed, and returned to Athos, who was awaiting with apparent
calmness, but with real anxiety, the result of the interview.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 he said to the lieutenant.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 replied Comminges, 芒聙聹it seems that it is impossible.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur de Comminges,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹I have been a soldier all my life
and I know the force of orders; but outside your orders there is a
service you can render me.芒聙聺

芒聙聹I will do it with all my heart,芒聙聺 said Comminges; 芒聙聹for I know who you
are and what service you once performed for her majesty; I know, too,
how dear to you is the young man who came so valiantly to my aid when
that old rogue of a Broussel was arrested. I am entirely at your

芒聙聹Thank you, sir; what I am about to ask will not compromise you in any

芒聙聹If it should even compromise me a little,芒聙聺 said Monsieur de Comminges,
with a smile, 芒聙聹still make your demand. I don芒聙聶t like Mazarin any better
than you do. I serve the queen and that draws me naturally into the

芒聙聹Since there is no harm,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹in my knowing that D芒聙聶Artagnan is

芒聙聹I have received no orders on that point.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, then, do me the kindness to give him my regards and tell him

芒聙聹Which cannot last,芒聙聺 interrupted Comminges; 芒聙聹the cardinal said so;
there is no prison here.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But there are oubliettes!芒聙聺 replied Athos, smiling.

芒聙聹Oh! that芒聙聶s a different thing; yes, I know there are traditions of that
sort,芒聙聺 said Comminges. 芒聙聹It was in the time of the other cardinal, who
was a great nobleman; but our Mazarin芒聙聰impossible! an Italian adventurer
would not dare to go such lengths with such men as ourselves.
Oubliettes are employed as a means of kingly vengeance, and a low-born
fellow such as he is would not have recourse to them. Your arrest is
known, that of your friends will soon be known; and all the nobility of

easy on that score. I will, however, inform Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan of your
arrival here.芒聙聺

Comminges then led the count to a room on the ground floor of a

D芒聙聶Artagnan was confined, and that, level with the orangery, where Athos

Athos, when he reached his appointed room, observed through the
gratings of his window, walls and roofs; and was told, on inquiry, by

芒聙聹Yes, 芒聙聶tis too true,芒聙聺 said Comminges, 芒聙聹芒聙聶tis almost a prison; but what a

la F脙篓re, on the side of Broussel, Blancmesnil and Viole! For shame!
you, a Frondeur!芒聙聺

芒聙聹On my word of honor,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹one must be either a Mazarinist or

Viole; but with the Duc de Beaufort, the Ducs de Bouillon and d芒聙聶Elbeuf;
with princes, not with presidents, councillors and low-born lawyers.
Besides, what a charming outlook it would have been to serve the
cardinal! Look at that wall芒聙聰without a single window芒聙聰which tells you
fine things about Mazarin芒聙聶s gratitude!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 replied De Comminges, 芒聙聹more especially if it could reveal how
Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan for this last week has been anathematizing him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Poor D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶芒聙聺 said Athos, with the charming melancholy that was

芒聙聹Unruly,芒聙聺 Comminges smiled; 芒聙聹you wish to terrify me, I suppose. When he

Gascon songs, which make one die of laughing.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And Du Vallon?芒聙聺 asked Athos.

芒聙聹Ah, he芒聙聶s quite another sort of person芒聙聰a formidable gentleman, indeed.
The first day he broke all the doors in with a single push of his

芒聙聹So much the better,芒聙聺 said Athos.

芒聙聹Do you think anything else was to be expected of them?芒聙聺 asked

Athos, on the other hand, reflected that this recent gentleness of his

Unwilling to injure them by praising them too highly, he replied:
芒聙聹They? They are two hotheads芒聙聰the one a Gascon, the other from Picardy;
both are easily excited, but they quiet down immediately. You have had

This, too, was the opinion of Comminges, who withdrew somewhat
reassured. Athos remained alone in the vast chamber, where, according
to the cardinal芒聙聶s directions, he was treated with all the courtesy due
to a nobleman. He awaited Mazarin芒聙聶s promised visit to get some light on
his present situation.




Chapter LXXXIII.
Strength and Sagacity.


Now let us pass the orangery to the hunting lodge. At the extremity of
the courtyard, where, close to a portico formed of Ionic columns, were
the dog kennels, rose an oblong building, the pavilion of the orangery,
a half circle, inclosing the court of honor. It was in this pavilion,
on the ground floor, that D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos were confined,
suffering interminable hours of imprisonment in a manner suitable to
each different temperament.

D芒聙聶Artagnan was pacing to and fro like a caged tiger; with dilated eyes,
growling as he paced along by the bars of a window looking upon the
yard of servant芒聙聶s offices.

Porthos was ruminating over an excellent dinner he had just demolished.

The one seemed to be deprived of reason, yet he was meditating. The
other seemed to meditate, yet he was more than half asleep. But his
sleep was a nightmare, which might be guessed by the incoherent manner
in which he sometimes snored and sometimes snorted.

芒聙聹Look,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹day is declining. It must be nearly four
o芒聙聶clock. We have been in this place nearly eighty-three hours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hem!芒聙聺 muttered Porthos, with a kind of pretense of answering.

芒聙聹Did you hear, eternal sleeper?芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, irritated that any
one could doze during the day, when he had the greatest difficulty in
sleeping during the night.

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 said Porthos.

芒聙聹I say we have been here eighty-three hours.芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis your fault,芒聙聺 answered Porthos.

芒聙聹How, my fault?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, I offered you escape.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By pulling out a bar and pushing down a door?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Porthos, men like us can芒聙聶t go out from here purely and simply.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Faith!芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹as for me, I could go out with that purity and
that simplicity which it seems to me you despise too much.芒聙聺

D芒聙聶Artagnan shrugged his shoulders.

芒聙聹And besides,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹going out of this chamber isn芒聙聶t all.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Dear friend,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹you appear to be in a somewhat better
humor to-day than you were yesterday. Explain to me why going out of
this chamber isn芒聙聶t everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because, having neither arms nor password, we shouldn芒聙聶t take fifty
steps in the court without knocking against a sentinel.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Very well,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹we will kill the sentinel and we shall have
his arms.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but before we can kill him芒聙聰and he will be hard to kill, that
Swiss芒聙聰he will shriek out and the whole picket will come, and we shall
be taken like foxes, we, who are lions, and thrown into some dungeon,
where we shall not even have the consolation of seeing this frightful
gray sky of Rueil, which no more resembles the sky of Tarbes than the
moon is like the sun. Lack-a-day! if we only had some one to instruct
us about the physical and moral topography of this castle. Ah! when one
thinks that for twenty years, during which time I did not know what to
do with myself, it never occurred to me to come to study Rueil.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What difference does that make?芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹We shall go out all

芒聙聹Do you know, my dear fellow, why master pastrycooks never work with

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹but I should be glad to be informed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is because in the presence of their pupils they fear that some of
their tarts or creams may turn out badly cooked.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, then they would be laughed at, and a master pastrycook must never

芒聙聹And what have master pastrycooks to do with us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹We ought, in our adventures, never to be defeated or give any one a

芒聙聹By whom, then, were we beaten?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹By Mordaunt.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, but we have drowned Monsieur Mordaunt.芒聙聺

Chapter LXXXIV.
Strength and Sagacity芒聙聰Continued.


Supper was eaten in silence, but not in sadness; for from time to time
one of those sweet smiles which were habitual to him in moments of
good-humor illumined the face of D芒聙聶Artagnan. Not a scintilla of these
was lost on Porthos; and at every one he uttered an exclamation which
betrayed to his friend that he had not lost sight of the idea which
possessed his brain.

At dessert D芒聙聶Artagnan reposed in his chair, crossed one leg over the
other and lounged about like a man perfectly at his ease.

Porthos rested his chin on his hands, placed his elbows on the table

imparted to that colossus an admirable appearance of good-fellowship.

芒聙聹Well?芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, at last.

芒聙聹Well!芒聙聺 repeated Porthos.

芒聙聹You were saying, my dear friend芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹No; I said nothing.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; you were saying you wished to leave this place.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah, indeed! the will was never wanting.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To get away you would not mind, you added, knocking down a door or a

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true芒聙聰I said so, and I say it again.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I answered you, Porthos, that it was not a good plan; that we
couldn芒聙聶t go a hundred steps without being recaptured, because we were

芒聙聹That is true; we should need clothes and arms.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, rising, 芒聙聹we have them, friend Porthos, and

芒聙聹Bah!芒聙聺 said Porthos, looking around.

芒聙聹Useless to look; everything will come to us when wanted. At about what

芒聙聹An hour after sunset.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your arm is still strong enough, is it not, Porthos?芒聙聺

Porthos unbuttoned his sleeve, raised his shirt and looked complacently

芒聙聹Yes, indeed,芒聙聺 said he, 芒聙聹I believe so.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There!芒聙聺 he cried.

芒聙聹Capital!芒聙聺 exclaimed the Gascon. 芒聙聹Really, Porthos, you are a gifted

芒聙聹I have heard speak,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹of a certain Milo of Crotona, who

芒聙聹Because your strength is not in your head, Porthos,芒聙聺 said his friend.

_na脙炉vet脙漏_.

芒聙聹Well, my dear friend, let us approach the window and there you can

Porthos went to the window, took a bar in his hands, clung to it and

芒聙聹Well! friend, the cardinal, although such a genius, could never have

芒聙聹Shall I take out any more of them?芒聙聺 asked Porthos.

芒聙聹No; that is sufficient; a man can pass through that.芒聙聺

Porthos tried, and passed the upper portion of his body through.

芒聙聹Yes,芒聙聺 he said.

Chapter LXXXV.
The Oubliettes of Cardinal Mazarin.


At first, on arriving at the door through which Mazarin had passed,

Ten men would have found difficulty in moving that tub, but by some

D芒聙聶Artagnan, as we have said, perceived a hole in that place and in this

He called Porthos to look at it.

芒聙聹Were our object money only,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹we should be rich directly.芒聙聺

Mazarin had left his lamp in the gallery to make people believe that he

The two friends were crouching down, meantime, behind a tub in the side

Mazarin came within three steps of D芒聙聶Artagnan and pushed a spring in

The cardinal reached a second door without perceiving he was being

He then turned to the left, down a corridor which had escaped the

His piercing look was fixed in vain on the satirical face of the Gascon

芒聙聹To purchase back my liberty?芒聙聺 said the cardinal.

Chapter LXXXVI.
Conferences.

There was no one in the whole group in a bad humor except Cardinal
Mazarin.




Chapter LXXXVII.
Thinking that Porthos will be at last a Baron, and D芒聙聶Artagnan a Captain.


At the expiration of ten minutes Aramis arrived, accompanied by Grimaud

governor of the fortress.芒聙聺

Chapter LXXXVIII.
Shows how with Threat and Pen more is effected than by the Sword.


D芒聙聶Artagnan knew his part well; he was aware that opportunity has a
forelock only for him who will take it and he was not a man to let it
go by him without seizing it. He soon arranged a prompt and certain
manner of traveling, by sending relays of horses to Chantilly, so that
he might be in Paris in five or six hours. But before setting out he
reflected that for a lad of intelligence and experience he was in a
singular predicament, since he was proceeding toward uncertainty and
leaving certainty behind him.

芒聙聹In fact,芒聙聺 he said, as he was about to mount and start on his dangerous
mission, 芒聙聹Athos, for generosity, is a hero of romance; Porthos has an
excellent disposition, but is easily influenced; Aramis has a
hieroglyphic countenance, always illegible. What will come out of those
three elements when I am no longer present to combine them? The
deliverance of the cardinal, perhaps. Now, the deliverance of the
cardinal would be the ruin of our hopes; and our hopes are thus far the
only recompense we have for labors in comparison with which those of
Hercules were pygmean.芒聙聺

He went to find Aramis.

芒聙聹You, my dear Chevalier d芒聙聶Herblay,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹are the Fronde incarnate.
Mistrust Athos, therefore, who will not prosecute the affairs of any
one, even his own. Mistrust Porthos, especially, who, to please the
count whom he regards as God on earth, will assist him in contriving

chivalric.芒聙聺

Aramis smiled; his smile was at once cunning and resolute.

芒聙聹Fear nothing,芒聙聺 he said; 芒聙聹I have my conditions to impose. My private
ambition tends only to the profit of him who has justice on his side.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan: 芒聙聹in this direction I am satisfied.芒聙聺 He
pressed Aramis芒聙聶s hand and went in search of Porthos.

芒聙聹Friend,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you have worked so hard with me toward building up
our fortune, that, at the moment when we are about to reap the fruits

芒聙聹Bravo, Porthos! and you would be right, my friend; for in losing him

芒聙聹Never fear, dear friend,芒聙聺 said Porthos. 芒聙聹I shall see you through the

芒聙聹Bravo!芒聙聺 thought D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹on this side I think the cardinal will be

芒聙聹My dear Athos,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹I am going away. I have only one thing to

芒聙聹I needed nothing less than that consideration, my dear D芒聙聶Artagnan, to

D芒聙聶Artagnan started alone on his journey, without other escort than his

The disappearance of Mazarin was not as yet generally known. Anne of
Austria was informed of it and concealed her uneasiness from every one.
In the chamber of D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos the two soldiers had been
found bound and gagged. On recovering the use of their limbs and

inhabitants of the ch脙垄teau.

Bernouin alone knew a little more than the others. Bernouin, seeing
that his master did not return and hearing the stroke of midnight, had
made an examination of the orangery. The first door, barricaded with
furniture, had aroused in him certain suspicions, but without
communicating his suspicions to any one he had patiently worked his way

direction of Enghien. He could no longer cherish any doubt that the
cardinal had been carried off by the three prisoners, since the
prisoners had disappeared at the same time; and he had hastened to
Saint Germain to warn the queen of that disappearance.

Anne had enforced the utmost secrecy and had disclosed the event to no
one except the Prince de Cond脙漏, who had sent five or six hundred
horsemen into the environs of Saint Germain with orders to bring in any
suspicious person who was going away from Rueil, in whatsoever

Now, since D芒聙聶Artagnan did not constitute a body of horsemen, since he

obstructed in any way.

On entering the courtyard of the old ch脙垄teau the first person seen by

At the sight of D芒聙聶Artagnan, who entered the courtyard on horseback,

芒聙聹Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan!芒聙聺 cried the latter, like a man who has the

芒聙聹Himself, Monsieur Bernouin.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And why have you come here?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To bring news of Monsieur de Mazarin芒聙聰the freshest news there is.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He is as well as you and I.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Absolutely nothing. He felt the need of making a trip in the Ile de

芒聙聹Here you are.芒聙聺

芒聙聹His eminence had something to communicate to her majesty, something

Whether he spoke seriously or in jest, since it was evident that under

D芒聙聶Artagnan approached the sovereign with every mark of profound

letter

It was, however, merely a letter of introduction. The queen read it,

that simple and ingenuous air which he knew how to assume on occasions.
The queen, as he went on, looked at him with increasing astonishment.
She could not comprehend how a man could conceive such an enterprise

芒聙聹How, sir!芒聙聺 she cried, as D芒聙聶Artagnan finished, 芒聙聹you dare to tell me the

芒聙聹Pardon, madame, but I think that either I have expressed myself badly

芒聙聹I listen to you, and I wonder at you, sir,芒聙聺 said the queen. 芒聙聹In fact,

芒聙聹Your majesty, on your side,芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹is as much mistaken as

芒聙聹You are in error, sir,芒聙聺 answered the queen. 芒聙聹I am so little mistaken

芒聙聹I am sure your majesty will not commit such an act of imprudence,

would be dead; and indeed, so convinced is he of this, that he

芒聙聹Well, then, I will content myself with arresting you!芒聙聺

芒聙聹It is evident, sir, that your position has kept you out of relation to

芒聙聹Your pardon, madame, I know all that. And therefore my friends will

芒聙聹I think,芒聙聺 returned Anne of Austria, fixing upon him a glance, which in

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹I threaten simply and solely because I

芒聙聹芒聙聵I consent to ratify the treaty proposed by the Parisians.芒聙聶芒聙聺

Anne was caught, she could not draw back芒聙聰she signed; but scarcely had

D芒聙聶Artagnan started on seeing these tears. Since that period of history
queens have shed tears, like other women.

The Gascon shook his head, these tears from royalty melted his heart.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 he said, kneeling, 芒聙聹look upon the unhappy man at your feet.
He begs you to believe that at a gesture of your majesty everything

And D芒聙聶Artagnan, full of splendid pride and manly intrepidity, placed in

There are moments芒聙聰for if everything is not good, everything in this

芒聙聹You were right, sir,芒聙聺 said Anne. 芒聙聹I misunderstood you. There are the

芒聙聹I never rightly understood those men,芒聙聺 said the queen, as she watched
