In twenty-four hours D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos conducted Mazarin to the
queen; and the one received his commission, the other his patent of
nobility.

On the same day the Treaty of Paris was signed, and it was everywhere
announced that the cardinal had shut himself up for three days in order
to draw it up with the greatest care.

Here is what each of the parties concerned gained by that treaty:

Monsieur de Conti received Damvilliers, and having made his proofs as
general, he succeeded in remaining a soldier, instead of being made
cardinal. Moreover, something had been said of a marriage with
Mazarin芒聙聶s niece. The idea was welcomed by the prince, to whom it was of
little importance whom he married, so long as he married some one.

The Duc de Beaufort made his entrance at court, receiving ample
reparation for the wrongs he had suffered, and all the honor due to his
rank. Full pardon was accorded to those who had aided in his escape. He
received also the office of admiral, which had been held by his father,
the Duc de Vend脙麓me and an indemnity for his houses and castles,
demolished by the Parliament of Bretagne.

The Duc de Bouillon received domains of a value equal to that of his
principality of Sedan, and the title of prince, granted to him and to
those belonging to his house.

The Duc de Longueville gained the government of Pont-de-l芒聙聶Arche, five
hundred thousand francs for his wife and the honor of seeing her son
held at the baptismal font by the young king and Henrietta of England.

Aramis stipulated that Bazin should officiate at that ceremony and that
Planchet should furnish the christening sugar plums.

The Duc d芒聙聶Elbeuf obtained payment of certain sums due to his wife, one
hundred thousand francs for his eldest son and twenty-five thousand for
each of the three others.

The coadjutor alone obtained nothing. They promised, indeed, to
negotiate with the pope for a cardinal芒聙聶s hat for him; but he knew how
little reliance should be placed on such promises, made by the queen
and Mazarin. Quite contrary to the lot of Monsieur de Conti, unable to
be cardinal, he was obliged to remain a soldier.

And therefore, when all Paris was rejoicing in the expected return of
the king, appointed for the next day, Gondy alone, in the midst of the
general happiness, was dissatisfied; he sent for the two men whom he
was wont to summon when in especially bad humor. Those two men were the
Count de Rochefort and the mendicant of Saint Eustache. They came with
their usual promptness, and the coadjutor spent with them a part of the
night.




Chapter LXXXIX.
Difficult for Kings to return to the Capitals of their Kingdoms.


Whilst D芒聙聶Artagnan and Porthos were engaged in conducting the cardinal
to Saint Germain, Athos and Aramis returned to Paris.

Each had his own particular visit to make.

Aramis rushed to the H脙麓tel de Ville, where Madame de Longueville was
sojourning. The duchess loudly lamented the announcement of peace. War
had made her a queen; peace brought her abdication. She declared that
she would never assent to the treaty and that she wished eternal war.

But when Aramis had presented that peace to her in a true light芒聙聰that is
to say, with all its advantages; when he had pointed out to her, in
exchange for the precarious and contested royalty of Paris, the
viceroyalty of Font-de-l芒聙聶Arche, in other words, of all Normandy; when
he had rung in her ears the five hundred thousand francs promised by
the cardinal; when he had dazzled her eyes with the honor bestowed on
her by the king in holding her child at the baptismal font, Madame de
Longueville contended no longer, except as is the custom with pretty
women to contend, and defended herself only to surrender at last.

Aramis made a presence of believing in the reality of her opposition
and was unwilling to deprive himself in his own view of the credit of
her conversion.

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 he said, 芒聙聹you have wished to conquer the prince your
brother芒聙聰that is to say, the greatest captain of the age; and when women
of genius wish anything they always succeed in attaining it. You have
succeeded; the prince is beaten, since he can no longer fight. Now
attach him to our party. Withdraw him gently from the queen, whom he
does not like, from Mazarin, whom he despises. The Fronde is a comedy,
of which the first act only is played. Let us wait for a
_d脙漏nouement_芒聙聰for the day when the prince, thanks to you, shall have
turned against the court.芒聙聺

Madame de Longueville was persuaded. This Frondist duchess trusted so
confidently to the power of her fine eyes, that she could not doubt
their influence even over Monsieur de Cond脙漏; and the chronicles of the
time aver that her confidence was justified.

Athos, on quitting Aramis, went to Madame de Chevreuse. Here was
another _frondeuse_ to persuade, and she was even less open to
conviction than her younger rival. There had been no stipulation in her
favor. Monsieur de Chevreuse had not been appointed governor of a
province, and if the queen should consent to be godmother it could be
only of her grandson or granddaughter. At the first announcement of
peace Madame de Chevreuse frowned, and in spite of all the logic of
Athos to show her that a prolonged war would have been impracticable,
contended in favor of hostilities.

芒聙聹My fair friend,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹allow me to tell you that everybody is
tired of war. You will get yourself exiled, as you did in the time of
Louis XIII. Believe me, we have passed the time of success in intrigue,
and your fine eyes are not destined to be eclipsed by regretting Paris,
where there will always be two queens as long as you are there.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh,芒聙聺 cried the duchess, 芒聙聹I cannot make war alone, but I can avenge
myself on that ungrateful queen and most ambitious favorite-on the
honor of a duchess, I will avenge myself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Madame,芒聙聺 replied Athos, 芒聙聹do not injure the Vicomte de Bragelonne芒聙聰do
not ruin his prospects. Alas! excuse my weakness! There are moments
when a man grows young again in his children.芒聙聺

The duchess smiled, half tenderly, half ironically.

芒聙聹Count,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹you are, I fear, gained over to the court. I
suppose you have a blue ribbon in your pocket?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, madame; I have that of the Garter, which King Charles I. gave me
some days before he died.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Come, I am growing an old woman!芒聙聺 said the duchess, pensively.

Athos took her hand and kissed it. She sighed, as she looked at him.

芒聙聹Count,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹Bragelonne must be a charming place. You are a man
of taste. You have water芒聙聰woods芒聙聰flowers there?芒聙聺

She sighed again and leaned her charming head, gracefully reclined, on
her hand, still beautiful in form and color.

芒聙聹Madame!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, 芒聙聹what were you saying just now about growing
old? Never have I seen you look so young, so beautiful!芒聙聺

The duchess shook her head.

芒聙聹Does Monsieur de Bragelonne remain in Paris?芒聙聺 she inquired.

芒聙聹What think you of it?芒聙聺 inquired Athos.

芒聙聹Leave him with me,芒聙聺 replied the duchess.

芒聙聹No, madame; if you have forgotten the history of 脜聮dipus, I, at least,
remember it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Really, sir, you are delightful, and I should like to spend a month at

芒聙聹Are you not afraid of making people envious of me, duchess?芒聙聺 replied

芒聙聹No, I shall go _incognito_, count, under the name of Marie Michon.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You are adorable, madame.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But do not keep Raoul with you.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why not?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Because he is in love.芒聙聺

芒聙聹He! he is quite a child!芒聙聺

芒聙聹And 芒聙聶tis a child he loves.芒聙聺

Athos became thoughtful.

芒聙聹You are right, duchess. This singular passion for a child of seven may

芒聙聹And at his return you will send him to me. I will arm him against

芒聙聹Alas, madame!芒聙聺 exclaimed Athos, 芒聙聹to-day love is like war芒聙聰the
breastplate is becoming useless.芒聙聺

Raoul entered at this moment; he came to announce that the solemn
entrance of the king, queen, and her ministers was to take place on the
ensuing day.

The next day, in fact, at daybreak, the court made preparations to quit
Saint Germain.

Meanwhile, the queen every hour had been sending for D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹I hear,芒聙聺 she said, 芒聙聹that Paris is not quiet. I am afraid for the
king芒聙聶s safety; place yourself close to the coach door on the right.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Reassure yourself, madame, I will answer for the king芒聙聶s safety.芒聙聺

As he left the queen芒聙聶s presence Bernouin summoned him to the cardinal.

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Mazarin to him 芒聙聹an _脙漏meute_ is spoken of in Paris. I shall
be on the king芒聙聶s left and as I am the chief person threatened, remain
at the coach door to the left.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Your eminence may be perfectly easy,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan; 芒聙聹they will
not touch a hair of your head.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Deuce take it!芒聙聺 he thought to himself, 芒聙聹how can I take care of both?
Ah! plague on芒聙聶t, I will guard the king and Porthos shall guard the
cardinal.芒聙聺

This arrangement pleased every one. The queen had confidence in the

of Porthos, which he had experienced.

The royal procession set out for Paris. Guitant and Comminges, at the

D芒聙聶Artagnan on one side, Porthos on the other; then the musketeers, for
two and twenty years staunch friends of D芒聙聶Artagnan. During twenty he
had been lieutenant, their captain since the night before.

The cort脙篓ge proceeded to Notre Dame, where a _Te Deum_ was chanted. All
Paris were in the streets. The Swiss were drawn up along the road, but
as the road was long, they were placed at six or eight feet distant
from each other and one deep only. This force was therefore wholly
insufficient, and from time to time the line was broken through by the
people and was formed again with difficulty. Whenever this occurred,
although it proceeded only from goodwill and a desire to see the king
and queen, Anne looked at D芒聙聶Artagnan anxiously.

Mazarin, who had dispensed a thousand louis to make the people cry
芒聙聹Long live Mazarin,芒聙聺 and who had accordingly no confidence in
acclamations bought at twenty pistoles each, kept one eye on Porthos;
but that gigantic body-guard replied to the look with his great bass
voice, 芒聙聹Be tranquil, my lord,芒聙聺 and Mazarin became more and more
composed.

At the Palais Royal, the crowd, which had flowed in from the adjacent
street was still greater; like an impetuous mob, a wave of human beings
came to meet the carriage and rolled tumultuously into the Rue Saint
Honor脙漏.

When the procession reached the palace, loud cries of 芒聙聹Long live their
majesties!芒聙聺 resounded. Mazarin leaned out of the window. One or two
shouts of 芒聙聹Long live the cardinal芒聙聺 saluted his shadow; but instantly
hisses and yells stifled them remorselessly. Mazarin turned pale and
shrank back in the coach.

芒聙聹Low-born fellows!芒聙聺 ejaculated Porthos.

D芒聙聶Artagnan said nothing, but twirled his mustache with a peculiar
gesture which showed that his fine Gascon humor was awake.

Anne of Austria bent down and whispered in the young king芒聙聶s ear:

芒聙聹Say something gracious to Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan, my son.芒聙聺

The young king leaned toward the door.

芒聙聹I have not said good-morning to you, Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 he said;
芒聙聹nevertheless, I have remarked you. It was you who were behind my
bed-curtains that night the Parisians wished to see me asleep.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And if the king permits me,芒聙聺 returned the Gascon, 芒聙聹I shall be near him
always when there is danger to be encountered.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Sir,芒聙聺 said Mazarin to Porthos, 芒聙聹what would you do if the crowd fell
upon us?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Kill as many as I could, my lord.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Hem! brave as you are and strong as you are, you could not kill them

芒聙聹芒聙聶Tis true,芒聙聺 answered Porthos, rising on his saddle, in order that he

芒聙聹I think I should like the other fellow better than this one,芒聙聺 said

The queen and her minister, more especially the latter, had reason to

they pass athwart a multitude, presage an _脙漏meute_.

D芒聙聶Artagnan turned toward the musketeers and made a sign imperceptible
to the crowd, but very easily understood by that chosen regiment, the
flower of the army.

The ranks closed firmly in and a kind of majestic tremor ran from man
to man.

At the Barri脙篓re des Sergents the procession was obliged to stop.
Comminges left the head of the escort and went to the queen芒聙聶s carriage.
Anne questioned D芒聙聶Artagnan by a look. He answered in the same language.

芒聙聹Proceed,芒聙聺 she said.

Comminges returned to his post. An effort was made and the living
barrier was violently broken through.

Some complaints arose from the crowd and were addressed this time to
the king as well as the minister.

芒聙聹Onward!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, in a loud voice.

芒聙聹Onward!芒聙聺 cried Porthos.

But as if the multitude had waited only for this demonstration to burst

At the same time through the streets of Grenelle, Saint Honor脙漏, and Du
Coq, a double stream of people broke the feeble hedge of Swiss guards

that of D芒聙聶Artagnan.

This new eruption was more dangerous than the others, being composed of
armed men. It was plain that it was not the chance combination of those
who had collected a number of the malcontents at the same spot, but a
concerted organized attack.

Each of these mobs was led by a chief, one of whom appeared to belong,
not to the people, but to the honorable corporation of mendicants, and
the other, notwithstanding his affected imitation of the people, might
easily be discerned to be a gentleman. Both were evidently stimulated
by the same impulse.

There was a shock which was perceived even in the royal carriage.
Myriads of hoarse cries, forming one vast uproar, were heard, mingled
with guns firing.

芒聙聹Ho! Musketeers!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan.

The escort divided into two files. One of them passed around to the

D芒聙聶Artagnan, the other Porthos. Then came a skirmish, the more terrible
because it had no definite object; the more melancholy, because those
engaged in it knew not for whom they were fighting. Like all popular
movements, the shock given by the rush of this mob was formidable. The
musketeers, few in number, not being able, in the midst of this crowd,
to make their horses wheel around, began to give way. D芒聙聶Artagnan
offered to lower the blinds of the royal carriage, but the young king

芒聙聹No, sir! I wish to see everything.芒聙聺

芒聙聹If your majesty wishes to look out芒聙聰well, then, look!芒聙聺 replied
D芒聙聶Artagnan. And turning with that fury which made him so formidable, he

芒聙聹Make room!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan. 芒聙聹Zounds! give way!芒聙聺

At these words the man with a pistol and sword raised his head, but it
was too late. The blow was sped by D芒聙聶Artagnan; the rapier had pierced
his bosom.

芒聙聹Ah! confound it!芒聙聺 cried the Gascon, trying in vain, too late, to
retract the thrust. 芒聙聹What the devil are you doing here, count?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Accomplishing my destiny,芒聙聺 replied Rochefort, falling on one knee. 芒聙聹I
have already got up again after three stabs from you, I shall never
rise after this fourth.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Count!芒聙聺 said D芒聙聶Artagnan, with some degree of emotion, 芒聙聹I struck
without knowing that it was you. I am sorry, if you die, that you

Rochefort extended his hand to D芒聙聶Artagnan, who took it. The count
wished to speak, but a gush of blood stifled him. He stiffened in the

芒聙聹Back, people!芒聙聺 cried D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹your leader is dead; you have no

Indeed, as if De Rochefort had been the very soul of the attack, the

D芒聙聶Artagnan returned to help Porthos, if Porthos needed help; but
Porthos, for his part, had done his work as conscientiously as
D芒聙聶Artagnan. The left of the carriage was as well cleared as the right,
and they drew up the blind of the window which Mazarin, less heroic

Porthos looked very melancholy.

芒聙聹What a devil of a face you have, Porthos! and what a strange air for a
victor!芒聙聺

芒聙聹But you,芒聙聺 answered Porthos, 芒聙聹seem to me agitated.芒聙聺

芒聙聹There芒聙聶s a reason! Zounds! I have just killed an old friend.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Indeed!芒聙聺 replied Porthos, 芒聙聹who?芒聙聺

芒聙聹That poor Count de Rochefort.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well! exactly like me! I have just killed a man whose face is not
unknown to me. Unluckily, I hit him on the head and immediately his
face was covered with blood.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And he said nothing as he died?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes; he exclaimed, 芒聙聵Oh!芒聙聶芒聙聺

芒聙聹I suppose,芒聙聺 answered D芒聙聶Artagnan, laughing, 芒聙聹if he only said that, it
did not enlighten you much.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, sir!芒聙聺 cried the queen.

芒聙聹Madame, the passage is quite clear and your majesty can continue your
road.芒聙聺

In fact, the procession arrived, in safety at Notre Dame, at the front

chanted a _Te Deum_.

As the service was drawing to a close a boy entered the church in great
excitement, ran to the sacristy, dressed himself quickly in the choir
robes, and cleaving, thanks to that uniform, the crowd that filled the
temple, approached Bazin, who, clad in his blue robe, was standing
gravely in his place at the entrance to the choir.

Bazin felt some one pulling his sleeve. He lowered to earth his eyes,
beatifically raised to Heaven, and recognized Friquet.

芒聙聹Well, you rascal, what is it? How do you dare to disturb me in the
exercise of my functions?芒聙聺 asked the beadle.

芒聙聹Monsieur Bazin,芒聙聺 said Friquet, 芒聙聹Monsieur Maillard芒聙聰you know who he is,
he gives holy water at Saint Eustache芒聙聰芒聙聰芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, go on.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, he received in the scrimmage a sword stroke on the head. That
great giant who was there gave it to him.芒聙聺

芒聙聹In that case,芒聙聺 said Bazin, 芒聙聹he must be pretty sick.芒聙聺

芒聙聹So sick that he is dying, and he wants to confess to the coadjutor,
who, they say, has power to remit great sins.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And does he imagine that the coadjutor will put himself out for him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To be sure; the coadjutor has promised.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Who told you that?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Monsieur Maillard himself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You have seen him, then?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Certainly; I was there when he fell.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What were you doing there?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I was shouting, 芒聙聵Down with Mazarin!芒聙聶 芒聙聵Death to the cardinal!芒聙聶 芒聙聵The
Italian to the gallows!芒聙聶 Isn芒聙聶t that what you would have me shout?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Be quiet, you rascal!芒聙聺 said Bazin, looking uneasily around.

芒聙聹So that he told me, that poor Monsieur Maillard, 芒聙聵Go find the
coadjutor, Friquet, and if you bring him to me you shall be my heir.芒聙聶
Say, then, Father Bazin芒聙聰the heir of Monsieur Maillard, the giver of
holy water at Saint Eustache! Hey! I shall have nothing to do but to
fold my arms! All the same, I should like to do him that service芒聙聰what

芒聙聹I will tell the coadjutor,芒聙聺 said Bazin.

In fact, he slowly and respectfully approached the prelate and spoke to

affirmative sign. He then returned with the same slow step and said:

芒聙聹Go and tell the dying man that he must be patient. Monseigneur will be
with him in an hour.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Good!芒聙聺 said Friquet, 芒聙聹my fortune is made.芒聙聺

芒聙聹By the way,芒聙聺 said Bazin, 芒聙聹where was he carried?芒聙聺

芒聙聹To the tower Saint Jacques la Boucherie;芒聙聺 and delighted with the
success of his embassy, Friquet started off at the top of his speed.

When the _Te Deum_ was over, the coadjutor, without stopping to change
his priestly dress, took his way toward that old tower which he knew so
well. He arrived in time. Though sinking from moment to moment, the
wounded man was not yet dead. The door was opened to the coadjutor of
the room in which the mendicant was suffering.

A moment later Friquet went out, carrying in his hand a large leather
bag; he opened it as soon as he was outside the chamber and to his
great astonishment found it full of gold. The mendicant had kept his
word and made Friquet his heir.

芒聙聹Ah! Mother Nanette!芒聙聺 cried Friquet, suffocating; 芒聙聹ah! Mother Nanette!芒聙聺

He could say no more; but though he hadn芒聙聶t strength to speak he had
enough for action. He rushed headlong to the street, and like the Greek
from Marathon who fell in the square at Athens, with his laurel in his
hand, Friquet reached Councillor Broussel芒聙聶s threshold, and then fell
exhausted, scattering on the floor the louis disgorged by his leather
bag.

Mother Nanette began by picking up the louis; then she picked up
Friquet.

In the meantime the cort脙篓ge returned to the Palais Royal.

芒聙聹That Monsieur d芒聙聶Artagnan is a very brave man, mother,芒聙聺 said the young
king.

芒聙聹Yes, my son; and he rendered very important services to your father.
Treat him kindly, therefore, in the future.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Captain,芒聙聺 said the young king to D芒聙聶Artagnan, on descending from the

That was a great honor for D芒聙聶Artagnan and for Porthos. Porthos was

芒聙聹What was the matter with you, baron?芒聙聺 D芒聙聶Artagnan said to him as they

芒聙聹I was trying,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹to recall where I had seen that

芒聙聹And you couldn芒聙聶t remember?芒聙聺

芒聙聹No.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Well, search, my friend, search; and when you have found, you will

芒聙聹_Pardieu!_芒聙聺 said Porthos.




Chapter XC.
Conclusion.


On going home, the two friends found a letter from Athos, who desired
them to meet him at the Grand Charlemagne on the following day.

The friends went to bed early, but neither of them slept. When we
arrive at the summit of our wishes, success has usually the power to
drive away sleep on the first night after the fulfilment of long
cherished hopes.

The next day at the appointed hour they went to see Athos and found him
and Aramis in traveling costume.

芒聙聹What!芒聙聺 cried Porthos, 芒聙聹are we all going away, then? I also have made
my preparations this morning.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Oh, heavens! yes,芒聙聺 said Aramis. 芒聙聹There芒聙聶s nothing to do in Paris now
there芒聙聶s no Fronde. The Duchess de Longueville has invited me to pass a
few days in Normandy, and has deputed me, while her son is being
baptized, to go and prepare her residence at Rouen; after which, if
nothing new occurs, I shall go and bury myself in my convent at
Noisy-le-Sec.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And I,芒聙聺 said Athos, 芒聙聹am returning to Bragelonne. You know, dear
D芒聙聶Artagnan, I am nothing more than a good honest country gentleman.
Raoul has no fortune other than I possess, poor child! and I must take
care of it for him, since I only lend him my name.芒聙聺

芒聙聹And Raoul芒聙聰what shall you do with him?芒聙聺

芒聙聹I leave him with _you_, my friend. War has broken out in Flanders. You
shall take him with you there. I am afraid that remaining at Blois
would be dangerous to his youthful mind. Take him and teach him to be
as brave and loyal as you are yourself.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Then,芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹though I shall not have _you_, Athos, at
all events I shall have that dear fair-haired head by me; and though
he芒聙聶s but a boy, yet, since your soul lives again in him, dear Athos, I
shall always fancy that you are near me, sustaining and encouraging
me.芒聙聺

The four friends embraced with tears in their eyes.

Then they departed, without knowing whether they would ever see each
other again.

D芒聙聶Artagnan returned to the Rue Tiquetonne with Porthos, still possessed
by the wish to find out who the man was that he had killed. On arriving
at the H脙麓tel de la Chevrette they found the baron芒聙聶s equipage all ready
and Mousqueton on his saddle.

芒聙聹Come, D芒聙聶Artagnan,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹bid adieu to your sword and go with
me to Pierrefonds, to Bracieux, or to Du Vallon. We will grow old
together and talk of our companions.芒聙聺

芒聙聹No!芒聙聺 replied D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹deuce take it, the campaign is going to
begin; I wish to be there, I expect to get something by it.芒聙聺

芒聙聹What do you expect to get?芒聙聺

芒聙聹Why, I expect to be made Mar脙漏chal of France!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ha! ha!芒聙聺 cried Porthos, who was not completely taken in by
D芒聙聶Artagnan芒聙聶s Gasconades.

芒聙聹Come my brother, go with me,芒聙聺 added D芒聙聶Artagnan, 芒聙聹and I will see that
you are made a duke!芒聙聺

芒聙聹No,芒聙聺 answered Porthos, 芒聙聹Mouston has no desire to fight; besides, they
have erected a triumphal arch for me to enter my barony, which will
kill my neighbors with envy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹To that I can say nothing,芒聙聺 returned D芒聙聶Artagnan, who knew the vanity
of the new baron. 芒聙聹Then, here芒聙聶s to our next merry meeting!芒聙聺

芒聙聹Adieu, dear captain,芒聙聺 said Porthos, 芒聙聹I shall always be happy to
welcome you to my barony.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Yes, yes, when the campaign is over,芒聙聺 replied the Gascon.

芒聙聹His honor芒聙聶s equipage is waiting,芒聙聺 said Mousqueton.

The two friends, after a cordial pressure of the hands, separated.
D芒聙聶Artagnan was standing at the door looking after Porthos with a
mournful gaze, when the baron, after walking scarcely more than twenty
paces, returned芒聙聰stood still芒聙聰struck his forehead with his finger and
exclaimed:

芒聙聹I recollect!芒聙聺

芒聙聹What?芒聙聺 inquired D芒聙聶Artagnan.

芒聙聹Who the beggar was that I killed.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Ah! indeed! and who was he?芒聙聺

芒聙聹芒聙聶Twas that low fellow, Bonacieux.芒聙聺

And Porthos, enchanted at having relieved his mind, rejoined Mousqueton
and they disappeared around an angle of the street. D芒聙聶Artagnan stood
for an instant, mute, pensive and motionless; then, as he went in, he
saw the fair Madeleine, his hostess, standing on the threshold.

芒聙聹Madeleine,芒聙聺 said the Gascon, 芒聙聹give me your apartment on the first
floor; now that I am a captain in the royal musketeers I must make an
appearance; nevertheless, reserve my old room on the fifth story for
me; one never knows what may happen.芒聙聺
